resortCharacterDescription: Vallorcine is a traditional Savoyard village with wooden chalets, a historic church, and a quiet, lived-in atmosphere. Le Tour is a smaller hamlet at the base of the gondola. Both retain authentic Alpine character, unlike the purpose-built resorts elsewhere in the valley. The area is part of the Chamonix valley but feels more rural and peaceful.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system comprises 13 lifts, including two gondolas from the villages of Le Tour and Vallorcine, two chairlifts, and nine drag lifts. The gondolas provide quick access to the upper slopes, and the network is generally uncrowded, with few queues outside peak periods. The lifts are modern but not all high-speed.
linkedResortsDescription: The Balme-Vallorcine ski area is part of the larger Chamonix valley ski region. It is directly connected to the Charamillon sector in Switzerland, allowing cross-border skiing. With the Mont Blanc Unlimited pass, skiers can access other Chamonix areas including Grands Montets, Brévent-Flégère, and Les Houches, as well as Courmayeur in Italy and Verbier in Switzerland, though these are not physically linked by lifts and require shuttle or train connections.

terrainDescription: Bardonecchia's terrain is spread across two interconnected ski areas: Colomion-Les Arnauds-Melezet and Jafferau, linked by a free ski bus. The Colomion sector features wide, well-groomed blues and reds through wooded slopes, with gentle nursery slopes at Campo Smith at the base. Runs from the top of Colomion down to Les Arnauds are wide and well-groomed with enough pitch to stay engaging. The Jafferau area is higher and more alpine, with reds that are a touch steeper and three black runs offering sustained steep pitches. The Melezet area contains the Olympic halfpipe and a snow park with jumps and rails. The terrain is predominantly intermediate, with long cruising pistes and tree-lined runs.
offPisteDescription: Off-piste terrain is accessible from the top lifts, particularly north-facing bowls above Jafferau that hold powder well after storms. There are also accessible tree lines on both sides of the resort. For serious off-piste exploration, a local guide is recommended to find the best snow.
valueDescription: Accommodation in Bardonecchia is very reasonably priced, with many hotels offering great value rooms especially during the week. Food and drink on the mountain and in town are cheap, with traditional trattorias serving hearty meals at fair prices. The resort is known for being budget-friendly, particularly for families and budget-conscious travelers.
familyDescription: Bardonecchia is highly family-oriented with dedicated beginner areas such as Campo Smith, which has easy-access lifts, a safe and contained learning zone, and dedicated programmes for young children. The ski schools are excellent, with English-speaking instructors available. Off-slope activities include a free sledging area near Campo Smith and the Bardonecchia alpine coaster. Teenagers can use the snow park with rails and jumps. The town itself is safe for wandering, with pizzerias, trattorias, and gelaterias that children enjoy.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski in Bardonecchia is low-key and relaxed, with a few local bars such as Bar Medail and Sitting Bull offering a laid-back atmosphere. The nightlife is more about sipping wine than partying, with bars clustered around Via Medail. There is no lively club scene, but the town has plenty of restaurants and bars for a quiet evening.
resortCharacterDescription: Bardonecchia is a genuine Italian town with cobbled streets, traditional trattorias, and local life, rather than a purpose-built ski resort. The village has a historic centre and is inhabited year-round, giving it an authentic alpine atmosphere. It was originally a transport town, and the atmosphere is relaxed and family-friendly.
liftSystemDescription: The lift network consists of a mix of chairlifts, surface lifts, and one gondola. Some lifts are older and slow, particularly the long button lifts, but queues are rare on weekdays. The two ski areas are connected by a free ski bus rather than by lifts. The Melezet snowpark is served by a 4-seater chairlift.
linkedResortsDescription: Bardonecchia's ski area consists of two separate sectors, Colomion-Les Arnauds-Melezet and Jafferau, which are connected by a free ski bus rather than by lifts. The resort is not directly linked to other resorts by ski lifts.

terrainDescription: The Jafferau sector is a high-altitude ski area with terrain predominantly above 2000m, consisting of long red and blue runs above the treeline. The runs are wide and groomed, with a focus on intermediate cruising. The reds are steeper than those on the Colomion side, and the snow tends to hold better due to the altitude. The return run to the base can be tricky at the end of the day. There are no advanced runs, but the terrain includes open bowls with bumps and gullies above treeline.
offPisteDescription: Off-piste terrain on Jafferau consists of north-facing bowls above the treeline that hold powder well after storms. These open bowls offer bumps, jumps, and gullies for advanced skiers. Access is from the top lifts, and a short hike can reach the summit for additional lines. Local guides can show the best stashes.
valueDescription: The Jafferau sector is covered by the Bardonecchia lift pass, which is noted for being good value compared to other Italian resorts. Accommodation and dining in Bardonecchia are reasonably priced, and the resort offers a traditional, affordable atmosphere.
familyDescription: Jafferau is not suitable for families with beginners, as the terrain is entirely intermediate and above treeline. There are no nursery slopes or easy runs. Families with intermediate skiers may find the long reds enjoyable, but the area lacks the gentle slopes and facilities needed for young children.
resortCharacterDescription: Jafferau is a high-altitude ski sector with an alpine character, featuring open terrain above the treeline and panoramic views of the Dauphiné Alps. It is accessed by a free bus from Bardonecchia town, and the atmosphere is quiet and uncrowded, especially on weekdays.
liftSystemDescription: The Jafferau sector has 5 lifts, including chairlifts and surface lifts, serving the high-altitude terrain. The lifts are generally older but queues are rare on weekdays. The area is accessed by a free bus from Bardonecchia town.
linkedResortsDescription: Jafferau is connected to the Colomion-Les Arnauds-Melezet sector of Bardonecchia by a free shuttle bus. The two sectors are not linked by lifts, so transport is required between them. Bardonecchia is a separate ski area from the nearby Via Lattea (Milky Way) ski area.

terrainDescription: The terrain is dominated by gentle, beginner-friendly slopes on the Hochälpelekopf, with 9 km of blue pistes. More experienced skiers can explore 12 km of red runs and 4 km of black descents, including the challenging Schwarzenberg descent which offers panoramic views of the surrounding landscape. The ski area is natural and family-oriented, with a mix of open slopes and tree-lined runs.
valueDescription: Day lift passes are priced at €46.50 for adults, €41.90 for seniors, €38.90 for youths, and €25.70 for children, with a special rate of €6.80 for toddlers born in 2020 or later. The '3 Täler Pass' offers access to 31 ski areas in Vorarlberg, Tyrol and Allgäu with a total of around 360 km of pistes, providing good value for those wanting to explore beyond Bödele. The resort's proximity to Dornbirn (10 km, 12 minutes by car) makes it ideal for budget-conscious day trippers.
familyDescription: The resort is particularly popular with families, featuring dedicated children's areas, practice lifts, and a ski school offering lessons for children and families. The mascot 'Rübe' occasionally greets young visitors. The gentle blue slopes and natural setting provide a safe environment for beginners. The ski school at Bödele offers flexible course times and personal attention, with a focus on family-friendly atmosphere.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski options at the mountain itself are limited to rustic huts like the Dornbirner Hütte, which serves traditional Austrian cuisine. For a livelier scene, the nearby city of Dornbirn (10 km away) offers numerous bars and restaurants such as '7er Bar', 'Frei', and 'Snash Burger Bar', as well as cinemas (Cinema Dornbirn, Cineplexx Hohenems) and the 'Spielboden' theatre. Winter activities in Dornbirn include ice skating and guided winter hikes.
resortCharacterDescription: Bödele is a small, natural ski area on the Losenpass between Dornbirn and Schwarzenberg, primarily serving as a local recreation area for the Bodensee region. It is not a resort village; accommodation is in nearby towns. The area has a rustic, family-friendly character with traditional huts and a focus on day visitors. The first ski lift in Central Europe was built here in 1907, giving it historical significance.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of 12 lifts, primarily surface lifts (tow lifts) with one quad chairlift. Named lifts include Hochälpele, Weißtanne 1+2, Seeblick, Alpenblick, Lanklift, Oberlose 1+2, Meiereilift, Schanzenblick, Haldenlift, and Haglift. There are no gondolas. The lifts are modern but basic, suitable for a small area, and operate from 9:00 to 16:15 daily.
linkedResortsDescription: Bödele is part of the '3 Täler Pass' which provides access to 31 ski areas in Vorarlberg, Tyrol and Allgäu, totaling around 360 km of pistes. This includes nearby areas like Damüls-Mellau (43 km away) where the Guggernüllilift operates for night skiing on Thursdays from 18:00 to 21:00, and Diedamskopf (36 km away) where the Grunholz lift offers night skiing every Wednesday and Friday from 19:00 to 22:00.

terrainDescription: The ski area is compact but varied, with pistes weaving past chalets and mountain restaurants. The upper mountain from the Sparrhorn six-pack chairlift offers mostly low-angle trails and a large freeride playground. The Hohstock summit has slopes with a gradient of up to 60%, accessed by T-bars. There are several black pistes, including piste #1 accessed via a tunnel, and piste #3 with high-altitude snow. Blue and red runs are plentiful, with long rolling routes such as piste #10. 95% of pistes are above 2,000 metres, and most slopes face south with views of the Aletsch Glacier and 4,000-metre peaks.
offPisteDescription: The off-piste terrain includes a large free-ride area on the Hohstock, with a tunnel accessing the 'Tunnel Westseite' and Rote Blatte snow bowl. However, the terrain is rocky and requires caution; a guide is recommended. There are 6 marked itineraries. After fresh snowfall, the Hohstock slope is opened for freeriders. The easy-angled off-piste near the bottom can be rocky and icy, and the upper mountain off-piste is dotted with rocks, ravines, and avalanche risk.
valueDescription: A six-day adult lift pass costs CHF 395, with a day pass at CHF 74. Children's day passes are CHF 37. The resort is part of the Magic Pass, offering access to nearly 100 ski areas. Accommodation ranges from budget hostels to luxury hotels, with ski-in/ski-out options available. Restaurants are noted as pricey by visitors.
familyDescription: The resort features the free Hexenland children's area at Chiematte, where children learn to ski with bewitched figures, a tubing track, and inflatables. The Swiss ski school offers all-day programs. The car-free village and ski-in/ski-out accommodation make it convenient for families. A 7.1-kilometre sledging run and winter hiking trails provide non-skiing activities. However, the ski school is largely Swiss-German, so English-speaking children may feel isolated.
apresSkiDescription: Apres-ski is low-key with two bars and no clubs. The Crystal Bar at the Kelchbach lift transforms into the Wave Lounge with disco balls on certain afternoons. The Hexenbar and Schwinlibar offer mountain-side drinks. During the January Hexen festival, there is a party atmosphere with DJ music on the slopes. Most evenings are quiet, with dining at mountain restaurants.
resortCharacterDescription: Belalp is a car-free high-altitude settlement at 2,094 metres, consisting of scattered chalets and mountain restaurants, with the historic Hotel Belalp from 1858. The base village Blatten is a small, traditional Valais village with preserved old buildings. The resort retains alpine farming traditions with summer grazing and cheese production. It is not a purpose-built 1970s station.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system comprises a cable car and gondola from Blatten to Belalp, plus three chairlifts (including a six-pack bubble on Sparrhorn) and four surface lifts. Total capacity is 9,870 persons per hour. The upper mountain T-bars to Hohstock and Sparrhorngrat are sometimes closed due to snow conditions. Queues are rare, and the lifts are modern but the system is compact.
linkedResortsDescription: Belalp is not directly linked to other ski areas by lifts, but it is close to the Aletsch Arena resorts (Riederalp, Bettmeralp, Fiesch-Eggishorn) and is part of the Magic Pass, which provides access to nearly 100 ski areas in Switzerland.

terrainDescription: The terrain consists of four groomed slopes starting from the mountain station at 1,356 meters, with the longest run being 4.5 km. The slopes are predominantly easy (70% beginner) with some intermediate sections (30%), and no advanced terrain. The 'Donauwellenabfahrt' is a 3 km run towards Obermulten with gentle hollows. All slopes lead back to the valley station, and the vertical drop is 262 meters.
valueDescription: Day passes cost €30 for adults and €18 for children, with a six-day pass at €84 for adults and €62 for children. The resort is known for being affordable compared to Alpine resorts, and family discounts are available.
familyDescription: The gentle slopes and short vertical drop (262m) make it suitable for families with young children. The Belchenbahn cable car provides easy access to the summit, and all slopes return to the valley station, eliminating the need for complex navigation. Sledding and winter hiking trails are also available, and the resort has a family-friendly atmosphere with kid-friendly amenities.
resortCharacterDescription: The resort is centered around the Belchenbahn cable car, with a historic mountain inn (Belchenhaus) at the summit. The area is known for its panoramic views of the Black Forest, Vosges, and Alps on clear days. The atmosphere is tranquil and uncrowded, with a focus on nature and traditional Black Forest charm.
liftSystemDescription: The resort is served by a single gondola lift, the Belchenbahn, which uses Expo Skyliner cabins. It operates daily from 09:00 to 16:30 during the winter season. The lift provides access to the summit at 1,356 meters, with a vertical rise of 262 meters. There are no chairlifts or surface lifts.
linkedResortsDescription: Belchen is part of the Liftverbund Feldberg, an interconnected network of 14 lifts across multiple areas including Seebuck, Grafenmatt, Fahl, Todtnau, Muggenbrunn, Notschrei, and Belchen. A single ski pass covers all lifts in the Liftverbund, allowing access to a total of 63 km of piste and 38 lifts across the Feldberg region.

terrainDescription: The ski area offers 35 km of groomed runs, with a mix of gentle beginner hills, wide carving slopes, and challenging steep descents. The slopes are south-facing on a sunny terrace, with panoramic views of the Matterhorn and the Rhône Valley. Modern snowmaking covers the slopes, ensuring reliable conditions until spring.
valueDescription: A 6-day adult ski pass costs around CHF 239, with dynamic pricing that rewards early booking. Families benefit from discounts starting from the second child. Accommodation ranges from a youth hostel and self-catering apartments to mid-range hotels and luxury wellness resorts, offering options for different budgets.
familyDescription: Bellwald is a car-free village, making it safe for children to walk around. The ski school offers programs for children from age three. The nursery slopes are gentle and separate from steeper runs. Family ski passes offer discounts for the second child onwards. In summer, there are adventure playgrounds and easy hiking trails.
resortCharacterDescription: Bellwald is a traditional, car-free Alpine village at 1,600m, the highest village in the Goms district. It sits on a sunny terrace overlooking the Rhône Valley, with views of the surrounding peaks. The village is made up of scattered hamlets including Ried, Eggen, Bodmen, and Fürgangen. It retains an authentic character with working farms, cowbells, and a quiet atmosphere, unlike purpose-built resorts.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of two chairlifts and two surface lifts, with a combined capacity of 6,100 persons per hour. The lifts provide access to the ski area up to 2,560m. Modern snowmaking systems cover the slopes, ensuring reliable snow cover throughout the season.

terrainDescription: The ski area is dominated by intermediate red runs, with wide, sunny descents and some more technical sections. Notable runs include the black Diego at Belvedere and the 3-Tre run, which drops 3,300 metres from Col Rodella down to Pian de Frataces. The terrain spans open snow-covered meadows on Belvedere and the lower ridges of Pordoi, with most pistes facing south-east. The Ciampedie area above Vigo di Fassa offers gentler beginner terrain, while the Buffaure-Ciampac area provides excellent red runs and a black descent to Alba.
offPisteDescription: Off-piste skiing is available from the top of Sass Pordoi (2,950 m), with routes such as the Val Mezdi descending to Passo Pordoi. A mountain guide is recommended. The area also offers freeride opportunities on north-west facing slopes beneath Sella Brunech, and guided off-piste tours on Sass Pordoi are offered by mountain guides.
valueDescription: Accommodation in Canazei and the Val di Fassa valley is generally more affordable than in neighbouring resorts like Selva or Cortina. The Val di Fassa/Carezza lift pass is cheaper than the full Dolomiti Superski pass, though the latter is needed for the Sellaronda. Self-catering apartments and campsites are available, and the FlySki Shuttle offers discounted transfers for online bookings. Some reviews note that prices have risen in recent years, but the area still offers good value compared to other Dolomiti Superski resorts.
familyDescription: The ski area has dedicated beginner areas near the valley stations in Canazei and Campitello, with ski schools offering English-speaking instruction. However, the local pistes are predominantly intermediate red runs, so beginners may need to progress quickly. The Ciampedie area above Vigo di Fassa offers gentler terrain and is less crowded. Accommodation options include family-friendly hotels and apartments, and the resort scores highly for family friendliness in user reviews. The ski schools are well-regarded, and there are off-slope activities such as the alpine coaster in Bardonecchia (nearby) and thermal spas in Pozza di Fassa.
apresSkiDescription: Canazei has a relaxed après-ski scene with around 16 bars and pubs and one club. The atmosphere is authentic Italian rather than rowdy, with bars in the village centre offering drinks and live music. Mountain huts serve food and drinks until late afternoon. The village is not wild for après but lively enough with a good balance of bars, cafes, and restaurants. Some venues offer live music on weekends.
resortCharacterDescription: Canazei is a traditional Ladin village with stone and wood buildings, set in the Fassa Valley. It retains an authentic alpine character with a pedestrianised centre and historic church. The village is not purpose-built but has grown around tourism, with modern lift infrastructure. The surrounding villages of Campitello, Alba, and Pozza di Fassa also offer traditional charm, with a mix of hotels, apartments, and B&Bs.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system includes modern gondolas and cable cars, such as the new 3S cable car from Campitello to Col Rodella (opened winter 2025/26) with a capacity of 2,800 people per hour. There are also chairlifts and surface lifts. Queues are generally manageable except at peak times, especially in the morning from Alba to Belvedere. The lifts connect the Belvedere and Col Rodella areas and provide direct access to the Sellaronda circuit. The system is efficient and well-maintained, with a total of 23 lifts in the local area.
linkedResortsDescription: The Belvedere-Col Rodella-Passo Pordoi ski area is directly linked to the Sellaronda circuit, providing access to Arabba-Marmolada, Val Gardena, and Alta Badia. It is also part of the Dolomiti Superski network, which includes 12 ski areas and 1,220 km of slopes. A free shuttle bus connects to other Val di Fassa ski areas such as Ciampac-Buffaure and Ciampedie. The nearby Tre Valli ski area (Alpe Lusia and Passo San Pellegrino) is accessible by bus and is covered by the Dolomiti Superski pass.

terrainDescription: The ski area offers 29 km of groomed slopes, with a 6 km panoramic run descending 800 vertical meters from the Bischlinghöhe to the valley. The terrain includes a carving slope and a training slope with timing equipment. Slopes are wide and well-groomed, with a 360° mountain panorama overlooking the Tennengebirge mountains. The area is divided into 13 km of blue runs, 15 km of red runs, and 1 km of black runs.
valueDescription: Children under 4 years ride free. Discounted rates are available for teenagers and senior citizens, and family packages and multi-day passes are offered. The W³ shuttle provides free transport from the train stations of Bischofshofen, Pfarrwerfen, and Werfen to the lifts, reducing travel costs for those arriving by public transport. A season pass valid across 8 ski regions in the Salzkammergut area is also available.
familyDescription: The resort is marketed as a family ski area with a dedicated KID's Snowpark featuring eight themed runs, a covered conveyor belt, and chill-out areas. The nursery area at the valley station is flat and separate from faster traffic. Children under 4 ski for free, and the ski school offers lessons for children. The village is compact and walkable, with ski-in/ski-out accommodation options.
apresSkiDescription: The resort has nine mountain huts and restaurants, including the Bischlinghöhe summit restaurant with panoramic views and a parasol bar, and the Sportalm Strussing in the ski area. Evening activities include horse-drawn sleigh rides and alpaca treks. The village is quiet and cozy, with a few restaurants serving Austrian cuisine; there is no vibrant nightlife.
resortCharacterDescription: Werfenweng is a traditional alpine village, not a purpose-built resort. It is a pioneer in sustainable tourism with a 'gentle mobility' concept, encouraging eco-friendly travel. The village is small and charming, with a focus on tranquility and nature. The setting offers a 360° mountain panorama of the Tennengebirge mountains.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of 9 lifts: two gondolas (the 8-person Ikarus and the Rosnerköpfl village lift), two chairlifts, several drag lifts, and two magic carpets. The Ikarus gondola takes 6 minutes to reach the Bischlinghöhe at 1,840 m. The total transport capacity is approximately 10,000 people per hour, so waiting times are rare. A modern snowmaking system covers all 29 km of slopes.
linkedResortsDescription: Werfenweng is part of the Ski amadé network, which includes resorts such as Gastein, Grossarl, and Schladming. Additionally, a season pass valid across 8 ski regions in the Salzkammergut area, including Dachstein West, is available. The Dachstein West ski area (over 50 km of pistes) is linked via the season pass, and multi-day passes also provide access to Dachstein Krippenstein and other nearby areas.

terrainDescription: The ski area consists of 5 km of pistes (50% beginner, 34% intermediate, 16% advanced) served by 5 lifts. The longest run is the 3 km family run, suitable for beginners, along with the easy Seewiese descent and the Sonnenlift descent at Naggler Alm. A 2.5 km intermediate FIS downhill run provides a more challenging option. The terrain is dominated by gentle, family-friendly slopes with a vertical drop of 394 m from 1,324 m summit to 930 m base.
valueDescription: A six-day adult lift pass costs €249, with a one-day adult pass at €49.90 and a one-day child pass at €27.90. The Weissensee Premium Card offers free use of the cable car for guests of partner facilities during specific periods (e.g., January 7–17, 2027). Self-catering accommodation and a compact village layout help keep costs down compared to larger resorts.
familyDescription: The children's ski area, Kinder-Skiwelt Weissensee, is located at the lakeshore next to the chairlift and includes a 21 m conveyor belt for beginners, a 48 m conveyor belt for moderately advanced skiers, a 125 m button lift, a ski carousel, and a children's lounge with refreshments. The ski school offers group and individual lessons for children and adults. After mastering the nursery slopes, children can progress to the 290 m Seewiesen lift and the 250 m Sonnen button lift on Naggler Alm. A free shuttle bus connects to the larger Nassfeld ski area for more advanced family members.
apresSkiDescription: On-mountain dining includes the Bergbahnstüberl at the valley station with a panoramic terrace, and the Tschatschaleria at the Seewiesenlift offering après-ski. In the village of Techendorf-Süd, bars such as Senso e Vita, Café In & Go, and Schuler's Weinstube serve drinks and Jagatee. The resort also offers night skiing on the family piste every Thursday and on full moon nights from 4:30 pm to 10:00 pm (from January 3).
resortCharacterDescription: The resort is a small, family-oriented ski area on the southern shore of Lake Weissensee, a glacial lake at 930 m elevation in the Gailtal Alps. The village of Techendorf is a traditional lakeside settlement with a Protestant majority and a car-free centre. In winter, the frozen lake becomes Europe's largest natural ice surface (6.5 km²), used for speed skating, ice hockey, and ice stock sport. The atmosphere is quiet and relaxed, focused on nature and outdoor activities rather than nightlife.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system comprises 5 lifts: one 4-seater chairlift (Weissensee Bergbahn) and four T-bar lifts (Schulernöckl, Sonnenlift Naggler Alm, Verbindungslift Naggler Alm, and Seewiesen lift). Total capacity is 3,980 persons per hour. The chairlift runs from 09:00 to 17:00. Lifts are modern but modest in scale, with no gondolas or high-speed detachable chairs.
linkedResortsDescription: The Weissensee region is part of a network of ski areas in southern Carinthia. The nearby Nassfeld ski resort (110 km of pistes, 30 lifts, 100% snow reliability) is a 35-minute drive away and accessible via a free shuttle bus from Weissensee. Other small family ski resorts within a 45-minute drive include Weissbriach, Kötschach-Mauthen, Griminitzen, Hrast/Feistritz, Emberger Alm, and Goldeck.

terrainDescription: The Bergeralm ski area offers 29 km of groomed pistes, with 21% easy (6 km), 55% medium (16 km), and 24% difficult (7 km) runs. Beginner slopes are concentrated at the upper Hochsonn lift, while the mountain features a mogul slope and the 'Manni Pranger' race course for timed runs. The terrain includes wide cruising runs on the upper mountain and steeper red and black descents, with a snowpark and a children's fun park. The top of Nösslachjoch is a known spot for snowkiting.
valueDescription: A six-day adult lift pass costs €278.40, with a one-day adult pass at €57.30. Children born 2010–2017 pay €28.50 for a day, and those born 2018 or younger ski free with a paying parent. Seniors born 1941 or older ride free, and those born 1942–1961 pay €54.40 for a day. Family discounts apply: one paying parent plus one paying child, with additional children at 50% of the child fare. Free parking is available at the valley station, and the resort is accessible by train from Innsbruck, reducing travel costs.
familyDescription: The resort has a dedicated children's ski area (Kinderland) with magic carpets and playful learning zones, a children's fun park, and a children's ski cross course. Ski schools offer professional instruction for all ages. A 6 km illuminated toboggan run runs from the top lift to the valley. Twelve vacation apartments are available at the middle station (1,550 m), right next to the Panorama restaurant, providing ski-in/ski-out convenience for families.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski is low-key and family-friendly, with cozy lounges and sun terraces at the Bärentraum service restaurant and Panorama self-service restaurant serving mulled wine, local beers, and homemade pastries. Night skiing operates on Wednesday, Friday, and Saturday evenings from 6:30 pm to 9:30 pm under floodlights, and the illuminated toboggan run is also open in the evening. There are no loud nightclubs or bars.
resortCharacterDescription: The Bergeralm ski arena is a modern, purpose-built facility located near Steinach am Brenner in the Wipptal valley. The valley station sits directly under a large bridge of the Brenner motorway, with free parking and lift access. Accommodation is available in the nearby town or in 12 apartments at the middle station. The resort emphasizes sustainability, using hydropower and green electricity, and is accessible by train via Steinach am Brenner station, a short walk from the lifts.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of 2 gondolas, 2 chairlifts, 3 T-bar lifts, and 2 conveyor belts, providing quick access to the slopes. Night skiing is available on selected evenings (Wed, Fri, Sat) from 6:30 pm to 9:30 pm, and early morning skiing runs from 6:30 am on weekends from mid-March. The resort has modern snowmaking covering a significant portion of the pistes, ensuring reliable conditions.
linkedResortsDescription: The standard Bergeralm ski pass covers only the Bergeralm ski area. However, regional Tyrol passes such as the Snow Card Tirol and Freizeitticket Tirol may include access to nearby resorts including Schlick 2000, Kühtai, Axamer Lizum, and the Stubai Glacier. These passes allow skiing across multiple areas in the region.

terrainDescription: The main ski area is on Darlux mountain, accessed by two chairlifts and a surface lift, with 20 km of prepared slopes reaching up to 2,550 m. The valley run descends directly to the village of Bergün. A separate beginner area, Tèct/Zinols, has two ski lifts and a magic carpet. The longest run is 6 km from the top of Darlux to Bergün. The terrain includes wide blue runs, red runs, and a few black runs, with natural snow coverage. The slopes are groomed daily.
offPisteDescription: Off-piste skiing is very limited. The resort relies on natural snow and the slopes are groomed; off-piste areas are not maintained and are generally not skiable. The visitor rating for off-piste is 1 out of 5, indicating almost no off-piste terrain.
valueDescription: Lift passes are reasonably priced, with a one-day adult pass costing around CHF 43-47 and a six-day pass around CHF 201. Accommodation options include several budget hotels and pensions, with a rating of 4 out of 5 for cheap rooms. Luxury hotels are limited, rated 2 out of 5. The resort is known for being more affordable than larger Swiss resorts.
familyDescription: The resort has a dedicated beginner area, Tèct/Zinols, with two ski lifts and a magic carpet, ideal for children learning to ski. Childcare facilities are limited, rated 2 out of 5, with no dedicated nursery. The slopes are uncrowded, making it a safe environment for families. The 6 km toboggan run from Preda to Bergün is a popular family activity.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski is low-key, with two bars and pubs in the village, including the Snowhill Bar. The mountain restaurant La Diala offers a sun terrace with views. The Fondue-Schlitten (fondue sled) is a unique dining experience. The atmosphere is relaxed and family-friendly, not rowdy.
resortCharacterDescription: Bergün/Bravuogn is a traditional Swiss village with sgraffiti-decorated Engadin houses, a Roman tower, and a historic village square. It is located on the UNESCO Albula railway line. The resort has a charming, authentic alpine character, not a purpose-built modern development.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of 5 lifts: 2 chairlifts and 3 surface lifts. Queues are very rare due to low crowds. The lifts operate from 09:00 to 16:30. The main access to Darlux is via two chairlifts. A sports bus runs between the village and the Darlux valley station.
linkedResortsDescription: The Bergün ski area is standalone and not linked to other resorts. The standard lift pass covers only the Darlux and Tèct/Zinols slopes. Nearby resorts like Savognin or St. Moritz require separate tickets.

terrainDescription: The terrain is northwest-facing, with 19 pistes (4 green, 4 blue, 7 red, 4 black) ranging from 1,000 m to 2,000 m. The lower slopes feature gentle greens and blues for beginners, while the upper mountain offers reds and blacks with steeper pitches. The Lac de Pelluaz sector provides descents on the Pelluaz, La Variante, and Les Chalets Verts runs. The longest run is 4 km. Snowmaking covers 11 km of slopes with 90 snow guns. From the top, there are panoramic views of Mont Blanc and Lake Geneva.
valueDescription: A six-day adult lift pass costs €156, and a family package for 2 adults and 2 children starts at €101 for one day. The daily adult pass is €28.80. The resort is consistently described as one of the least expensive in the region, with budget-friendly accommodation including self-catering apartments and a hotel. Reviews highlight excellent value for money compared to larger Chablais resorts.
familyDescription: Bernex is a family-friendly resort with a dedicated beginners' zone at La Mouille, served by a conveyor belt and a child lift. The ESF ski school has 50 instructors offering lessons for all ages. Childcare is available from 4 months, and the resort has a mascot (Piou Piou et Blancheau). Additional family facilities include a natural ice rink, a 4-season toboggan run, and a cinema. The resort is certified as family-oriented with a relaxed atmosphere.
apresSkiDescription: The après-ski scene is low-key with 9 restaurants and 2 bars/cafés. The village has a cinema and a natural ice rink that hosts disco evenings during February holidays. Dining options include Savoyard cuisine. There are no discos or large nightclubs.
resortCharacterDescription: Bernex is an authentic Alpine village with traditional wooden chalets and old stone houses, centered around the historic Church of Bernex (L'église Saint-Ours) on a hill. It retains its village character with a compact centre, artisan shops, and a relaxed atmosphere, unlike large purpose-built resorts. The resort is built around the original mountain community, giving it a lived-in feel.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of 12 lifts: 3 chairlifts (including a detachable 4-seater with a loading carpet) and 9 drag lifts. A shuttle train runs from the village centre to the slopes. The resort has over 100 snow guns for snowmaking. The lifts operate from 08:45 to 16:30 daily.
linkedResortsDescription: Bernex is part of the Les Montagnes d'Évian domain, which links Bernex with Abondance and Thollon-les-Mémises. The combined area offers over 80 km of pistes (35 runs: 6 green, 8 blue, 15 red, 6 black) served by 26 lifts including a gondola and 6 chairlifts. A joint ski pass is available for the linked resorts.

terrainDescription: The Berwang-Bichlbach-Rinnen ski area offers 35.3 km of groomed pistes, with 67% classified as easy (blue), 28% as intermediate (red), and 6% as difficult (black). The ski circuit connects the three villages, allowing skiers to follow the sun across the valley. A notable route is the 'Bärenrunde,' which loops from Bichlbach up to the Almkopf, then via the Sonnalmbahn in Berwang and the Biliglift to the Thanellerkarlift, continuing on a ski trail to the Panoramabahn Rastkopf, with an optional steep natural-snow run from the Rastkopf. Night skiing is available on Thursdays on the Egghof Sun Jet slope. The slopes are fully covered by snowmaking.
valueDescription: A day lift pass in main season costs €68 for adults, €54.50 for youths, and €34 for children. The resort is part of the Tiroler Zugspitz Arena, and regional ski passes such as the Snow Card Tirol may offer multi-day savings. Accommodation ranges from hotels to self-catering apartments, with many options within walking distance of the lifts. The ski area's manageable size means lift queues are minimal, reducing time spent waiting.
familyDescription: The Berwang-Bichlbach ski area is certified as a quality-tested family ski area by Tirol Werbung. The Bobo-Kinderland children's area provides a dedicated learning zone with gentle slopes. The Thanellerbahn chairlift features a conveyor belt for easy loading of small children, and a magic carpet is installed at its valley station. The ski school offers courses for beginners of all ages. Additional family activities include tobogganing, ice skating, and curling. The village is compact and walkable, with short distances to lifts and amenities.
resortCharacterDescription: Berwang is a traditional Tyrolean village at 1,336m elevation, the highest village in the Tiroler Zugspitz Arena. The village has a relaxed, authentic atmosphere with a mix of farms and tourist infrastructure. The ski area is accessed from two sides via the Fernpass federal road, making it convenient for day trips. The village offers a range of accommodation, from hotels to apartments, and is car-friendly.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system comprises 12 lifts: 2 gondolas, 4 chairlifts, and 5 T-bars. Recent investments include the Obere Karbahn II gondola (opened December 2025), which replaced a T-bar and extends the terrain 140m higher to 1,743m, with a middle station at the former top of the old lift. The Panoramabahn Rastkopf was added in 2020, and the Thanellerbahn is a child-safe 6-seater chairlift with individual seats, weather protection hoods, and a conveyor belt for boarding. Lifts operate daily from 8:40 to 16:15.
linkedResortsDescription: The Berwang ski area is linked with the neighbouring villages of Bichlbach and Rinnen, forming a single ski circuit with 35.3 km of pistes. It is part of the Tiroler Zugspitz Arena, which includes several other ski resorts such as Ehrwalder Alm, Wetterstein, Grubigstein, Marienberg, and Zugspitze, but these are separate ski areas not directly connected by lifts.

terrainDescription: The terrain consists of easy and medium-difficulty pistes, with tree-lined slopes offering sweeping views of the Zugspitz Arena. The longest run is the Talabfahrt at 4 km with a vertical drop of 800 m, followed by the Standartabfahrt at 3 km. Other named runs include the Almabfahrt (1.6 km), Jochliftabfahrt (1 km), and Knäblesjochabfahrt (2.5 km). The slopes are predominantly wide and gentle, suitable for beginners and families.
valueDescription: A one-day adult lift pass costs €68, with youth passes at €54.50 and child passes at €34. Free parking is available at the valley station. Shared transfer options from airports start around €21, and midweek transfers can save up to 50%. The resort's small size and lack of crowds may reduce overall trip costs compared to larger resorts.
familyDescription: The resort features a dedicated children's area called Biberland (Biberland Kinderareal) at the base, with magic carpets, playful terrain, and expert ski instructors. The Kinderland Biberwier and Biber's Buzzebahn are specifically designed for kids. A scenic toboggan run from mid-mountain to the village is accessible to all ages. The slopes are predominantly easy and medium-difficulty, with tree-lined runs that are safe for children. The resort is part of the Tiroler Zugspitz Arena, which offers a total of 139 km of pistes for more advanced family members.
apresSkiDescription: The resort has 2 bars/pubs and 3 restaurants. The Sunnalm restaurant on the slopes serves traditional Tyrolean meals such as goulash, Käsespätzle, and Apfelstrudel. The après-ski scene is quiet and relaxed, with no night skiing or large party venues.
resortCharacterDescription: Biberwier is a traditional Tyrolean village with alpine lodgings, offering a peaceful and authentic atmosphere. The resort is small and uncrowded, with free parking at the valley station. It is not a purpose-built resort but a genuine village with a relaxed character, focused on daytime mountain enjoyment.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system includes a high-speed six-person chairlift (Marienbergbahn), a double chairlift, and four surface lifts. Total capacity is 7,040 persons per hour. Operating hours are 08:30 to 16:30. The lifts are modern and efficient, with minimal queues outside peak weeks.
linkedResortsDescription: Biberwier-Marienberg is part of the Tiroler Zugspitz Arena, a linked ski area that includes Lermoos Grubigstein, Ehrwalder Alm, Ehrwalder Wettersteinbahnen, Berwang-Bichlbach, and the Zugspitze. The combined area offers 139 km of pistes. A single lift pass grants access to all these resorts.

terrainDescription: The ski area sits between 1,630m and 1,940m with a vertical drop of 300m. It has 7km of pistes, split 33% beginner and 67% intermediate, with no advanced runs. The main piste is the red 'Standard' run, and there is a steep run called 'Hundschopf' that leads to the old chairlift top station. A chairlift built in 2007 opened up new longer pistes. The outer run 'Hinter Weissenboden' was closed during a visit in February 2022.
offPisteDescription: Off-piste terrain includes unmarked slopes beside the chairlift and in the area of the closed outer run 'Hinter Weissenboden'. A February 2022 visit reported fresh snow in hollows made off-piste skiing enjoyable, with some hard patches. Freeriders can find numerous unmarked slopes, and ski tours from Biel offer powder descents.
valueDescription: A day lift pass costs 40 CHF for adults and 26 CHF for children, making it one of the cheaper Swiss ski areas. The resort is small with only 7km of pistes, but the low price reflects its size. Accommodation is available in the Alphotel Biel-Kinzig and holiday apartments.
familyDescription: The resort is marketed as family-friendly, with a dedicated children's ski lift (Kinderskilift Weissenboden) and a children's pony lift. The ski area is small and easy to navigate, with beginner slopes making up 33% of the pistes. The cable car from Bürglen takes 15 minutes, and the area is largely fog-free.
resortCharacterDescription: The ski area is located on the Biel mountain in the Schächental valley, accessed by a small 8-person aerial cableway in two sections from Bürglen. It is a natural, family-friendly area with panoramic views of the Uri Alps, not a purpose-built resort village. Accommodation is limited to a few mountain huts and holiday apartments.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of a small 8-person aerial cableway in two sections (Brügg-Riedlig and Riedlig-Biel) as the only access, plus a chairlift (built in 2007), a ski lift (550m long), and a children's ski lift. The cableway operates every 20 minutes with a midday break. The chairlift replaced an older ski lift and opened new terrain.

terrainDescription: The terrain at Bielmonte consists of 19 km of pistes between 1,220 m and 1,620 m, with a vertical drop of 402 m. The slopes are predominantly intermediate (71%), with 22% beginner and 7% advanced. The most challenging run is the black-rated 'Dosso Grande', which has a 65% gradient and descends to 1,300 m. The resort is exposed to the sun all day, with slope orientations of 38% north, 27% south, 19% east, and 16% west. The longest run is 3 km. The resort also offers 20 km of cross-country trails, including a 9 km loop through beech and spruce forests at Bocchetto Sessera.
familyDescription: Bielmonte is marketed as family-friendly, with two ski schools, three moving carpets for beginners, and covered parking at the base of the slopes. Equipment hire is available from two shops on site. A free shuttle bus runs from Biella railway station on weekends in December. The resort also offers night skiing on illuminated slopes and a 9 km cross-country loop through forests. User reviews describe it as ideal for families and perfect for beginners.
apresSkiDescription: Apres-ski at Bielmonte is very low-key. The Monte Marca refuge offers a 360° view and serves local drinks like bombardino and Menabrea beer, along with dishes such as pizzoccheri and local cured meats. However, user reviews indicate that nightlife is essentially non-existent, with no bars or clubs beyond the mountain huts.
resortCharacterDescription: Bielmonte is a small ski station located within the Oasi Zegna Nature Park, a 100 km² reserve created by Ermenegildo Zegna. The resort is purpose-built with a covered car park, ticket office, and hospitality area at the base. It is not a traditional alpine village but rather a day-trip destination, with most visitors coming from the nearby Po Valley. The setting is scenic, with views over the Po Valley and Monte Rosa glaciers on clear days.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system at Bielmonte consists of 8 lifts, including several double chairlifts and a single-chair lift (Monte Marca), plus surface lifts and moving carpets for beginners. The lifts are older, with some being biposto (two-person) and one monoposto. User reviews mention the lifts are obsolete ('obsolescenza degli impianti'). The resort also has a covered moving carpet for beginners.
linkedResortsDescription: Bielmonte is a standalone ski resort not connected by lifts to other areas. Nearby resorts include Oropa (9 km away) and Alpe di Mera (9.3 km away), but they require separate lift passes. The resort is part of the Oasi Zegna Nature Park.

terrainDescription: The Bonneval-sur-Arc ski area ranges from 1,800m to 3,000m, offering 32km of groomed pistes across 26 runs: 6 green, 10 blue, 7 red, and 3 black. The vertical drop is 1,200m. The terrain is dominated by wide, gentle cruising runs above the treeline, with a few steeper pitches. Most runs face north, ensuring good snow cover. The longest run is over 5km, linking several trails from top to bottom. The beginner area is located in the heart of the village at the Tralenta development, with dedicated nursery slopes and special trails for young children.
offPisteDescription: Bonneval-sur-Arc is renowned for its extensive off-piste and backcountry terrain, accessible from four main massifs: Pointe d'Andagne (3,217m), Albaron (3,637m), Ouille du Midi (3,042m), and L'Iseran (2,000m). The most accessible off-piste skiing is on two flanks of the Pointe d'Andagne, offering runs of up to 1,150m vertical to the valley floor. Terrain includes steep couloirs, open powder fields, glaciers, and technical steeps, much of it north-facing to preserve snow quality. The unique microclimate of the Maurienne valley, influenced by the Retour d'Est weather system from the Gulf of Genoa, often brings abundant snow even when other areas are dry. A guide is recommended; the ESF ski school and local guiding companies offer supervised off-piste excursions.
valueDescription: Lift passes at Bonneval-sur-Arc are among the cheapest in the French Alps. For the 2025/26 season, a one-day adult pass costs €36 (€37.20 at the window), a six-day adult pass costs €177, and a family pass (2 adults + 2 children) costs €620. Children under 5 and seniors over 75 ski free. The resort is part of the Espace Haute Maurienne Vanoise, where a six-day pass includes one free day at each of the five other valley resorts (Aussois, Val Cenis, La Norma, Valfréjus, Bessans), totalling over 350km of pistes. Accommodation is limited but ranges from self-catering apartments to hotels, with many options near the slopes. The village has a cash point and free car parking near the lifts.
familyDescription: Bonneval-sur-Arc is a family-friendly village resort with dedicated nursery slopes at the edge of the village in the Tralenta development, separate from faster traffic. The ESF ski school offers lessons from age 3, and childcare is available from 3 months to 5 years old. The village emblem, Bonny the marmot, accompanies children in snow discovery programs. The small size of the ski area means family members are never far apart. There is a playground, a sledging run, and a boardercross area. The traffic-free old village with narrow streets is safe for walking in boots. The resort also offers a snow nursery and a nursery for very young children.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski in Bonneval-sur-Arc is very quiet and relaxed. The old and new villages together have around 10 cafes, bars, and restaurants, serving mostly Savoyard cuisine and pizza. There is no rowdy nightlife or late-night club; the scene is more about cosy bars and terraces with local wine or genepi. Live music is rare, and a reliable late-night bar is hard to find, especially mid-week. The village's small size means most venues close early. For those seeking more lively options, the nearby resort of Valfréjus has a more active après scene with pubs like Rock n Beer.
resortCharacterDescription: Bonneval-sur-Arc is a member of the 'Les Plus Beaux Villages de France' association, known for its picturesque historic character. The old village features traditional Savoyard stone chalets with slate roofs, narrow winding lanes, and no street names, electric wires, or antennas. The entire old village is listed in the national heritage directory. It was spared destruction during World War II, preserving its original architecture dating back to 1532. Next to the old village is the small modern development of Tralenta at the foot of the lifts, where most accommodation, ski shops, and the ESF school are located. The village is situated at the foot of the Col de l'Iseran, the highest road pass in France, and part of its territory lies within Vanoise National Park.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system at Bonneval-sur-Arc consists of 10 lifts: 3 chairlifts (all fixed-grip quads) and 7 surface lifts (drag lifts). The main access chair is the Vallonnet, with a base area that can be congested due to limited space. The highest chairlift reaches 3,000m. Lifts are efficient with almost no queues, though the mid-mountain Lacs tows can have short lines as they provide the only connection to the 3000 chair. The lifts are older and dated, reflecting the low lift pass price. Operating hours are typically 9:00am to 4:30pm, with the base chair opening at 9:10 and upper lifts 20-30 minutes later. There is no snowmaking on all runs; the resort relies heavily on natural snow.
linkedResortsDescription: Bonneval-sur-Arc is part of the Espace Haute Maurienne Vanoise, a linked ski area that includes five other resorts: Aussois, Val Cenis, La Norma, Valfréjus, and Bessans. Together they offer over 350km of groomed pistes and 200km of cross-country trails, served by 65 lifts. A six-day lift pass from any of the resorts includes one free day at each of the other five. The resorts are connected by a free ski bus service (with a lift pass) or a €10 weekly bus pass. The area also offers three free days at Bardonecchia and Montgenèvre in Italy with a season pass.

terrainDescription: The Bormio ski area is north-facing, with most runs above treeline on Cima Bianca. The majority of pistes are red, including the Stelvio World Cup downhill run (3.2 km, 1,010 m vertical drop). There is one short black run. The longest run from summit to base is 6 km with 1,800 m vertical drop. The main beginner area is at Bormio 2000 (mid-mountain), served by magic carpets and easy drag lifts. The separate San Colombano area offers additional tree-lined red and blue runs.
offPisteDescription: Off-piste skiing within the main Bormio ski area is limited, but there are two designated freeride zones. The higher zone features steep open powder slopes with cliff drops and boulder hucks. The lower zone is a short glade area for tree skiing when snow conditions permit. The nearby Santa Caterina area offers more extensive freeride terrain in the Stelvio National Park.
valueDescription: A day lift pass costs €56-€61, with a 6-day pass ranging from €226 to €305. Children under 8 ski free, and family discounts are available. Accommodation ranges from budget guesthouses to luxury spa hotels. Dining in the historic town is reasonably priced, with many trattorias serving local Valtellina dishes. The Alta Valtellina pass provides access to multiple areas for a single price.
familyDescription: The main beginner area is at Bormio 2000, accessed by gondola from town, with magic carpets and easy drag lifts. The ski school is rated highly by visitors. The separate Cima Piazzi-San Colombano area is specifically family-friendly, with easy and intermediate slopes, a family park, and the first alpine coaster in Lombardy. The town has an ice rink, tobogganing, and thermal baths for non-skiing activities. The historic centre is pedestrian-friendly.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski is relaxed and Italian. Slope-side bars at Bormio 2000, such as BeWhite, are lively in the late afternoon. In the historic town, there are several informal bars, wine bars, and a couple of small nightclubs. The main evening activity is dining at trattorias and visiting the thermal spas (Bagni Vecchi, Bagni Nuovi).
resortCharacterDescription: Bormio is a historic medieval spa town, not a purpose-built ski resort. The old quarter around Piazza Cavour and Via Roma has cobbled streets, historic palatial buildings, fountains, and churches. The town has three thermal bath complexes fed by natural hot springs. It has a year-round appeal and feels more like a cultural destination than a ski village.
liftSystemDescription: The main lift system consists of a gondola from town to Bormio 2000 (mid-mountain), then a cable car and several chairlifts to Cima Bianca. Lifts are modern and efficient, with short queues reported. Free ski buses connect Bormio to Santa Caterina and San Colombano. Snowmaking covers all main pistes.
linkedResortsDescription: Bormio is part of the Alta Valtellina ski area, which includes the linked resorts of Santa Caterina Valfurva, Cima Piazzi-San Colombano, and Livigno. The Bormio Skipass covers Bormio, Santa Caterina, and Cima Piazzi-San Colombano, offering 110 km of slopes connected by a free ski bus. The Alta Valtellina pass adds Livigno, bringing the total to around 230 km. Santa Caterina is known for its challenging terrain and freeride routes, while Cima Piazzi-San Colombano is family-friendly with easy slopes and an alpine coaster.

terrainDescription: The ski area of Borno – Monte Altissimo is set within a thick conifer forest, with runs winding through the trees. The terrain consists of 10 pistes (1 blue, 7 red, 2 black) totaling 20 km, with the longest run being 2.5 km. The slopes are predominantly intermediate (red runs), with some challenging black runs and a single beginner blue. The slopes are well-groomed and maintained, with artificial snowmaking on some sections, particularly the Ogne piste.
valueDescription: Borno is known for its excellent value, with a day pass of €36, the cheapest in Lombardy. The resort offers affordable accommodation and dining, making it a budget-friendly option for families and cost-conscious skiers. The quality-price ratio is frequently praised by visitors, with many noting that the resort provides a satisfying skiing experience without spending a fortune.
familyDescription: Borno is highly family-oriented, with a well-equipped ski school offering lessons for all ages and abilities, including a conveyor belt and a 4-seat chairlift in the beginner area. The slopes are suitable for beginners and intermediates, and the resort offers off-slope activities like ice skating, snowshoeing, and sledding. The village is quiet and safe, with a relaxed atmosphere ideal for families.
apresSkiDescription: Apres-ski in Borno is low-key, centered around the Barissimo at the base of the lifts and the Rifugio Monte Altissimo at the summit, which serve local food and drinks. The village itself has a few bars and restaurants, but the atmosphere is relaxed rather than lively, suitable for families and those seeking a quiet evening.
resortCharacterDescription: Borno is a traditional, tranquil mountain village at 1000m altitude, surrounded by conifer forests. It is not a purpose-built resort but a living village with its own character, offering a peaceful atmosphere away from mass tourism. The village has a few amenities and the ski area is a short distance from the centre.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system at Borno consists of a mix of chairlifts and surface lifts, with a total of 4 lifts (according to official data). The lifts are well-maintained and queues are rare, even on busy days. A modern snowmaking system has been installed on the Ogne piste, improving snow reliability.

terrainDescription: The terrain at Bosco Gurin consists of wide, gentle slopes on the upper mountain, suitable for children and beginners, with a demanding black run descending to the valley. The longest marked run is about 3 km. Notable pistes include the scenic runs down from Punta del Sole.
valueDescription: A day lift pass costs 57 CHF for adults and 22 CHF for children. No information on accommodation or dining costs is provided in the source text.
familyDescription: The resort has practice facilities for children at the bottom station. The slopes are wide and not too steep, making them suitable for children. The village is quiet and family-friendly.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski is laid-back, with cozy spots like Bar Gurin and Ristorante Sole offering local cuisine and Swiss wines. There is not a lot of nightlife, mostly hotel bars, but the atmosphere is relaxed.
resortCharacterDescription: Bosco Gurin is a picturesque Walser village, the highest in Ticino at 1506 m, and the only village in Ticino where German is officially spoken. It has an authentic, traditional atmosphere and is difficult to reach without a car.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system includes a 4-seater chairlift from the village to Rossboda, and several surface lifts accessing the upper slopes. The lifts have a combined capacity of 3,300 skiers per hour.

terrainDescription: The ski area offers 20 km of natural snow slopes with runs for all abilities (blue, red, black). The slopes are wide and spacious, even on busy weekends. A children's ski lift on flat terrain is ideal for beginners, and the red Tschu-Tschu cable car connects the mountain station with the ski lift, popular with children. The terrain is mostly open and above treeline, with a mix of gentle and steeper sections.
valueDescription: A day lift pass costs CHF45 for adults, and the children's ski lift is free to use. The cable car from Chur to the mountain costs 32 CHF round-trip for non-skiers, but ski pass holders likely have it included. Overall, the resort offers affordable pricing compared to larger Swiss resorts, though accommodation and food costs are not detailed in the source text.
familyDescription: The resort is well-suited for families with a free children's ski lift on flat terrain, located next to two mountain restaurants. The red Tschu-Tschu cable car is a highlight for children, connecting the mountain station to the ski lift. The AIS sports school offers ski and snowboard lessons, and equipment rental is available on the mountain. The cable car from Chur provides quick access, and the slopes are wide and uncrowded.
resortCharacterDescription: Brambrüesch is Chur's local mountain, reached by a 12-minute cable car from the city centre. It is not a traditional ski resort village but a high plateau with mountain restaurants and wide-open spaces. The setting is more about convenience and nature than alpine charm, offering a quick escape from the city to a natural snow paradise.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of a two-stage cable car from Chur (gondola to Känzeli, then cable car to the plateau), a chairlift, a ski lift, and the red Tschu-Tschu cable car connecting the mountain station to the ski lift, totaling 5 lifts. The cable car operates from 8:30 am to 5 pm daily, with Saturday evening service until 10 pm in season. The children's ski lift runs from 9 am to 4 pm and is free.
linkedResortsDescription: Brambrüesch is part of the Dreibündenstein snowshoe arena, which connects it to the winter sport regions of Feldis and Pradaschier via marked snowshoe trails. However, the ski area itself is not directly linked to other resorts by lifts.

terrainDescription: The ski area spans 47.6 km of groomed pistes (48% easy, 49% intermediate, 3% advanced) plus 13 km of ski routes, with a vertical drop of 1,096 m from 904 m to 2,000 m. Most runs are wide and well-groomed, predominantly below treeline on east-facing slopes. The highest point at Glattjoch (2,000 m) offers open, treeless pistes. The longest marked run is over 5 km from Glattjoch down to the Palud base. The area is split between Brand and Bürserberg, connected by a panoramic cable car at around 1,700 m. All three valley runs are of intermediate difficulty, so beginners cannot ski to the base without downloading.
offPisteDescription: Off-piste skiing is available between pistes and on marked ski routes. The black ski route 16 in the Lorenzital section is popular with free riders. Red routes 14 and 15 from the Glattjoch chairlift offer unpisted descents down to the Palud valley. On the Bürserberg side, unpisted red route 17 starts at the Fuchsbau restaurant and drops to Tschengla. A steep off-piste descent visible from the top of the Glattjochbahn chairlift requires booting up from the lift station and should only be attempted with a local guide. Beyond the main ski area, good off-piste terrain requires ski touring equipment and a guide.
valueDescription: Day lift pass pricing for the 2025/26 season starts at €48.50 for adults, €46 for seniors, and €29 for children (6–18 years). Multi-day WildPass tickets (2 days or more) grant access to five additional ski areas in the Montafon Brandnertal network. Mountain restaurant prices are described as modest by Alpine standards. Accommodation ranges from self-catering apartments to four-star hotels, with many properties offering ski-in/ski-out access. The resort is considered good value for families, with free lift passes for children under 6.
familyDescription: The resort has three dedicated practice lifts: a drag lift in Brand village (not directly connected to the main area), a magic carpet in the Palüd area, and a beginner lift at Tschengla in Bürserberg. The Palüd area features Brandolin's Fuchspark, a publicly accessible children's zone with a conveyor belt, a t-bar, and child-friendly descents. There are 20 km of easy blue runs, though the entire ski area cannot be explored on blue slopes alone as many connections are intermediate. Two ski schools operate on-site: Schischule Brandnertal and Skischule Brand Alpin. The Kinderland learning area in Brand caters to children aged 3–5. The villages are traffic-free in the centre, and many accommodations are within walking distance of lifts.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski is centred in Brand, with the Heuboda bar (opposite the Dorfbahn) being the liveliest spot, offering music and cocktails. The Muhlebach bar features live music and a large flatscreen TV for sports. The Papageno Bar at the Alpen Tenne restaurant has music and games. For a quieter evening, hotel bars in both Brand and Bürserberg are open to the public. Night tobogganing is offered on Tuesdays and Fridays on the illuminated Eggen run. Other activities include husky sledding with 17 huskies (Husky Toni), ice climbing at the Flurschrofa icefall, and a disco at George@Joe in nearby Bürs. The resort is not known for rowdy nightlife; visitors seeking that are directed to St. Anton.
resortCharacterDescription: Brand is a traditional ribbon resort with a long main street, a church, and a mix of traditional and modern buildings. The village centre is compact, with shops, hotels, and the Dorfbahn gondola station. Bürserberg is a quieter, bucolic village with a church and a few hotels, situated 4 km from Brand. The upper part of Bürserberg (Tschengla) is a plateau with a cluster of hotels and cross-country trails. The valley is surrounded by peaks including Schesaplana (2,964 m) and Zimba (2,634 m). The resort has turned away from mass tourism in favour of a family-friendly, quality experience. The villages retain traditional Austrian character with wooden architecture and alpine charm.
liftSystemDescription: The lift network comprises 13 lifts: 4 gondola lifts (Dorfbahn, Palüdbahn, Einhornbahn I, and the new Loischkopfbahn), 4 chairlifts, 4 surface lifts, and 1 magic carpet. The Loischkopfbahn, a 10-person gondola, replaced two older chairlifts for the 2025/26 season, with a capacity of 2,500 people per hour. The Dorfbahn and Palüdbahn gondolas start directly in Brand village, providing ski-in/ski-out access. A panoramic cable car (Panoramabahn) connects the Brand and Bürserberg sectors across a gorge. The total lift capacity is 17,000 riders per hour. Queues are rare outside peak holiday weeks. The oldest chairlift (a double from Bürserberg) is scheduled for replacement by another 10-person gondola.
linkedResortsDescription: Brandnertal is part of the Montafon Brandnertal ski association. With a multi-day WildPass (2 days or more), skiers can access five additional areas: Silvretta Montafon, Gargellen, Golm, Silvretta Bielerhöhe-Partenen, and Kristberg-Silbertal, totalling 296 km of pistes and 77 lifts. The standard Brandnertal pass covers only the connected Brand and Bürserberg domain. The association guarantees snow reliability across the region, and guests can explore the entire Montafon ski runs in addition to Brandnertal.

terrainDescription: The terrain at Brauneck consists of 30.7 km of groomed slopes, with 11% easy, 68% intermediate, and 21% difficult. The longest run is the 4 km Familienabfahrt, while the challenging Garlandabfahrt (World Cup run) drops 350 m over 1 km. The slopes are wide and offer views of the Bavarian foothills and Karwendel mountains. The area is known for its well-prepared pistes and has hosted famous ski racers such as Martina and Andreas Ertl and the Gerg sisters.
valueDescription: Day lift passes cost €53 for adults, €46 for youth, and €26 for children. The resort offers a combination ticket with Munich public transport (BOB train and ski bus) that includes the day pass. User reviews note a good price-performance ratio, and the resort is considered one of the more affordable options in Bavaria.
familyDescription: The resort is particularly family-friendly, with dedicated beginner areas at the valley stations in Wegscheid (Draxl- and Jaudenhang) and Lenggries (Streidl- and Zielhang), served by drag lifts, rope tows, and conveyor belts. The 'Funny Villa' and 'Kids Park' cater to children from age three. Six ski schools offer lessons. The 4 km Familienabfahrt is a medium-difficulty run suitable for progressing families. Night skiing on the Streidlhang (Tues-Sat 7-10 pm) adds evening entertainment.
apresSkiDescription: The resort has 16 bars and pubs, including slope-side snow bars and rustic mountain huts serving Bavarian food. The Panorama Restaurant offers a large sun terrace. Night skiing on the Streidlhang features music. The après-ski scene is relaxed and family-oriented, with a focus on cozy huts rather than rowdy parties.
resortCharacterDescription: Brauneck is a traditional Bavarian ski resort located near the village of Lenggries, about 60 km south of Munich. The area has a rustic, alpine character with many mountain huts and a panoramic restaurant. The village of Lenggries is a typical Upper Bavarian settlement on the Isar river. The resort has seen modern investments in lifts and snowmaking, but retains its traditional charm.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system comprises 15 lifts: 1 gondola, 4 chairlifts (including two modern 6-seater chairlifts: Milchhäuslexpress and Schrödelsteinbahn), and 10 surface lifts. The hourly capacity is 19,650 persons. While recent investments have modernized parts of the system, some older lifts can lead to queues on busy weekends. The resort has invested in snowmaking covering 60% of slopes with 166 snow-making machines.

terrainDescription: The ski area has two dedicated beginner zones: Hüttenberg Kinderland with a magic carpet and tow ropes, and Grotzenbüel Kinderland with a 150-meter magic carpet and Bartli-Parcours. The main slopes include three blue runs, with the Hüttenberg–Niederschlacht run being short and straightforward. Higher up, the Gumen and Selbengrat sectors offer steeper terrain. The slopes are served by gondolas and chairlifts from the village. The terrain is predominantly easy to intermediate, with a few steeper sections.
valueDescription: A full-day adult ski pass costs CHF 54–58, with a child pass at CHF 27–30. A six-day adult pass is around CHF 289. The funicular from Linthal costs CHF 7.60 single for adults, with discounts for children and half-fare cardholders. Accommodation ranges from fairy-tale hotels to mountain huts and self-catering apartments. Parking at the funicular base station is available.
familyDescription: The resort has two children's ski areas with magic carpets and fun features. The ski school offers lessons for children. The car-free village is safe for walking. Family-friendly hotels include Märchenhotel Braunwald with a fairy-tale theme, indoor play areas, and supervised kids' activities, and Cristal Hotel next to the Hüttenberg beginner area. A 3 km sledge run from Grotzenbüel to Hüttenberg is popular. There is also an ice skating rink and horse carriage rides.
apresSkiDescription: Apres-ski is low-key with a few bars in the village. The Märchenhotel offers evening storytelling and wine tasting for adults. Mountain restaurants like Chämistube and Berggasthaus Gumen provide cozy evening dining. There are no nightclubs or late-night party venues.
resortCharacterDescription: Braunwald is a traditional car-free village on a terrace above the Linth valley, first mentioned in 1421. Access is via a funicular from Linthal, opened in 1907. The village has a mix of historic hotels and modern infrastructure, with alpine chalet-style architecture. It is not purpose-built but has grown organically.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system includes a funicular from Linthal to the village, plus gondolas and chairlifts. There are 10–11 lifts with a capacity of 6,420 persons per hour. The main gondolas are the Hüttenberg–Grotzenbüel and Gumenbahn. The lifts are modern but not high-speed. Queues are rare outside peak periods.

terrainDescription: The Bürchen/Törbel ski area is situated on a high plateau above the Rhone Valley, with slopes running between 1,476 m and 2,532 m. The terrain comprises 10 km of easy runs, 10 km of intermediate runs, and 5 km of difficult runs, totaling 25 km of groomed pistes. The slopes are groomed daily and are set amidst birch and Swiss stone pine trees. The area offers a vertical drop of 1,056 m.
valueDescription: A day lift pass for adults costs SFr. 53 (approximately €58) during the main season, with children's tickets at SFr. 35. Children under 6 years old ski for just CHF 5 per day. The ski area has been included in the Magic Pass since the 2023/2024 winter season, providing additional value. Several budget hotels and pensions are available in the village.
familyDescription: The Bodmen children's paradise features a dedicated slope with small tunnels, slalom poles, and clearly marked jumps, designed for first steps in the snow. A large children's playground is also on site. Childcare is available for 7 hours per day. Children under 6 ski for CHF 5 per day. The resort mascot is MoosAlbi. A magic carpet and a child lift are present.
apresSkiDescription: The resort has one disco, several bars and cafés, and a mountain restaurant at Moosalp. Wellness facilities include a public sauna, fitness centre, indoor pool, and spa. Other activities include paragliding, indoor tennis, a toboggan run, and 31 km of hiking trails. Torchlight tours and curling are also available.
resortCharacterDescription: Bürchen is a traditional Valais mountain village situated on a terrace high above the Rhone Valley, surrounded by birch woods. The village has an educational birch walk. The ski area is located on the Moosalp high plateau, offering views of the Bietschhorn and the Mischabel massif.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of 8 lifts, including surface lifts and one triple chairlift. Waiting times are very rare, even during peak periods. The lift staff are described as polite and helpful.
linkedResortsDescription: Bürchen/Törbel is part of the Rund um Visp ski area, which includes the Augstbord and Vispertal resorts (including Visperterminen). A free ski bus connects Bürchen/Törbel with the nearby resorts of Unterbäch and Eischoll. The combined area offers approximately 100 km of slopes across multiple villages.

terrainDescription: The Brentonico ski area comprises two sectors, Polsa and San Valentino, with a total of 24 km of pistes. The terrain is predominantly intermediate (14 km of red runs), with 7 km of easy blue runs and 3 km of difficult black runs. The longest pistes are the Polsa and Montagnola runs, which wind through wooded sections. The slopes are wide and sunny, and 80% of the skiable area is covered by artificial snow. The vertical drop is from 1,150 m to 1,600 m.
valueDescription: A day lift pass costs €48, making it one of the cheapest ski resorts in Trentino. The resort is small and family-run, with affordable accommodation options including self-catering apartments and campsites in the villages of Polsa and San Valentino. The proximity to Verona and Lake Garda reduces travel costs, and the resort is known for its good value compared to larger Alpine destinations.
familyDescription: The resort is highly family-friendly, with dedicated children's areas including the Polsa Baby Park (featuring snow games, a practice bobsled run, and a sledding run) and the Happy Snow park in San Valentino. The nursery slopes are served by five conveyor belts and a ski lift, and the ski school offers lessons for children from age three. The slopes are wide, sunny, and safe, and the resort is less than an hour's drive from Trento and Verona, making it easily accessible for families.
apresSkiDescription: The apres-ski scene is quiet, with four bars and pubs and one club in the area. The resort is known for its relaxed, family-friendly atmosphere rather than lively nightlife. There are eight restaurants serving local Trentino cuisine, and the villages of Polsa and San Valentino offer a calm evening environment.
resortCharacterDescription: Brentonico is a traditional mountain village on the Monte Baldo plateau, with a historic centre featuring 16th-century palaces, churches, and a botanical garden. The ski area is located in the hamlets of Polsa and San Valentino, which are small, quiet settlements with alpine-style buildings. The resort is not purpose-built; it retains an authentic, rural character with working farms, chestnut groves, and a natural ice rink.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of three modern four-seat chairlifts, one ski lift, and five conveyor belts (tapis roulants). The chairlifts are brand new and serve the main slopes. The practice areas are equipped with treadmills for easy access. Artificial snowmaking covers 80% of the pistes, ensuring reliable conditions. The lifts operate from 9:00 to 16:45.

terrainDescription: The terrain is dominated by wide, gently pitched cruising runs on south-facing slopes above the treeline. The ski area connects the slopes of Pez d'Artgas mountain between the villages of Brigels, Waltensburg and Andiast. A terrain park with a funslope and skimovie route is available. Off-piste terrain between the prepared trails offers easy-to-ski free riding. The slopes are sunny and suitable for intermediate-level skiers.
offPisteDescription: Off-piste skiing is possible between the prepared trails across hundreds of acres of easy-to-ski terrain. The area is not steep, making it accessible for intermediate skiers looking to venture off-piste. Freeride is allowed and there is a designated freeride area. The south-facing slopes can become sunbaked, so powder conditions are best shortly after a storm.
valueDescription: A six-day adult lift pass costs 252 CHF, and a six-day child pass costs 138 CHF. Lunch on the mountain at a chalet like Burleun Brigels costs under 20 CHF for a full meal. The resort is smaller and less expensive than nearby major resorts like Laax, offering good value for a family ski holiday.
familyDescription: The resort is family-oriented with a Snowli-Kinderland learning area directly at the valley station, featuring magic carpets and gentle slopes. The ski school offers lessons for children. The village is compact with short distances to lifts and kid-friendly lodging and dining. A free local bus connects the village districts to the valley station. The slopes are quiet and uncrowded, providing a stress-free environment for beginners and families.
apresSkiDescription: Apres-ski is relaxed with 7 bars and pubs and one club in the village. The Pipa Lounge at Hotel Mulin offers a convivial atmosphere. Evening dining is available at several restaurants within a short stroll. The scene is low-key and family-friendly rather than rowdy.
resortCharacterDescription: Brigels is a traditional Swiss village with a compact cluster of chalets, hotels, and farms. It has a lovely old town centre with a romantic feel. The resort is not purpose-built; it retains an authentic alpine character.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of 4 chairlifts, 2 T-bar lifts, 2 rope lifts, 1 conveyor belt, and 2 magic carpets, with a total capacity of 6,550 persons per hour. Some lifts are older, but the system includes modern features like a funslope and timed race course. The main chairlift from Brigels to Crest Falla undergoes regular maintenance.

terrainDescription: The Plose ski area offers 42.5 km of pistes across a south-facing slope. The terrain is 46% beginner, 22% intermediate, and 32% advanced, with no expert-rated runs. The summit plateau and mid-mountain areas feature wide, forgiving cruisers. The Trametsch run is South Tyrol's longest valley run at 9 km with 1,400 m vertical descent. The Palmschoss slope is 2.4 km with steep curves and forest sections. The Crazy Horse black piste descends from the Pfannspitze. Snowmaking covers most of the network.
valueDescription: Adult day lift tickets range from €59 to €68 depending on season, with peak winter at €68. Junior tickets (ages 8–15) cost €42, seniors €50, and children under 8 ski free. Children born between 2008 and 2017 receive a 30% discount on adult prices, and seniors over 65 get 10% off. Disabled persons receive a 20% discount. Parking at the valley station is free. Multi-day tickets reduce the daily rate by €5–8. The Dolomiti Superski pass includes Plose, offering access to 1,200 km of slopes.
familyDescription: The Plose ski area is considered beginner- and family-friendly, with 20 of its 42 km of slopes marked blue. The Rossalm area has dedicated slow zones and progression terrain.
