Children under 8 receive a free ski pass when accompanying an adult paying full price. The RudiRun toboggan run is 9 km long, and the Plosebob offers a 1 km rail-guided bobsled track. A St. Nicholas event takes place on December 6. The gondola base is a 15-minute bus ride from Brixen, which provides non-ski options and year-round infrastructure.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski options include the Vallazza Apreskibar at the Palmschoss chairlift base, the P3 at the valley station with a large sun terrace, the Plosestodl pizzeria, and the Ban Rutzner. High on the mountain, La Finestra offers an aperitivo view of the Brixen valley. The scene is described as more about decent meals than shots bars, with a visitor rating of 3.8 out of 5 for après-ski.
resortCharacterDescription: The Plose is the local mountain of Brixen, a bilingual medieval town with year-round infrastructure including restaurants, bakeries, and wine bars. The gondola valley station is in St. Andrä, 7 km from Brixen. Brixen is the oldest town in Tyrol with a cathedral and cultural offerings. The resort is not purpose-built; it is based around an existing town with proper accommodation and services.
liftSystemDescription: The ski area has 7 lifts: 2 gondola lifts, 4 chair lifts, and 1 drag lift. The main access is an eight-person gondola from St. Andrä to Kreuztal mid-station, then a second gondola to the summit at 2,446 m. A new ultra-modern gondola was built in 2023. Lift capacity is 12,500 persons per hour. Operating hours are 08:30 to 16:30. Some lifts are modern, while others are older but sufficient to avoid queues.
linkedResortsDescription: Plose is part of the Dolomiti Superski network, which includes 12 ski regions with 1,200 km of slopes. A combined ski pass also covers the Gitschberg Jochtal ski area, as well as local lifts in Funes, Velturno, Luson, and Terento. The Sellaronda circuit is accessible via the Dolomiti Superski pass, though Plose is not directly connected to the Sellaronda; it is a separate area within the network.

terrainDescription: The ski area offers a mix of terrain: wide carving slopes on the Weißspitz sector, steep black runs on the Leppleskofel chairlift (the highest in East Tyrol at 2,683 m), and attractive valley runs that are snow-covered until spring. The slopes are predominantly sunny and treeless above the treeline, providing open skiing. The beginner area at the base features three surface lifts and a practice lift, with short, child-friendly descents.
offPisteDescription: Off-piste terrain is accessed by skinning up the slopes, with miles of touring terrain in the surrounding Hohe Tauern National Park. The high altitude and cold temperatures preserve powder snow well into April. Freeriding options are plentiful around the Leppleskofel area when conditions are good. Hiring a guide is recommended for safe exploration of the backcountry.
valueDescription: Lift passes are priced at €64–70 for adults and €32–35 for children (aged 6–18), with children under 6 free. Multi-day Ski)Hit passes are valid in five other East Tyrolean ski areas and on the Ankogel and Mölltal Glacier in Carinthia. Free parking is available at the base. The resort is marketed as an insider tip, offering good value compared to larger, more crowded resorts.
familyDescription: The resort has a dedicated beginner area at the base with three surface lifts, a practice lift, and two conveyor belts for children. Ski school and ski kindergarten are available, with childcare from age 3 for 4 hours a day at €51. The slopes are mostly easy for children, and there is a playground, adventure park, and a fun park. The car-free village and free ski bus add convenience for families.
apresSkiDescription: Apres-ski is low-key, with a few bars and cafes in the village and on-mountain huts. The Eggenhütte is a popular off-piste chalet serving traditional Austrian food and drinks such as glühwein, housemade sausage, and kaiserschmarrn. The umbrella bar at the top of Mooseralm offers a sunny spot for a drink. There are no discos or late-night venues.
resortCharacterDescription: St. Jakob im Defereggental is a traditional Austrian village with authentic atmosphere, traditional houses, and a quiet, tranquil setting away from mass tourism. The base area is basic with ski shops, a few hotels, and ski bars. The resort is not purpose-built; it retains a genuine village character with a car-free village center and free parking.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of 7 lifts: a 6-seater gondola (Brunnalm), three chairlifts (including the new Leppleskofel 6-seater with heated seats and weather hoods, the highest in East Tyrol at 2,683 m; the Weißspitz 6-seater; and the Mooserberg 3-seater), two T-bar lifts (Feistritz and St. Leonhard), and a practice lift. Total lift capacity is 12,734 persons per hour. The Leppleskofel chairlift was added in 2022/23.
linkedResortsDescription: The resort is not physically connected to other ski areas by lifts, but multi-day Ski)Hit passes are valid in five other East Tyrolean ski areas: Grossglockner Kals-Matrei, Lienz Zettersfeld Hochstein, Obertilliach, Sillian-Hochpustertal, and also on the Ankogel and Mölltal Glacier in Carinthia. The nearest linked resort by car is Lienz, about 35 minutes away.

valueDescription: A day lift pass for adults costs CHF 49, with children's tickets at CHF 31. Families of three or more paying persons receive a 10% discount on day tickets. The region is described as providing good value for money, with many Alpine inns and restaurants.
familyDescription: The Mythenregion is particularly popular with families, offering a 10% discount on day tickets for families of three or more. The compact ski area allows parents to keep children in view. However, specific children's facilities are not detailed in the source text.
resortCharacterDescription: The resort is part of the Mythenregion, a collection of small ski areas in the Swiss Prealps. The area features many Alpine inns and restaurants, giving it a traditional, rustic character. The Rotenflue summit area has several restaurants. The region is popular with nature lovers in summer as well.
liftSystemDescription: The Rotenfluebahn is a modern cable car that replaced an older one, now reaching within a few metres of the summit at 1,571 m. The valley station is linked by bus to Schwyz railway station. The wider Mythenregion has a total of 11 ski lifts and 4 cable cars, though the specific lift count for this resort is 7.
linkedResortsDescription: The resort is part of the Mythenregion, which also includes the ski areas of Brunni-Haggenegg, Holzegg, Handgruobi-Rotenfluh, and Ibergeregg. Together they offer 50 km of varied pistes. Additionally, the Adventure Region Mythen includes Sattel-Hochstuckli and Neusell-Rothenthurm, though these are separate ski areas.

terrainDescription: The Brusson-Estoul ski area offers gentle, low-to-medium difficulty slopes spread across approximately 10 km of pistes. The terrain is characterized by wide, forested runs and open alpine meadows, with most slopes facing east and receiving morning sunshine. The Estoul-Palasinaz chairlift provides access to the Fontane piste, a long blue run descending through larch forests, while the Campeille-Litteran chairlift reaches 2,235 m and serves red runs such as the Champeille. The area is dominated by beginner (47%) and intermediate (53%) terrain, with no advanced pistes, making it ideal for relaxed cruising and learning.
valueDescription: Brusson is one of the most affordable ski destinations in the Aosta Valley. The Magic Pass, costing €110, provides up to six days of skiing across 11 small resorts including Brusson, with free accommodation for up to two minors in participating hotels. Local Brusson lift passes are cheaper than the full Monterosa Ski pass, and accommodation options such as family-run hotels and self-catering apartments offer good value. Dining at local trattorias and mountain huts is reasonably priced, with traditional Aosta Valley dishes like polenta concia and fonduta available at moderate cost.
familyDescription: Brusson is highly family-friendly, with a dedicated Baby Snow Park at Estoul featuring inflatables, rafts, and a safe play area for children. The gentle slopes and small, uncrowded ski area make it ideal for beginners and young skiers. The Brusson ski school is known for personalized attention, and the village offers ice skating and easy access to cross-country trails. The traffic-free village centre and lakeside area provide a safe environment for families to explore on foot.
resortCharacterDescription: Brusson is a traditional Alpine village in the Val d'Ayas, characterized by stone and wooden chalets, a historic centre, and a relaxed mountain atmosphere. It is not a purpose-built resort; the village retains its authentic identity with local culture, including a lake, the ruins of Graines Castle, and the Chamousira gold mines. The architecture is genuine, and the village maintains a quiet, friendly character, offering a peaceful escape from larger, more commercialized resorts.
liftSystemDescription: The Brusson-Estoul ski area has four lifts: two double chairlifts and two surface lifts. The Estoul-Palasinaz chairlift provides access to blue runs, while the Campeille-Litteran chairlift reaches 2,235 m for red runs. The lifts are modern and well-maintained, with low skier numbers ensuring minimal queues. The area is not lift-connected to the main Monterosa Ski domain, but the same lift pass covers both. The lifts operate from early December to mid-March, with snowmaking covering 15 km of slopes.
linkedResortsDescription: Brusson is part of the Monterosa Ski area, which includes Champoluc, Gressoney-La-Trinité, Gressoney-Saint-Jean, and Alagna Valsesia. However, Brusson's local slopes are not lift-connected to the main Monterosa domain; skiers must travel by car or shuttle (approximately 12 km to Champoluc) to access the larger area. The Monterosa Ski pass covers both Brusson and the linked resorts, providing access to 180 km of pistes across three valleys. The connections between valleys (Bettaforca Pass and Passo dei Salati) are susceptible to closure in high winds.

terrainDescription: The Brévent/Flégère ski area is south-facing, located opposite Mont Blanc, with most runs above the treeline. The two sectors are connected by a cable car. The terrain consists of bowl-shaped valleys and open slopes, offering a mix of easy slopes in the upper section and more challenging valley runs down to Chamonix and Les Praz, both classified as difficult. A beginner area with several lifts is located at the Plan Praz base station, and the Savoy ski area at the foot of the resort provides gentle nursery slopes.
valueDescription: A day lift pass for the Brévent/Flégère area costs €74 for adults and €62.90 for children during main season. Chamonix is generally more expensive than nearby resorts, with higher accommodation and dining costs reflecting its international popularity. However, the short transfer from Geneva (under 90 minutes) reduces travel expenses for a short break, and the Mont Blanc Unlimited pass offers access to multiple areas across three countries, providing good value for those planning to explore widely.
familyDescription: The Brévent/Flégère area includes dedicated beginner zones: the Savoy ski area at the foot of the resort in Chamonix centre, and the Plan Praz base station with several lifts for beginners. The Savoy offers green slopes and a children's area with snowmaking. Additionally, Les Planards, a 2-minute walk from Chamonix centre, provides a larger beginner area with 4 runs (2 green, 1 blue, 1 red) and 41 snow cannons. The south-facing slopes and bowl-shaped valleys offer family-friendly terrain, and the area is connected by cable car between Brévent and Flégère, allowing families to explore at their own pace.
apresSkiDescription: Chamonix town has a buzzing centre with plenty of restaurants and bars within walking distance. Popular après-ski venues include Chambre Neuf and Elevation 1904, offering live music and dancing. The dining scene ranges from hearty Savoyard fare at La Caleche to Michelin-starred experiences at Albert 1er, with over 170 restaurants in the valley. The town's nightlife is energetic and varied, with options from relaxed cocktail bars to late-night clubs.
resortCharacterDescription: Chamonix is a historic Alpine town, not a purpose-built resort. It has a pedestrianised high street with shops, restaurants, and bars, blending traditional architecture with modern amenities. The town is lively year-round with a cosmopolitan atmosphere, and neighborhoods like Argentière offer quieter traditional charm. The Brévent/Flégère ski area is accessed from Chamonix centre and Les Praz, with the Savoy beginner area at the foot of the resort.
liftSystemDescription: The Brévent/Flégère area has 17 lifts, including a new high-speed detachable 6-person chairlift (Évettes) installed for the 2026/2027 season. The two sectors are connected by a cable car. The area includes a mix of gondolas, chairlifts, and surface lifts, with a beginner area at Plan Praz served by several lifts. The lift system is modern and efficient, with recent upgrades.
linkedResortsDescription: The Brévent and Flégère sectors are connected by a cable car, but the other Chamonix ski areas (Grands Montets, Balme, Les Houches) are not linked by lifts; they are connected by a free shuttle bus. The Mont Blanc Unlimited pass provides access to all Chamonix areas plus Les Houches, Megève, Courmayeur (Italy), and Verbier (Switzerland), totalling over 400 km of pistes across three countries.

terrainDescription: The terrain consists of a single long run from the summit at 1,141m down to the valley, with a moderate gradient suitable for beginners and intermediates. The upper section has blue and red pistes, while the lower area has a T-bar lift serving easy slopes for less experienced skiers. There are also ungroomed ski routes for more sporty skiing.
offPisteDescription: Off-piste skiing is permitted and guided freeride tours are offered with local mountain guides. However, the terrain is limited due to the small size of the ski area and low altitude.
valueDescription: Day tickets are very affordable at €28 for adults, €26 for youths, and €17.50 for children. Parking at the base station is free. Accommodation in nearby villages like Buching and Halblech offers budget-friendly options.
familyDescription: The ski area is specifically designed for families and beginners, with easy slopes and a dedicated nursery area served by a T-bar lift. A 2.5 km natural toboggan run from the summit is illuminated until 10pm, providing evening entertainment. Ski school is available for children and adults.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski is low-key, centred on the Buchenberg Alm mountain restaurant with a large sun terrace serving traditional Allgäu food. The nearby village of Buching has a few bars and restaurants, but the resort is not known for nightlife.
resortCharacterDescription: The resort is a small, family-oriented ski area in the Ostallgäu region, located near the village of Buching. It is not a purpose-built resort but rather a local hill with a double chairlift and a few T-bars, set in a rural landscape with views of the Allgäu Alps.
liftSystemDescription: The ski area is served by a single double chairlift that takes skiers from the valley to the summit at 1,141m.
linkedResortsDescription: The ski area is part of a network of small resorts in the Ostallgäu region. Nearby ski areas include Tegelberg (Schwangau), Breitenberg, Alpspitz-Nesselwang, and others, which can be combined for a varied holiday. However, they are not directly linked by lifts; a car or bus is needed to move between them.

terrainDescription: The ski area covers 22 km of well-groomed slopes with a balanced mix of blue and red runs. The base area at 854 m features gentle practice slopes and the Pletzi-Bär Park with a magic carpet for beginners. Higher up, from the summit at 1,456 m, there are 3 km of black marked pistes descending to Ortisei, plus red runs and a natural snow descent for freeriders. The longest run is the Familienabfahrt at 4.5 km. Slopes are generally uncrowded even during peak season.
offPisteDescription: A marked, controlled, and avalanche-safe natural snow descent remains ungroomed, providing an authentic off-piste experience. Ski tourers find ideal conditions in the Buchensteinwand Ski Touring World, and diverse deep-snow slopes meander from the summit to the three villages. The resort also offers ski routes for advanced skiers.
valueDescription: Lift passes are priced at €50 for a one-day adult ticket, €38 for youth, and €27 for children. A six-day adult pass costs €239, and a six-day child pass costs €133. The resort is part of the 3-Country Leisure Arena, so a 3-day or longer pass grants access to eight resorts across Tyrol, Salzburg, and Bavaria, adding significant value. Accommodation ranges from 4-star wellness hotels to family-run guesthouses.
familyDescription: The Pletzi-Bär Park at the base station is a dedicated children's learning zone with a magic carpet, obstacles, and a wave run, supervised by the mascot Pletzi bear. Two ski schools offer English-speaking instruction. Nursery slopes are separate from faster traffic, and the uncrowded slopes allow safe progression. Off-slope activities include two toboggan runs, ice skating, and winter hiking trails. The villages of St. Ulrich and St. Jakob are walkable and car-free in parts.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski is low-key and centered at the base station, where the Café Restaurant buach.blick has an umbrella bar with a lively atmosphere. The resort has around 10 bars and pubs and 2 clubs, but the scene is relaxed. Mountain huts such as Hoametzl-Hütte and Kröpfllalm serve traditional Tyrolean dishes for early evening dining. For more vibrant nightlife, larger resorts like St. Johann in Tirol are a short drive away.
resortCharacterDescription: The resort is set in the scenic Pillersee Valley, a traditional Tyrolean landscape with three small villages (St. Ulrich am Pillersee, St. Jakob in Haus, and Hochfilzen) that retain an authentic alpine character. The Jakobskreuz, the world's largest accessible summit cross at 1,456 m, is a landmark. The atmosphere is calm and unhurried, away from large ski circuits. Accommodation includes historic country hotels and family-run guesthouses.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of 2 chairlifts and 6 surface lifts, with additional conveyor belts in the children's area. Lifts are modern and well-maintained, and queues are rare even during peak season. The ski area is connected across three villages by ski buses and lifts, with a total capacity of 10,035 persons per hour.
linkedResortsDescription: Buchensteinwand is part of the 3-Country Leisure Arena (3 Länder Freizeit-Arena), which encompasses eight ski resorts in the three-country corner of Tyrol, Salzburg, and Bavaria. A 3-day or longer ski pass grants access to all areas within this network, including nearby resorts such as Steinplatte and Fieberbrunn.

terrainDescription: The Buffaure-Ciampac ski area offers 25 km of slopes, with 24% beginner, 56% intermediate, and 20% advanced. The Ciampac side features a famous black run of 2.2 km with a vertical drop of 643 m, average gradient of 32%, and maximum gradient of 56%, descending from 2,160 m to 1,517 m at Alba. There are five red runs and two black runs at Ciampac. On the Buffaure side, there is a black run called 'Vulcano' with a 566 m vertical drop. The area also includes a long red run 'Panorama' that descends from the Buffaure refuge to the valley floor. The terrain is mostly intermediate, with some challenging black runs, and is used as a connection to the Sellaronda.
valueDescription: Lift pass prices for the Val di Fassa/Carezza area are €73 for a one-day adult ticket, €48 for youth, and €35 for children (as of the 2025-2026 season). Accommodation in the Val di Fassa valley is noted for good value, with many affordable hotels and apartments. The area offers a Dolomiti Superski pass for access to the wider network.
familyDescription: The Ciampac area has a high-altitude playground called Ciampark with a free snow tube and sled slope, and a ski school camp with a moving sidewalk that can be used free of charge. There are four mountain restaurants. The Buffaure side has a Family Park Capitel and well-equipped field schools. The area is suitable for families with beginner slopes and dedicated children's facilities.
apresSkiDescription: The Pozza di Fassa - Buffaure - Ciampac area has 4 bars and pubs. Night skiing is available on a 1 km lighted run at the Aloch slope in Buffaure. The nearby village of Canazei offers a wider selection of après-ski bars, but the immediate area is quiet.
resortCharacterDescription: The Buffaure-Ciampac area is part of the Val di Fassa valley, which consists of traditional Ladin villages such as Pozza di Fassa, Vigo di Fassa, and Alba di Canazei. These villages feature historic town centres with wooden verandas, cobbled streets, and a mix of traditional and modern amenities. The area is not purpose-built but retains alpine charm with a strong cultural identity.
liftSystemDescription: The Buffaure-Ciampac ski area has 12 lifts (according to snow-online) or 13 (known fact). The Ciampac gondola is a 10-person high-performance cable car that takes about 10 minutes from Alba to the summit. The Buffaure gondola is an 8-person gondola. There are also chairlifts, including a 4-person chair with canopy (Orsa Maggiore), and surface lifts. The lifts are modern and efficient, with most having snowmaking coverage.
linkedResortsDescription: The Buffaure-Ciampac area is part of the Val di Fassa ski region, which connects to the Belvedere-Col Rodella ski area via the Alba-Col dei Rossi cable car. This allows access to the Sellaronda circuit, a 40 km loop around the Sella Massif that connects to Val Gardena, Arabba, and Alta Badia. The area also links to the Carezza ski area via bus, and to the San Pellegrino and Marmolada areas. The Dolomiti Superski pass covers all these areas.

terrainDescription: The ski area is divided into six sectors: Cinque Laghi, Pradalago, Grostè, Monte Spinale, Monte Vigo (Folgarida and Marilleva), and Doss del Sabion (Pinzolo). Most slopes are treelined except the highest on Grostè, which is above the treeline. The highest lift-served point is Passo Grostè at 2,444m (2,504m according to some sources). The terrain is predominantly intermediate, with 45% intermediate, 30% beginner, and 25% advanced runs. Notable runs include the 3-Tre World Cup slalom, the Spinale Direttissima with the Schumacher Streif, and the Canalone Miramonti. The ski area spans 156km of interconnected runs across the linked resorts.
offPisteDescription: Off-piste skiing is limited due to restrictions from the Adamello Brenta Nature Park. The best off-piste terrain is on the Grostè side, where marked black runs and off-piste itineraries descend through rock corridors, including the Canalone Miramonti couloir. These routes require good snow conditions and a mountain guide. Off-piste depends on natural snowfall, typically strongest from January to early March. Most of the promising off-piste terrain is subject to park restrictions.
valueDescription: Lift passes use dynamic pricing, with a six-day adult pass for the Madonna-Pinzolo area costing between €280 and €320. Children under 8 ski free. There is no cheap beginner pass. Accommodation ranges from €1,045 to €23,450 per week, with many hotels offering half-board. The resort is considered affordable compared to other world-class resorts, with package holidays often based on half-board stays.
familyDescription: Madonna di Campiglio has three beginner areas. The main nursery slopes are at Campo Carlo Magno, a bus ride from the village, with gentle slopes and magic carpet lifts. There are also beginner areas on Pradalago near the Mini Ursus snowpark and a private slope at the Rainalter ski school. The resort has family parks in Folgarida, Daolasa, and Marilleva. Ski schools offer lessons for children. The village is pedestrianised and walkable, but beginners need to use the ski bus to reach the main learning area.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski is sophisticated and Italian-style, with Aperol Spritz on terraces. Des Alpes on the main square is the default gathering point as lifts close. There is a weekly torchlight descent on the 3-Tre slope visible from village restaurants. Night skiing is available with Sunset Ski. The village has many wine bars and restaurants, and the scene is more relaxed than rowdy.
resortCharacterDescription: Madonna di Campiglio is a traditional, car-free village with elegant 19th-century buildings. The village centre is pedestrianised, compact, and walkable. It has a smart Italian mountain town feel with boutique shops and refined hotels. It is not a purpose-built resort; it has historical charm dating back to the Habsburg era.
liftSystemDescription: The lift network includes gondolas, chairlifts, and surface lifts. A modern high-capacity gondola goes from Madonna di Campiglio to Passo Grostè. The Pinzolo-Campiglio Express is a 16-minute gondola linking the two resorts. Recent upgrades include a new six-seater chairlift with heated seats on the Malga Panciana-Ometto line. The system is efficient but can get crowded at peak times. 95% of runs have artificial snowmaking.
linkedResortsDescription: The Ski Area Campiglio Dolomiti di Brenta links Madonna di Campiglio with Pinzolo, Folgarida, and Marilleva, forming 156km of interconnected runs served by 58 lifts. Additionally, the Superskirama lift pass covers eight resorts including Passo Tonale, with 150 lifts and 380km of pistes.

terrainDescription: The ski area sits on a high plateau at around 1,429 m, with slopes rising to Colle del Caprio and higher zones near Monte Miletto. Runs are distributed across five main sectors: Capo d’Acqua, Del Caprio, Anfiteatro, Lavarelle, and San Nicola. Beginner-friendly pistes such as Scuola, Lavarelle, and San Nicola are located near the base, offering gentle gradients for learning. Intermediate runs like Capo d’Acqua and Lupo Grigio provide a gradual progression, while more technical descents—Cresta Plattner, Cristallo, Del Caprio, and Olimpica—demand solid technique and offer sustained pitches. The terrain is set within a natural amphitheatre of mountains, and on clear days the views extend to the Adriatic Sea to the east and the Gulf of Naples to the west.
familyDescription: Campitello Matese is rated the top family destination in Molise. The resort offers dedicated beginner slopes (Scuola, Lavarelle, San Nicola) near the base, providing a safe environment for children and first-timers. The compact layout and proximity of accommodation to the lifts make it easy to manage with kids. Several ski schools operate on-site, including Scuola Sci Campitello 2000, Scuola Sci Muccilli, and Scuola Sci Riccardo Plattner, offering lessons for all ages. The atmosphere is relaxed and family-friendly, with a human scale that avoids the overwhelming feel of larger resorts.
resortCharacterDescription: Campitello Matese is a purpose-built ski resort that developed from the 1970s onward, originally a shelter for shepherds. It lies on a broad plateau at 1,450 m, surrounded by the Matese massif. The architecture is functional, with accommodation and services clustered near the lifts rather than forming a historic village centre. The setting is a natural amphitheatre of mountains, and on clear days the panorama reaches both the Adriatic and Tyrrhenian seas. The atmosphere is authentic and low-key, focused on outdoor activities rather than commercial entertainment.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of seven lifts: three chairlifts (Capo d’Acqua, Del Caprio, Anfiteatro), a magic carpet (nastro trasportatore), a chairlift at La Piana/Lavarelle, and a surface lift at San Nicola. The Del Caprio chairlift also carries pedestrians. Lifts operate daily from 8:00 to 16:45. The network is modern but modest in scale, serving the main sectors efficiently. Queues are generally short except on peak weekends. The resort also has snow-making facilities covering most slopes, helping to maintain conditions during marginal weather.

terrainDescription: The ski area sits in a karstic basin at around 1800m, with slopes ranging from 1800m to 2000m. The terrain includes a mix of gentle beginner slopes and more challenging runs, notably the Canalino, a 1000m red run with a significant gradient that remains shaded. The resort has 13–15 pistes, with a substantial portion rated advanced or expert (around 40% expert according to one source), and a snowpark called Upside Down. A 5km Nordic arena is also available for cross-country skiing, and a Fun Park with sledges and tubes is served by a magic carpet.
offPisteDescription: Off-piste skiing and ski mountaineering are possible on the Campocatino massif, with several itineraries offering views extending to the Tyrrhenian Sea. The karstic basin provides natural terrain for off-trail skiing, though specific marked off-piste routes are not detailed in the sources.
valueDescription: Lift pass prices are relatively low: a daily pass costs €30 on holidays and €25 on weekdays, with afternoon passes at €22 and €18 respectively. Hourly passes range from €13 to €20. Discounts are available for affiliated members, young people, and residents. The resort is considered good value compared to larger Italian ski areas.
familyDescription: The resort offers dedicated facilities for families: a Baby Park with inflatable games, a Fun Park with sledges and tubes served by a magic carpet, and a ski school area with its own skilift and magic carpet. The Nordic arena provides a 5km cross-country loop. The village of Guarcino below offers traditional restaurants and the famous Amaretti di Guarcino cookies.
resortCharacterDescription: Campocatino is a small ski area situated in a karstic basin at 1800m in the Apennine mountains, above the medieval town of Guarcino. The resort itself consists of lift bases, a few restaurants, and ski schools, but no significant accommodation at the slopes; visitors typically stay in Guarcino or nearby. The area is known for its dark skies and hosts an astronomical observatory.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of one modern quad chairlift and several surface lifts (skilifts and magic carpets), totaling 6 lifts. The quad chair provides main access to the upper slopes, while the surface lifts serve beginner areas and the Fun Park. No high-speed gondolas or detachable chairs are present.

terrainDescription: The ski area lies on the western side of the Majella massif, with slopes between 1,145 m and 2,350 m. The runs are predominantly easy blue runs, including the sunny Tavola Rotonda, Serra Campanile, and Quartarana slopes. One red run (Le Capre, 190 m) and one black run (Porrara, 2,100 m long with a 700 m vertical drop through a steep channel) provide moderate and expert challenges. A green run for children is also available.
valueDescription: A day lift pass costs around €30, making it one of the cheapest ski resorts in Italy. Accommodation in family-run B&Bs and dining at local trattorias are budget-friendly compared to Alpine resorts, with the village offering affordable self-catering options. The Consorzio Sci dei Parchi skipass provides access to multiple areas at a reasonable price.
familyDescription: The resort has two ski school areas with moving carpets, a children's snow park (Giovilandia Kinder Park), and a synthetic ice skating rink open even in summer. The slopes are mostly easy blue runs, and the village is pedestrian-friendly with a quiet, safe atmosphere. The medieval centre offers family-friendly dining and a Christmas market in season.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski is low-key, centred on cozy restaurants and bars in the medieval village. La Scarpetta Di Venere is a recommended spot for local Abruzzese cuisine. The atmosphere is quiet and family-oriented, with no lively nightlife scene; evenings are typically spent enjoying hearty meals and local wines.
resortCharacterDescription: Campo di Giove is a traditional medieval mountain village with stone houses, narrow alleys, and a historic centre featuring the Church of Sant'Eustachio and Cantelmo Castle ruins. It is not a purpose-built resort; the ski area is located about 3 km from the town. The village offers authentic Abruzzese character with local shops, a small museum, and a peaceful atmosphere.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of one chairlift (1,455 m long, 513 m vertical drop) connecting the base at 1,160 m to Guado di Coccia at 1,676 m, plus two ski lifts (Serra Campanile and Quartarana) and three surface lifts. An advanced artificial snowmaking system covers the slopes, allowing the season to extend into spring. Lifts are modern but limited in number.
linkedResortsDescription: Campo di Giove is part of the Consorzio Sci dei Parchi (Ski Consortium of Parks), which includes the nearby resorts of Scanno Monte Rotondo, Passo Godi, and Passo Leonardo. A single skipass grants access to all these areas, expanding the available terrain beyond the local slopes.

terrainDescription: The ski area comprises three main sectors: Baia, Campo Felice, and Brecciara, with 24 runs totaling about 40 km. Beginner-friendly runs include the blue Florian, while intermediate skiers can enjoy red runs like Guasto and Brecciara. Advanced skiers have black runs such as Direttissima and Canalone, and the Delle Aquile black run descends from Monte Rotondo with over 600 metres of vertical drop. The resort also features cross-country skiing trails and a snowpark.
offPisteDescription: Off-piste skiing is available around the Brecciara lift, offering wide expanses of untouched snow. A local guide is recommended for safety.
valueDescription: Campo Felice offers discounted lift passes for children under 16. Ski pass options include daily, multi-day, and seasonal passes, as well as the 'Skipass dei Parchi' which covers Campo Felice, Ovindoli-Monte Magnola, and Gran Sasso d'Italia, and the 'Skipass Due Nevi' covering Campo Felice and Gran Sasso d'Italia. The resort is known for being good value compared to larger Alpine resorts, though specific prices are not given.
familyDescription: The resort has a pedestrianised centre, a children's playground, a snow-tubing area, and a 'Fun Kids Area' for safe play. Beginner areas include the 'Manovia Baby' and the 'Brecciara' practice carpet. Ski-in/ski-out accommodation is available, and children under 16 receive discounted lift passes.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski options include 'Il Rifugio' at the base, offering live music and drinks, and 'La Stazione' for cocktails and dancing. The resort also has several restaurants and bars, with hot chocolate being a popular winter drink.
resortCharacterDescription: Campo Felice is a ski resort on a karst plateau in the Apennines, surrounded by five peaks over 2,000 metres. The resort opened in the 1970s (some sources say late 1950s) and has developed into a modern winter sports destination. The village area is pedestrianised and offers a relaxed atmosphere, with accommodation ranging from hotels to apartments.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system includes 15 lifts: 5 quad chairlifts (including detachable grip), 4 double chairlifts, and 6 surface lifts (platters, ski tows, magic carpets). The lifts start at 1,400m and reach 2,064m. The resort has an advanced snowmaking system with over 300 cannons covering all runs.
linkedResortsDescription: Campo Felice is part of the Tre Nevi ski area, which also includes Ovindoli-Monte Magnola and Campo Imperatore. The resort allows skiing on both sides of the mountain, connecting the Altopiano delle Rocche (Rocca di Cambio, Rocca di Mezzo, Ovindoli) and Campo Felice (Lucoli Tornimparte). A combined ski pass, the 'Skipass dei Parchi', covers all three resorts.

terrainDescription: The terrain at Campo Imperatore is dominated by intermediate-level runs, with long red runs and open slopes on the plateau. The resort offers a mix of blue, red, and black runs, along with over 60 km of cross-country skiing trails and ski mountaineering routes. A snow park with half-pipe and boardercross is available for snowboarders. The slopes are set on the Campo Imperatore plateau at the foot of the Gran Sasso massif, providing dramatic views of Corno Grande.
offPisteDescription: Off-piste skiing is a major draw at Campo Imperatore, with the surrounding mountain offering huge vertical descents and freeride opportunities. When conditions are good, there are several off-piste runs of about 10 km in length. The terrain is natural and wild, attracting adventurous skiers. Ski mountaineering routes are also available.
valueDescription: Campo Imperatore offers good value for money, with day passes that are relatively low-cost compared to Alpine resorts. There are various ski pass options including daily, multi-day, and seasonal passes, as well as the 'Parks Ski Pass' that covers multiple resorts in the area. The resort is known for its excellent quality-price ratio.
familyDescription: Campo Imperatore has a beginners' nursery slope and ski and snowboard schools, but the resort is generally better suited to intermediate skiers due to its limited beginner terrain and natural layout. Families with confident intermediate skiers may enjoy the quiet atmosphere and scenic setting.
apresSkiDescription: Apres-ski at Campo Imperatore is limited, with a low rating from visitors. There are a few mountain refuges and the hotel/hostel offering refreshments, but the scene is quiet and focused on the mountain experience rather than nightlife.
resortCharacterDescription: Campo Imperatore is a natural high-altitude plateau within Gran Sasso National Park, not a purpose-built resort village. The ski area is accessed via a long cable car from Fonte Cerreto. At the top, there is a historic hotel (where Mussolini was imprisoned) and a hostel, but no traditional alpine village. The setting is wild and rugged, with dramatic views of Corno Grande and the Apennines.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of 4 lifts: a long cable car from Fonte Cerreto to the plateau (one of the longest in Europe), one chairlift, and two surface lifts. The lifts are described as ultra-modern and well-equipped. The cable car also operates in summer for hiking access.
linkedResortsDescription: Campo Imperatore is part of the Tre Nevi ski area, which also includes Campo Felice and Ovindoli-Monte Magnola. A single ski pass, the 'Parks Ski Pass', covers all three resorts. However, the resorts are not physically connected by lifts; they are separate areas accessible by road.

terrainDescription: The ski area offers 9 km of pistes across 12 runs, with a mix of beginner (47%), intermediate (20%), and advanced (33%) terrain. The longest run is 8 km. Specific slopes include the 3.5 km Panoramica (a red run) and the 2 km Vallone (a black run). A dedicated nursery slope is available for beginners and children. The resort is known as the top beginner terrain in Lazio.
valueDescription: A daily lift pass costs €20 on weekdays and €30 on weekends, with reduced rates for children up to 14 years at €15, and free for children under 5 when accompanied by an adult with a pass. Afternoon passes (from 12:30) are available for €20. Special rates exist for groups and schools, and multi-day passes are offered. Accommodation is available in nearby Filettino, with options such as self-catering apartments at Le Conifere Garden Residence and hotels like Hotel Olimpia.
familyDescription: The resort features a dedicated nursery slope (campo scuola) for beginners and children. 47% of runs are beginner-friendly (blue runs), and there are activities such as sledding, snow tubing, snowscoot, and a snow park. The ski school offers lessons for children. The atmosphere is calm and family-oriented, with a focus on a relaxed mountain experience.
apresSkiDescription: Apres-ski options are limited to a few mountain restaurants and rifugi, such as Ristorante Genziana (known for funghi and carni alla brace), La Perla dei Simbruini (traditional ciociara cuisine), and Lo Chalet (on the slopes, serving zuppe, polenta, and salsicce). There is no dedicated nightlife scene; the resort is quiet in the evenings.
resortCharacterDescription: Campo Staffi is a small ski resort located in the Monti Simbruini Natural Park, in the province of Frosinone, at an altitude of 1,692m. The resort consists of a ski area with a few facilities, and most accommodation is in the nearby town of Filettino, 15 km away. The setting is natural and forested, with a focus on outdoor activities and a relaxed, authentic mountain atmosphere.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of 4 lifts: 2 double chairlifts and 2 surface lifts. The resort claims a capacity of 8,000 skiers per hour, and queues are generally short except on peak weekends. The lifts serve a vertical drop of 500m from 1,700m to 2,000m.
linkedResortsDescription: Campo Staffi is not part of a larger lift-linked ski area. However, a 20 km cross-country ski circuit connects Campo Staffi to Monte Livata. Nearby small ski areas include Cappadocia - Campo Rotondo and Monte Livata, but these are separate resorts.

terrainDescription: The resort offers 11 km of slopes, including a 6 km intermediate run that is one of the longest in the Apennines. The terrain is served by two ski lifts (Vallonina–Cima Organo and Selletta) and includes off-trail skiing through beech woods. Slopes are well-groomed and maintained, with a new covered magic carpet and snowcat added recently.
offPisteDescription: Off-trail skiing through beech woods is possible, providing tree skiing opportunities in the forested slopes around the Vallonina and Selletta lifts.
valueDescription: Campo Stella is one of the cheapest ski resorts in Italy, with adult day passes around €15–20 (2015 prices). The resort offers excellent value for money, with well-maintained slopes and low prices.
familyDescription: The resort has a high proportion of beginner terrain (64% of slopes) and a new covered magic carpet for the beginner area. The ski school has experienced instructors. Cross-country skiing is available on two circuits (5 km Vallonina and 3 km Fonte Nova). The slopes are close to the town of Leonessa, which offers medieval and Renaissance sites for off-slope activities.
apresSkiDescription: Apres-ski is low-key, with a few local restaurants and bars in the nearby town of Leonessa, known for its medieval and Renaissance character. The town offers local cuisine featuring potatoes and meat.
resortCharacterDescription: The resort is a small ski area located near the historic town of Leonessa, which features medieval and Renaissance architecture. The slopes are a short drive from the town centre, and the area is known for its scenic mountain views with rocky massifs reminiscent of the Dolomites.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of two modern ski lifts: a two-seat chairlift (Rubbio) and another lift serving the Vallonina–Cima Organo and Selletta areas. The lifts are recently enhanced.

terrainDescription: The terrain is characterized by steep mountains and rugged peaks, with runs following the natural contours. The resort sits in the shadow of Pico del Aspe (2645m) and offers panoramic views of the Aragon valleys, the Rioseta and Tortiellas cirque, the Aspe Valley in France, and numerous peaks over 3000m such as Balaitus and Picos del Infierno. The slopes are considered challenging, with a reputation as one of Spain's toughest resorts, but there is also a large beginner area at the base.
valueDescription: The lift pass includes access to the neighboring Astún resort, effectively doubling the skiable terrain for the same ticket price. The combined area, known as Territorio 100K, offers 101 km of marked slopes (102 runs), making it one of the largest ski areas in Spain for the price of a single-resort pass. Specific lift pass prices are not mentioned in the source text.
familyDescription: The resort has a dedicated children's area called Candanchulandia with indoor and outdoor activities, and a sledging area with a conveyor belt for easy access. Ski schools offer group and private lessons, and over 80,000 schoolchildren visit annually. Accommodation is close to the slopes, and there is a large beginner area at the base. The resort is considered family-friendly with services tailored to families.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski at Candanchú centers on Barambán, a bar at the foot of the slopes that offers a lively atmosphere for families and friends. For a wider selection, the historic town of Jaca, 15 minutes away, provides shopping, tapas bars, and cultural attractions. The resort also has several mountain restaurants and a bar at the summit.
resortCharacterDescription: Candanchú is a historic village resort, founded in 1928 as the oldest ski resort in Spain. The village contains the remains of the Old Monastery Hospital of Santa Cristina de Somport at its eastern end. The resort is set in an alpine landscape with rocky peaks and is located near the French border, 27 km north of Jaca.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of 21 surface lifts and 6 chairlifts, but visitor reviews describe it as antiquated and slow, with some recommending the neighboring Astún for better lift infrastructure. There are plans for a new gondola connecting to Astún, and a new chairlift (Balsas) is being installed to strengthen that connection.
linkedResortsDescription: Candanchú is linked to the neighboring Astún resort via a free shuttle service and a combined ski pass, forming the Territorio 100K with 101 km of marked slopes (102 runs). A gondola connection is planned, and a new chairlift (Balsas) is being installed to strengthen the link. Future plans include connecting to Formigal-Panticosa to create over 200 km of skiable terrain.

terrainDescription: The terrain is dominated by wide, sunny runs with a mix of easy and intermediate slopes, and a few steep black runs like König Laurin (65% gradient) and Pra di Tori. The three main areas—Laurin, Paolina, and Pra di Tori—connect seamlessly, allowing efficient exploration. The resort also has Italy's first children's snow park and family funlines, making it suitable for all levels.
valueDescription: Carezza offers good value compared to larger Dolomites resorts. A one-day adult lift pass costs approximately €72-€73, with online discounts available. The resort is less crowded, meaning no lift queues and uncrowded slopes. Accommodation includes ski-in/ski-out hotels and budget options in nearby villages. Multi-day passes can be combined with Val di Fassa areas for added variety.
familyDescription: Carezza is highly family-friendly, with Italy's first children's snow park, two Family Fun Parks, and themed ski trails like the König Laurin children's ski tour. The resort offers ski schools and professional childcare in nearby villages. The sunny, gentle slopes are ideal for beginners, and the compact layout means families can easily meet at common destinations. Night skiing and sledding add evening entertainment.
apresSkiDescription: Carezza has no dedicated apres-ski or nightlife. The resort is quiet in the evenings, with dining options limited to mountain huts and hotel restaurants. For a livelier scene, visitors must travel to other villages in Val di Fassa.
resortCharacterDescription: Carezza is a purpose-built ski resort centered around the Costalunga Pass, with ski-in/ski-out hotels and lift stations. There is no traditional village center; the main base is at the Paolina valley station with parking, ski school, and rental shops. The nearest villages are Nova Levante (Welschnofen) and Tires, which offer more traditional charm.
liftSystemDescription: Carezza has 13 modern lifts, including gondolas and chairlifts. A standout is the cabrio cable car from Tires (San Cipriano) to Fromm Alm, nearly 4 km long, climbing 644 vertical meters in 7 minutes, with an open-air balcony and an underground base station. The three main areas connect seamlessly, and queues are rare. The lift system is efficient and well-maintained.
linkedResortsDescription: Carezza is not directly lift-connected to other resorts, but a ski bus links it to Val di Fassa areas (Buffaure, Ciampac, Catinaccio, Belvedere, Col Rodella). A multi-day Carezza-Val di Fassa combined pass provides access to these partner areas. Additionally, the Dolomiti Superski pass covers 1,200 km of slopes across 12 regions, including Carezza, though most require road transport.

terrainDescription: The ski area extends from 1,650m to 2,280m on a south-facing slope above the Leventina Valley. The terrain is compact and easy to navigate, with gentle slopes and open terrain that suit beginners and intermediates. The resort features FIS-authorised slopes for giant slalom, downhill, and Super G, indicating steeper sections for advanced skiers. Runs are classified as blue, red, and black, with a mix of open and tree-lined sections. The south-facing exposure provides good light conditions throughout the winter.
valueDescription: Lift passes are reasonably priced, with an adult day pass at 43 CHF and a family day pass (2 adults + children under 11) at 95 CHF. A six-day adult pass costs 190 CHF. The resort is known as an affordable option for families in Ticino, though parking can be challenging on peak days, requiring early arrival or alternative transport.
familyDescription: The resort is designed for families, with a dedicated Kid's Village featuring a magic carpet at both the base and mid-station. The ski school offers structured lessons for different age groups, focusing on young skiers in a safe environment. Additional winter activities include a snow park, a 3 km toboggan run, and snowshoeing routes. The Osteria Belvedere mountain restaurant has a small playground, and the compact layout means short distances between lifts, slopes, and rest areas.
resortCharacterDescription: Carì is a traditional alpine village situated on a sunny terrace at 1,642m, the highest permanent settlement in the canton of Ticino. It has developed into a tourist resort since 1950 but retains a strong connection to local mountain life, with an authentic and unhurried atmosphere. The village is compact and car-friendly, with a free car park at the base of the chairlift. The resort is inhabited year-round, offering a genuine alpine experience.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of 4 lifts: a quad chairlift, a double chairlift, and two surface lifts. The double chairlift at the base can experience long queues on busy days, while the quad chair to the top serves the upper mountain. There is also a magic carpet in the Kid's Village. Lifts operate from 9:00 to 16:00.

terrainDescription: The terrain at Cerkno is all downhill from the summit plateau at 1,291m, with runs fanning out down the mountain. The resort has 18km of groomed pistes, mostly blue and red, with one black run. The longest run is Počivalo, a 1.9km scenic tree-lined blue run. Other notable runs include Brdo (1.8km) and Davča-Lom. The slopes are a mix of open and tree-lined, with a cross-country circuit on the plateau.
offPisteDescription: Off-piste skiing is minimal at Cerkno. The resort's runs are all groomed, and there are no marked off-piste routes. Private ski instructors can take skiers away from the pistes for off-piste experiences, but the area is not known for extensive backcountry access.
familyDescription: Cerkno is a family-friendly resort with a highly-rated ski school that teaches children and adults. There is a dedicated children's snow playground with a magic carpet and a ski kindergarten near the summit. The resort is popular for school ski camps and family groups. The slopes are mostly gentle, making it suitable for learning.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski is low-key. The main options are the Apres Ski Bar Počivalo at the base, which serves traditional local dishes and drinks, and the Alpska Perla restaurant at the summit, which offers a mix of local and international cuisine. There is no significant nightlife scene; the resort is quiet in the evenings.
resortCharacterDescription: Cerkno is a modern, mid-sized ski resort opened in 1984, located on the slopes of Črni vrh. It is known for its modern infrastructure, including covered chairlifts, and has a relaxed, family-oriented atmosphere. The resort is not a purpose-built village but rather a ski centre with a hotel and restaurant at the top station. The surrounding area is scenic, with views of the Julian Alps.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system at Cerkno includes modern covered chairlifts, notably the Lom six-seater and Brdo four-seater, which are the only covered chairlifts in Slovenia. The resort has a total of 7-8 lifts, including two six-seater chairlifts, two four-seater chairlifts, two double chairlifts, and two surface lifts. The lifts are efficient and reduce waiting times.

terrainDescription: The terrain is dominated by gentle beginner and intermediate slopes, with a few steeper red and black runs. The resort is set in a forested bowl, with runs winding through larch trees. The south-facing aspect ensures good sunshine. Night skiing is a highlight, with a 3 km floodlit run offering a 400-metre vertical drop.
valueDescription: Lift passes are very affordable, with a one-day adult pass at €25 and a six-day pass at €130 in high season. Accommodation is also budget-friendly, with a studio for two people costing around €320 per week and a four-person apartment around €500. Ski equipment rental is €90 per week for adults and €40 for children. Overall, the resort offers one of the lowest total stay costs in the region, making it an excellent choice for budget-conscious families.
familyDescription: The resort is well-suited for families, with a dedicated beginner area and a Club Piou Piou snow garden with a rope tow for children aged 3 and above. Childcare is available from 3 months old, and the ESF ski school offers lessons. The village is quiet and safe, with ski-in/ski-out access from some accommodations. The slopes are mostly gentle, and the resort's small scale means no long queues.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski is low-key, with 8 restaurants and 6 bars/cafes, plus a nightclub. The atmosphere is relaxed and family-oriented, with no rowdy party scene. Dining options include local restaurants like Les Alisiers and La Gaudina, serving Savoyard and regional dishes.
resortCharacterDescription: Chabanon-Selonnet is a traditional mountain village with a population of around 450, set in the Blanche Valley. The village has a historic core with an old church and castle tower, and the ski area was developed in 1970. The resort is car-free in parts, with a compact layout. The atmosphere is quiet and authentic, with larch forests and a river running through.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of 12 lifts, including one double chairlift and 11 surface lifts (drag lifts). There are no gondolas or detachable chairlifts. The lifts are adequate for the small ski area, and queues are rare except during peak periods. The resort has modern snowmaking covering 30 hectares.
linkedResortsDescription: Chabanon is a standalone ski area with no direct lift connection to other resorts. The nearest ski areas are Saint Jean Montclar (8.3 km), Le Grand Puy (8 km), and others, but they are not linked by lifts.

terrainDescription: The ski area consists of 13 runs totalling 15 km, with no black runs. The terrain is predominantly south-facing, so slopes become soft by mid to late morning. The Clot Chenu chairlift accesses the summit at 2000m. 80% of pistes are covered by snowmaking. The runs are evenly split between green (3), blue (5), and red (5), making it suitable for beginners and intermediates.
valueDescription: The Champsaur 3 Gliss ski pass covers Chaillol, Saint-Léger-les-Mélèzes, and Laye, allowing access to three linked ski areas. The resort is marketed as having attractive pricing, though specific lift pass prices are not provided in the source.
familyDescription: The resort has a dedicated snow front for children and beginners, a Piou Piou club for the youngest, and an ESF ski school offering private and group lessons. There is a toboggan run and a snow garden. The ski area has no black runs, and the three linked resorts are connected by a free ski bus, making it easy for families to explore.
resortCharacterDescription: The resort is centred on the village of Saint-Michel-de-Chaillol, a traditional alpine village at 1220m altitude, with the ski slopes starting just above the village at 1600m. The resort is described as a family village station with human scale and alpine charm, featuring ancient sculptures on doors, churches, chapels, and fountains in the hamlets.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of 10 lifts: 2 chairlifts (including the Clot Chenu chairlift) and 8 surface lifts. 80% of pistes are covered by snowmaking. No information on lift age or queue times is provided.
linkedResortsDescription: Chaillol is part of the Champsaur 3 Gliss ski pass, which also covers the nearby resorts of Saint-Léger-les-Mélèzes and Laye. A free ski bus connects the three ski areas, allowing skiers to explore all three on one pass.

terrainDescription: The ski area extends from 1,109 to 1,600 meters on the eastern side of Pierre-sur-Haute, alternating between forest and wild highland moors (les hautes Chaumes). The resort has developed its ski area mainly in the forest, which provides natural protection against the weather. The 12 km of downhill runs include a 2,800-meter Granges piste, trails at the foot of Pierre-sur-Haute, and a more technical Couzan sector. The slopes are exposed to the northwest and southwest. The sequence of trails from Gentianes, Replat, Bruyères, and Sangliers forms a route from the top to the snow front. The longest run is 2 km. The ski area is modest in size due to the recent classification of certain forested regions, and the Conseil général de la Loire focuses on quality with sodding, snow barriers, and 90 snow guns.
valueDescription: A six-day adult lift pass costs €105, and a one-day pass is €21 in high season. Ski equipment rental for a week is €90 for adults and €40 for children. A studio for two people rents for €320 per week during school holidays, and a family apartment for four costs €500. The total cost of a stay for two adults (excluding transport and food) is €710, and for two adults and two children it is €1,086. Average property prices are €1,350 per m² for apartments and €1,100 per m² for chalets. These prices are significantly lower than in large Alpine resorts.
familyDescription: The resort has a dedicated beginners' area with two slopes (blue and green) and a safe tobogganing area for the whole family. The ESF ski school offers lessons at the foot of the slopes. The discovery area provides safety conditions for a first skiing experience. The resort is described as a family-friendly mid-range mountain resort, and visitor reviews consistently call it a great family resort. Summer activities include an accrobranche park, mountain biking, and hiking via the chairlift.
resortCharacterDescription: Chalmazel is the only downhill ski resort in the Monts du Forez, located in the Massif Central. The resort is situated at 1,120 meters in a place called Les Bois, 4 km from the village of Chalmazel and linked by a 6 km departmental road. It was created in 1953 and developed in the 1960s with support from the Conseil Général. The snow front includes reception, rental stores, and apartments. The resort mainly attracts daily visitors from the Forez plain, Roanne, and Saint-Étienne, all within 80 km. The medieval Château de Chalmazel, built from 1231, is in the village and offers guest rooms and reception rooms. The setting is a mix of forest and wild highland moors, with a Natura 2000 site restricting expansion.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of 1 detachable bubble chairlift (the Jasseries, installed in 2002 replacing a 1967 gondola) and 7 drag lifts. The Jasseries chairlift is equipped with a liquid shock absorber system to limit wind effects. The Couzan ski lift was modernized in 2003, the Granges lift in 2006, and the Cimes lift was replaced in 2008 with a longer layout. The Pierre-sur-Haute gondola, built in 1967, was one of the first 'egg' lines by Poma. The lift park was developed alongside piste layout and snow front construction. The resort has 8 lifts total.

terrainDescription: The ski area consists of three old double chairlifts and a magic carpet serving around 10 km of groomed trails. All runs are blue and red (intermediate) with no black pistes; the reds are gentle progression runs suitable for intermediates building confidence. The terrain is south-facing and sunny, with well-groomed slopes each night. Off-piste opportunities exist in low-angle alpine meadows and tree skiing areas visible from the chairlifts.
offPisteDescription: Off-piste terrain consists of low-angle alpine meadows and open tree skiing areas accessible from the chairlifts. The terrain is not extreme, suitable for intermediates exploring beyond groomed runs. Backcountry ski touring is popular, with routes connecting to Valtournenche.
valueDescription: A day lift pass costs €25–30, with weekday passes up to €6 cheaper than weekends. Multi-day passes from Cervinia or the Aosta Valley Magic Ski Pass (€100 for 6 days at 11 small areas) are valid at Chamois. Accommodation in the village is limited but affordable, and the campsite at Buisson costs €25 per night. Dining and drinks are reasonably priced, especially at local bars like Bar Chamois.
familyDescription: Chamois is car-free and very quiet, making it safe for children. The ski area has a magic carpet and gentle blue runs near the base, with progression reds above. The compact village means everything is walkable. Ski schools are available, and the uncrowded slopes allow beginners to learn without pressure. The cable car access from Buisson is straightforward.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski is low-key and local. Bar Chamois at the base of the slopes is a popular spot for affordable drinks and snacks, often frequented by locals. The village has a few restaurants serving traditional Valdostan dishes like polenta and fondue. The atmosphere is relaxed, with no loud nightlife.
resortCharacterDescription: Chamois is a traditional, car-free alpine village accessible only by cable car from Buisson. It sits on a cliff above the Aosta Valley with stunning views of the Matterhorn. The village has cobblestone alleys, a historic clock tower, and a handful of hotels and restaurants. There are no roads, giving it a peaceful, timeless atmosphere.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of three old double chairlifts (Chairs A, B, and C) that run consecutively from the village to the summit, plus a magic carpet for beginners. The chairs are slow and dated but reliable. Access to the resort is via a cable car from Buisson in the valley, which is not included in the ski pass.
linkedResortsDescription: Chamois is part of the Aosta Valley ski pass system. Multi-day passes from Cervinia/Valtournenche are valid at Chamois, and the Aosta Valley Magic Ski Pass allows skiing at 11 small resorts including Chamois. The resort is not physically connected by lifts to other areas, but the pass provides access to nearby Torgnon and other valleys.

terrainDescription: The ski area at Champex-Lac covers 15 km of pistes, with 55% beginner (blue), 32% intermediate (red), and 13% advanced (black) runs. The terrain is dominated by wide, gentle slopes around the frozen lake and forested areas, with the summit at La Breya (2,200 m) offering panoramic views. The longest descent is 7 km. Slopes are uncrowded and easy to navigate, making it ideal for relaxed skiing.
offPisteDescription: The surrounding Saint Bernard region, which includes Champex-Lac, is popular with freeriders and ski tourers due to its natural terrain and quiet backcountry feel. The area offers opportunities for off-piste skiing in the surrounding forests and slopes, though specific marked off-piste routes at Champex-Lac are not detailed in the source text.
familyDescription: Champex-Lac is well-suited for families, with quiet, gentle slopes that are easy for beginners and children. Ski schools are available in the village, and the uncrowded environment allows for stress-free learning. The village is compact and walkable, with a frozen lake adding to the scenic appeal. The resort is described as family-friendly and ideal for relaxed mountain days.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski at Champex-Lac is relaxed and low-key. Popular spots include Bar Les Châtaigniers and Bar du Lac, a cozy lakeside bar with scenic views. Dining options include Restaurant Le Mistral. The nightlife is laid-back, focusing on local flavors and quiet evenings rather than loud parties.
resortCharacterDescription: Champex-Lac is a traditional Swiss village situated on the shore of Lac de Champex, at the foot of the Mont Blanc Massif. The resort is known for its picturesque setting with a frozen lake in winter and forested surroundings. It offers a peaceful, uncrowded atmosphere, contrasting with larger, busier resorts. The village is compact and walkable, with an authentic alpine charm.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system at Champex-Lac consists of 4 lifts: 2 double chairlifts and 2 surface lifts, with a total uplift capacity of 2,000 skiers per hour. Snow-making is available on 1 km of pistes using 2 snow cannons. The lifts are modest but sufficient for the small ski area, and queues are rare due to low visitor numbers.
linkedResortsDescription: Champex-Lac is part of the Espace St-Bernard ski area, which includes La Fouly and Vichères–Liddes. A combined pass provides access to all three areas, with a ski bus connecting the villages. The total ski area across the network is 113 km of pistes.

terrainDescription: The terrain consists of 15 km of groomed pistes served by 7 lifts, with a vertical drop of 1,067 m from 2,494 m to 1,427 m. The slopes are predominantly intermediate (59%) with wide blue and red runs, plus some technical black runs at the top. Beginners have a dedicated area at the Laris sector with gentle slopes and a Baby Snow Park. The longest run is 3 km. The slopes are well-oriented, holding dry, firm snow that lasts well into spring.
valueDescription: Champorcher is one of the cheapest ski areas in the Aosta Valley, making it a budget-friendly option for families. Lift passes are affordable, and accommodation includes family-run hotels and self-catering apartments. The resort is known for being economical compared to larger, more commercial resorts.
familyDescription: The resort features a Baby Snow Park for children, wide gentle slopes in the lower areas ideal for beginners, and a ski school with English-speaking instructors. The Laris sector offers quiet and safe learning areas. The village is intimate and peaceful, suitable for families seeking a relaxed atmosphere.
apresSkiDescription: The apres-ski scene is low-key, centered on mountain huts and village restaurants serving Aosta Valley specialties such as polenta concia, onion soup, and chestnut gnocchi. The atmosphere is warm and familiar, with no dedicated nightlife. Restaurants like Ristorante Lou Talapen and Le Bon Plaisir offer authentic regional cuisine.
resortCharacterDescription: Champorcher is a traditional alpine village with centuries-old history, set in a wild natural landscape at the eastern end of the Aosta Valley. It offers views of Monte Rosa and is surrounded by the Mont Avic Natural Park. Accommodation includes family-run hotels and self-contained flats, giving it an authentic, unpretentious character.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system comprises 7 lifts including a gondola, a high-speed quad chair, a double chair, and surface lifts. The lifts are partly modernized, with recent investment in upgrades. The Laris Express chairlift provides quick access to the upper sectors. The system is efficient for the size of the area, with minimal queues.
linkedResortsDescription: Champorcher is a satellite resort of the Monterosa Ski area, sharing the same ski pass but not physically connected by lifts to the main valleys of Champoluc, Gressoney, and Alagna. It offers a separate, self-contained ski area in its own valley, with 15 km of slopes. The Monterosa Ski area overall includes 180 km of pistes across three valleys, known for uncrowded slopes and world-class off-piste terrain.

terrainDescription: The Champéry – Les Crosets – Champoussin – Morgins area offers 82 km of marked pistes within the Swiss sector of the Portes du Soleil, with 71% of runs classified as beginner, 21% intermediate, and 7% advanced. The terrain is dominated by wide, groomed slopes suitable for beginners and intermediates, particularly around Champéry's Grand Paradis run, a long descent under the Dents Blanche. The Swiss Wall (La Chavanette) provides a steep mogul field with a 76% gradient, accessible from Les Crosets and Champéry. The area connects to the broader Portes du Soleil domain, which includes over 600 km of pistes across 12 resorts.
offPisteDescription: Off-piste opportunities exist primarily above the Ripaille/Grand Paradis run, where alpine terrain drops into forested slopes. The area between Les Crosets and Champéry holds freeride lines that reward exploration after fresh snowfall. The wider Portes du Soleil domain offers additional off-piste terrain around the Hauts Forts sector above Avoriaz and the bowls near Mont Chéry, but these require a full area pass and are not directly within the Swiss sector. Ski touring is available at the Rando-Parc in nearby Morgins, with trails of varying difficulty taking 90 minutes to 3 hours.
valueDescription: A six-day adult Portes du Soleil lift pass costs approximately €310 (2025/26 season), covering all 196 lifts across the Franco-Swiss domain. A local Swiss sector pass covering Champéry, Les Crosets, Champoussin, and Morgins costs CHF 70 per day (approximately €65) for adults, with children's passes at CHF 43 per day. Accommodation in Champéry ranges from traditional hotels like Hotel Suisse to self-catered apartments, with prices generally higher than on the French side due to Swiss franc pricing. The resort offers free ski passes for children under 5.
familyDescription: Champéry provides a family-friendly environment with a pedestrianized village centre, a Disney-style train shuttle to the cable car, and the Palladium sports centre featuring two ice rinks, swimming pools, and a climbing wall. Ski passes are free for children under 5. Les Crosets offers dedicated nursery slopes and ski-in/ski-out convenience, while the wider Swiss sector includes gentle blue runs ideal for progressing beginners. The area is quieter than French resorts like Morzine, reducing crowding on beginner slopes. The Swiss Ski and Snowboard School in Champéry provides lessons for all ages.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski in Champéry is relaxed, with most bars closing around 10pm midweek. Key venues include Le Bar des Guides, a pub-like bar popular with locals, and R.E.D., a dance club with a large terrace hosting DJs. Mitchell's Sports Bar and At Home on the main street offer post-ski drinks. The Hotel Suisse bar is known for its lively atmosphere. The wider Swiss sector has about 30 bars and cafes, but the scene is quieter than French resorts like Morzine. Evening activities include torchlight descents and fireworks displays during special events.
resortCharacterDescription: Champéry is a traditional Swiss village over 150 years old, with wooden chalets lining a pedestrianized main street. Les Crosets is a small, car-free micro-resort on a sunny plateau at 1,670m, consisting of a few chalets, hotels, and restaurants. Champoussin and Morgins are similarly quiet, traditional villages. The area retains authentic Alpine character, unlike purpose-built resorts such as Avoriaz. The backdrop is the Dents du Midi mountain range. The village centre of Champéry has shops, restaurants, and a weekly market.
liftSystemDescription: The Swiss sector has 32 ski lifts, including the Champéry–Planachaux 125-person cable car that reaches 2,000m in under 5 minutes, and a six-seater chairlift at Grand-Paradis providing rapid access into the Portes du Soleil system. A free Disney-style train shuttles skiers around Champéry to the cable car. The lifts are modern and efficient, with recent investments in snowmaking. The full Portes du Soleil area has 196 lifts, including gondolas, chairlifts, and surface lifts.
linkedResortsDescription: The Champéry – Les Crosets – Champoussin – Morgins area is part of the Portes du Soleil, one of the world's largest linked ski areas, connecting 12 resorts across France and Switzerland with over 600 km of marked pistes and 196 lifts. The Swiss sector links directly to Avoriaz via the Pas de Chavanette, and to Châtel via Morgins. The full circuit, known as the Grand Tour, covers approximately 50 km and takes a strong intermediate a full day, passing through Avoriaz, Châtel, Morgins, Champoussin, Les Crosets, and Champéry before returning via the Swiss Wall. A single Portes du Soleil lift pass grants access to all resorts without border surcharges.

terrainDescription: The ski area is split into two interconnected sectors: Recoin (Chamrousse 1650) and Roche Béranger (Chamrousse 1750), with a third village Bachat Bouloud (Chamrousse 1700) also linked. The terrain above Chamrousse 1650 is steep and varied, featuring seven black runs and seven red runs, including the 4 km men's Olympic downhill course from the 1968 Winter Games and the Casserousse Couloir with 850 m of vertical descent. The slopes above Chamrousse 1750 are predominantly blue runs accessed by chairlifts, cutting through forest with views over Grenoble. The west- to south-westerly aspect means slopes are often icy in the morning and slushy in the afternoon, especially late season. North-facing aspects at the summit help preserve snow quality. The resort has 90 km of marked pistes between 1,400 m and 2,250 m, with 28% easy, 50% intermediate, and 22% advanced runs according to one source, and 40% beginner, 35% intermediate, 25% expert according to another.
offPisteDescription: Off-piste skiing is not world-class but offers fun features between pistes and extensive tree runs that provide shelter in poor visibility. In good snow conditions, skiers can drop off the back of the top lift towards the Lacs Robert for decent powder turns. The Belledonne massif surrounding the resort offers serious backcountry potential with accessible touring routes leading to peaks and couloirs beyond the lift system. Local guides can show lines through the surrounding terrain. The ESF runs off-piste skiing half-days. Avalanche safety gear is recommended. One visitor review notes that off-piste in the forest is fantastic.
valueDescription: Lift pass prices are among the more affordable in the French Alps: a day adult pass costs €44, a six-day adult pass €238, a day child pass €28, and a six-day child pass €159 (2026 data). Visitor reviews consistently mention very affordable lift prices compared to larger resorts. Accommodation options range from budget apartments to a few hotels, with ski-in/ski-out available at both Recoin and Roche Béranger. The resort is only 30 km from Grenoble, allowing day-tripping from the city to save on accommodation costs.
familyDescription: Chamrousse is well-suited to families with mixed abilities. Dedicated nursery areas exist at both Recoin and Roche Béranger, with gentle gradients and magic carpet lifts. The Bachat Bouloud area is a special beginner zone with a sled run for children. The ESF ski school offers children's programmes with English-speaking instructors. The three villages are connected by a free shuttle bus, making it easy to move between accommodation and ski school meeting points. Off-slope activities include sledging, dog sledding, and snowmobiling. The compact layout means ski school, hire shops, and restaurants are within walking distance of most accommodation.
apresSkiDescription: The resort has 25 bars and restaurants spread across the two main villages. L'Embuscade Pub is a noted venue, and the K-Dox pub at the foot of the slopes next to the Arselle chairlift is a popular post-ski spot. The nightlife is fairly low-key, centred on these bars and restaurants, with most serving French cuisine and a few pizzerias. Visitor reviews describe plenty of bars and restaurants for apres-ski, but the atmosphere is relaxed rather than rowdy.
resortCharacterDescription: Chamrousse consists of three purpose-built villages at different altitudes: Chamrousse 1650 (Recoin), Chamrousse 1700 (Bachat Bouloud), and Chamrousse 1750 (Roche Béranger). Many apartment blocks in Chamrousse 1750 were originally concrete but have been recently rebuilt into more sightly buildings, bringing them in line with the more traditional Savoyard-style architecture in Chamrousse 1650. The resort is unpretentious and focused on skiing, with a relaxed village atmosphere. Recoin at 1,650 m serves as the main hub with shops, restaurants, and direct lift access; Roche Béranger at 1,750 m has a quieter feel with its own amenities and slope access. The proximity to Grenoble (30 km) means easy access to city restaurants and cultural attractions.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system comprises 19 lifts (per known facts), though sources vary: one reports 26 lifts (9 chairlifts, 16 surface lifts, 1 gondola), another 16 lifts. The main access points use modern high-speed quad chairlifts and six-seaters, with a gondola connecting the lower areas. Surface lifts exist but are not unavoidable on most routes. The uplift capacity is 30,000 skiers per hour, so queues clear quickly even on busy weekends. A free shuttle bus connects the three villages. The lift network is well-designed for the Olympic heritage, with pistes flowing naturally across the mountainside.
linkedResortsDescription: The Chamrousse ski area is self-contained and not linked to any other resort. It consists of three interconnected sectors: Chamrousse 1650 (Recoin), Chamrousse 1700 (Bachat Bouloud), and Chamrousse 1750 (Roche Béranger). These are connected by ski lifts, cable cars, and ski runs, allowing skiers to move between them without leaving the piste network. The standard lift pass covers only the Chamrousse ski area.

terrainDescription: The Charmey ski area sits in the Dents Vertes cirque of the Fribourg Alps, with 30 km of pistes spread across 10 to 12 runs between 890 m and 1,630 m. The terrain is 53% intermediate, 40% advanced, and 7% expert, with a snowpark and a dedicated slalom or giant slalom training run available by reservation. Slopes face multiple directions: 24% north, 17% east, 22% south, and 37% west, giving varied conditions throughout the day. The gondola from the village rises to Vounetse, the main starting point, and the area includes forest-lined trails and open slopes with views over La Gruyère.
offPisteDescription: The resort promotes virgin slopes for freeride enthusiasts, with unspoilt terrain outside the marked pistes where skiers and snowboarders can ride according to their mood. The ski area includes an itinerary marked for freeride, and the varied slope orientations and wind protection help preserve snow quality in off-piste zones.
