The system can transport over 24,000 people per hour, minimising queues. Lifts operate daily from 09:00 to 16:30 (until 15:00 on 24 December). Dynamic pricing applies for hourly, daily, and multi-day tickets. Snowmaking covers 27 km of runs, ensuring reliable conditions.
linkedResortsDescription: The Liftverbund Feldberg connects several smaller ski areas into one network: Seebuck, Grafenmatt, Fahl, Todtnau, Muggenbrunn, Notschrei, and Belchen. A single ski pass covers all lifts in the association. The total linked area offers 28 lifts and around 50–63 km of pistes (depending on the source), with cross-country trails extending over 120 km across the high plateau. The network also includes the Herzogenhorn area and trails to Hinterzarten and Schluchsee. The Feldberg Snowpark features Germany's longest jib line.

terrainDescription: The terrain at Feldis is dominated by intermediate red runs (7.8 km) with a smaller portion of advanced black runs (2.2 km) and no designated beginner blue runs. The slopes are oriented predominantly south (40%) and west (47%), with only 9% north-facing. The vertical drop is 522 m from the base at 1480 m to the summit at 1974 m. The chairlift to Mutta provides access to a variety of slopes offering panoramic views of Piz Beverin, Tödi, and the Calanda group. The pistes are clearly laid out and family-friendly, with a mix of open and tree-lined runs.
valueDescription: A day lift pass for adults costs CHF 35–39 depending on the season, and for children CHF 21. The resort is small with limited accommodation options, but self-catering apartments and budget-friendly hotels are available in the nearby village. The ski area is compact, so visitors can save on lift pass costs compared to larger resorts. No further details on value or budget-friendliness are provided in the source text.
familyDescription: Feldis is popular with families, offering a child-friendly chairlift and clearly laid out pistes that are easy for beginners and children. The resort features three toboggan runs and a natural ice rink on Alp Raguta for skating and curling. The small, uncrowded ski area and relaxed atmosphere make it suitable for leisurely skiing with children. Mountain restaurants like the Feldis ski hut serve local specialities and hot chocolate.
resortCharacterDescription: Feldis is a small, traditional Swiss village situated on a plateau above the Hinterrhein at about 1500 m. It has retained its original charm and offers panoramic views of the surrounding Alps, including Piz Beverin, Tödi, and the Calanda group. The resort is quiet and nature-oriented, away from mass tourism, with a focus on unspoilt nature and relaxation. In summer, it is a popular starting point for hikes and mountain tours.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system comprises a cable car (Luftseilbahn) from Rhäzüns (663 m) to Feldis (1465 m), a chairlift (Sesselbahn) from Feldis to Mutta (1965 m), and two surface lifts. The cable car operates daily from 06:05 to 22:30 during the summer season, while the chairlift is seasonal. The system is small but adequate for the limited terrain, with a total of 4 lifts. No information on lift age or efficiency is provided.

terrainDescription: The Fendels ski area sits on a north-facing high plateau above the Inn Valley, with 66% of slopes oriented north. The terrain is compact and forested, offering 8.5–16 km of prepared pistes (depending on source) across 52 hectares. The layout includes gentle nursery slopes at the top gondola station, long tree-lined blue runs, and a few steeper red and black pistes. The longest run is 3 km. The vertical drop is 946–1,362 m from a summit of 2,271–2,300 m. Snowboarders benefit from natural flow through forest sections without long flat traverses.
valueDescription: A Fendels-only adult day pass costs €54, with a 6-day pass at €291; child day passes are €32 and 6-day €175. The combined Kaunertal Glacier + Fendels pass offers up to 30% discount when booked online. Ski rental for adults ranges from €80–€160 for 3 days and €130–€250 for 6 days; children’s rental is €35–€110 for 6 days, with free child rental (up to 8 years) at select shops when accompanying an adult. The Ski6 network pass (covering Serfaus-Fiss-Ladis, Nauders, Kaunertal, Fendels, and Venet) costs around €300 for 6 days. Accommodation in self-catering apartments and guesthouses keeps costs moderate.
familyDescription: Fendels is purpose-built for families, with a dedicated children’s area called BIBI-Land featuring a magic carpet, platter lift, and a parcours with figures and challenges. The ski school offers group lessons for children (€35–€70 per day) and private instruction. The nursery slopes are at the top gondola station, close to the lift, and the village is compact with short walking distances. A 4.5 km natural toboggan run starts at the Sattelklause restaurant and is open for night tobogganing on Thursdays from 17:00–23:00. There is also a floodlit night-skiing slope on selected days. The free ski bus connects to the Kaunertal Glacier for additional terrain.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski in Fendels is low-key, centred on traditional Tyrolean taverns and the Sattelklause restaurant at 1,900 m, which has a spacious sun terrace serving regional specialties. In the nearby village of Ried, Bistro Pizzeria Emi and Café Kóhle offer evening dining. The main evening activity is the 4.5 km toboggan run, floodlit on Thursday nights. The atmosphere is relaxed rather than rowdy, with no large nightclubs.
resortCharacterDescription: Fendels is a small, traditional Tyrolean village (population 259) in the Oberinntal valley, 13 km south of Landeck. Until 1959 it was only reachable by cable car; a road was built later. The village retains authentic alpine charm with wooden houses and a quiet atmosphere. The ski area is compact and uncrowded, described as a 'gentle winter tourism' destination. The village is car-free in the core, with parking at the valley station. Accommodation consists of guesthouses and self-catering apartments.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system comprises 6 lifts: a gondola from Ried im Oberinntal (valley station at 1,325 m) to the Fendels plateau, a chairlift from the plateau to Sattelboden (1,900 m), and four surface lifts serving the nursery and intermediate slopes. The gondola and chairlift are modern, with a total capacity of 24,100 persons per hour. Lifts operate daily from 9:00 to 16:00. Night skiing is available on selected Thursdays on a 4 km floodlit run. A free ski bus runs to the Kaunertal Glacier (20 minutes) for those with a combined pass.
linkedResortsDescription: Fendels is part of the Ski6 network, which includes Serfaus-Fiss-Ladis (214 km of pistes), Nauders, Kaunertal Glacier, and Venet. A combined Kaunertal Glacier + Fendels pass allows skiing in both areas, though they are not directly lift-linked; a free ski bus connects them in about 20 minutes. The Ski6 pass covers 340–350 km of total pistes across the Tyrolean Oberland. Fendels itself is a small area (11–16 km), but the combined pass gives access to extensive terrain including the high-altitude Kaunertal Glacier (up to 3,200 m) and the family-friendly Serfaus-Fiss-Ladis region.

terrainDescription: The terrain is on a sunny high plateau at 1,600 m, dominated by wide blue and red runs that are ideal for beginners and intermediates. There is a short black slope and a 6 km ungroomed ski route descending to Ebensee for advanced skiers. The slopes offer panoramic views of Lake Traunsee and the Dachstein mountains. The ski area covers 10–11 km of pistes, with 76% beginner, 17% intermediate, and 7% advanced runs.
offPisteDescription: Off-piste skiing is limited to a 6 km ungroomed descent from the summit to Ebensee, which is unsecured and requires good snow cover. There are also 4 marked ski touring routes and approximately 5 km of freeride terrain. The area is not a major off-piste destination, but the valley descent provides a challenging option for experienced skiers.
valueDescription: Lift passes are reasonably priced: a day ticket costs €59.50 for adults, with discounts for seniors and children born in 2020 or younger ski free with a parent. A 6-day pass is €312.10. Parking at the valley station is free, and a free ski bus (Traunsee Wintershuttle) is available. Accommodation options range from the on-mountain Berggasthof Edelweiss to hotels in Ebensee.
familyDescription: The resort is highly family-friendly with a dedicated children's area called Franzi's Dragonpark, featuring two conveyor belts, a rope lift, and playful features. The ski school at the mountain station offers courses for all levels. Children born in 2020 or younger ski free with a parent. The sunny plateau and gentle slopes make it a safe learning environment.
apresSkiDescription: Apres-ski is low-key, centered around four mountain huts and restaurants on the plateau serving traditional Austrian dishes like Wiener Schnitzel and Kaiserschmarrn. Occasional torch-lit descents are offered. The village of Ebensee at the base has limited nightlife. The atmosphere is relaxed and family-oriented.
resortCharacterDescription: Feuerkogel is not a purpose-built resort village but a mountain plateau accessed by cable car from Ebensee. The plateau features a traditional mountain inn (Berggasthof Edelweiss) and several huts, offering a quiet, rustic alpine experience. The base town of Ebensee is a traditional Austrian town on Lake Traunsee.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of a cable car from Ebensee to the plateau (10-minute ride, operating 8:30–17:00 daily), plus 7 additional lifts on the plateau including a high-speed six-pack chair and surface lifts. Total lift capacity is 8,720 persons per hour. Snowmaking covers the slopes. The lifts are modern and queues are rare.
linkedResortsDescription: Feuerkogel is part of the Kartenverbund Dachstein West, a multi-day ski pass that grants access to over 160 km of slopes across several ski areas in the Salzkammergut region, including Dachstein West, Dachstein Krippenstein, Karkogel, Sonnleitenlifte Abtenau, Skilift Lammertal Lungötz, St. Martin am Tennengebirge, and Zinkenlifte Bad Dürrnberg. This allows skiers to explore a wider variety of terrain beyond Feuerkogel's own 11 km.

terrainDescription: The ski area spans two mountains, Rossbrand at 1,600m and Grossberg at 1,380m, with additional slopes in the Neuberg district. Of the 20km of groomed pistes, 50% are intermediate, 43% advanced, and 7% expert, with the longest run measuring 3km. The terrain is dominated by wide, gentle runs suitable for beginners and families, but also includes a black run and the Michaela Kirchgasser FIS piste for advanced skiers. A fun slope and a small snowpark add variety.
valueDescription: A 1-day adult lift pass costs €70 in high season and €65.50 in pre/post season. Multi-day passes for 2 days cost €133.50 and for 3 days €200. Family day tickets are available from €98 for one adult and one child. From 2-day tickets onward, passes are valid across the entire Ski amadé area, adding value for those wanting to explore nearby resorts. Special offers include Best Ager Week, Mini's Week, Ladies Week, and Easter Family Action, which can reduce costs further.
familyDescription: The resort is very beginner-friendly with three ski schools offering magic carpets and practice lifts. The Bögrain platter lift provides a gentle practice slope, and blue runs on Großberg and Neuberg are ideal for learners. The slopes are uncrowded. Special family offers include a Mini's Week package for toddlers (born 2020 or later) at €189 for a 6-day ski pass, rental, and course, and a Young Family Card allowing parents to share a single ticket. The Family Bonus gives free passes to third and subsequent children when parents buy passes. A separate nursery area at the Rettenegg lift is also available.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski options include the Kalahari Bar with live music and DJs, the 'I know you' dance bar, and the Happy Alm offering live après-ski from 4pm with fun shows and karaoke. The Alm Stadl is a rustic alpine hut near the Papageno lift. The overall atmosphere is cozy and intimate, with mulled wine and traditional pastries in village pubs.
resortCharacterDescription: Filzmoos is a traditional Austrian mountain village nestled below the Gosaukamm range, with the Bischofsmütze and Dachstein massifs as a backdrop. The village has a historic church, traditional wooden chalets, and a car-free center. It is not a purpose-built resort but a working town with year-round tourism, offering a picturesque alpine setting. The village is compact and walkable, with most accommodation within a few minutes of the lifts.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system comprises 8 lifts: a 6-seater gondola (Papageno), a 6-seater high-speed chairlift (sixpack Mooslehen), a 4-seater chairlift (Grossberg), and 5 drag lifts (Bögrain, Rettenegg, Geierberg, Schwaigalm, Halsegg). Lifts operate from 8:00 AM to 4:00 PM (some from 8:30 or 9:00). The system is modern but small, with minimal queues. The drag lifts in Neuberg require good lift-riding skills.
linkedResortsDescription: Filzmoos is part of the Ski amadé network, which includes over 760km of pistes across 25 resorts. From 2-day lift passes onward, tickets are valid across the entire Ski amadé area, giving access to nearby resorts such as Flachau, Schladming, and Radstadt. A ski bus connects Filzmoos to other resorts in the region.

terrainDescription: Flachau's terrain focuses on wide cruising runs through forests and open bowls above the treeline. The south-east facing slopes mean conditions soften early in the afternoon. Higher north-facing runs hold snow well, while lower slopes benefit from daily grooming and extensive snowmaking coverage.
offPisteDescription: Tree skiing through the forests below Grießenkareck offers gentle powder runs after snowfall, suitable for those new to off-piste. Serious off-piste enthusiasts will find the area limited, though guided tours can unlock more adventurous lines. The wider Ski Amadé area provides access to more serious freeride terrain for day trips.
familyDescription: Dedicated nursery areas sit right in the village, served by easy-access gondolas that keep beginners away from faster traffic. The ski school offers English-speaking group lessons for children from age two, with ski play lessons and children's ski courses from age four. There are eleven ski schools in the area, and facilities include snow kindergartens for the youngest visitors.
apresSkiDescription: The resort has a relaxed après-ski scene with traditional restaurants serving hearty regional specialities. At the valley stations, bars like 'Dampfkessel' and 'Hofstadl' offer food and drinks, while 'Downtown' and 'Ema's Pub' provide dancing. The après-ski options are all within accessible distance of the centre of Flachau.
resortCharacterDescription: Flachau is a valley village with traditional character, featuring church spires and wooden chalets. It sits at the heart of the Salzburger Sportwelt in the interconnected Ski Amadé area, retaining an authentic village atmosphere rather than a purpose-built resort feel.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of gondolas and high-speed chairlifts, with most chairlifts having weather protection bonnets, heated seats, and automatically closing safety bars for children. The G-Link cable car provides a fast connection between Flachau-Wagrain/Grießenkareck and Wagrain/Grafenberg-Alpendorf. Users rate lift efficiency highly, with minimal queuing time.
linkedResortsDescription: Flachau is part of the Snow Space Salzburg ski area, directly connected to Wagrain and St. Johann-Alpendorf, covering 210km of pistes. Through the Panorama Link gondola lift, it connects to the Shuttleberg Flachauwinkl-Kleinarl and Zauchensee-Flachauwinkl ski areas, totaling 210km of pistes. The wider Ski Amadé area unlocks 760km of pistes across multiple interconnected resorts.

terrainDescription: The terrain at Flumserberg is dominated by intermediate red runs, with a mix of north-facing slopes that help preserve snow quality. The main skiing area ranges from 1,200m to 2,222m, with the highest point at Leist. Runs from Maschgenkamm (2,020m) and Prodkamm feature steep moguled sections at the top that open into wider zig-zag runs. A 6km ungroomed ski route from Seebenalp to Oberterzen allows skiing down to 665m, offering a vertical drop of 1,557m. The lower area has gentle blue runs for beginners, while experts head towards Leist peak. The resort offers a panorama of Lake Walen and the Churfirsten mountain range.
valueDescription: Lift passes at Flumserberg are relatively affordable for Switzerland, with a day pass costing CHF70-74 (€75-79) depending on the day. A six-day pass is CHF316. Accommodation and food are cheaper than in more famous Swiss resorts, though still expensive by international standards. The resort is popular with day-trippers from Zurich, which keeps prices competitive. Self-catering apartments and hotels in the villages offer budget options.
familyDescription: Flumserberg is well-suited for families, with two dedicated children's areas at Prodalp and Tannenboden featuring practice lifts and magic carpets. The HäxäWäldli adventure ski run offers a forested trail for kids. Ski school is available with English-speaking instructors, costing CHF45 for a half-day and CHF90 for a full day. Children under 5 ski free. The free practice area at Tannenboden allows beginners to learn without a lift pass. The resort is compact and walkable, and the SeeJet gondola provides easy access from the train station.
apresSkiDescription: Apres-ski is very limited, with only a few hotel bars and restaurants in Tannenboden. There is no dedicated nightlife scene. The main evening activity is the Friday Nightride event, which includes night skiing on the 2km Chrüz slope and a party at Bergrestaurant Chrüz. Most visitors are day-trippers or families who leave the mountain early.
resortCharacterDescription: Flumserberg is a collection of small alpine villages (Tannenboden, Tannenheim, Flumserberg) situated on a terrace above Lake Walensee. The resort has a genuine local character, popular with Swiss families and day-trippers from Zurich. The architecture is traditional Swiss chalet style, not purpose-built concrete blocks. The villages are compact and offer stunning views of the lake and the Churfirsten mountains. However, the off-mountain experience is limited, with few shops and amenities.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of 17 lifts, including 4 gondolas (the SeeJet 8-seater from Unterterzen and the BergJet scenic gondola), 8 chairlifts (including high-speed six-packs), and 5 surface lifts. The lifts are modern and well-maintained, with a total capacity of 25,000 persons per hour. The SeeJet gondola provides direct access from the train station at Unterterzen to the slopes in about 15 minutes. Night skiing is available on the Chrüz slope on Fridays.

terrainDescription: The Folgaria ski area offers 18 marked pistes, with a mix of 1 black, 10 red, and 7 blue runs. The longest run is 4 km. The terrain is predominantly intermediate, with wide, well-groomed slopes. There are six dedicated ski learning areas for beginners. The slopes are served by modern lifts and extensive snowmaking covering 74 km of pistes.
valueDescription: Folgaria offers excellent value for money, with a 6-day adult ski pass costing around €284 and a day pass at €55. Accommodation and dining costs are significantly lower than in major Dolomite resorts; reviewers note that a comparable holiday costs about half the price of Ortisei. The resort is ranked first worldwide for value for money by GoSnomad. Ski rental and lessons are also competitively priced.
familyDescription: Folgaria is highly family-friendly, with six dedicated ski learning areas featuring safe, gentle slopes. The resort offers snow baby parks, kinderheims, and a children's entertainment program. Over 150 certified instructors are available at the Alpe Cimbra ski schools, offering group and private lessons. The new 10-seater Leitner gondola provides easy access with cabins that accommodate skis, strollers, and pets. The village is compact and walkable, and there is an ice skating rink at Palafolgaria.
apresSkiDescription: The après-ski scene in Folgaria is relaxed and welcoming. Notable bars include Jon's Bar, Bar Pizzeria Da Franco, and Café Centrale. Some hidden basement bars offer a more lively atmosphere. The resort also has an ice skating rink at Palafolgaria. Evening dining features local Italian cuisine at reasonable prices.
resortCharacterDescription: Folgaria is a traditional alpine village in Trentino, with a compact, walkable center featuring local shops, restaurants, and hotels. The architecture is alpine-style with stone and wood buildings. The resort has a lively but relaxed atmosphere. The new Francolini gondola provides a modern link from the village to the slopes, blending with the historic setting.
liftSystemDescription: The Alpe Cimbra lift system comprises 41 lifts, including a new 10-seater Leitner gondola connecting Francolini to Sommo Alto (2.8 km in 8 minutes), 6 high-speed quad chairs, 11 quad chairs, and 17 surface lifts. The new gondola, inaugurated for the 2025/26 season, replaces a historic cable car from the 1970s. Some older chairlifts remain, with reviews noting they can be slow. Total uplift capacity is 42,300 persons per hour. Snowmaking covers 74 km of pistes with 90 snow cannons.
linkedResortsDescription: Folgaria is part of the Alpe Cimbra ski area, which includes the linked resorts of Lavarone and Luserna. A single ski pass provides access to all three areas, with a total of 100 km of pistes and 41 lifts. The areas are connected by ski buses and some linking lifts. The wider area also includes the Fiorentini slopes.

terrainDescription: The ski area spans over 60 km of groomed runs within the Folgarida-Marilleva sector, part of the larger Campiglio Brenta Dolomites ski area which totals over 150 km of interconnected slopes. Local terrain is dominated by wide, gentle blue runs such as 'Bamby' and 'Sghirlat' for beginners, and red runs like 'Mastellissima' (3 km from Monte Vigo to Marilleva 1400), 'Orso Bruno', and 'Spolverino'. Advanced skiers can tackle black runs including 'Nera Folgarida' (1.8 km, 600 m vertical drop) and the steep, old-school 'Orti' run in Marilleva. The slopes are well-groomed and supported by an extensive snowmaking system covering 45 km of pistes. The area connects to Madonna di Campiglio and Pinzolo, providing access to a further 22% of black-rated terrain, particularly on Monte Spinale.
offPisteDescription: In good snow conditions, off-piste skiing is possible in the open bowls above the treeline, which can hold powder after fresh snowfall. The terrain around Monte Spinale and towards Marilleva offers opportunities for experienced skiers, though no specific off-piste routes are detailed in the source material.
valueDescription: A six-day adult skipass in high season costs €373, with lower prices in low season (€323 for six days). The resort is part of the Skirama Dolomiti network, which is included in the Epic Pass, potentially offering savings for pass holders. Accommodation ranges from ski-in/ski-out hotels to apartments, with prices varying by season. The resort also offers a free child skipass (born 2018–2022) when accompanied by an adult paying full price.
familyDescription: The resort features three dedicated Family Parks: Marilleva Family Park and Folgarida Kids Park, with inflatable games, toboggan runs, and entertainment activities. The nursery slopes are close to the village, and the ski school offers lessons for children. The resort is compact and walkable, with ski school meeting points within easy reach of accommodation. The official mascot Snowy the fox engages children in the enchanted forests of the Brenta Dolomites.
apresSkiDescription: Apres-ski is low-key, centered on the terraces at Malghet Aut, where skiers can relax with drinks before the last descent. The village itself has a few bars and restaurants, but the scene is quiet compared to larger resorts like Madonna di Campiglio. Mountain huts such as those at Malghet Aut offer panoramic views and local dishes.
resortCharacterDescription: Folgarida is a purpose-built resort, but designed to blend with traditional alpine architecture, with buildings kept below the treeline due to national park regulations. The village is compact and convenient, with ski-in/ski-out access. It is an attractive, family-friendly base away from the bustle of Madonna di Campiglio.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system comprises 23 lifts, including 6 gondolas, 2 high-speed six-person chairlifts, 5 quad chairs, and 5 surface lifts. Recent upgrades include the Ometto chairlift with heated seats and a capacity of 2,800 people per hour, and the Daolasa-Val Mastellina cable car providing quick valley-to-slope access. The system is modern and efficient, though queues can form at peak times, particularly at the Spolverino lift.
linkedResortsDescription: Folgarida-Marilleva is part of the Skiarea Campiglio Dolomiti di Brenta, which also includes Madonna di Campiglio and Pinzolo, offering over 150 km of interconnected slopes accessible with a single skipass. It is also part of the larger Skirama Dolomiti network, which includes Pejo, Ponte di Legno-Tonale, Andalo-Fai della Paganella, Monte Bondone, and Folgarida-Lavarone. The area is included in the Epic Pass for European skiing.

terrainDescription: The Foppolo-Carona ski area offers 30 km of pistes served by 12 lifts, with a vertical drop from 2200m to 1635m. The Foppolo side features predominantly red and blue runs suitable for intermediate skiers, while the Carona side includes a black run on Monte Valgussera. The area is linked by piste, allowing skiers to explore both sides. Snowmaking covers the slopes to ensure reliable conditions.
valueDescription: A daily lift pass for the Foppolo-Carona area costs €40 for adults (festive rate). Accommodation is available in budget apartments and hotels, with self-catering options to save on dining. Restaurants offer pizzas for around €10 and beers for €3–4. Ski hire is cheap, around £80 for a week's snowboard rental. The resort is known as a budget-friendly destination, especially for day trips from Milan or Bergamo.
familyDescription: Foppolo is family-friendly with a dedicated ski school (Kinderpark Sci 90) offering 2-hour group lessons for children at €40. There is a sledging run in the via Foppelle area and a playground. The resort is quiet during weekdays, making it safe for beginners, but gets busier at weekends with day-trippers from Milan and Bergamo. Accommodation is close to slopes, and there is free parking.
apresSkiDescription: Apres-ski is low-key, with a few restaurants and bars in the village. During the week, many establishments close early, but at weekends the atmosphere livens up with day visitors. There is a disco for evening entertainment. The resort is more suited to those seeking a quiet evening after skiing rather than a vibrant nightlife scene.
resortCharacterDescription: Foppolo is a traditional Italian mountain village in the Brembana Valley, with a mix of old stone buildings and modern apartment blocks. The village center has a few shops, restaurants, and a church. The infrastructure is somewhat dated, with many apartments showing their age, but the setting is scenic with views of the Orobie Alps. It is not a purpose-built resort but a genuine village that has grown around skiing.
liftSystemDescription: The Foppolo-Carona ski area has a total of 12 lifts, including chairlifts and surface lifts. The Foppolo side has 4 chairlifts, while the Carona side adds additional lifts. Some lifts are older and slower, but the network provides access to all slopes. The lifts are generally uncrowded on weekdays but can have queues on weekends.
linkedResortsDescription: Foppolo is linked by piste to the resort of Carona, forming the Brembo Ski area which also includes San Simone. A single ski pass gives access to all three resorts, with a total of 30 km of pistes and 12 lifts. The Carona side offers more challenging terrain, including a black run.

terrainDescription: The ski area is split into two zones connected by a cat-bus service. The lower Davost zone at valley level (around 900 m) has gentle slopes, moving walkways, ski lifts, and a race course, designed for beginners and children. The upper Varmost zone on Monte Crusicalas reaches 2,073 m and offers black and red pistes, including the longest slope in Friuli Venezia Giulia: a 6 km run through woods with views of the eastern Dolomites. The terrain is 71% intermediate, 26% beginner, and 3% advanced, with 13 km of pistes total.
valueDescription: Lift pass prices are among the lowest in the region: an adult day pass costs €44, a youth (2007–2018) day pass €10, and children (2019–2023) ski free. A 6-day adult pass is €211. Accommodation includes over 4,000 beds in B&Bs, apartments, and hotels, with many self-catering options. The resort is described as a low-cost destination for families, with costs more contained than larger Alpine resorts.
familyDescription: The Davost zone has a dedicated nursery area with moving walkways and a race course, plus the Fantasy Snow Park with bobsleds, trampolines, and inflatables. An ice rink, indoor swimming pool, and wellness centre provide off-slope activities. Ski school offers lessons for children, and the village is compact and walkable. The resort is marketed as ideal for families with children learning to ski.
apresSkiDescription: The resort has 5 bars and pubs and 10 restaurants. Winter events include Christmas markets and a New Year's Eve torchlight procession. Summer features the Rock in For concert series. The atmosphere is quiet and family-oriented rather than party-focused.
resortCharacterDescription: Forni di Sopra is a traditional mountain village with stone and wood houses, set in the Friulian Dolomites Natural Park (UNESCO World Heritage). It consists of three hamlets: Vico, Andrazza, and Cella. The village has Roman origins and retains an authentic, unpurpose-built character. It is the most visited mountain settlement in Friuli Venezia Giulia, popular in both summer and winter.
liftSystemDescription: The resort has 8 lifts: 3 quad chairs, 1 triple chair, and 4 surface lifts. All slopes are covered by artificial snowmaking, ensuring reliable conditions. The lifts operate from 09:00 to 16:00. The system is modern and well-maintained, with a capacity of 7,650 persons per hour.
linkedResortsDescription: The Forni di Sopra skipass is also valid at Piancavallo, Ravascletto-Zoncolan, Sappada, Sauris, Sella Nevea, and Tarvisio, allowing access to multiple ski areas in Friuli Venezia Giulia under a single pass. These resorts are not physically connected by lifts but are within driving distance.

terrainDescription: The terrain is dominated by wide, gentle green and blue runs, with a 5 km blue run called La Grande Bleue descending from Pointe de Razis. There are 10 black pistes, including the Chabrières speed skiing slope, the fastest in the world with a 98% gradient. The slopes are set among larch forests and offer long sunny days due to southern exposure. The resort has 110 marked runs covering 185 km, with a vertical drop of 1,100 m.
offPisteDescription: The Forêt Blanche ski area is renowned for backcountry skiing, with off-piste bowls and tree runs available in the larch forests. The Totem Park by the Sibières chairlift features natural obstacles for freeride. However, detailed off-piste itineraries are not described in the sources.
valueDescription: Lift passes are moderately priced, with a one-day adult pass at €58 and a six-day low-season pass at €209.50. Accommodation costs can be reduced by renting in the smaller villages of Vars Sainte-Marie or Saint-Marcellin rather than the resort centre. The resort offers Saturday lift pass discounts for budget-conscious skiers. However, recent reviews note that lift pass prices have increased significantly.
familyDescription: Risoul has obtained the Famille Plus label, guaranteeing family-friendly facilities. A free beginners' area with three lifts (including a magic carpet and rope tows) is available with the Risoul Initiation pass. Children from age 3 can take lessons with the French Ski School. The resort offers many easy green and blue runs, and the village of Risoul has a traditional, safe atmosphere with wooden chalets.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski is limited compared to larger resorts. Risoul has a slightly livelier scene with bars and restaurants, while Vars is quieter. The CoolZone at the snowpark offers a relaxed atmosphere with drinks and views of the freestyle area. Night skiing is available on a 0.5 km lit run on Tuesdays.
resortCharacterDescription: The resort consists of two distinct villages: Vars, a purpose-built 1960s development with functional architecture spread across four hamlets (Saint-Marcellin, Sainte-Marie, Sainte-Catherine, and Les Claux), and Risoul, which has a more traditional Savoyard character with wooden-clad chalets and stone walls. Risoul has a compact, walkable centre, while Vars is more spread out with a bus network.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system has undergone recent modernisation with the installation of four new six-seater chairlifts, reducing wait times. The resort has 37 lifts in total, including gondolas, chairlifts, and drag lifts. However, some older chairlifts remain and have been noted as slow. The link between Vars and Risoul has been re-established with new lifts.
linkedResortsDescription: La Forêt Blanche is a linked ski area connecting the resorts of Vars and Risoul, offering 185 km of pistes. The link has been recently re-established with new lifts. It is the largest ski area in the Southern French Alps.

terrainDescription: The resort offers FISI-approved slopes, with a mix of blue and green runs suitable for beginners and intermediates. The Malanotte downhill run is equipped with snow-making. The longest run is 5 km. A dedicated children's area, Frabolandia, features inflatables, trampolines, and bobsleighs.
familyDescription: Frabosa Soprana has dedicated blue and green slopes for children, and the Frabolandia Snow Park offers inflatables, trampolines, a ball pool, playhouses, slides, and bobsleighs. The resort provides personalised services for families and has a wellness centre and solarium areas.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski options include mountain chalets serving traditional local dishes, particularly Raschera DOP cheese. The village also has bars and restaurants.
resortCharacterDescription: Frabosa Soprana is a traditional Italian mountain village that has been a tourist resort since the late 1800s. The old village centre retains its characteristic architecture with narrow streets and stone buildings. The resort offers a warm, welcoming atmosphere.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system includes 7 lifts, comprising chairlifts and surface lifts. The Monte Moro chairlift, inaugurated in 1948, was one of the first in Italy. A chairlift connects Frabosa Soprana to the Prato Nevoso ski resort, providing access to the wider Mondolè Ski area.
linkedResortsDescription: Frabosa Soprana is connected via chairlift to Prato Nevoso, and together with Artesina forms the Mondolè Ski area. The combined area offers over 100 km of slopes and 30 lifts.

terrainDescription: The resort has 11 km of groomed pistes, consisting of 2 km of blue runs and 9 km of red runs. There are no black runs. The main attraction is the 9 km valley run from the summit to Obertraun, which is the longest downhill run in Upper Austria with 1,500 meters of vertical descent. The terrain is high alpine and somewhat rough, with wide, scenic slopes. A short chairlift near the summit serves beginner terrain, and Krippi Park at the valley station offers a gentle learning area with a magic carpet.
offPisteDescription: Krippenstein is a freerider's paradise with over 30 km of marked off-piste variants. The terrain includes rough rocky ridges, varied tracks, and selective forest routes in areas like Schönberg, Imisl, and Eisgrube, offering 1,500 meters of altitude per ride. Powder-filled descents, open bowls, and tree runs provide backcountry-style skiing. The resort hosts avalanche safety training and guided freeride tours through the FreeSports Arena, with courses for all levels: Freeride Easy, Freeride Amateur, and Freeride Pro.
familyDescription: Krippi Park at the valley station has a magic carpet and T-bar lift for beginners, providing a safe learning area for children. The ski school offers private courses for beginners and advanced skiers. There is a family-friendly slope near Gjaidalm at the middle station, and the resort is described as laid-back and family-friendly. The compact layout and short walks from accommodation to lifts make it convenient for families.
resortCharacterDescription: The resort is a small, off-the-beaten-path ski area above the village of Obertraun, part of the UNESCO World Heritage region Salzkammergut. It has a tranquil, laid-back atmosphere far from mainstream crowds. The base village Obertraun is a peaceful lakeside village at 512 meters. The mountain station at 2,100 meters offers panoramic views of Hallstatt and the Dachstein glacier. The resort emphasizes low-impact tourism and sustainability, with all lift systems powered by renewable energy.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of 6 lifts: 3 gondolas/trams, 1 quad chair, and 2 surface lifts. The Dachstein Krippenstein cable car has two sections from Obertraun to the summit, operating every 15 minutes from 08:30 to 16:50 (lift hours 9:00–16:00). The cable cars are described as ultramodern and are supplied with electricity from renewable energy sources. A short chairlift near the summit serves beginner terrain.
linkedResortsDescription: With a multi-day ski pass of 1.5 days or more, skiers can access the wider Dachstein West ski region, which includes Dachstein West (Gosau, Russbach, Annaberg), Feuerkogel Ebensee, Karkogel & Sonnleitenlifte Abtenau, Skilift Lammertal Lungötz, St. Martin am Tennengebirge, Zinkenlifte Bad Dürrnberg, and Werfenweng (with season pass). This gives access to over 160 km of slopes and 70 lifts. The Dachstein West season pass also includes these areas.

terrainDescription: The ski area at Füssener Jöchle-Grän consists of 9 km of pistes, with 67% easy (blue) and 33% medium (red) runs. The main descent is a long red family slope from the top of the gondola at 1,821 m down to Grän at 1,155 m. Additional blue and red runs are located on the north side of the Joch around the Jochalpe chairlift. The terrain is predominantly gentle and well-suited for beginners and intermediate skiers, with a few steeper sections on the red runs. The slopes are mostly open above the treeline, offering views of the Allgäu Alps.
valueDescription: Day lift tickets for the 2024/25 season cost €49 for adults, €34 for youths, €19 for children, and €22 for seniors. The resort is part of the Tannheimer Tal ski pass network, which covers 20 lifts across four local ski areas. Wider network passes such as the Superschnee Allgäu-Tirol-Kleinwalsertal (covering 500 km of slopes and 200 lifts) and the Vitales Land pass are also available. Accommodation in Grän includes family-run guesthouses and hotels, with many offering packages.
familyDescription: The resort has a dedicated children's area and a ski school with English-speaking instructors. Beginners and children can practice on the Schachenlift area in the valley, which features a conveyor belt and gentle slopes. The main gondola provides easy access to the upper mountain, and the long red family run from the top to the base is suitable for intermediate skiers. The village of Grän is traffic-free in the center, making it safe for families to walk to the lifts.
resortCharacterDescription: Grän is a traditional Tyrolean village located at the entrance to the Tannheim Valley, near the German border. The resort is not purpose-built; it retains authentic alpine charm with guesthouses, farms, and a small village center. The ski area is accessed via a modern gondola from the edge of the village. The surrounding landscape includes the Haldensee lake and views of the Allgäu Alps.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of 5 lifts: one 8-person gondola, one chairlift (high-speed six-seater), three T-bar lifts, and one conveyor belt. The gondola transports skiers from Grän at 1,155 m to the top station at 1,821 m. The lifts are modern and efficient, with the gondola offering panoramic views. The resort is rarely crowded, so lift queues are minimal.
linkedResortsDescription: Füssener Jöchle-Grän is one of four ski areas in the Tannheimer Tal, along with Tannheim-Neunerköpfle, Zöblen-Rohnenspitze, and Schattwald-Wannenjoch. A single Tannheimer Tal ski pass covers all 20 lifts in these four areas. Additionally, the Superschnee Allgäu-Tirol-Kleinwalsertal pass provides access to 500 km of slopes across 8 ski areas in Tyrol, the Kleinwalsertal, and the Allgäu, including 200 lifts. The Vitales Land pass covers 70 cable cars and lifts in northern Tyrol and eastern Allgäu.

terrainDescription: The resort's 6 km of pistes are centered around a 2.3 km long 'Skiautobahn' (ski highway) that serves as a main warm-up run, and an adventure piste featuring three XXL banked curves and waves. A permanent race track is available for training and competitions. Slopes are well-groomed and snowmaking covers 90% of the area. The terrain is divided into easy (53%), intermediate (40%), and advanced (7%) sections, with the majority suitable for beginners and intermediates.
valueDescription: A day lift pass for an adult costs €48, with a family day pass at €110. Season passes are available from €399 in early bird pricing. Multi-day and group discounts are offered. Some visitors have noted that the pricing feels high relative to the small size of the ski area (6 km of pistes). Ski rental and ski school are available at standard rates. The resort also participates in the Mur-Mürz Top-Skipass and Steiermark Joker passes, which provide access to a wider network of ski areas and thermal baths.
familyDescription: The resort features the 'Schi GaalaXie' children's area, a 2-hectare zone with a 60 m covered magic carpet and a 'living room' rest area on the piste. The 'Bikini Bottom' kidspark offers playful learning with cartoon characters. Ski school provides courses for children from age 2.5, including weekend, half-day, and private lessons. All lifts start from the same base area, making it easy to keep track of children. Night skiing on a 1.4 km floodlit piste is available on select evenings for family fun.
apresSkiDescription: The resort has three rustic après-ski huts located on the slopes, offering a cosy atmosphere. The Gaalerhof restaurant at the base station serves Styrian culinary specialities. During night skiing events, the huts are open for a social end to the day. Night skiing operates on select evenings from 19:00 to 21:00.
resortCharacterDescription: Gaaler Lifte is a small, family-run ski area located in the Gaaltal valley in Styria, surrounded by the scenic landscape of the Murtal region. The base area is at the village of Gaal, with a traditional alpine feel. The resort is not a large purpose-built complex but rather a local ski hill with a few lifts and huts, offering an intimate and authentic Austrian skiing experience.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of one double chairlift, two surface lifts (T-bars), and a covered magic carpet for the children's area. Lifts operate daily from 9:00 to 16:00, with night skiing on select evenings from 19:00 to 21:00. The lifts are modern and well-maintained, with a capacity of 2,150 persons per hour.
linkedResortsDescription: Gaaler Lifte is part of the Mur-Mürz Top-Skipass, which provides access to 14 ski areas in the Mur and Mürz valleys. Additionally, the Steiermark Joker pass covers 28 ski areas and 5 thermal baths across Styria. These passes allow skiers to explore a wider region beyond the local slopes.

terrainDescription: The ski area covers 8 km of groomed slopes, with 1.5 km classified as easy (19%) and 6.5 km as intermediate (81%). The longest run is 2.8 km, which is used for super-G training with a vertical drop of 265 m. Slopes are set on the edge of the Kočevski Rog forest, offering views of the Dolenjska and Bela Krajina regions. The resort has a dedicated nursery slope (Otroška, 600 m) and a sledding trail. The terrain is suitable for carving skiing.
valueDescription: An adult day lift pass costs €26 (2024 price), with youth tickets at €21 and children at €17. A season pass for adults is €350. Equipment rental and ski school are available at the resort. The resort is 80 km from Ljubljana, making it accessible for day trips. Accommodation is available in the nearby spa town of Dolenjske Toplice (12 km) or the village of Semič (13 km).
familyDescription: The resort is marketed as family-friendly, with a dedicated children's slope (Otroška, 600 m) and a surface lift named Medo for young skiers. Ski schools are run by Smučarsko društvo Gače and Smučarsko društvo Krka Rog, staffed by former competitors and coaches. Family discounts are available on ski courses. The resort also has a sledding trail and an adventure park for children over 5 years old.
resortCharacterDescription: Gače is a small, family-run ski resort situated on the edge of the Kočevski Rog forest at 965 m elevation. It is not a purpose-built resort village but a recreational center with a single mountain hut (Koča Gače) offering food and drinks. The resort operates year-round with summer activities including hiking, cycling, and an adventure park. The nearest accommodation is in the spa town of Dolenjske Toplice (12 km) or the village of Semič (13 km).
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of one triple chairlift (Gričice, built by Leitner in 2003) and five surface lifts (Medo, Gače, Bis, Vrh levo, Vrh desno). Total lift capacity is 5,400 skiers per hour. The chairlift has a vertical rise of 264 m. No high-speed detachable lifts are present.

terrainDescription: The Galibier-Thabor ski area is spread over three linked sectors: Les Vernays and Setaz to the south, and Crey du Quart to the east, the latter linking with Valmeinier. The lower slopes of Thimel mountain in the Setaz sector are wooded, offering tree-lined runs back to the village. Most terrain sits on open, rolling ridges between Valloire and Valmeinier, with the majority of pistes being intermediate reds. There are a handful of black runs, including two on the Grand Plateau (Cotérieux and Méregers) and two at the Cornafond chairlift. Green and blue runs make up over half the domain, with beginner zones at altitude in both main sectors. An easy circuit of narrow, zigzagging trails links all sectors.
offPisteDescription: The best off-piste opportunities are in the Crey du Quart sector on the Valloire side and Le Gros Crey on the Valmeinier side. A steep off-piste descent from the Armera piste above the treeline drops onto the blue Grapil piste. After fresh snowfall, powder can be found around Les Verneys. Black runs Mérégers, Dahu, Chamois, and Edelweiss are normally left ungroomed, developing mogul fields. A guide is recommended for exploring off-piste terrain.
valueDescription: Lift passes are moderately priced: a six-day adult pass costs €257.10 in low season and a day pass is €53.60 (2025/26 prices). The resort is less well-known than mainstream French resorts, and accommodation and dining are generally affordable. Self-catering apartments and mid-range hotels are common. The resort is popular with school groups seeking good value.
familyDescription: Valloire has a free trackless mini-train connecting lift terminals and accommodation, plus a free shuttle bus to outlying hamlets. The VALKID space offers a dedicated fun area for children. There are multiple beginner zones, including a compact area at Les Vernays with a free ski-tow, and additional nursery slopes at altitude. Off-slope activities include a full-size outdoor ice rink, a ten-pin bowling alley (open until 2am), and a cinema. The village is flat and easy to walk around.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski is subdued, centred on relaxed local bars such as Pub L’Atelier (with occasional live music) and Le Bar à Zing. Most evening entertainment revolves around leisurely dinners in traditional Savoyard restaurants. Additional options include the outdoor ice rink, a ten-pin bowling alley (licensed until 2am), and a cinema. In mid-to-late January, an international ice and snow-sculpture competition decorates the village streets.
resortCharacterDescription: Valloire is a traditional Alpine village centred around a 17th-century Baroque church, with narrow old-town streets at its core and wider avenues on the periphery. The valley-floor location makes it flat and easy to stroll. The community includes 17 outlying hamlets. The atmosphere is friendly and relaxed, with a traditional French ambience. Valmeinier, the linked resort, is more purpose-built with modern apartment blocks.
liftSystemDescription: The ski area has 30 lifts: 2 gondolas (cable cars), 17 chairlifts, 10 drag lifts, and 1 carpet lift. The two main access points from Valloire are gondolas at the southern and eastern edges of the village, serving the Setaz and Crey du Quart sectors. Two roadside chairlifts at Les Clots and Les Vernays provide additional access to Setaz. Four lifts are accessible to pedestrians. The lift system is rated 6/10 for efficiency.
linkedResortsDescription: The Galibier-Thabor ski area links the resorts of Valloire and Valmeinier, forming a single domain of 160 km of pistes. The two resorts are connected by slopes and lifts, with a single ski pass valid across the entire area. Valloire is a traditional Alpine village, while Valmeinier is a more purpose-built resort. The linked area covers three mountains: Le Gros Crey, Le Grand Plateau, and La Setaz des Pres.

terrainDescription: Galsterberg offers around 15 km of well-groomed slopes, with half rated red and black. The highlight is the 1.2 km long Vorderkar slope (2a), which is 60 metres wide and suitable for carving. A permanent race track and training piste are located on the descent from the Kalteck top station. Beginners have 5 km of slightly marked slopes, with the gentlest terrain at the top station of the gondola lift, accessible by a button lift. The terrain is forest-lined and varied, with a long descent from the summit at 1,986 m offering views of the Dachstein mountains.
familyDescription: The resort features a dedicated children's area near the base station with a magic carpet and beginner lift. The 'Gallic Ski Village' adventure area is themed after ancient Gauls, with up to ten-metre-high snow figures, tunnels, and costumed characters. The LITTLE amadeus Kinderschneeland includes a play castle, wave track, magic carpet, snow castle, snow tubing, and a ski jump. A children's restaurant serves lunch, and an indoor toddlers' group is available for the youngest. The ski school provides lessons for children, and the resort is described as a family ski mountain.
apresSkiDescription: The resort has one bar/pub. The Priklalm at 1,220 m offers live acts and a rustic atmosphere. The 5 km illuminated toboggan run operates in the evening (Tuesday, Thursday, Saturday from 26 December to 15 March). Traditional guesthouses serve hearty meals and local wine beside fireplaces.
resortCharacterDescription: Galsterberg is a small, family-friendly ski resort in the Enns Valley of Styria, offering a peaceful alternative to larger destinations. It has an authentic alpine atmosphere with unspoiled nature and a focus on small-scale tourism. The resort limits overdevelopment and uses traditional building materials. The village of Pruggern is nearby, and the resort is part of the Schladming-Dachstein region.
liftSystemDescription: The resort has 4 lifts: a 10-person gondola (EUB Galsterbergalm), a 4-person chairlift (Gipfelbahn Vorderkahr), and two surface lifts (Galsterbergalmlift and Tellerlift). The gondola provides quick access from the valley to 1,650 m. The chairlift reaches the summit at 1,981 m. Lifts operate from 08:30 to 16:00 daily. The system is modern and efficient, with a capacity of 3,594 persons per hour.
linkedResortsDescription: Galsterberg is one of seven ski mountains in the Schladming-Dachstein region, which together offer 222 km of slopes and 99 lifts. The region includes the 4-mountain ski area (Hauser Kaibling, Planai, Hochwurzen, Reiteralm) plus Fageralm, Ramsau, and Galsterberg. With a Ski amadé pass, guests access over 760 km of slopes and 270 lifts across five regions, including Salzburger Sportwelt, Gastein, Hochkönig, and Grossarltal.

terrainDescription: The beginner zone at the base of the mountain is wide and well-maintained, with six 'Adventure Land' features spread across the slopes that provide progressive challenges for children and novices. Early intermediates can enjoy a variety of local blue and red runs, while advanced skiers head to the Breitspitzbahn gondola for steeper terrain. Most runs are above the treeline, so conditions can be bleak in poor weather, but the slopes are well-groomed and fast lifts provide quick access.
offPisteDescription: Off-piste routes are extensive and can remain untracked for several days after a snowfall, according to visitor reviews. Galtür also serves as a springboard for ski touring, with accessible routes from the Breitspitzbahn area. The resort has designated freeride areas and a funpark for snowboarders and thrill-seekers.
valueDescription: Lift passes are reasonably priced, with a 6-day adult pass costing around €257.50 and a single-day pass at €52.50. Accommodation is notably cheaper than in neighbouring Ischgl, making Galtür a budget-friendly base for accessing the larger Paznaun ski area. The free ski bus to Ischgl adds further value, and the resort offers good-value self-catering apartments and family-run hotels.
familyDescription: The resort has a dedicated beginner zone at the base of the mountain and six 'Adventure Land' fun areas across the slopes designed specifically for families and children. Ski schools with English-speaking instructors are available. Off the slopes, there are winter walking trails, cross-country tracks, a night sledging track, and an excellent sports centre, providing plenty of activities for non-skiers and families.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski in Galtür is limited to a few quiet bars and restaurants in the village, with no large clubs or late-night venues. The resort's atmosphere is calm, and those wanting a livelier scene take the free bus to Ischgl, which has extensive nightlife with bars, clubs, and live music events.
resortCharacterDescription: Galtür is a traditional Tyrolean village with charming alpine architecture, set at the head of the Paznaun Valley. It retains a peaceful, authentic character, with wooden chalets and a compact layout. The village is car-free in parts and offers a quieter alternative to the larger, more commercial Ischgl.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of 10 lifts, including two gondolas, a high-speed six-person chairlift, a high-speed quad, and five surface lifts. Lifts are modern and efficient, with fast access to the slopes. The resort also has modern snowmaking infrastructure covering a significant portion of the pistes.
linkedResortsDescription: Galtür is part of the Paznaun valley ski area, linked by free ski bus to Ischgl, Kappl, and See. Ischgl itself connects to Samnaun in Switzerland, forming the Silvretta Arena with 239 km of pistes (or 293 km according to some sources). The combined area offers extensive terrain for all levels, with a single lift pass covering all four resorts.

terrainDescription: The ski slopes are located on Monte Scirocco and Monte Nardello within the Aspromonte National Park. Runs wind through pine forests and offer panoramic views of the Strait of Messina, the Aeolian Islands, and Mount Etna. The resort has one black run, three red runs, and one blue run, with the longest descent approximately 1 mile. The terrain is relatively short and suited to a range of abilities, with the slopes facing south towards the sea.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski options include Chalet Gambarie for drinks and Ristorante Da Nonna for meals. A local tradition is the 'Gambarie sandwich' filled with cold cuts, cheeses, and pickled products, available at various spots around the resort. The atmosphere is lively but low-key.
resortCharacterDescription: Gambarie is a small mountain town located at 1,350 metres above sea level in the heart of the Aspromonte National Park, surrounded by peaks over 2,000 metres. It is not a purpose-built resort but a traditional village with hotels, bars, shops, and restaurants. The slopes are integrated into the natural landscape of pine forests, and the resort offers views of the sea and Sicily.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of four chairlifts and one ski lift that connect the town centre to Monte Scirocco and Monte Nardello. The lifts have a combined capacity of 2,400 skiers per hour. Recent years have seen investment in upgrading infrastructure, including new tracks and improved facilities.

terrainDescription: The Schafberg ski area above Gargellen is centred on a high alpine plateau with wide, open meadow slopes. The terrain is predominantly gentle, with long blue runs that are ideal for beginners and intermediates. The longest descent from the top of the gondola back to the village is 8 km. The slopes are mostly above treeline, with some tree-lined sections on the lower mountain. The resort's highest lift reaches 2,130 m, and the vertical drop is 710 m.
offPisteDescription: Gargellen is known for its off-piste and ski touring terrain. The Nidla slope offers steep freeride lines that hold powder for days after a storm. The Madrisa round tour is a famous cross-border ski tour that goes from Gargellen to Klosters in Switzerland and back, requiring a guide. The piste map includes itinerary routes that are not groomed but are avalanche-controlled. The area under the old Schafberg chairlift provides additional freeride lines. The resort's location in a snowy corner of the Alps ensures deep snow cover for off-piste skiing.
valueDescription: A six-day adult lift pass costs €269 (as of 2026), and a day ticket starts from €51. Children's day tickets are €36.50, and children under 6 ski free. Lift passes of 2.5 days or more are valid across all Montafon and Brandnertal resorts, giving access to 295 km of pistes. Accommodation options include 10 hotels with 465 beds, and there are budget-friendly pensions and hostels available.
familyDescription: Gargellen is well-suited for families with young children. There is a nursery slope with a drag lift in the village and another nursery area near the top of the Gargellenbahn gondola. The ski school has 45 instructors and offers group and private lessons. Childcare is available for children from 36 months, with 4 hours of care per day costing €77 (without lunch). Facilities include a children's carousel, magic carpet, child lift, adventure park, and playground. The long blue runs (up to 8 km) are gentle enough for progressing beginners, and the village is compact and walkable.
apresSkiDescription: The après-ski scene in Gargellen is low-key and relaxed. There are 7 restaurants, 8 bars and cafés, and 1 disco. Popular spots include the Bergrestaurant for hearty meals and the Schafberg Bar for drinks. The village is quiet and sleepy, with a handful of family-run hotels. Nightlife is not a focus; the resort is more suited to those seeking a peaceful evening after skiing.
resortCharacterDescription: Gargellen is a traditional Austrian village nestled in a high alpine valley on the Swiss border. Historically known as the 'Smugglers Village', it features rustic charm with a handful of tasteful, family-run hotels. The village is compact and walkable, with the gondola station at the edge of town. It is not a purpose-built resort; the architecture is traditional, and the atmosphere is quiet and sleepy. The resort is car-free in the centre, with parking at the gondola base.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system includes a gondola (Gargellenbahn) that rises from the village to Schafberghűsli, a high-speed six-person chairlift, a quad chair, a double chair, and several surface lifts. The total uplift capacity is 8,400 skiers per hour. Snowmaking is available with 50 snow cannons covering the main runs. The lifts are modern and efficient, with minimal queues due to the resort's small size.
linkedResortsDescription: Gargellen is part of the Montafon ski region, which includes four main areas: Silvretta Nova, Gargellen, Hochjoch, and Golm, totalling 219 km of pistes. The Montafon-Brandnertal lift pass also covers Brandnertal, giving access to 295 km. Additionally, Gargellen is close to the Swiss resort of Klosters, and the Madrisa round tour allows skiing across the border. The Silvretta Montafon ski area is linked by a common lift pass, and a free ski bus connects the villages.

terrainDescription: The ski area at Geißkopf offers 8 km of slopes with 9 marked runs, served by 8 lifts. The terrain is predominantly intermediate, with 66% of runs classified as red (intermediate), 23% as blue (easy), and 11% as black (difficult). The main summit is at 1,097 m, with a vertical drop of 281 m. The longest run is the Familienabfahrt Riegel at 2 km. The Geißleinpark at the base provides dedicated beginner slopes with magic carpets. The Nordhang runs (I and II) are red-rated and popular for carving, while the Herrenabfahrt Riegel is the only black run. The slopes are spread across the Geißkopf and neighboring Einödriegel, with a 6-seater chairlift and several drag lifts providing access.
valueDescription: A day lift pass for adults costs €41, with youth passes at €33 and children at €21. Family tickets and half-day options are available at lower rates. The resort is in the Bavarian Forest, where overall costs for accommodation, food, and lift passes are noticeably lower than in the Alps. The ski school and equipment hire are competitively priced, and the resort offers a natural toboggan run and floodlit skiing on certain evenings at no extra charge beyond the lift ticket.
familyDescription: The resort is rated as top for families and beginners. The Geißleinpark at the base features gentle slopes, magic carpets, and a dedicated children's area with a heated hut (Wärme-Häusl) for warming up. The ski school is located directly at the base station and offers lessons for all ages. A 2 km natural toboggan run is accessible via the chairlift. The slopes are mostly wide and well-groomed, with easy blue runs and a progression to intermediate reds. The resort also has a snow park for older children and freestylers.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski is centered on the Tauscher ski bar at the central cash desk, which offers hot après-ski hits and drinks. There are also three snow bars and the Geißkopfhütte mountain restaurant at the summit, serving regional dishes. The Forstgasthof in the valley provides a cosy stop. Night skiing is available from Wednesday to Saturday, 18:00–22:00, on the Geißkopf and Nordhang T-bar lifts, with 2.8 km of floodlit runs.
resortCharacterDescription: Geißkopf is a small, family-oriented ski area on a mountain in the Bavarian Forest, developed since the 1960s as a local recreation area. The base is near the village of Bischofsmais, with a compact layout of lifts and slopes. The summit has a 23 m wooden observation tower and a mountain restaurant. The resort is not a purpose-built ski station but a natural mountain setting with a mix of forested and open runs. In summer, it transforms into a major mountain bike park with 14 downhill trails.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of 8 lifts: one 6-seater detachable chairlift (Geißkopfbahn), four T-bar lifts, one button lift, and two magic carpets. The chairlift was modernized in recent years. Lifts operate from 08:30 to 16:30 daily. Night skiing is offered on Wednesday to Saturday from 18:00 to 22:00 using the Geißkopf and Nordhang T-bar lifts. The lifts are well-maintained and queues are rare outside peak periods. The resort has a capacity of 6,600 persons per hour.

terrainDescription: The terrain consists of 15.5 km of groomed pistes, with 7 km easy (blue), 7 km intermediate (red), and 1.5 km difficult (black). The black run, Direttissima Terzerhaus, is the steepest natural piste in Lower Austria with a 74% gradient. A fun slope with steep curves, waves, and sound effects is located at the valley station, and a speed track with time measurement is also present. The summit area offers freeride terrain. The slopes are served by two modern chairlifts (Bodenbauer-Express 4-seater and Gipfelbahn 2-seater) and a drag lift (Birkischlepper).
offPisteDescription: Off-piste skiing is available in the summit area, with designated freeride zones. Two signposted ski touring routes of varying difficulty are marked, and an avalanche transceiver checkpoint is provided at the access area. The Snowpark Gemeindealpe offers natural terrain with kickers for freestyle. The freeride area below the summit is noted for deep snow conditions.
valueDescription: A day ticket costs €50.50 for adults and €26.50 for children. A 6-day pass is €234.50 for adults and €122 for children. A season ticket is available for €299 for adults and €157 for children with the NÖ Card. Discounts include the Mountain Adventure Pass, guest cards, and a senior card for ages 61 and over. A combined ticket with a return train ride from St. Pölten is also offered. The ski pass is valid across the linked resorts of Mariazell and Annaberg. Holders of a valid ski pass can travel free on the Mariazellerbahn between Mitterbach and Mariazell.
familyDescription: The resort offers a magic carpet for beginners, used exclusively by the ski school. A fun slope with waves and sound effects provides entertainment for children. The snow park has natural terrain features. A 4.6 km toboggan run is available. In summer, an 800 m pushchair-accessible panoramic trail circles the summit. The Terzerhaus mountain restaurant has an adventure climbing playground and a giant swing. The ski school offers lessons for all ages.
apresSkiDescription: Apres-ski options include the Terzerhaus mountain restaurant at the summit, offering regional dishes and panoramic views. The s'Balzplatzerl at the middle station and the Zum Bäreneck ski hut also serve food and drinks. At the valley station, the Zum Sessellift inn provides refreshments. On Wednesdays, the slopes are open until 7 p.m. for night skiing. The scene is relaxed and family-oriented rather than rowdy.
resortCharacterDescription: Mitterbach is a small village located in the Ötscher-Tormäuer Nature Park, the largest nature reserve in Lower Austria. The resort is not purpose-built; it has a traditional alpine character with rustic huts and inns. The lift infrastructure is modern, with two chairlifts and a drag lift, but the village retains its charm. The area is also a Natura 2000 protected site, home to black grouse, and the resort closes for six weeks in spring to protect wildlife.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of four lifts: the Bodenbauer-Express 4-seater chairlift (800 m to 1,300 m, 10 min ride), the Gipfelbahn 2-seater chairlift (1,300 m to 1,626 m, 7 min in winter), the Birkischlepper drag lift (637 m, 4 min), and the Grabnerlift drag lift (176 m, 1.5 min). A magic carpet (15 m) is used by the ski school. The total uphill capacity is 3,800 people per hour. The lifts are modern, with the chairlifts built in 2003 and the drag lift added in 2013. A new piste guidance system with panoramic boards was installed.
linkedResortsDescription: The Gemeindealpe Mitterbach ski pass is valid across the linked resorts of Mariazell (Bürgeralpe) and Annaberg, allowing access to all three ski areas with a single pass. This provides a combined ski area with additional terrain and lifts.

terrainDescription: The Gerlosstein ski area sits beneath the 2,266 m Gerlossteinwand and offers 10 km of well-groomed pistes between 929 m and 1,836 m. The terrain is predominantly intermediate (80% red runs), with one blue run and a short black section. Runs are tree-lined and wide, providing plenty of space. A home run of over 900 m vertical descends from the top. A beginner piste with a magic carpet is available near the base. The area is not directly connected to Zell am Ziller by slopes or lifts.
familyDescription: The Gerlosstein area is marketed as family-friendly, with a dedicated beginner piste and a magic carpet for learning. A 200 m snow tubing run is free to use and open daily. The longest natural toboggan run in Zillertal (7 km) starts at the Berghotel Gerlosstein, is illuminated until 1 a.m., and is free with a valid lift pass. The ski area is compact and quiet, making it suitable for families with young children. Ski schools are available in nearby Zell am Ziller.
resortCharacterDescription: Gerlosstein is a small, quiet ski area located at Hainzenberg, accessed by a tandem cable car from the village. The area consists of a single hotel (Berghotel Gerlosstein at 1,650 m) and a few lifts, giving it a peaceful, uncrowded atmosphere. It is part of the larger Zillertal Arena but is not connected by piste to other sectors. The setting is alpine, with views over the Zillertal valley, and the architecture is traditional Tyrolean chalet style.
liftSystemDescription: The Gerlosstein ski area has 5 lifts: one tandem cable car (Gerlossteinbahn), two chairlifts (Larchkopflift, Arbiskogel), one T-bar (Sonnalm), and one magic carpet (Babylift Gerlosstein). The cable car provides access from Hainzenberg to the ski area at 1,650 m. Lifts operate daily from 08:30 to 16:30. The chairlift from Ramsau was decommissioned in 2016.
linkedResortsDescription: Gerlosstein is part of the Zillertal Arena, the largest connected ski area in the Zillertal valley, which links the villages of Zell am Ziller, Gerlos, Königsleiten, and Hochkrimml. The Arena offers 150 km of pistes and 52 lifts. However, Gerlosstein itself is not directly connected by piste to Zell am Ziller or the other sectors; it is accessed via the Gerlossteinbahn cable car from Hainzenberg. A Zillertal Arena ski pass or the Zillertaler Superskipass provides access to all sectors, including Gerlosstein.

terrainDescription: The terrain consists of two connected mountains, Gitschberg and Jochtal, linked by the Glasboden cable car. Gitschberg offers higher-altitude skiing with more exposed, north-facing runs that hold snow well into spring, while Jochtal provides tree-lined alternatives and wider bowls above the treeline. The slopes are predominantly wide, groomed blue and red runs, with a few black sections that rarely exceed a solid red gradient. Long blue cruisers are common, suitable for progression. The sunny location means most runs face south-east, softening conditions in the afternoon. Piste grooming is to a high standard typical of German-speaking Europe.
valueDescription: Day lift passes cost €58 in regular season and €68 during peak periods, roughly 15% cheaper than the nearby Kronplatz resort. Junior passes are €48, seniors €52, and children born between 2018 and 2022 ski for free when accompanied by a paying parent. Multi-day passes and early-booking discounts further reduce costs. The resort is positioned in the mid-market bracket for South Tyrol, offering good value for families and those not requiring the extensive terrain of the Dolomiti Superski pass.
familyDescription: The resort is particularly family-friendly, with ski-in/ski-out accommodation in the village of Vals, where runs lead directly to hotels. Children under eight ski for free when a parent holds a lift pass. The Gimmy Fun Ride and a kids' fun park with a ski movie, kids cross, and speed check provide entertainment. Ski schools offer courses for children from age three, with a ski kindergarten at the Jochtal valley station. The Familienhotel Huber includes a kids' club, indoor and outdoor pools, and a water slide. The overall atmosphere is relaxed, with wide, uncrowded slopes suitable for building confidence.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski is low-key, centred on a few pubs in the base villages. The Mühle pub in Valles and the Schlupfe pub in Maranza are the most popular spots for an after-ski drink. The resort is not known for a vibrant nightlife; the focus is on relaxed evenings with local food and drink.
resortCharacterDescription: The resort is set in the traditional South Tyrolean villages of Meransen and Vals, which offer basic amenities and a quiet, family-oriented atmosphere. Vals allows ski-in/ski-out access, while Meransen sits on the sunny Gitschberg side. The area is not purpose-built; it retains a small-scale, authentic alpine character with wooden chalets and local restaurants. The villages are compact and walkable, with no large-scale commercial development.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system comprises 14 lifts, including modern 8-seater cable cars (Gaisjoch and Schilling) that connect the two mountains. The network is efficient and uncrowded, with minimal queues outside Italian school holidays. Moving between the Gitschberg and Jochtal sides takes 20–30 minutes via the connecting lifts. The lifts operate from 08:30 to 16:30 daily.
linkedResortsDescription: The Gitschberg and Jochtal mountains are two distinct ski areas connected by the Glasboden cable car, forming a single linked resort. The resort is part of the Dolomiti Superski network, which provides access to 12 ski areas including Val Gardena, Alta Badia, Kronplatz, and Cortina d'Ampezzo, though a separate full-area pass is required to ski outside the local area. The local pass covers only the Gitschberg Jochtal slopes.

terrainDescription: The ski terrain at Glacier 3000 is split between a high-altitude glacier plateau and steep descents to the valley. On the summit glacier at 2,971 m, five well-groomed wide runs are served by three long T-bars, offering easy intermediate cruising. Below the plateau, a quad chair gives access to a single prepared red run (the Red Run) that descends 8 km with 1,700 m vertical drop. A 265 m tunnel, opened in 2023, leads to a 3 km black run (the Black Wall) with pitches up to 41 degrees and a 1,000 m+ vertical descent to Col du Pillon at 1,546 m. The Combe d'Audon is a 7 km black run with 1,137 m vertical. All runs are above treeline on the glacier, with no tree cover.
offPisteDescription: Glacier 3000 is renowned for extreme freeride terrain. From the Scex Rouge summit, off-piste descents drop precipitously over the face under the cable car, with cliffs, chutes, and a 102% (46°) pitch on the Black Wall. The off-piste area includes hundreds of acres of steep terrain, often requiring a guide. Lift-accessible sidecountry is available, with natural features like cliffs and kickers. The freeride itineraries are marked and can hold powder for several days after a storm. The terrain is exposed and high-consequence, suitable for expert skiers.
valueDescription: A day lift pass at Glacier 3000 costs CHF 79 (2025) to CHF 89 (2026) for adults, CHF 71 for youth/seniors, and CHF 45–51 for children. A six-day pass is CHF 340, and a season pass is CHF 499. The Magic Pass (CHF 499) includes Glacier 3000 plus many other resorts in the region, such as Villars-Gryon-Diablerets, Leysin, and others. There is no accommodation at the base; visitors stay in Les Diablerets or Gstaad, where lodging ranges from budget hostels to luxury hotels. The cable car round-trip for pedestrians is CHF 89 adult / CHF 45 child, with 50% discounts for Swiss Travel Pass holders.
familyDescription: Childcare facilities at Glacier 3000 are limited (rated 2.0 out of 5). The beginner terrain consists of a few gentle slopes on the glacier, suitable for skiers who already know the basics; first-timers are better served at nearby Villars. The resort has a snowpark and a fun park with sledging, but no dedicated beginner area at the base. Children under 6 travel free on the cable car. Ski schools are available with multilingual instructors (rated 4.0). Accommodation is not at the resort, so families must commute from Les Diablerets or Gstaad via bus or car.
resortCharacterDescription: Glacier 3000 is a high-altitude ski area, not a traditional village resort. The base is at Col du Pillon, a cable car station with a restaurant and free parking. The summit station at Scex Rouge (2,971 m) is a modern glass-and-metal building designed by Swiss architect Mario Botta, featuring a panoramic roof terrace, a self-service restaurant (Le 3), a table-service restaurant (Botta), and a chalet-style eatery (Le Carnotzet). There is no accommodation or nightlife at the resort itself; the nearest villages are Les Diablerets (15 minutes by bus) and Gstaad (30 minutes by bus), which offer traditional Swiss alpine charm.
liftSystemDescription: Access to Glacier 3000 is via a two-stage cable car from Col du Pillon (1,546 m) to the mid-station Cabane des Diablerets, then to the summit Scex Rouge (2,971 m) – total journey 15 minutes. On the glacier, three long T-bars serve the upper runs, and a quad chair serves the red run. A removable 150 m Sunkid Mega Comfort Star surface tow was installed in 2021 to access the Combe d'Audon black run; it can be raised to 5 m for night grooming. A 265 m tunnel through the mountain, opened in 2023, provides access to the Black Wall run. The lift system includes 3 gondolas/cable cars, 3 chairlifts, and 4 surface lifts (total 10 lifts per some sources). The old chairlift shared with foot passengers is slow but uncrowded.
linkedResortsDescription: Glacier 3000 is part of the Magic Pass network, which includes the linked resorts of Les Diablerets, Villars, Gryon, Leysin, Vercorin, Grimentz, Zinal, St-Luc/Chandolin, Leukerbad, Les Marecottes, Nax Mont Noble, Anzère, Saas Fee, and many others. It is also connected to the Gstaad Super Ski Region (Gstaad, Zweisimmen, Saanenmöser) via a free bus or short drive. The same lift pass (Magic Pass) grants access to all these areas, providing a wide variety of terrain and snow conditions.

terrainDescription: The ski area is located on the Zugspitzplatt plateau at approximately 2,600 m elevation, between the Schneefernerkopf (2,874 m), Wetterwandeck (2,698 m), and Zugspitze (2,962 m). The terrain consists of 16.8 km of pistes, with 39% easy (blue) and 61% medium (red) runs, and no black runs. The longest run is 5 km. The slopes are groomed natural snow, and the area is compact, with all runs accessible from a central lift system. The runs face south-east, catching the morning sun.
offPisteDescription: The glacier offers off-piste opportunities on the deep snow slopes between the groomed pistes, particularly on the north-facing aspects. Freeriders can find powder stashes after snowfall, but the terrain is limited in extent and requires awareness of glacial crevasses. Guided off-piste tours are recommended for safety.
valueDescription: A day lift pass costs around €62, with a six-day pass approximately €310. Children aged 6-15 receive a 50% discount, and under-6s ski free. Family discounts are available. The Top Snow Card, which includes access to the Tiroler Zugspitz Arena and Garmisch-Partenkirchen, offers additional value for those wanting to explore a larger area.
