valueDescription: Lift passes are affordable compared to larger Swiss resorts, with a day pass at 54 CHF and a six-day pass at 282 CHF for adults. Children's passes are significantly cheaper at 32 CHF per day. The resort is included in the Magic Pass, which provides access to multiple Swiss ski areas for a single season price, offering excellent value for those planning to ski multiple resorts. Accommodation in the village of Les Marécottes and nearby Salvan tends to be less expensive than in Chamonix or Verbier.
familyDescription: The resort has a magic carpet for beginners, and the ski school offers lessons. The village is small and family-friendly, with an Alpine zoo and a natural swimming pool. The gondola provides easy access to the slopes. However, the terrain is predominantly steep, so families with very young children may find the limited gentle slopes a challenge. The resort is part of the Magic Pass, which can be cost-effective for families.
apresSkiDescription: Apres-ski is low-key and relaxed. The valley station has a bar where skiers gather after the last run. The village offers a few restaurants serving local Valais cuisine, such as La Pinte. For a more lively scene, nearby Martigny or Chamonix are a short drive away. The resort's Alpine zoo and heated swimming pool provide alternative evening entertainment.
resortCharacterDescription: Les Marécottes is a traditional Swiss Alpine village, not a purpose-built resort. It retains a quiet, authentic character with a small church and traditional chalets. The gondola station is in the village center. The resort is popular with locals and offers a relaxed, unhurried atmosphere compared to larger resorts.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of a modern 12-person gondola installed in 2025, which takes skiers from the village to the mid-mountain area. From there, a high-speed quad chairlift (Velarde) ascends to the summit at 2,220m. A magic carpet serves the beginner area. The lifts are efficient and rarely have queues, even on weekends. The lower section from the gondola base to the village is not served by a piste, so skiers must download at the end of the day.
linkedResortsDescription: Les Marécottes is part of the Ski St Bernard area, a group of five small ski areas (Bruson, La Fouly, Champex-Lac, Vicheres-Liddes, and Les Marécottes) offering a combined 100km of pistes served by 17 lifts. However, the areas are not directly lift-linked; they are connected by road or shuttle. The resort is also within easy reach of the larger Verbier 4 Vallées and Portes du Soleil ski areas.

terrainDescription: The terrain is on the Tabe massif at 1500m with a vertical drop of 600m. The 21 runs include wide beginner areas and technical upper slopes. The resort has a snowpark, boarder-cross, and freeride area. Slopes have northern exposure and are supplemented by 80 snow cannons. The Col du Cadène at the summit offers panoramic views of the Ariège valleys.
offPisteDescription: Off-piste terrain is available in the Cresta del Pic de Han area, which holds powder after snowfall. The resort also has a designated freeride area.
valueDescription: A 1-day lift pass costs €29, with a 6-day pass at €147. Ski equipment rental is €100 per week for adults and €50 for children. Accommodation for a studio for two is around €320 per week, and a four-person apartment around €500. The resort is described as a budget-friendly option for weekend trips.
familyDescription: The resort is known as a family destination, with a dedicated children's area, sledge trails, and an ESF ski school. Olympic mogul champion Perrine Laffont learned to ski here. The resort is compact and human-scale, making it easy for families to navigate.
resortCharacterDescription: Les Monts d'Olmes is a small, purpose-built resort created in 1968, financed by local textile industry barons. The settlement consists of individual chalets and a few community facilities, giving it a modest, human-scale character. It is not a traditional alpine village but a functional resort set in a preserved natural environment.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of 11 lifts, primarily surface lifts with one fixed-grip chairlift. The resort has 80 snow cannons covering 40 hectares to supplement natural snow. Lifts are modest and suited to the resort's size.

terrainDescription: The ski area spans from 1,550 m to 2,720 m with a vertical drop of 1,170 m. The terrain is north-west facing, which helps preserve snow quality, and consists of wide, undulating slopes that run through larch forests. The upper mountain offers 360° panoramic views of the Écrins massif and Serre-Ponçon lake. The resort has 100 km of pistes: 36 runs in total (9 green, 5 blue, 17 red, 5 black). The lower slopes are wide and gentle for beginners, while the upper sectors feature steeper red and black runs such as Grand Cabane and Myrtilles. The Bois Méan sector (1,800 m) and the historic centre (1,650 m) are connected by fast lifts.
valueDescription: A day lift pass costs approximately €44–€44.50, which is considered reasonable for the Southern Alps. The resort offers a range of accommodation from self-catering apartments to hotels, with many ski-in/ski-out options. Parking can be expensive and complicated, with paid parking in some areas. The resort has a special 'Premières Traces' pass for beginners that limits access to lower chairlifts at a reduced price. Overall, the resort is seen as good value for families and intermediate skiers compared to larger Alpine resorts.
familyDescription: Les Orres is certified with the Famille Plus label, indicating strong family-friendly facilities. There are two snow gardens, magic carpets (one free access), and dedicated beginner zones above the Prélongis chairlift. The ski schools (ESF and ESI) offer English-speaking group lessons for children from age four. The resort has three village levels (1550, 1650, 1800) connected by free shuttle buses, making it easy for families to move around. Wide blue runs like Edelweiss are ideal for family skiing, and there are two toboggan areas at 1650 and 1800. The resort also offers childcare for children from 36 months, with 8 hours of care per day.
apresSkiDescription: The resort has 16 restaurants and 16 bars/cafes, a cinema, a bowling alley, and a nightclub. The après-ski scene is relaxed, with sunny terraces and local cuisine, though some reviews note that the resort can be noisy at night due to partygoers. There is an outdoor heated pool for relaxation after skiing. The resort also offers activities such as dog sledding, snowmobiling, and paragliding. The atmosphere is more family-oriented than rowdy, with live music at some bars on weekends.
resortCharacterDescription: Les Orres is a purpose-built resort designed in the 1960s as a ski-in/ski-out village. It consists of three levels: Les Orres 1550, 1650, and 1800. The 1650 sector retains a 1970s architectural style with concrete apartment blocks, while the 1800 sector (Bois Méan) features contemporary wood and stone residences that blend into the natural environment. The resort centre is car-free, with free shuttle buses connecting the sectors. It is set in a larch forest with panoramic views of the Serre-Ponçon lake and the Écrins National Park. The atmosphere is relaxed, family-friendly, and unpretentious.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of 17 lifts: 7 chairlifts (including a new 6-seater Pic Vert chairlift installed for the 2025/26 season), 8 drag lifts, and 2 magic carpets. The lifts are modern and efficient, with fast connections between the 1650 and 1800 sectors. Queues are generally manageable except during peak holiday weeks. Some reviews mention that the ridge lifts can be slow. The resort also operates pedestrian lifts for non-skiers to access high-altitude restaurants.
linkedResortsDescription: Les Orres is a standalone ski area not linked to other major resorts. It is within driving distance of nearby resorts such as Crevoux, Vars, and Pra Loup, but there is no interconnected lift system. The resort's ski pass only covers its own slopes.

terrainDescription: The ski area at Les Pléiades is south-facing, ensuring sunny conditions on most days. Slopes are wide and gentle, making them ideal for beginners and families, with a modest vertical drop of 360 metres. The terrain offers views of Lake Geneva and the Savoy Alps, and the resort's small size means uncrowded runs. The slopes are served by a network of surface lifts and one quad chair, covering a compact area between 1,080 and 1,360 metres.
familyDescription: Les Pléiades is specifically designed for families, with a dedicated children's area called the Snowgarden and a ski school offering small-group lessons. The slopes are wide and gentle, providing a safe learning environment for beginners. The resort is accessible by a scenic cog railway from Vevey, eliminating the need for car travel, and ski passes are noted as affordable. Mountain restaurants serve traditional Vaudois dishes in a relaxed, family-friendly atmosphere.
resortCharacterDescription: Les Pléiades is a small, family-oriented ski resort perched on the heights of the Vaud Riviera, offering panoramic views of Lake Geneva and the Alps. It is not a purpose-built station but a natural mountain setting with a relaxed, authentic atmosphere. The resort is accessible by the Train des Pléiades, a scenic railway from Vevey, and is part of the Swisstainable network promoting sustainable tourism. In winter, the area also offers cross-country skiing, snowshoeing, and sledding, while summer brings hiking and mountain biking.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system at Les Pléiades consists of 6 lifts: 1 quad chairlift and 5 surface lifts, according to recent data. The resort is reached from Vevey by a cog railway that takes about 40 minutes, and the lifts provide access to a small ski area with a vertical drop of 360 metres. No information is available on the age or efficiency of the lifts, but the system is adequate for the resort's modest size.

terrainDescription: The Les Portes du Mont-Blanc ski area spans approximately 100 km of pistes across three linked sectors: Combloux, Le Jaillet (Megève), and La Giettaz, with Cordon accessible only by road. The terrain sits between 1,020 m and 1,930 m altitude, with slopes winding through alpine forests and mountain pastures, offering views of the Mont-Blanc massif, Aravis and Fiz ranges. The 61 marked runs break down as 11 green, 14 blue, 28 red, and 8 black, with 52% of the terrain rated easy, 35% intermediate, and 13% advanced. The area includes a boardercross, a snowpark at Torraz, and 18 picnic spots. The Jaillet sector features the Ours des Prés recreational area for families.
offPisteDescription: La Giettaz, at the eastern end of the Portes du Mont-Blanc circuit, is known for its freeride terrain, particularly the Torraz sector which offers a 360-degree panorama of the Aravis chain and powder that stays untracked longer than busier areas. The area includes a dedicated freeride zone and a snowpark at Torraz. There are 12 marked ski touring itineraries across the domain, and the Bureau des Guides in Combloux offers off-piste outings.
valueDescription: Lift passes for the Portes du Mont-Blanc area are priced at €51.50 for an adult day ticket, with a beginner day pass at €17.50 valid on specific beginner lifts. A six-day adult pass costs €257.50. The early-bird season pass is €576, breaking even after about 12 days of skiing. Accommodation in Combloux and La Giettaz is generally more affordable than in Megève, with self-catering apartments and chalets available. The area is considered good value for families and those who ski primarily on the western flank.
familyDescription: The Portes du Mont-Blanc area holds the 'Famille Plus' certification and is designed for families. Ski lessons are available from age 3.5, and crèche/kindergarten facilities accept children from 3 months to 6 years old. Beginner zones are located near the villages, with gentle green runs at Combloux and Le Jaillet, and a dedicated beginner day pass (€17.50) valid on specific lifts. The area includes several play areas for children, a boardercross, and a sledge slope. The relaxed, less crowded atmosphere compared to larger resorts makes it suitable for families with mixed abilities.
resortCharacterDescription: The Portes du Mont-Blanc area comprises three traditional mountain villages: Combloux, known as the 'Pearl of Mont-Blanc' with authentic, natural character and panoramic views; La Giettaz, a typical Savoyard village nestled among rocks and trees; and the Jaillet sector of Megève, which is traditional and elegant. The villages retain their alpine charm with wooden farmhouses, quiet lanes, and a relaxed pace. Cordon is part of the area but not ski-linked.
liftSystemDescription: The Portes du Mont-Blanc ski area is served by 23 lifts, including 2 gondolas, 7 chairlifts, 11 button lifts, and 3 magic carpets. The lifts connect the sectors of Combloux, Le Jaillet, and La Giettaz. Several chairlifts have been adapted for disabled skiers, and 7 button lifts are suitable for uni-ski or dual-ski users. The area also has free shuttles between Combloux and the Jaillet or Cuchet lifts.
linkedResortsDescription: Les Portes du Mont-Blanc is part of the larger Évasion Mont-Blanc ski domain, which covers over 400 km of pistes across seven resorts: Megève, Saint-Gervais, Saint-Nicolas-de-Véroce, Combloux, La Giettaz, Les Contamines-Montjoie, and Hauteluce. However, the ski link within Portes du Mont-Blanc only connects Combloux, Le Jaillet (Megève), and La Giettaz. Access to other Évasion resorts such as Saint-Gervais and Les Contamines requires a bus or car journey (e.g., 20-minute bus from Saint-Gervais to Les Contamines). The Évasion pass is needed to ski the full domain. Additionally, the Mont Blanc Unlimited pass provides access to Chamonix, Les Houches, Courmayeur, and Crans-Montana.

terrainDescription: The terrain is spread across four interconnected massifs: Les Tuffes, La Serra, Le Noirmont, and La Dôle (Switzerland). Les Tuffes offers gentle, well-oriented slopes ideal for beginners and families, while La Serra and Le Noirmont provide more technical descents with better snow conditions due to higher elevation and shelter from wind. The north-facing aspect helps preserve snow quality, and runs meander through spruce forests, valleys, and panoramic ridges. From the summit of La Dôle (1,677m), there are views of Mont Blanc and Lake Geneva. The resort has 33 tracks (7 green, 10 blue, 11 red, 5 black) and 40 modern lifts.
offPisteDescription: Off-piste opportunities exist in the powder spots nestled among the fir trees, particularly in the Le Noirmont sector which is higher and sheltered from the wind. The north-facing aspect and northerly location mean the Jura sometimes receives heavier snowfall than the nearby Alps, providing fresh snow for telemarkers, skiers, and snowboarders. Remote slopes through the pines offer additional off-piste terrain, though avalanche safety should be observed.
valueDescription: Les Rousses is a cheap alternative to Alpine resorts, with a six-day adult lift pass at €200.50 (2025-2026 season) and a day pass at €40.50 for the full Jura sur Léman area. The resort offers good-value family packages and affordable accommodation in chalets, apartments, and hotels. Self-catering options and family-friendly deals keep costs down, and the lift pass covers several other small resorts locally, providing excellent value for budget-conscious skiers.
familyDescription: The resort is family-friendly with four villages (Les Rousses, Bois-d'Amont, Lamoura, Prémanon) offering a warm atmosphere. Beginners' areas and children's play zones are available, with gentle slopes at Les Tuffes ideal for first-timers. Five safe sledging areas are located in the villages and at the foot of Alpine slopes. Ski schools are present, and the resort has a relaxed pace suitable for families with young children. The compact village layout means amenities are within walking distance.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski is low-key and authentic, centered on two main venues: La Bonbonnière and Le Patio, which offer a warm atmosphere with local drinks, platters of Jura cheeses and charcuterie. There are no large, flashy parties; the scene is simple and convivial, reflecting the Jura spirit. The resort also has restaurants serving traditional Savoyard cuisine, and the village centre has tea rooms for a quieter evening.
resortCharacterDescription: Les Rousses is a traditional, authentic Jura mountain resort comprising four villages with stone and wood architecture, cheese dairies, and small local shops. It is not purpose-built but retains a historic character, with the Fort des Rousses (a former military fortress) now used as Comté cheese maturing cellars. The resort was the first in France to receive the Flocon Vert label for sustainable development, emphasizing environmental responsibility. The atmosphere is peaceful and family-friendly, with a strong local identity.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system includes 40 modern lifts, with 2 new chairlifts installed to reach the top of Les Tuffes in 5 minutes. The lifts run on green energy, and the resort has invested in efficient infrastructure. The system connects the four massifs, with a single Franco-Swiss lift pass covering both French and Swiss sectors. Queues are generally short outside peak periods, and the lifts are well-maintained.
linkedResortsDescription: Les Rousses is part of the Jura sur Léman Franco-Swiss ski area, linking the French massifs of Les Tuffes, La Serra, and Le Noirmont with the Swiss massif of La Dôle. A single lift pass allows unlimited skiing across the border, providing over 50 km of Alpine slopes. The linked area also connects to other small resorts locally, and the Nordic ski area extends into Switzerland with over 200 km of cross-country trails.

terrainDescription: The ski area spans six valleys with 136 marked trails. According to the Wikipedia entry, the trail breakdown is 20 green, 40 blue, 31 red, and 5 black runs. The WeSki piste map lists 30 green, 60 blue, 35 red, and 11 black runs, reflecting a more recent count. The longest single run is 4.7 km (2.9 mi). Two snowparks are present, along with 4 freeride tracks and 2 boardercross trails. The terrain is predominantly north-east facing, with most pistes rated for intermediate skiers. The upper mountain above Saint-Sorlin-d'Arves offers wide, flowing pistes with long vertical drops, while lower sectors like Le Corbier and La Toussuire have a mix of gentle beginner slopes and more technical red and black runs.
offPisteDescription: Off-piste terrain is concentrated around the upper mountain of Saint-Sorlin-d'Arves, where big open bowls, snowfields, and steep chutes can be accessed. The backside of the Pointe du Corbier ridge, couloirs on Les Perrons, and the alpine valley between Le Corbier and La Toussuire are noted as promising powder stashes. The resort has 4 designated freeride trails. Local guides offer off-piste adventures, including tracking chamois and marmots. A visitor review on snow-forecast describes Les Sybelles as 'an unbeatable freeride friendly resort at this price point' and 'probably the best off-piste offering in Europe for the money', though conditions depend on snowfall.
valueDescription: A six-day adult lift pass costs around €282, roughly a third less than top-tier French resorts like Chamonix or Val d'Isère where a six-day pass can exceed €450. A single adult day pass is €56.40 (2024/25 season), with a Saturday pass available for €28 when purchased online before Wednesday. Child day passes are €45.60, and children under 5 ski free. The resort markets itself as offering 'unbeatable value for money' with accommodation to suit all budgets, including self-catering apartments and family-run hotels. The six villages provide a total of 43,700 beds, with many package deals available.
familyDescription: The resort is designed with families in mind. La Toussuire has a day nursery and leisure centre, plus ski schools with clubs for children from age 3 (Piou-Piou club) and snow gardens. Saint-Sorlin-d'Arves hosts weekly family events including avalanche dog demonstrations, a Big Air night show, sledding, fireworks, and a torchlight parade. The mascot Sorlinette (a cow) entertains children. Six nursery and nursery clubs operate across the domain. The ski area offers 21 fun zones including Snowpy Mountain, a tubing track, and a zip line. Many pistes are wide and gentle, with separate beginner areas near each village.
apresSkiDescription: Apres-ski is low-key and French-focused. La Toussuire has a bowling alley, cinema, and disco, plus 20 restaurants and 20 bars. Le Corbier offers 8 restaurants and 9 bars. Mountain dining includes Le Saint S'O above Saint-Sorlin, where an accordionist plays on the terrace, and Le Cailin in the Toussuire sector for homemade food. In Saint-Sorlin, Hors Piste bistro serves Japanese-style steak tataki and grilled beef flambéed with eau de vie, while Les Cèdres offers traditional raclette. The Telegraph notes that the lunch menu is overwhelmingly Savoyard (mâchon, matouille, diots de Savoie) and that British visitors are rare, giving the apres-ski an authentic local feel.
resortCharacterDescription: The six villages vary greatly in character. Saint-Sorlin-d'Arves (1,600m) is the most picturesque, with rustic stone chalets, old barns with hay storage, and a lived-in agricultural atmosphere. It retains traditional architecture and a walkable centre. In contrast, Le Corbier and La Toussuire are purpose-built 1960s/70s resorts with large mid-century apartment blocks and stark hotel buildings, described by Powderhounds as 'unsightly high-rise development' and 'simply awful'. Saint-Jean-d'Arves and Les Bottières are smaller and more traditional, while Saint-Colomban-des-Villards is a quiet outpost. The overall feel is authentic and French, with little international glitz.
liftSystemDescription: The lift network comprises 68 lifts: 28 chairlifts (13 detachable), 30 drag lifts, 9 beginner conveyor lifts, and 1 gondola (opened 2023). The new Sybelles Express 6-seat chairlift from Le Corbier reaches Mont Corbier (2,265m) in 8 minutes. A second cable car to the Ouillon summit (2,431m) is planned by end of 2026. The lifts run on 100% renewable electricity. Some fixed-grip chairs and surface lifts remain, particularly in older sectors. A visitor review on snow-forecast criticises 'too many old fashioned fixed chair lifts' and the need to use drags, but notes recent investment in new chairlifts.
linkedResortsDescription: Les Sybelles connects six village resorts into a single ski area: Le Corbier, La Toussuire, Saint-Sorlin-d'Arves, Saint-Jean-d'Arves, Les Bottières, and Saint-Colomban-des-Villards. The domain was created in 2003 by uniting these resorts. The linked area offers 310 km of pistes and 68 lifts, with a central meeting point at L'Ouillon (2,431 m). The resorts are connected by ski lifts and pistes, with inter-resort shuttles running between Les Bottières and La Toussuire, and between Le Corbier and La Toussuire. Future plans exist to link Les Sybelles to the Alpe d'Huez Grand Domaine at Vaujany.

terrainDescription: Les Trois Vallées offers 335 marked trails across three interconnected valleys, with 48 beginner (14%), 135 easy (40%), 119 intermediate (36%), and 33 difficult (10%) runs. The longest single descent is the 12 km Cîme de Caron. Terrain ranges from gentle greens around Méribel's Altiport and Les Menuires' Croisette to long cruising reds linking Val Thorens to Les Menuires, and challenging blacks like Lac Noir and Les Bosses. The predominantly north-facing slopes and high altitude (summit 3,230 m) help preserve snow quality, while 2,300 snow cannons supplement natural snowfall.
offPisteDescription: Off-piste terrain is extensive and largely unregulated, with the guiding principle that 'if you can see it, you can ski it'. Notable zones include the Combe de Caron and glacier couloirs off Pointe de Thorens in Val Thorens, the Mont Vallon bowls near Méribel, and three patrolled Liberty Ride sectors in Les Menuires. The area has significant avalanche and crevasse risk, especially on glacier terrain, making a local guide advisable. Freeriders also use the 90,000 m² snowpark in Val Thorens for jumps and rails.
valueDescription: A 6-day adult lift pass for the entire Les 3 Vallées area costs €409 for the 2025–2026 season, equivalent to roughly $75–$90 per day, which is notably cheaper than comparable US mega-resorts. Accommodation varies widely: Les Menuires and Brides-les-Bains offer the most budget-friendly options, with self-catered apartments and mid-range hotels, while Courchevel and Val Thorens have luxury chalets and ski-in/ski-out hotels. Dining ranges from affordable Savoyard bistros to 10 Michelin-starred restaurants, so overall trip cost can be tailored to any budget.
familyDescription: Several resorts within Les 3 Vallées hold Famille Plus certification, including Méribel and Les Menuires. Méribel's Altiport zone is a dedicated beginner area with carpet lifts and gentle gradients, separated from faster traffic. Les Menuires provides free magic carpets, nursery lifts, kids' clubs from three months old, sledging, and ice skating. Val Thorens has nursery slopes steps from hotels and ESF-run snow gardens. Across the area, 67 blue runs and numerous green runs give beginners safe progression, and ski schools (ESF, New Generation, Magic in Motion) offer English-speaking instruction.
apresSkiDescription: Apres-ski varies by resort. Val Thorens is the liveliest, with on-mountain parties at La Folie Douce, nightclubs like Discothèque le Malaysia (open 11 PM–4 AM), and slope-side bars. Méribel offers a vibrant but more diverse scene at Rond-Point ('The Ronnie'), Jack's Bar, and its own Folie Douce. Les Menuires has relaxed lounge bars such as Chouette and Medz'ery with affordable drinks. Courchevel features upscale cocktail bars and gourmet dining. The area has 10 restaurants with a total of 17 Michelin stars, concentrated in Courchevel and Val Thorens.
resortCharacterDescription: The seven resorts range from purpose-built high-altitude stations to traditional Savoyard villages. Val Thorens (2,300 m) is a compact, pedestrianised purpose-built resort with modern ski-in/ski-out apartments. Les Menuires (1,850 m) features functional 1970s architecture with recent renovations. Courchevel (1,850 m) is a luxury purpose-built resort with six villages. Méribel (1,450 m) blends traditional wooden chalets with modern amenities. Saint-Martin-de-Belleville (1,450 m) is a historic village with a baroque church and stone buildings. Brides-les-Bains (600 m) is a spa town linked by gondola. Orelle (900 m) is a collection of traditional hamlets.
liftSystemDescription: The lift network comprises 183 lifts: 3 cable cars, 37 gondolas, 69 chairlifts, and 74 surface lifts, with a total uphill capacity of 260,000 skiers per hour. Key connections include the 5,122 m 3 Vallées Express gondola linking Orelle to Val Thorens, and the Olympe gondola from Brides-les-Bains to Méribel. The system is modern and efficient, with high-speed detachable chairlifts on main sectors. Snowmaking is supported by 2,300 cannons, and 73 grooming machines maintain the pistes nightly, operated by 160 groomers.
linkedResortsDescription: Les Trois Vallées connects seven ski resorts across three valleys (Saint-Bon, Allues, Belleville) plus a fourth valley (Maurienne) via Orelle, linked since 1996. The resorts are Courchevel (with sub-resorts Le Praz, Courchevel 1550, Moriond 1650, Courchevel 1850), La Tania, Méribel (including Méribel-Mottaret), Brides-les-Bains, Val Thorens, Les Menuires, Saint-Martin-de-Belleville, and Orelle. The entire area is accessible on a single lift pass and offers 600 km of groomed pistes, making it the largest connected ski area in the world linked solely by lifts and slopes.

terrainDescription: The Leukerbad ski area occupies a southeast-facing bowl above the tree line, with views of the Dauberhorn and numerous 4,000m peaks of the Valais and the Rhône valley. It offers 50 km of groomed pistes, comprising 3 km of blue, 20 km of red, and 27 km of black runs, plus 6 km of ski routes. The longest descents are 10–14 km, starting from the top of the Schwalbennest chairlift (2,610 m) and combining multiple runs to the bottom cable car or gondola stations. The terrain is undulating, allowing skiers to vary their line on repeated runs. Tree-lined red and black runs descend from Rinderhütte (2,350 m) to the valley stations at 1,411 m and 1,540 m. Five mountain restaurants are located on the slopes, and the lift system includes a cable car, two gondolas, a high-speed 6-person chairlift, and two T-bar drag lifts.
offPisteDescription: Off-piste skiing is accessible between the groomed runs, particularly in the open bowl above Rinderhütte, where easy terrain lies between pistes. More challenging descents can be reached by booting up from the top of the Rinderhalde T-bar or the Schwalbennest chairlift towards Torrenthorn (2,997 m) and Schaffberg (2,806 m). Patrolled freeride routes (numbers 21 and 22) start from the Schwalbennest chair, traversing skier’s left into chutes and tree-lined descents ending at Torrentalp. Glade skiing is possible between Torrentalp and Flaschen. A guide is recommended for safety when venturing away from the main ski area.
valueDescription: Leukerbad uses dynamic pricing: early bookers, weekday visitors, and low-season skiers pay less. A day ticket for an adult ranges from CHF 45.50 to CHF 65, depending on booking timing. A 6-day adult pass costs approximately CHF 288. Children (6–15 years) pay CHF 27.50–39 per day, and children under 6 ski free. The Snowpark Sportarena is free with the Leukerbad Guest Card. The resort also offers a 'Kids on Ski' programme: children aged 3–5 receive five half-day ski lessons, six days of ski hire and a ski pass, plus seven nights' accommodation in their parents' room, all free when accompanied by two full-paying adults.
familyDescription: The resort has a dedicated children’s area, Snowpark Sportarena Erli, located on the edge of town. It features five beginner lifts (conveyors, a rope tow, and a platter tow) and a Snowli Park for children aged 2½–6. Use of the Snowpark is free with the Leukerbad Guest Card. The 'Kids on Ski' programme provides children aged 3–5 with five half-day ski lessons, six days of ski hire and a ski pass, plus seven nights' accommodation in their parents' room. The official Swiss Snow Sports School offers English-speaking instructors. The main Torrent ski area is not suitable for beginners, but the nursery slopes at Erli are separate and safe from faster traffic.
resortCharacterDescription: Leukerbad is a traditional Alpine village set at 1,411 m in a dramatic amphitheatre of cliffs, with the Daubenhorn rising 1,500 m above the town. It is known as the highest and largest Alpine thermal baths spa resort in Europe, with several thermal bath complexes. The village centre is compact and walkable, with shops, restaurants, and hotels. The ski area is accessed via cable cars from the edge of town, and the village retains a rustic character with traditional Swiss architecture.
liftSystemDescription: The Torrent ski area is served by six lifts: a cable car from Leukerbad to Rinderhütte (1,411 m), two gondolas (Flaschen–Torrentalp and Torrentalp–Rinderhütte), a high-speed 6-person chairlift (Schwalbennest), and two T-bar drag lifts (Kumme and Rinderhalde). Additionally, the Snowpark Sportarena has five beginner lifts (conveyors, rope tow, platter tow). Queues can occur at the cable car and gondola during peak morning times but are generally not severe. The lifts operate from mid-December to early April, with some summer operation for the cable car.

terrainDescription: The ski area offers 60 km of pistes, predominantly blue and red runs, with wide, well-groomed slopes in the Les Fers area. The terrain is best suited to beginner and intermediate skiers, with a terrain park and half-pipe for freestyle. Slopes are marked with SOS points rather than numbers. The resort is linked by ski bus to Les Mosses and La Lécherette, expanding the total to 100 km of pistes.
offPisteDescription: Off-piste skiing offers some variety of routes that can remain untracked for a day or two after a snowfall, according to visitor reviews. There is little dedicated advanced terrain on the piste map, with only a single black run above the terrain park.
valueDescription: An adult day ski pass costs from CHF 68 when purchased online, with children's passes from CHF 25 and junior passes from CHF 37. The resort accepts the Magic Pass, which provides access to multiple areas. Accommodation options include budget hostels and luxury hotels, with a variety of price points. The resort has 18 restaurants and 8 bars.
familyDescription: The resort has excellent child care facilities including a nursery at reasonable prices. A points-based lift pass system allows beginners to do a few runs without buying a full pass. The Vallée Blanche beginners area has a magic carpet and is separate from faster traffic. The slopes are safe and well-patrolled, with school groups often present. The resort is popular with families and school ski courses.
apresSkiDescription: There are 18 restaurants and 8 bars in the resort. The revolving restaurant Kuklos at the top of the Berneuse cable car offers panoramic views of Lake Geneva and the surrounding mountains. Other on-mountain dining options include Refuge de Mayen and Chalet les Fers. The Cable Bar and Restaurant Bar Le Bel Air are at the base. A tobogganing park provides evening entertainment.
resortCharacterDescription: Leysin is a traditional alpine village with a history as a TB sanatorium destination. It is designated as part of the Inventory of Swiss Heritage Sites, with historic chalets and a village center. The resort offers spectacular views across the Rhône Valley towards the Dents du Midi. It is not purpose-built but has grown organically, with a mix of old and new buildings.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of 13 lifts including a cable car (Télécabines la Berneuse), 7 chairlifts, and 4 surface lifts. Most lifts are modern and fast, though the Solepraz and terrain park chairs are slower two-person lifts. Lifts operate from 8:30 to 16:15. The resort is linked to Les Mosses and La Lécherette by a free ski bus running every 30 minutes, with a journey time of 9 minutes.
linkedResortsDescription: Leysin is linked by a free ski bus to the Les Mosses and La Lécherette ski areas, forming a combined ski area of over 100 km of pistes. The bus journey takes about 9 minutes and runs every 30 minutes. The combined area has 23 lifts and 46 trails, with a mix of beginner and intermediate terrain. Les Mosses and La Lécherette have only surface lifts, no chairlifts.

terrainDescription: The Lienzer Bergbahnen comprise two separate ski areas: Zettersfeld and Hochstein. Zettersfeld is a south-facing, sun-drenched plateau with 21 km of groomed pistes, mostly blue and red runs, making it suitable for families and intermediate skiers. The terrain includes a dedicated kids' area with a practice lift (Bidner) and a child-safe 4-seater chairlift (Wartschenbrunn), plus a fun park with beginner and medium lines featuring kickers, rails, and boxes. Hochstein, by contrast, is steeper and more challenging, dominated by red runs and a long black FIS World Cup run that drops from 1,500 m to 670 m. The Hochstein also has a training slope used by international ski teams. The two areas are not lift-linked but are connected by a free ski bus. The overall vertical drop is 1,592 m (base 668 m to summit 2,278 m).
offPisteDescription: Off-piste skiing is officially prohibited in the Lienzer Bergbahnen resort, as noted in visitor reviews. However, the top station of the Zettersfeld cable car serves as a starting point for ski touring routes into the Hohe Tauern National Park, with numerous summit destinations in the Lienz Dolomites. The resort offers guided ski touring tips and recommendations, but lift-accessed off-piste is not permitted.
valueDescription: Lift pass pricing is moderate for the region. A one-day adult ticket costs €70 (high season), with a 50% discount for children aged 6–18 (year of birth 2007–2019) and free riding for children up to 5 years. Multi-day SkiHit passes are available, e.g., a 6-day adult pass costs €362 in peak season, €350 in mid-season, and €334 in low season. The SkiHit pass also grants access to six other ski areas in East Tyrol and Carinthia (Großglockner Resort Kals-Matrei, St. Jakob, Sillian, Obertilliach, Mölltal Glacier, Ankogel). The Kärntner Skipass and Tirol Snow Card are also valid. Free ski buses operate with a guest card, and many partner hotels offer shuttle services. Accommodation ranges from budget hostels to luxury hotels, with a variety of options in the town of Lienz.
familyDescription: Zettersfeld is explicitly marketed as a family-friendly ski area. The kids' area at the mountain station includes the Bidner practice lift on a wide, flat piste, and the Wartschenbrunn 4-seater chairlift provides safe access to the Fun & Race Area with waves, steep turns, and fun figures. Children up to 6 years ski for free, and those aged 6–18 receive a 50% discount on lift passes. The resort has two ski schools and a ski kindergarten. Non-ski activities include a natural toboggan run (2.3 km on Hochstein, floodlit), winter hiking trails, and a family park in summer. The town of Lienz is compact and walkable, with a 15-minute walk from the centre to the Zettersfeld valley station.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski in Lienz is centred on the historic town rather than the slopes. Bars and pubs include Paperlapup, Deep Blue, and Stöcklbar, with approximately 10 bars and 3 clubs in total. The nightlife starts after lift closing. Mountain huts on Zettersfeld and Hochstein offer local Tyrolean dishes and sunny terraces, but the main evening scene is in town. The resort is not known for a rowdy party atmosphere; it is more relaxed and traditional.
resortCharacterDescription: Lienz is a historic town in East Tyrol, not a purpose-built ski resort. It lies in a valley between the Hohe Tauern National Park and the Lienz Dolomites, with over 2,000 hours of sunshine per year, earning it the nickname 'sun city'. The town has a traditional Austrian character with quaint alleyways, a main square, and a mix of accommodation from hotels to guesthouses. The ski areas (Zettersfeld and Hochstein) are located on the mountains above the town, accessible by a short walk or free ski bus. The resort is described as an 'insider tip' away from mass tourism.
liftSystemDescription: The Lienzer Bergbahnen operate a total of 8 lifts across two areas. Zettersfeld has 6 lifts: one gondola (Zettersfeldbahn), three chairlifts (including a 4-seater Steinermandl and a child-safe 4-seater Wartschenbrunn), and two surface lifts (Bidner practice lift and others). Hochstein has additional lifts including the Schlossbergbahn gondola and chairlifts. The lifts operate from 9:00 to 16:00 (winter) and are modern, with a high-speed six-pack chairlift noted on some sources. The two areas are not connected by lift; a free ski bus runs between them. Snowmaking covers 75–100% of pistes (60 snow machines), ensuring reliable conditions.
linkedResortsDescription: The Lienzer Bergbahnen are part of the SkiHit lift pass network, which includes seven ski areas in East Tyrol and Carinthia: Großglockner Resort Kals-Matrei, St. Jakob im Defereggental, Zettersfeld-Lienz, Sillian-Hochpustertal, Obertilliach-Golzentipp, Mölltal Glacier, and Ankogel-Mallnitz. The Kärntner Skipass and Tirol Snow Card are also valid. These areas are not physically lift-linked but are accessible by car or bus within the region. The two local areas, Zettersfeld and Hochstein, are separate mountains connected by a free ski bus.

terrainDescription: The ski area offers over 13 km of groomed slopes, with the longest run being the Jezerní slope at 1.4 km, which provides views of Lake Lipno. The terrain is predominantly easy, with wide blue runs suitable for beginners and families. There is a snow park with three platforms, a straight and bent box, wallride, and bonk, as well as two parallel skicross tracks with a tunnel, banked turns, terrain waves, and jumps. Fox Park is a dedicated children's area with carpet lifts, a ski carousel, and a fairytale slalom. Night skiing is available on the illuminated Jezerní slope from 6 pm to 9 pm daily.
valueDescription: Lift passes are among the cheapest in Europe, with a day pass for an adult at 1,190 CZK (approximately €50) and a six-day pass at 3,490 CZK. A child day pass costs 810 CZK. Accommodation and dining are also budget-friendly, with self-catering apartments and local restaurants offering good value. Ski school lessons for 6 days (20 hours) cost €99. The resort is known for its low prices compared to Alpine resorts.
familyDescription: The resort is designed for families, with Fox Park offering a free children's area with carpet lifts, a ski carousel, and a fairytale slalom. Childcare is available from 36 months for 10 hours a day at €27. The ski school has 200 instructors and offers lessons for children. There are Thursday children's races, afternoon animation programs, and evening sledding under artificial lighting. Family ski passes are discounted on weekends. Additional facilities include a children's carousel, magic carpet, child lift, adventure park, and playground.
apresSkiDescription: The village has 14 restaurants and 10 bars and cafés, with one disco. The Stodola pub near the main cableway offers a window cooking approach and a kids' menu. The apres-ski scene is relaxed rather than rowdy, with a focus on family-friendly dining.
resortCharacterDescription: Lipno is a purpose-built resort developed in the 1950s alongside the Lipno Reservoir. The village is modern and functional, with hotels, restaurants, and ski facilities clustered around the lake. It lacks traditional alpine charm but offers a convenient layout with parking and ski-in/ski-out access.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of 7 lifts, including 3 quad chairlifts and 4 surface lifts. The main chairlift takes skiers to the top of the mountain. The resort has a modern snowmaking system covering 100% of the slopes with 59 snow machines. Night skiing is available on the illuminated Jezerní slope from 6 pm to 9 pm daily.

terrainDescription: Livigno's ski terrain is split across two sides of the valley: Carosello 3000 (including Costaccia) and Mottolino. The Carosello side is generally gentler and more family-friendly, with long cruising runs from the summit and well-maintained pistes. The Mottolino side offers steeper terrain, including a cluster of black runs off Monte Della Neve that take the fall line towards Teola. The majority of the 115 km of pistes are mid-range reds, with 12 black runs, 37 reds, and 29 blues. The vertical drop is about 1,000 m, and the pick of the runs are the long descents from the Carosello sector down to the base area at San Rocco. The slopes are wide and open, with minimal flat sections, and the layout allows skiers to choose between the two sides depending on conditions.
offPisteDescription: Off-piste skiing in Livigno is permitted only in certain areas on the Mottolino side, and only when accompanied by a local guide. The high altitude preserves snow well, and both ski areas offer lift-accessed itinerary routes between the pistes. Heliskiing is popular, with backcountry tours ranging from one-day tasters at €295 per person to six-day excursions. The resort organises weekly safety talks at the Birrificio brewery on Sundays, covering avalanche safety and use of transceiver, probe, and shovel. More serious backcountry options include trips across the Swiss border.
valueDescription: Livigno's duty-free status means that goods such as alcohol, tobacco, and electronics are sold without Italian VAT, making shopping cheaper than in the rest of Italy. However, prices in bars and restaurants are comparable to other Italian resorts, which are still considerably cheaper than France or Switzerland. Accommodation and ski hire are competitively priced, and the overall cost of a ski holiday in Livigno is lower than many comparable Alpine resorts. The duty-free allowance for tourists is the same as for travellers from non-EU countries.
familyDescription: Livigno is suitable for families, with dedicated kids' areas at both Carosello 3000 and Mottolino, including the Lupigno kindergarten for ages 4-8 that combines ski lessons with snow play. The Yepi Fun Park at Carosello 3000 offers a taste of freestyle for younger riders. The village has a long pedestrianised main street with shops, cafés, and activities, and free ski buses connect the different quarters. Families with young children may prefer accommodation near Carosello 3000, which is slightly removed from the livelier nightlife strip. The ski schools offer engaging children's programmes, and Italian restaurants welcome children without fuss.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski in Livigno is centred on a handful of bars at the base of the main lifts, with Stalet on the Carosello side being a popular terrace party spot. The Ice Umbrella Bar at Hotel Concordia offers an Austrian-style après atmosphere. In the village, Daphne's Pub at the southern end, Helvetia, and Kuhstall (with live music) are key venues. For late-night clubbing, Kokod nightclub is the main option, along with Bivio Club and Miky's Disco Club. The scene is lively but spread out along the valley, so the overall ambiance is less concentrated than in some resorts.
resortCharacterDescription: Livigno is a traditional mountain town that has grown from three original hamlets—Santa Maria, San Antonio, and San Rocco—into a linear resort village stretching about 4 km along the valley floor. It retains a lived-in feel with a long main street of shops, cafes, and restaurants, rather than being a purpose-built ski station. The architecture is a mix of traditional stone and wood buildings and modern developments, and the village has a genuine Italian atmosphere. The resort is known as 'Little Tibet' due to its remote, high-altitude location.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system in Livigno consists of 31 lifts, including 6 cableways, 13 chairlifts, and 11 ski lifts. The lifts are modern and efficient, with high-speed gondolas like the Carosello 3000 10-person gondola and the Mottolino 12-person gondola. A free Skilink shuttle service connects the two sides of the valley in about 5 minutes, allowing skiers to easily switch between Carosello 3000 and Mottolino. The lifts operate from late November to early May, with some summer operation for mountain biking.
linkedResortsDescription: Livigno is not directly linked by lifts to other resorts, but day trips to the nearby resorts of Bormio (Italy) and St. Moritz (Switzerland) are possible by road.

terrainDescription: The ski area at Lurisia – Monte Pigna offers 30 km of marked runs (35 km per some sources) spread across 15 pistes, with a composition of 27% beginner (blue), 47% intermediate (red), and 27% expert (black) terrain. The slopes wind through larch woods and provide panoramic descents with views of the Cuneo plain, the Matterhorn, and Monte Rosa on clear days. The exposure is predominantly sunny, keeping slopes bathed in light for most of the day. The longest run is 3 km, and the vertical drop is 922 m (base 836 m, summit 1,758 m). The historic cable car from the 1960s serves as the main access, complemented by four surface lifts.
valueDescription: Lift pass prices are among the lowest in the Piedmont region: a day pass costs €27–€28 for adults and €22 for children. The resort is small and family-run, with limited on-mountain dining and no large commercial developments, keeping overall costs down. Accommodation is available in nearby Roccaforte Mondovì and at the historic Terme di Lurisia, though self-catering options are limited. The resort appeals to budget-conscious skiers seeking a quiet, uncrowded experience away from mass tourism.
familyDescription: The resort has 8 km of novice slopes (27% of total terrain), providing a safe area for beginners to learn. The sunny exposure and gentle gradients on the lower mountain are suitable for families, and the historic cable car offers a nostalgic ride. However, there is no dedicated ski school, childcare, or children’s entertainment mentioned in the sources. The quiet, low-key atmosphere and proximity to the Terme di Lurisia (spa) may appeal to families seeking a relaxed holiday, but facilities for young children are minimal.
resortCharacterDescription: Lurisia is a traditional, small-scale ski station set in the Ligurian Alps, originally developed around the 1960s. The centrepiece is a historic cable car that gives the resort a nostalgic, old-fashioned character. The village itself is a spa town (Terme di Lurisia) with a handful of hotels and restaurants, including the Baita Monte Pigna mountain hut serving local Piedmontese dishes. The atmosphere is quiet and uncrowded, far from the purpose-built concrete resorts of larger areas. The surrounding landscape is naturalistic, with larch forests and views of the Monte Rosa massif.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of 5 lifts: one historic cable car (gondola) and four surface lifts (ski lifts or carpets). The cable car dates from the 1960s and provides access from the base to the upper slopes. The total uplift capacity is modest, reflecting the small scale of the resort. In 2026, the lifts were taken over by a new manager after the previous company’s bankruptcy, ensuring continued operation. The system is not interconnected with any other ski areas, and there are no high-speed detachable chairlifts or modern gondolas.

terrainDescription: The ski area is split into two non-connected sectors: Belvedere and Monte Moro. Belvedere is a lower, tree-lined area with north-east aspect, offering gentle slopes and larch-lined pistes above the mid-station, flattening dramatically below for novices. Monte Moro is a high-altitude, south-facing sector with treeless alpine terrain above the chairlift, providing intermediate and advanced pistes. The Meccia run in Monte Moro offers a 1,700m vertical drop when conditions allow. The terrain is dominated by long, sustained intermediate runs, with 69% of the 32.1km groomed network classified as intermediate. The Belvedere sector has a school camp at the base of the Pecetto chairlift with two conveyor lifts for first-timers.
offPisteDescription: Off-piste skiing is available in both sectors. In Belvedere, larch forests provide tree skiing opportunities. In Monte Moro, long alpine off-piste descents into larch are possible. The glaciers on the Monte Rosa massif attract seasoned mountain travellers, and guides can take skiers over to Saas Fee in Switzerland via ski touring routes. Backcountry options are extensive but generally for the adventurous, with some of the best alpine descents from Monte Rosa ending in the valley above Macugnaga, though these are often accessed from the adjacent Alagna valley.
valueDescription: Lift pass prices are low compared to other Italian resorts: a day ticket for adults costs €30-34, children €22-25, and seniors €28-32. The six-day adult pass is €147. Accommodation in the villages of Staffa and Pecetto is affordable, with a range of hotels and apartments within walking distance of the lifts. The resort is known for being good value, with a ridiculously cheap lift pass price for the vertical drop available.
familyDescription: The Belvedere sector is ideal for families and beginners, with a school camp at the base of the Pecetto chairlift equipped with two conveyor lifts. The Fontanone slope is a 2km easy run from the Alpe Burki mid-station down to the village, suitable for progressing beginners. The slopes are uncrowded, and the village atmosphere is relaxed and safe. The Monte Moro sector also has a few beginner slopes at the top cable car station, but these are only suitable on fine weather days. The resort is considered one of the most family-friendly in Piedmont.
apresSkiDescription: The village of Staffa has a lively centre with a surprising number of restaurants, bars, and a nightlife scene, especially on weekends. There are 7 bars and pubs and 1 club in the resort. Mountain rifugi focus on authentic Piedmontese polenta and local cheeses. The atmosphere is relaxed and traditional, with no high-volume catering. The resort also offers snowshoeing and other non-ski activities.
resortCharacterDescription: Macugnaga is a traditional Walser village with 13th-century stone and wood architecture, located at the foot of the Monte Rosa massif. It consists of two hamlets: Staffa (base of Monte Moro lifts) and Pecetto (base of Belvedere lifts), separated by less than a kilometre. The resort feels like a mountaineering outpost rather than a purpose-built ski station, with a quiet, remote atmosphere. The valley is forested and scenic, and the resort retains a sense of tradition and authenticity.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of 9 lifts: 2 cable cars, 3 double chairlifts, 2 T-bars, and 2 surface lifts. The Monte Moro sector is served by a small 15-person cable car from Staffa to Alpe Bill (1,700m) and a larger 38-person cable car from Alpe Bill to Passo Moro (2,806m). The Belvedere sector uses three slow double chairlifts (Pecetto-Burki, Burki-Belvedere, and others) and two T-bars. The lifts are old, with some dating back to the 1950s, and there are no high-speed gondolas or heated seats. The system is well-maintained but slow, contributing to a quiet, uncrowded mountain experience.
linkedResortsDescription: The Monte Moro sector sits on the Swiss border, and from the top of Passo Moro (2,806m) it is possible to ski tour into the Saastal towards Saas Fee, Switzerland. However, there is no direct lift connection; returning to Italy requires skinning or a guided traverse. The resort also has ski touring links to the Monterosa Ski area via the glaciers, but these are not lift-served.

terrainDescription: The ski area is split into two sectors: Belvedere and Monte Moro. Belvedere, accessed by chairlift from Pecetto, offers 550m of vertical on north-east aspect, tree-lined slopes with larch forests, and a flat lower section ideal for novices. Monte Moro, accessed by a 15-person cable car from Staffa to Alpe Bill (1,700m), then a modern cable car and chairlift, provides 1,100m of vertical on sunny south-facing slopes with treeless high alpine terrain above the top chairlift and intermediate to advanced pistes. The Belvedere sector has a school field with two conveyor lifts at the base. Monte Moro has two short surface tows near the top cable car station for beginners on fine days. There is no valley trail from Monte Moro back to the village.
offPisteDescription: Off-piste terrain is available in both sectors. In Belvedere, larch forests provide tree skiing opportunities. In Monte Moro, long alpine off-piste descents into larch forests are possible. Backcountry options are extensive, with guided heli-skiing to various peaks, ski touring, and ski mountaineering available. Mountain guides can take skiers over the Monte Moro Pass into Saas Fee, Switzerland. The area is known for freeride potential, though the most challenging descents from the Monte Rosa massif are typically accessed from the adjacent Alagna valley.
valueDescription: Lift pass prices are low compared to major Alpine resorts: a day ticket for adults costs €30–34, for children €22–25, and for seniors €28–32. Six-day passes are around €147. Accommodation in the villages of Staffa and Pecetto is affordable, with a range of hotels and apartments within walking distance of the lifts. Mountain restaurants serve reasonably priced local food. The low lift pass price reflects the older lift infrastructure and limited piste length.
familyDescription: The Belvedere sector is well-suited for beginners and families. It has a dedicated school field at the base with two conveyor lifts, and the Fontanone slope (2 km, easy) runs from the Alpe Burki mid-station down to the village, providing a safe learning environment. The slopes are uncrowded, and the village is quiet. Ski schools are available, and the area offers a snowpark and cross-country ski trails. The Monte Moro sector has limited beginner terrain, only two short surface tows at the top, which are weather-dependent.
apresSkiDescription: The village of Staffa has a lively restaurant, bar, and nightlife scene, particularly on weekends, with 7 bars and pubs and 1 club reported. The atmosphere is relaxed and traditional, with local eateries serving Italian cuisine. The resort is not a party destination but offers a social scene for those who want it. Mountain rifugi provide lunch stops with local food and drinks.
resortCharacterDescription: Macugnaga is a traditional, unpretentious Italian ski resort set in the Valle Anzasca at the foot of Monte Rosa's east face. The commune consists of two villages: Staffa (1,327m) and Pecetto (1,369m), separated by less than a kilometre, with a historic 'dorf' of traditional Walser buildings dating from the 16th century. The resort is not purpose-built; it retains an ancient village character with narrow streets and traditional architecture. The setting is remote and scenic, with views of glaciers and the Himalayan Wall of Monte Rosa.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of 9 lifts (2 cable cars, 3 chairlifts, 2 ski lifts, 2 conveyor belts) across two sectors. The infrastructure is old: the cable car from Staffa to Alpe Bill has a capacity of only 15 people and can cause queues. Chairlifts are slow, with no high-speed or heated-seat options. However, all lifts are well-maintained. The Monte Moro sector uses a modern cable car from Alpe Bill to Passo Moro (2,800m) and a chairlift. The Belvedere sector uses two-seater chairlifts. A free skibus connects the two base areas.
linkedResortsDescription: Macugnaga is not part of a large interconnected ski area. The Monte Moro Pass (2,853m) provides a hiking/skinning route to Saas-Almagell in Switzerland, and guided tours can cross into Saas Fee. The resort is separate from the Monterosa Ski area (linking Champoluc, Gressoney, and Alagna), though those resorts are nearby in adjacent valleys. There is no lift connection to other resorts.

terrainDescription: Madrisa offers a mix of blue, red and black pistes, plus an ungroomed ski route and ski-touring opportunities. Half the slopes face south, making the lower section of the black run to Klosters Dorf (1124m) frequently closed or slushy; the east-facing slopes on Rätschenjoch and Schafcalanda hold snow better. The terrain includes long descents suitable for intermediate and advanced skiers.
familyDescription: Madrisa is marketed as the family mountain in Klosters, with 31 km of pistes and a dedicated beginners' area at the top of the Madrisabahn lift featuring children's snow parks and magic carpets. The ski area includes Madrisa Land, an adventure park designed for children with physical and visual impairments, offering a ball run, bouncy castle, slides, water games and swings. A height-adjustable chairlift allows children and people with disabilities to safely use the lift system. Nursery slopes are sunny and high enough for reliable snow, and ski lessons are available from official Swiss ski schools.
liftSystemDescription: Madrisa has 10 ski lifts, including a height-adjustable chairlift for accessibility. The main access is via the Madrisabahn from Klosters Dorf. There is one terrain park. The lift system is modern but the area is small, with only three main lifts serving the upper mountain.
linkedResortsDescription: Madrisa is one of six sectors in the Davos Klosters Mountains ski area, which also includes Parsenn, Jakobshorn, Rinerhorn, Pischa and Schatzalp/Strela. The sectors are not fully interconnected by lifts; Parsenn is the only area that physically connects Davos and Klosters. To move between sectors, skiers use free bus and train services (included with the regional lift pass). The Rhaetian Railway links Klosters and Davos in about 20 minutes, and local buses connect the valley stations. This setup provides access to over 300 km of pistes across the region.

terrainDescription: The Malbun ski area offers 23 km of pistes, with 48% easy (blue), 39% intermediate (red), and 13% advanced (black) runs. The terrain is dominated by gentle, wide blue slopes suitable for beginners and intermediates, with two black runs on the Hochegg chairlift for advanced skiers. The slopes face predominantly north (44%) and west (38%), with only 4% south-facing, meaning conditions can become shady early in the afternoon. The beginner area at Schneeflucht and the Malbi-Park in the village provide gentle learning zones with magic carpets.
valueDescription: Lift passes are reasonably priced: an adult day pass costs 52–55 CHF, a child day pass 30 CHF, and children born after 2018 ski free. The WELCOME Adventure Pass for overnight guests includes free public transport and discounts, while the optional ALL INCLUSIVE Adventure Pass covers chairlifts in Malbun, museums, and activities like ice skating. Accommodation is family-focused with ski-in/ski-out options, and the resort is considered good value compared to larger Alpine resorts.
familyDescription: Malbun is certified as a 'Family Destination' and is designed for families. The Malbi-Park beginner area in the village has magic carpets and a ski carousel, and the ski school offers lessons for children. Child care facilities are available. The village is traffic-free, and most hotels are ski-in/ski-out, including the family-focused JUFA Hotel with playrooms and supervised care. Off-slope activities include an ice rink, sledging, a winter experience trail, and llama walks. The resort is compact and uncrowded, making it easy to navigate with children.
apresSkiDescription: Apres-ski is very quiet. There are a few bars and restaurants in the village, but no large nightlife scene. The resort is known for its calm, family-oriented atmosphere, with evenings typically spent at hotel restaurants or enjoying fondue at mountain huts. The ice rink is floodlit in the evening for skating.
resortCharacterDescription: Malbun is a small, traditional alpine village nestled in a valley basin at 1,600 m. It is traffic-free and compact, with hotels and restaurants clustered around the slopes. The resort has a cosy, uncrowded feel, far from mass tourism, and is surrounded by mountain scenery. The architecture is typical alpine, with chalet-style buildings.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of three modern chairlifts, one of which has heated seats and bubbles, and a double ski lift. The lifts serve the 23 km of pistes and have a capacity of 6,000 persons per hour (according to skiweather.eu). The village has a magic carpet in the Malbi-Park beginner area. Lifts are rarely crowded due to the small size of the resort.

terrainDescription: The Maniva ski area spans 40 km of marked pistes across two zones: Passo Maniva and Dasdana. The terrain includes 18 runs: 1 black, 6 red, and 8 blue. Slopes wind through pine and fir forests and open snowy spaces, with altitudes ranging from 1,450 m at the base to 2,088 m at the summit. The longest run is 4.5 km.
familyDescription: Maniva has a dedicated beginner area with wide, safe slopes served by conveyor belts, suitable for children and first-time skiers. Three ski schools offer lessons for children and adults, and there is a separate bobsleigh and sled track with its own lift. Accommodation options include apartment rentals and hotels with family rooms, and some offer a 50% discount on skipasses for guests.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski is relaxed, with a few bars and restaurants near the slopes. Notable venues include Bar Ristorante Maniva, Rifugio Cima Rest, Igloo, and Chalet Dasdana, which offers panoramic views at 2,000 m. The scene is low-key, focused on drinks and socializing after skiing.
resortCharacterDescription: Maniva is a traditional mountain resort set in the Prealps, 50 km from Brescia. Accommodation consists of wooden chalets and rustic inns, such as Chalet Maniva and Locanda Bonardi. The area remains relatively untouched by mass tourism, with forested surroundings and natural landscapes.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system comprises 9 lifts: 3 chairlifts (including one quad and two doubles), 4 conveyor belts, and 2 ski lifts. The Persole-Dasdana chairlift, installed recently, reaches 2,088 m. Lifts are described as modern and efficient.

terrainDescription: The ski area offers 8 pistes totaling 11.5 km, with 7 km easy (blue), 3 km medium (red), and 1.5 km hard (black). The longest run is 2.5 km. The terrain is arranged in a star shape from the summit, with wide pistes. The Zuckerwiese in St. Sebastian is a dedicated beginner area with a practice lift. The only black piste is the FIS I north-west run, which descends under the 4-seater chairlift. The red family run provides intermediate terrain.
valueDescription: A day lift ticket costs €53 for adults and €28 for children. A 6-day pass is €246.50 for adults and €128.50 for children. Children up to 6 years ski free. Family discounts and guest passes are available. The resort is considered good value for families and beginners.
familyDescription: The resort is marketed as a family ski area. There is a children's ski school with a magic carpet and rope lift. The Zuckerwiese in St. Sebastian is a dedicated beginner slope with a practice lift. A 2.5 km toboggan run from the summit to the valley station offers evening tobogganing. Basti's Slope provides a slalom course with timekeeping and video recording. The resort also has a fun park for snowboarders. School ski courses are well catered for.
apresSkiDescription: Apres-ski is low-key. The main evening activity is the 2.5 km toboggan run, which is lit for night tobogganing. During the 'Magic of Lights' period, the lift operates until 8 PM and the mountain inn serves hot food until 7:15 PM. On Fridays, a touring ski evening allows skiing until 8:30 PM. Other winter activities include curling, ice skating, and horse-drawn sleigh rides in Mariazell.
resortCharacterDescription: Mariazell is a historic pilgrimage town with a basilica and traditional architecture. The Bürgeralpe ski area is a small, family-oriented mountain above the town, with a new gondola from the town center. The resort has a traditional, low-key character rather than a purpose-built feel. The town offers shopping, dining, and cultural attractions.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of the new 8-person gondola 'Bürgeralpe Express' from the town center, two 4-person chairlifts (North-West and Hofstatt), a practice lift (platter lift) at St. Sebastian, and a 4-seater chairlift from St. Sebastian. The lifts are modern, with the gondola being a recent investment. The total lift capacity is 4,067 people per hour. Snowmaking covers the pistes.
linkedResortsDescription: The Mariazeller Bürgeralpe is linked with the Gemeindealpe Mitterbach and Annaberg ski areas under the Mariazeller Land ski region. A combined ski pass is available. It is also part of the Bergerlebnispass, Ostalpen-Card, and Mur-Mürz Top Skipass, and offers a combination ticket with thermal baths (Steiermark Joker).

terrainDescription: The Marmolada ski area consists of a single long red run, the Bellunese (#60), descending 1,823 vertical metres from Punta Rocca at 3,269m to Malga Ciapela at 1,446m. A black variant, Sall del Mul (#65), branches off at mid-mountain. The run is north-facing, holding snow well, and offers panoramic views of the Dolomites. There are also 2 blue runs and 4 red runs according to official counts, but the glacier terrain is dominated by the long, sustained descent. No beginner runs exist on the glacier itself.
liftSystemDescription: The Marmolada ski area is served by a three-stage cable car from Malga Ciapela to Punta Rocca, with intermediate stations at Banc (2,362m) and Serauta (2,944m). The cable car cabins are cramped and can have queues of up to 30 minutes in the morning. Additional lifts include the Padon chairlift connecting to Passo Padon and a drag lift near the glacier. Total lift count is 5, all modern but with limited capacity.
linkedResortsDescription: Marmolada is part of the Dolomiti Superski network and is directly linked to the Arabba ski area via the Passo Padon chairlift. Through Arabba, skiers can access the Sellaronda circuit, which connects to Alta Badia, Val di Fassa, and Val Gardena, providing over 500km of lift-linked slopes. The Grande Guerra circuit, using a bus connection from Malga Ciapela to Alleghe, links Marmolada to Ski Civetta and Cortina d'Ampezzo. All connections are covered by the Dolomiti Superski pass except the bus and a horse tow near Armentarola.

terrainDescription: The terrain is tree-lined with views of Mont Blanc, offering a mix of green, blue, red, and black runs. The resort has 4 green, 5 blue, 8 red, and 3 black pistes. The beginner area is extensive with progression lifts, and the longest run is 4 km. The slopes are in forest and alpine pastures, with a majority of easy runs suitable for beginners and intermediates.
valueDescription: Lift passes are affordable, with an adult day pass at €32 and a six-day pass around €158-162. The resort also offers a season pass for €390 and participates in the Magic Pass for access to many resorts. Accommodation in the surrounding villages is generally budget-friendly. The beginner area has reduced rates for carpet lifts.
familyDescription: The resort is family-oriented with a dedicated beginner area and progression lifts. A free shuttle runs between parking areas and the slopes. There are sledding pistes and a nursery area. The ski school offers lessons for all ages. The village atmosphere is calm and safe for children, with traffic-free zones in the base areas.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski options are very limited, with only a few restaurants and bars in the surrounding villages. The resort is quiet in the evenings, suitable for families seeking a relaxed atmosphere.
resortCharacterDescription: The resort is a collection of traditional Savoyard villages (Bogève, Onnion, Saint-Jeoire, Viuz-en-Sallaz) with chalet-style architecture and renovated farms. The base areas are small and integrated into the landscape, offering a rustic, authentic alpine feel rather than a purpose-built station.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of 12 lifts: 2 magic carpets, 2 chairlifts, and 8 surface lifts (téléskis). The lifts are aging, with an average age of 42 years, but they provide adequate access to the terrain. Queues are generally short due to low crowds.

terrainDescription: The terrain at Matthias-Schmidt-Berg is compact with runs arranged in parallel, allowing skiers of different abilities to ski together. The longest run is 1.5 km, and night skiing is available on a 3 km floodlit run. Snowmaking covers all slopes, ensuring consistent conditions. The difficulty split is 33% easy, 25% intermediate, and 42% advanced, with the advanced runs being steep and challenging.
valueDescription: Lift passes are priced at €30 for a one-day adult ticket, €27 for youth, and €17 for children. Multi-day passes offer savings: a two-day adult pass is €49, and a five-day pass is €95. Ski rental for six days starts at around €17 (120 DKK). A family discount of €2 per child applies for families with two adults and at least one child. The resort is considered budget-friendly, especially for visitors from northern Germany and Denmark.
familyDescription: The resort is well-suited for families, with easy blue runs for beginners and a dedicated children's area. A summer toboggan run and a snowtubing facility provide additional fun. Two ski schools offer lessons, and the resort is described as family-friendly with helpful staff. The compact layout means families can stay together easily. The resort also offers a floodlit toboggan run and winter walking paths.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski is low-key, with a few mountain restaurants such as Matthiasbaude offering local Harz cuisine. The resort focuses on daytime activities like tobogganing and tubing rather than nightlife. Evening entertainment is limited to dining and possibly the floodlit night skiing. There are no nightclubs or bars in the immediate ski area.
resortCharacterDescription: St. Andreasberg is a historic mountain town with narrow, winding streets, situated at about 900 m elevation. The Matthias-Schmidt-Berg ski area is located on a natural hill above the town, with a modern double chairlift and a mountain inn at the top. The resort has a traditional alpine character rather than a purpose-built feel. The town offers a range of accommodation and dining options.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of three lifts: two double chairlifts and one T-bar. The main chairlift is modern and efficient, with a capacity of 4000 persons per hour. Queues are rare due to the compact size. Night skiing is possible on a 3 km floodlit run. Snowmaking covers all slopes. The lifts operate from 09:00 to 16:15 (or 17:15 on some days), with Monday and Tuesday as rest days.
linkedResortsDescription: Matthias-Schmidt-Berg is part of the Harz lift pass, which also includes the nearby Sonnenberg ski area. Sonnenberg offers additional slopes with four lifts and easy to intermediate terrain up to 870 m. The two areas are connected by the lift pass and offer a combined total of around 6 km of slopes. Additionally, cross-country ski trails connect to Altenau and Torfhaus.

terrainDescription: The Méaudre ski area is set in forested terrain with wide, gentle slopes. The 10 pistes range from green to red, with no black runs. The Gonçon and Forêt chairlifts open onto broad tracks through the woods. The red runs Gélinottes and Gamuza offer a boulevard-like descent with 600 metres of vertical drop, while the blue runs Ecureuils and Chevreuil merge for intermediate cruising. The beginner area is at village level with gentle lifts and a dedicated nursery slope.
offPisteDescription: Off-piste skiing at Méaudre is limited. There is one marked off-piste route called the Cerf route, which runs from the top of the domain to the base of the Forêt chairlift. A boardercross zone is located on the blue Gélinote and Chevreuil trails. The surrounding Vercors massif offers potential for off-piste, but it is not patrolled or marked. Most skiers stay on the groomed runs.
valueDescription: Méaudre is one of the more affordable ski resorts in the French Alps. An adult day lift pass costs €23.50, a six-day pass €127.80, and a week pass €149.20. Accommodation is budget-friendly: a studio for two people costs around €420 per week, and a family apartment for four is €600 per week. Equipment rental is €90 per day for adults and €40 for children. The resort offers good value for families and beginners.
familyDescription: Méaudre is well-suited for families. The resort has a dedicated beginner area at village level with gentle lifts and green runs. The ESF runs a children's ski school with group lessons. A kids' club (Halte-Garderie les Bout'choux) welcomes children from 3 months to 3 years old. The resort also has a boardercross zone and a giant zip line (Zipline Vercors). A free shuttle bus connects Méaudre to Autrans, giving families access to more terrain. The village centre is traffic-free, making it safe for children.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski in Méaudre is low-key. The village has 8 restaurants and 4 bars/cafes. Options include L'Auberge Le Sabot de Vénus for regional cuisine, La Cabane du Goncon for traditional dishes, and Le Grizzly for family dining. There is no nightclub or cinema. Evening activities include snowshoeing, dog sledding, and the Zipline Vercors. The atmosphere is relaxed and family-oriented.
resortCharacterDescription: Méaudre is a traditional, authentic Alpine village. It features slate roofs, old brick houses, and a baroque church in the centre. The village is located within the Vercors Regional Nature Park. It is not a purpose-built resort; it has a historic character with a village square and local shops. The resort is part of the commune of Autrans-Méaudre-en-Vercors, formed in 2016. The atmosphere is quiet and peaceful, attracting those seeking a calm mountain experience.
liftSystemDescription: The Méaudre lift system consists of 2 quad chairlifts and 2 T-bars, serving the 10 pistes. The chairlifts are the Gonçon and Forêt, both four-person detachable lifts. There are 26 snow cannons covering 7 hectares to supplement natural snow. The lifts operate from 9am to 5pm. The system is small but efficient, with minimal queues. A free shuttle bus connects to the Autrans ski area.
linkedResortsDescription: Méaudre is linked to the nearby Autrans ski area via a free shuttle bus that runs between the villages. The two areas are not connected by lifts, but a single lift pass gives access to both. The combined Autrans-Méaudre ski area offers 35 trails (including 3 black, 8 red, 9 blue, 11 green) across two separate domains. Autrans has 18km of pistes, while Méaudre has 10km. The shuttle makes it easy to explore both areas in one day.

terrainDescription: Mayrhofen's ski area is split across six distinct sectors: Penken, Horberg, Finkenberg, Ahorn, Rastkogel, and Eggalm. The Penken sector features wide, rolling runs that flow naturally on a board, including the Harakiri, Austria's steepest groomed piste at a 78% gradient. The Ahorn sector is a dedicated beginner and family mountain with gentle slopes and the Albert Adler tour, a series of winding blue runs offering 500–600 metres of vertical. The Rastkogel and Eggalm sectors offer long, open red runs with views towards Hintertux. The Horberg sector includes the Mountopolis Race Track, a maintained speed course used by professional teams. 89% of the skiable terrain sits above 1,700 metres, ensuring consistent snow quality.
offPisteDescription: Off-piste terrain in Mayrhofen is unmanaged and not patrolled, so riders must check avalanche bulletins and consider hiring a local guide. After a storm, the bowls above Rastkogel and the steeper aspects on Penken hold powder well. The trees under the Lärchwald quad provide good visibility during snowfall. The terrain between marked runs opens up considerably after fresh snow, with the flanks of Teufelsritt and the area under the Schneekar six-seater offering sustained pitches. Austrian off-piste culture requires caution, as stepping off a marked run enters unmanaged terrain.
valueDescription: The Zillertal Superskipass is automatically included on any pass of two days or more, covering all four major ski areas in the valley: Mayrhofen, Hintertux Glacier, Hochzillertal-Hochfügen, and the Zillertal Arena, totalling 548 kilometres of piste. Free ski buses connect all areas. Mountain lunch and beer are notably more affordable than in French resorts, and early January and late March offer lower accommodation prices. The resort's compact layout means most accommodation is within walking distance of lifts, reducing transport costs.
familyDescription: The Ahorn sector is a dedicated beginner and family mountain, reached by Austria's largest aerial tram (160 passengers per cabin).
