Ski touring from the Schilthorn summit offers extensive potential but requires a guide due to glaciated and committing terrain. Established off-piste itineraries and heliskiing are available. The Inferno race course (14.9 km from Schilthorn to Lauterbrunnen) is steep and sustained, skiable outside race days. Guiding is strongly recommended.
valueDescription: A day lift pass costs SFr. 83 for adults, SFr. 45 for youth, and SFr. 38 for children (approx. €91, €49, €41). Children under 16 ski for free every Saturday, and three free children's day tickets are included with an adult day ticket. The Jungfrau Ski Region pass covers 206 km of pistes across three resorts. Accommodation includes 2,000 hotel beds in the car-free village, with self-catering options available. Access requires cable car or train tickets from the valley.
familyDescription: The car-free village provides a safe environment for families. The sports centre includes a 25 m swimming pool, ice skating rink, curling rink, and tennis courts (summer). The Allmendhubel area has a dedicated kids' slope (Lilly's magic Kidsslope) with playful features. Toboggan runs from Mürren to Gimmelwald and from Allmendhubel are popular. Ski school offers lessons for children. The village has a Coop supermarket and several hotels.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski is relaxed and low-key, centred on hotel stubli bars and cafés. The Gondel Bar is popular for after-ski drinks, the Tächi Bar at Hotel Eiger has live music, and Bliemli Chäller stays open late. There is no loud nightclub scene; the atmosphere suits families and those seeking a quiet evening. The village has 14 restaurants and 14 bars.
resortCharacterDescription: Mürren is a traditional Walser mountain village perched on a cliff at 1,638 m, car-free and accessible only by cable car and train. The village features traditional wooden chalets, a main street with a Coop supermarket and sports shops, and unobstructed views of the Eiger, Mönch, and Jungfrau. It has a year-round population of 450 and 2,000 hotel beds. The atmosphere is tranquil and unspoilt, with no through traffic.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system has been modernised with the SCHILTHORNBAHN 20XX project (completed April 2026), replacing the main cable cars with four Funifor lifts that are faster, higher capacity, and wind-resistant. The new direct cable car from Stechelberg to Mürren (opened December 2024) is the world's steepest aerial tramway. The rest of the network includes older fixed-grip double chairs, a funicular to Allmendhubel, and a narrow-gauge railway from Grütschalp. Total lift count in the Schilthorn-Mürren area is 17.
linkedResortsDescription: Mürren-Schilthorn is part of the Jungfrau Ski Region, which also includes Grindelwald and Wengen. While Mürren is not directly lift-linked to the other resorts, the same ski pass covers all three areas. Skiers can travel between them via train and cable car through Lauterbrunnen. The linked area totals 206 km of pistes served by 44 lifts. The pass also includes the cog railway connections and cable cars needed to access the slopes.

terrainDescription: The resort comprises three connected domains: Refuge (westernmost, gentle slopes, family-friendly, requires a separate pass add-on), Jonction (central hub with most lifts, rentals, and food, known for crowding), and Wardeh (eastern expansion with wide-open bowls that hold snow longer). The terrain is roughly 30% beginner, 40% intermediate, 25% advanced, and 5% expert across 42 marked runs. Intermediate reds from Jabal Dib offer about 400 m of sustained vertical with width for full-radius turns. The pitch off the 2,465 m Mzaar summit is steep but short. Side-country between marked runs, especially in the Wardeh basin and around the Nabil lift, provides accessible freeride lines back onto the trail network.
offPisteDescription: The main off-piste draw is the side-country between marked runs, particularly in the Wardeh basin and around the Nabil lift, where skiers can find accessible freeride lines that rejoin the trail network. Avalanche control is not systematic; there is no end-of-day patrol sweep and no guarantee of search in a slide. Hiring a local guide is recommended for off-piste skiing. The snow is heavy maritime snow, prone to freeze-thaw cycles, with a skiable window typically from 10 a.m. to 1 p.m. when the surface softens from ice to corn before turning to slush.
valueDescription: A full-domain adult lift pass costs approximately $50 on weekdays and $75 on weekends and holidays for the 2025–26 season. A beginner-only Domaine du Soleil pass starts at $25 weekday and $40 weekend; adding Refuge brings it to roughly $35 and $50. Half-day passes (after noon) are $40 weekday and $55 weekend. Online advance purchase typically saves $10–$20 per ticket. Ski rental from highway shops between Feitroun and Faraya costs $15–$25 per day for a full set (skis, boots, poles), while resort-base rentals run $20–$40 per day. Accommodation ranges from the InterContinental Mzaar Mountain Resort & Spa at $250/night to the San Antonio Hotel at $90/night, with Airbnb chalets often offering the best value.
familyDescription: The Refuge domain is gated and calmer, with gradients of 10–15% and a proper ski-school structure, making it suitable for first-day skiers. The Jonction area has a 'Baby 1' lift for beginners but is also used by sledders and day-trippers. A dedicated Kids Ski Park at Warde is supervised by trained instructors. Ski school instructors speak multiple languages. The resort offers family-friendly activities such as ice skating, sledding, and snow tubing. Accommodation options include ski-in/ski-out hotels and chalets within walking distance of lifts.
apresSkiDescription: The resort has 9 restaurants and 9 bars. A sunset skiing experience is offered: a 1-hour trip to the top of Mzaar with wine, cheese, and charcuterie, followed by a private ski down or descent by dameuse. The après-ski scene is lively, with bars staying active after sunset and a social atmosphere where locals and visitors mingle. Traditional Lebanese dishes are available in village restaurants, and cozy cafés serve warm drinks during winter.
resortCharacterDescription: Mzaar Kfardebian is a developed ski resort that grew from a small beginning in 1960 when the first ski lift was installed and four chalets were built. It is now the largest ski resort in the Middle East, located in the Mount Lebanon range about one hour from Beirut. The resort has a mix of modern ski-in/ski-out hotels, traditional alpine-style chalets, and design-forward lodges. The base area at Jonction is the central hub with lifts, rentals, and food, while the surrounding village of Faraya offers additional restaurants and services. The resort is purpose-built but integrated into the mountain landscape.
liftSystemDescription: The resort operates about 20 lifts, a mix of modern detachable quad chairlifts and older fixed-grip doubles and triples. The Jonction base is a notorious bottleneck, with weekend wait times at the main quads regularly reaching 20 minutes between 11 a.m. and 1 p.m. The Wardeh domain sometimes closes on weekdays to save on diesel. Lift operating hours are typically 8 a.m. to 3:30 p.m. on weekdays and until 4 p.m. on weekends. Some lifts are reported to be in poor condition, and operational issues such as late openings and early closures have been noted by visitors.
linkedResortsDescription: Mzaar Kfardebian is the largest ski resort in Lebanon and is not physically linked to other resorts by lifts. However, it is part of a network of ski areas in the Mount Lebanon range, including Laqlouq (13 lifts, 9 km away), The Cedars (7 lifts, 33 km away), Zaarour Club (6 lifts, 8 km away), and Mount Hermon (10 lifts, 76 km away). These resorts are separate but visible from Mzaar's upper slopes on clear days. The Faqra ski resort is also under the same municipality of Kfardebian but is a distinct area.

terrainDescription: The terrain at Nara is set in a wide, sunny bowl above Leontica in the Blenio Valley, with slopes ranging from 1,400 m to 2,200 m. The resort offers 30 km of pistes, including one blue run, several red runs, and a green sledging run. The slopes are wide and open, with sections through conifer forests, and the longest run is 6 km. A snow park with jumps and rails adds variety. The terrain is suitable for beginners to advanced skiers, with gentle descents for learners and more challenging red runs for intermediates.
valueDescription: A full-day lift pass costs 45 CHF for adults and 33 CHF for children, with half-day options available. The resort offers discounted rates for families and groups, and holders of the Ticino Ticket receive a further reduction. Free parking is available at the base. Accommodation in the nearby village of Leontica includes self-catering apartments and small hotels, with prices generally lower than larger Swiss resorts.
familyDescription: Nara is well-suited for families, with a dedicated 5 km sledging run from Pian Nara to Cancorì, one of the longest in Ticino. The Swiss Ski School offers lessons for children and adults, and the snow park provides additional entertainment. The resort is compact and easy to navigate, with free parking and several mountain restaurants at different altitudes, including Bar Pela and La Pernice. The slopes include gentle beginner areas and a green run for sledging.
apresSkiDescription: The apres-ski scene is relaxed and low-key, centered around several mountain bars and restaurants at various altitudes, such as Nara Beach Sk Bar at 2,120 m, Bar Pela at 1,760 m, and La Giunta Aprez Sci at 1,540 m. These offer drinks, snacks, and local cuisine. There is no lively nightlife; evenings are quiet, with a focus on family-friendly dining.
resortCharacterDescription: Nara is a small, community-run ski area managed by the Amici del Nara SA group, with over 600 shareholders operating on a voluntary basis. The resort is set in a sunny bowl above the traditional village of Leontica in the Blenio Valley, offering a rustic and authentic alpine atmosphere. It is not a large purpose-built resort but a local winter sports area with free parking, traditional mountain restaurants, and a loyal following.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of 7 lifts: 2 double chairlifts and 5 surface lifts, serving the 30 km of pistes. The lifts are modern enough for the resort's size, and queues are rare due to the low visitor numbers. The main access is via the Leontica-Cancorì and Cancorì-Pian Nara chairlifts.

terrainDescription: The terrain is dominated by wide, well-groomed pistes spread across a broad ridge near the Italian border. The ski area is divided into five main sectors: Millennium/Nassfeld, Tressdorfer Höhe, Gartnerkofel, Madritsche, and Watschinger Alm. Most runs are long blue and red descents, with the longest being the 7.6 km Carnia run from the summit to Tröpolach. The north-facing runs tend to hold snow well, while the Italian-facing slopes offer sunnier conditions. The resort has 110 km of marked pistes, with 30 lifts providing access.
offPisteDescription: Off-piste skiing is possible after fresh snowfall, particularly in the free-ride zone off the upper Millennium Express towards Italy, which offers steep open runs. However, the resort's focus is on piste skiing, and real off-piste is limited by tree coverage. There are marked ski routes such as the Steinerne Wand couloir from Gartnerkofel, but these require caution and avalanche awareness. The backside of Kofelplatz/Madritschenkopf has potential but currently lacks lifts.
valueDescription: Lift passes are moderately priced, with a day pass around €65–70 in peak season. Accommodation is spread across several small settlements around the base areas, often accessed by car or ski bus, which can offer more budget-friendly options compared to purpose-built resorts. The resort is known for good value compared to other Austrian resorts, with reasonable prices for food on the mountain.
familyDescription: Nassfeld is well-suited for families, with dedicated beginner areas at Madritsche (Kid's Island) and near the Tröpolach gondola base, featuring gentle slopes and magic carpets. The ski school offers lessons for children, and there are family-friendly activities such as sledging, ice skating, and an adventure slope. The resort has clear piste signage and well-separated beginner zones, making it easy for families to navigate. Accommodation options include ski-in/ski-out hotels and apartments.
apresSkiDescription: Apres-ski is relatively low-key, with a focus on daytime skiing. There are 25 mountain huts and restaurants on the slopes, many offering regional cuisine and a relaxed atmosphere. In the evenings, the resort is quieter, with a few bars and restaurants in the base areas. Events such as the Nassfeld Mountain Alpenparty and Italian Days provide occasional entertainment. The proximity to Italy adds a Mediterranean touch to some dining options.
resortCharacterDescription: Nassfeld is not a single compact village but a ski area spread across several small settlements, including Tröpolach and Sonnenalpe. The base areas feature modern lift stations and hotels, with a functional rather than picturesque character. The resort is located in the sunny Gail Valley near the Italian border, and the architecture is a mix of traditional Austrian chalets and modern apartment blocks. The main lift hub is the Millennium Express gondola station in Tröpolach.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system is modern and efficient, with 30 lifts including 6 gondolas and 12 chairlifts, many with heated seats and weather shields. The Millennium Express gondola is Austria's longest cable car, spanning 6 km from Tröpolach to the high valley in 32 minutes. Uplift capacity exceeds 49,000 skiers per hour, keeping queues short even during peak periods. Recent investments include the Tröglbahn high-speed six-seater chairlift. The system provides good coverage across the five main sectors.
linkedResortsDescription: Nassfeld is part of a cross-border ski area that extends into Italy, with slopes crossing the border at Passo Pramollo. Skiers can access Italian terrain on a single lift pass. The resort also has three smaller family ski resorts in the region: Weissbriach, Kötschach-Mauthen, and Weissensee, though these are not directly lift-linked. There are plans for a future lift connection to Pontebba in Italy.

terrainDescription: The terrain at Nauders Bergkastel is spread across the Bergkastelspitze, Tscheyeck, and Gueserkopf mountains in the Oetztal Alps. The ski area offers wide carving runs, two valley runs (the red Bergkastel downhill and the blue Avagrass descent), and a 9 km long Tscheyecktal descent with a vertical drop of 1,266 metres. The most difficult run is 'Die Schwarze', a 3 km mogul slope. There are also five ski routes for off-piste, including the Gueser Kopf tour starting at 2,745 metres, and forest runs such as the west descent from the Almlift base station. The red Gaisloch descent is particularly wide and suitable for carving.
offPisteDescription: Off-piste skiing is available via five designated ski routes and freeride routes. The freeride routes include 'Down Under' and 'G3000', which lead into powder snow. The Gueser Kopf tour starts at the highest point of the ski area at 2,745 metres and offers a descent through the Schafkar in the Nauderer Tschey. The ski area also has deep-snow slopes and ski tours for experienced skiers.
valueDescription: Lift pass prices for the Zwei Länder Skiarena (which includes Nauders Bergkastel) are €272.50 for a 6-day adult pass in low season and €301.00 in high season, with children's passes at €122.50 and €135.50 respectively. A review notes that the resort is 'not so expensive' and offers a calm atmosphere. Accommodation is in chalet-style apartments and hotels, and the village is walkable.
familyDescription: Nauders Bergkastel is highly family-friendly, with the Goldpark Nauders at the Bergkastel mountain station. This includes the Nauderix Kinderland, a 6,500 m² area with a ski kindergarten for children from age two, a children's restaurant, magic carpets, a children's carousel, tubing track, playground, Indian tent, and an adventure forest with animal figures. The Snow-Base Bergkastel has two covered conveyor belts for beginners. All chairlifts have child safety features. The Flow Parks Nauders (Base Park, Scenic Park, Pine Park) are designed for families. The ski school offers pedagogical instruction, and there is a separate children's area at the valley station with a plate lift.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski in Nauders is calm rather than lively. The Yetibar plays après-ski hits and current charts, while the IBEX Club offers house, rock, hip-hop, and electro music with live music and theme parties. There are 4 bars and pubs and 1 club in the resort. A review notes that there is 'no apres ski' but the calm atmosphere is enjoyed. Night toboggan evenings are held on Tuesdays and Thursdays at the Lärchenalm.
resortCharacterDescription: Nauders is a village of chalet-style hotels and apartments, giving it a traditional alpine feel rather than a purpose-built concrete resort. The village is walkable and located on the main Alpine ridge at the Reschen Pass, close to the border with Italy and Switzerland. The ski area is easily accessible from the village.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system at Nauders Bergkastel consists of a gondola, chairlifts, drag lifts, and magic carpets. The total uphill capacity is 17,151 persons per hour. All chairlifts are equipped with child safety features. The lifts operate from 08:30 to 16:00 daily. The Bergkastel cable car takes skiers from the village to 2,750 metres.
linkedResortsDescription: Nauders Bergkastel is part of the Two Countries Ski Arena (Zwei Länder Skiarena), which connects three ski areas: Nauders Bergkastel (Austria), Schöneben-Haideralm (Italy), and Watles (Italy). A free ski bus shuttles between the resorts. The combined area offers 159 km of slopes. Additionally, the Solda Glacier Ski Resort (Italy) is also accessible via the same ski pass arrangement, reaching 3,250 metres. Another joint ski pass exists with Kaunertal Glacier and Fendels.

terrainDescription: The ski area at Nax Mont Noble offers a mix of beginner and intermediate pistes, with no advanced runs. The upper mountain above treeline is wide and open, with northwest and west-facing slopes that are steep and rolling, all rated intermediate. Lower down, the terrain becomes a playful tangle of dips, hollows, and mini-cliffy outcrops speckled with larch and pine trees. The summit at 2,640m offers a 360-degree panorama of the Rhône valley and surrounding peaks. The longest run is over 7 km, descending 1,135 vertical metres from summit to base.
offPisteDescription: Off-piste terrain at Nax Mont Noble is extensive and easily accessible from the lifts. From the summit of Mont Noble, a short hike leads to steep northeast-facing couloirs and gullies dropping into the Réchy valley, with 1,000 vertical metres of descent through cliffy upper sections and open faces to sparse forest. A more committing route involves a bootpack from Col de Cou up the north ridge of Mont Gautier, accessing 500 vertical metres of freeride terrain on a wide flank. The lower forest offers playful tree runs with pillow drops and valleys. Due to low visitor numbers, powder can remain untracked for days after a storm.
valueDescription: Lift passes at Nax Mont Noble are among the most affordable in the Valais, with a 2024/25 day ticket at CHF55 for adults and CHF27.50 for children. A six-day pass costs around CHF294. The resort is included in the Magic Pass, which grants access to over 50 Swiss ski areas for a single season price. Free parking is available at the base of the lifts. Accommodation in the village of Nax includes self-catering apartments and a traditional hotel, with prices lower than in larger resorts. On-mountain dining is limited to two restaurants, with reasonable prices for local dishes.
familyDescription: The resort has beginner runs near the village, including a small bunny slope, and 3 km of cross-country skiing trails, plus a natural ice-rink and snowtubing. The main ski area is dominated by intermediate terrain, which may be challenging for absolute beginners but suitable for progressing skiers. The village is quiet and family-oriented, with a traditional Swiss atmosphere. Ski school is available through the Swiss Ski School Nax Mont-Noble.
apresSkiDescription: Apres-ski at Nax is very quiet, with no dedicated nightlife venues. The village has a few bars and restaurants, such as the Hôtel Mont-Noble restaurant, which serves local beef and regional wines. The atmosphere is relaxed, with early evenings. For more lively options, visitors drive 20 minutes to Sion.
resortCharacterDescription: Nax is a traditional Swiss village with stone and wood houses dating back centuries, centered around a church. The ski area is a small, local operation with lifts starting from a roadside parking area rather than a purpose-built base. The village offers a tranquil, rural atmosphere with views over the Rhône valley. The resort is known locally as 'The Balcony of Heaven'.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system at Nax Mont Noble consists of five lifts: an older double chairlift (Dzorniva) from the base at 1,495m, a modern fixed-grip quad (La Vachette) installed in 2012, a surface tow (Les Planards) replaced in 2022, and a summit quad. The lifts operate from 9am to 4pm. Queues are rare except on sunny weekends. The system is efficient for the size of the area, with a vertical rise of 1,135m.
linkedResortsDescription: Nax Mont Noble is not physically linked to other ski areas by lifts, but it is included in the Magic Pass, which provides access to over 50 Swiss ski resorts including nearby Arolla, Anzere, Leukerbad, Ovronnaz, St-Luc/Chandolin, Grimentz-Zinal, and Télé Marécottes. It is also considered part of the Val d'Herens ski region, which includes Evolène and Arolla.

terrainDescription: The terrain is dominated by wide, gentle beginner slopes on the mountain's broad shoulders with consistent pitch and width. The intermediate runs drop more directly down the fall line, offering enough pitch for carving technique but no advanced or expert terrain. The most challenging run is the black 'Grüner Stich' (0.7 km, 200 m vertical drop), and there is a mogul slope. The resort has six marked runs, with 75% classified as beginner and 25% as intermediate. The longest run from the summit to the base is 4 km.
valueDescription: Adult day tickets cost €42, roughly €20 below major Allgäu destinations, with children at €27.50. A 'Vitales Land' lift pass is available, granting access to around 140 km of pistes and over 70 lift systems across the region, including Pfronten, Füssen, and Schwangau. The resort offers honest pricing for a small operation, with multi-day and season passes likely available for regular visitors. Parking is free at the base area.
familyDescription: The resort is well-suited for families with young children. The Ski-Kinderland features a themed enchanted forest practice area with a children's conveyor belt and mini-lift, plus a ski school fire brigade train. The limited terrain means parents can easily keep track of children, and lift queues remain minimal even on weekends. The compact layout reduces overwhelm for first-time skiers. Two ski schools offer courses for children and adults.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski is cosy and sociable. Sepp's Stadelalm, next to the base station, offers an umbrella bar and hearty hut evenings. A newly built après-ski tent near the base station is a popular spot. On the mountain, the Enzianstüble at the middle station serves snacks and homemade cakes on a terrace with views. The BergLodge at the top station offers Allgäu specialities in the evening. The AlpspitzKick zipline, the longest in Germany (1.2 km, speeds up to 120 km/h), operates from the mountain station to the middle station.
resortCharacterDescription: Nesselwang is an authentic Bavarian village in the Allgäu region, operating primarily on repeat local traffic rather than destination appeal. The resort is small-scale and unpretentious, with a traditional village centre and free parking at the base. It is one of the 10 most snow-sure ski areas in Germany, with comprehensive snowmaking covering 70% of the slopes. The village offers winter hiking trails, a natural toboggan run, and the Alpspitz-Bade-Center with saunas.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of 7 lifts: 1 gondola (the Alpspitzbahn cable car climbing 563 vertical metres), 1 quad chairlift, and 5 surface lifts. The gondola and a parallel surface lift (Stellenbichl) operate for night skiing from 18:00 to 21:00, with 2 km of floodlit runs. The total lift capacity is 6,300 persons per hour. The lifts are modern and well-maintained, with free parking at the base.
linkedResortsDescription: The 'Vitales Land' lift pass connects Nesselwang with a regional ski area of approximately 140 km of pistes and over 70 lift systems, including the resorts of Pfronten, Füssen, Schwangau, Göisried, Halblech, and on the Tyrolean side Jungholz, Tannheimer Tal, the Tyrolean Lechtal, and the Reutte Nature Park region. This allows skiers to access a wider variety of terrain beyond the local 8 km.

terrainDescription: The Postwiese ski area sits on the sunny side of the Kahlen Asten, offering open, connected terrain with wide pistes. The east slope area, served by a four-seater chairlift, provides four runs including a blue family-friendly descent through the 'cannon tube'. The runs are mostly mellow cruisers with a few steeper sections, and the terrain is well-suited to beginners and intermediates. The resort has 16 runs in total, with a longest run of 1 km.
valueDescription: Adult day tickets are €40, with junior tickets at €34 and child tickets at €27, making family skiing affordable for repeated visits. A joint 'Skiwelt Winterberg' ticket gives access to 46 lifts and 45 km of slopes across Winterberg, Altastenberg, and Neuastenberg, with free shuttle buses connecting the areas. The family card offers further discounts, and children's prices have remained largely stable despite rising costs.
familyDescription: The resort offers child-friendly ski courses with a magic carpet and conveyor belt at the Tenne for easy learning. The natural toboggan run with its own lift provides additional family fun. Floodlit skiing and tobogganing on Wednesday, Friday, and Saturday evenings extend the day. The short drive from the Ruhr Valley makes it convenient for day trips and weekend family outings, and the open, connected terrain is ideal for families.
resortCharacterDescription: Neuastenberg is a traditional mountain village in the Sauerland region, with a population of about 560, located on the crest of the Rothaargebirge. The ski area is situated on the southern slopes of the Kahlen Asten, with slopes accessible within minutes from the village. The resort has a 100-year tradition of winter sports and offers a mix of village charm and modern ski infrastructure, including a biathlon stadium, indoor go-cart track, and the West German Winter Sport Museum.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of 9 lifts, including a modern four-seater chairlift (Panorama chairlift) that provides access to the east slope area, and 7 surface lifts. A magic carpet and conveyor belt serve the beginner areas. Wait times are rarely longer than five minutes. The natural toboggan run has its own dedicated lift. Floodlit skiing is available on 4.5 km of slopes, the toboggan run, and the fun park on Wednesday, Friday, and Saturday evenings.
linkedResortsDescription: The Postwiese ski area is part of the 'Skiwelt Winterberg' network, which includes the Winterberg and Altastenberg ski areas, offering a total of 46 lifts and 45 km of slopes. A joint lift ticket provides access to all three areas, with free shuttle buses connecting them. The nearby Sahnehang and Skikarussell Altastenberg are also accessible via the same ticket.

terrainDescription: The terrain at Niederalpl consists of 12 km of well-groomed slopes, with 38% beginner and 62% intermediate runs. The slopes are broad and forgiving, ideal for beginners and intermediate skiers. There is a dedicated children's zone with a practice lift. A few steeper red runs and natural snow runs provide variety for more advanced skiers. The resort offers views of the Hohe Veitsch, Hochschwab, and Schneealm.
offPisteDescription: Niederalpl is known as a 'Schneeloch' (snow hole) where natural powder snow accumulates, making it popular with freeriders. The resort offers designated off-piste areas and natural snow runs. Artificial snow is rarely used, as natural snowfall is typically sufficient. The surrounding forests and terrain provide opportunities for off-piste skiing, though no specific couloirs or glades are mentioned.
valueDescription: Lift pass prices are moderate: a day pass for adults costs €49, a six-day pass €275. Children's day passes are €36, six-day €191. A notable value feature is that children up to 15 years old ski for free when accompanied by their parents. Accommodation options include self-catering apartments like the 'Holzbox Niederalpl' and the Kinderhotel Appelhof nearby. The resort is 90 minutes from Vienna and Graz, making it accessible for day trips.
familyDescription: Niederalpl is a family-friendly resort with a dedicated children's zone featuring a practice lift. The ski school 'Snowsports 121' is run by former World Cup snowboarder Marion Trska and freeride legend Tom Kalcher, offering instruction for children and adults. Children up to 15 ski for free with parents. The nearby Kinderhotel Appelhof in Mürzsteg offers extensive indoor and outdoor play areas, animal encounters, and wellness facilities. The resort's layout makes it easy for families to stay together on the slopes.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski options are limited but cozy. On-mountain dining includes 'Manis Stube' at the base station and 'Hansis Hittn' on the mountain, serving hearty Styrian food such as soups, bread, and Apfelstrudel. The 'Holzbox Niederalpl' has a restaurant and bar. The atmosphere is relaxed, with mulled wine and local specialties. There is no nightclub or large-scale party scene.
resortCharacterDescription: Niederalpl is a small, traditional ski resort in the Styrian Alps, located within the Naturpark Mürzer Oberland. It is described as a hidden gem (Geheimtipp) with a tranquil atmosphere, far from the crowds of larger resorts. The resort is not purpose-built; it has a natural, alpine character with wooden chalets and huts. The focus is on nature and sustainability, with eco-conscious practices in accommodations and operations.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of 5 lifts: one 4-person chairlift, one T-bar, two platter lifts, and a practice rope tow in the children's area. The lifts are modern and ensure short waiting times. The chairlift provides access to the main slopes, while the surface lifts serve the beginner and intermediate terrain. The resort has invested in modern snowmaking systems, though natural snow is usually sufficient.

terrainDescription: Niederau's local slopes are accessed via the Markbachjoch gondola and the Lanerköpfl chairlift, offering 24 km of pistes on the mountain. The terrain is dominated by gentle nursery slopes at the village center, ideal for beginners, with a progression to well-groomed blue and red runs. Intermediate skiers have access to a wealth of blue and red runs via the connection to the Ski Juwel area. Advanced skiers can challenge themselves on four black runs, including the 3 km long black runs #16 and #15 from the top of the Lanerköpfl chairlift (1,800m) down to Niederau (828m), and the testing FIS race course (red #23) from the Mittermoosjoch draglift. Ski routes #1 and #2 from the top of the Markbachjoch gondola offer steep, narrow off-piste descents. The lower slopes have a northerly exposition and snowmaking, but snow reliability can be an issue later in the season.
offPisteDescription: Off-piste terrain in Niederau is easily accessible and stays untracked longer than in busier resorts. After fresh snowfall, there are opportunities to ski deeper snow next to many easy pistes, with an easy way back to groomed runs. The most challenging off-piste is directly beneath the 1,850 m long Lanerköpfl chairlift, but tree stumps hidden beneath the snow require caution. Ski route #1 from the top of the Markbachjoch gondola is a narrow, straight-line descent nearly 2 km long with over 600 m vertical and an average gradient over 30 degrees, with no escape once committed. Ski route #2 is wider and slightly less steep but includes sudden changes in gradient. There are also off-piste areas above and below the treeline, and guided skiing is available through ski schools.
familyDescription: Niederau is a compact village with family-run hotels located right at the foot of the beginner slopes, many offering ski-in/ski-out access. The nursery slopes are in the village center, steps from accommodation and the ski school meeting point. The ski school has an excellent reputation (rated 4.79/5 by users) and offers childcare and lessons for children. Evening options include child-friendly meals in restaurants. The village is easy to navigate on foot, and the free ski bus connects to the wider Ski Juwel area. Activities for families include tobogganing from the top of the gondola, cross-country skiing, sleigh rides, snow tubing, and winter walking.
apresSkiDescription: The après-ski scene in Niederau is relaxed and low-key. The Schirm Bar at the foot of the slopes is a popular spot for drinks, and there is a cave bar that can be lively. Tobogganing from the top of the gondola back to the valley is a popular evening activity. The village is small enough that you can meet people from the slopes in the bars. Nightlife is quiet compared to larger resorts, with a few bars and restaurants offering a calm atmosphere.
resortCharacterDescription: Niederau is a traditional Austrian village in the Wildschönau Valley, characterized by wood-clad chalets and a striking Gothic church. It is not a purpose-built resort; the village has a historic, picturesque feel with family-run hotels and guesthouses. The village is compact and walkable, with a relaxed pace. The surrounding valley is scenic, with views of the Kitzbühel Alps.
liftSystemDescription: Niederau's local lift system includes the Markbachjoch gondola and the Lanerköpfl chairlift, plus several drag lifts. The wider Ski Juwel area has 45 modern lifts, including 9 gondolas/trams, 3 high-speed six-packs, 4 quad chairs, and 29 surface lifts. A free ski bus connects Niederau to Oberau and Auffach, and a high-speed gondola built in 2012 links Auffach to Alpbach, creating the Ski Juwel area. The Race 'n Boarder Arena in Niederau is served by a drag lift on Tennladen. Lift efficiency is rated highly by users (4.43/5).
linkedResortsDescription: Niederau is part of the Ski Juwel Alpbachtal Wildschönau ski area, which links the villages of Niederau, Oberau, and Auffach via a free ski bus service. In 2012, a high-speed gondola connected Auffach to Alpbach, creating a combined ski area of 145 km of pistes (32 km blue, 78 km red, 18 km black, 17 km ski routes) with 47 lifts. The area is marketed as Ski Juwel and offers one lift pass valid for all lifts. The linked area provides a variety of terrain for all abilities, with additional mountain restaurants and off-piste opportunities.

terrainDescription: The ski area on Niederhorn consists of 8 km of pistes served by 8 lifts. The terrain includes one easy run (Flösch), two moderately difficult runs (Slalom and Racing), and one difficult, unprepared run (Abfahrt). The slopes are located on the mountain between 1,164 m and 1,938 m, offering views of Lake Thun and the Bernese Alps. The main ski lift is a T-bar from the Vorsass middle station to the summit, and the runs are short but varied in gradient.
offPisteDescription: The only designated off-piste terrain is the Abfahrt run, which is not prepared or controlled and is rated difficult. In good snow conditions, freeriders use this run to descend to Beatenberg. No other off-piste areas or routes are mentioned in the source text.
valueDescription: Day lift passes cost CHF 65 from Beatenbucht and CHF 49 from Beatenberg for adults. Children aged 6–16 pay CHF 32.50 from Beatenbucht and CHF 24.50 from Beatenberg. With a Junior Card, children up to 16 travel free on the train and cable car; without it, only the first child pays and siblings ride free. A family package for two adults and two children costs CHF 159 and includes day tickets and a pasta meal with soft drinks. The resort is described as budget-friendly compared to larger Swiss resorts.
familyDescription: The resort is well suited for families. There is an alpine playground at the Vorsass middle station with wooden play structures, climbing frames, and a balance trail. The family package includes day tickets and a meal. Children up to 16 travel free with a Junior Card, and without it only the first child pays. The easy Flösch run is suitable for beginners, and the Trottibike scooter descent from Vorsass to Beatenberg is popular with children. The Berghaus Niederhorn restaurant has a children's playground. The resort is car-free at the summit and the village of Beatenberg is quiet and walkable.
resortCharacterDescription: Niederhorn is a natural mountain peak in the Emmental Alps, not a purpose-built ski resort. The summit is reached by a modern gondola from the village of Beatenberg, which itself is a quiet alpine village perched above Lake Thun. The summit has a restaurant (Berghaus Niederhorn) with a terrace and a viewing platform. The area is a nature reserve, known for wildlife such as ibex, chamois, and marmots. The ski area is small and operates only from January to early March, catering mainly to day visitors and families.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of three stages: a funicular from Beatenbucht to Beatenberg (opened earlier, part of the Thunersee–Beatenberg Bahn), a modern gondola from Beatenberg to the Niederhorn summit (opened in 1997, replacing a 1946 cable car), and a single T-bar ski lift from the Vorsass middle station to the summit. The gondola has an intermediate stop at Vorsass and operates with groups of three cabins carrying up to 17 passengers each. The funicular connects with boat services on Lake Thun. The lifts run from 09:30 to 15:45 during the winter season.

terrainDescription: The ski area is small, with a handful of groomed pistes that are predominantly intermediate (red) in difficulty. The slopes are mostly east-facing on a south-facing mountainside, with a few steep expert routes including the Hafelekarrinne, which has a 70% gradient and is one of Europe's most challenging ski slopes. A snowpark (Skylinepark) and a children's area with a magic carpet are also present.
offPisteDescription: Off-piste terrain is concentrated around the Hafelekar summit at 2,256 m, offering steep couloirs and powder slopes. The Karrinne is a famous steep ski route with a 70% gradient, reserved for experts. Freeriders access the powder early in the morning before the slopes are tracked, and the area is known for its challenging freeride options.
valueDescription: A day lift pass costs €56 for adults (2026/27 season), with reduced rates of €44.80 for youth and €33.60 for children. Accommodation in Innsbruck offers a range of prices, from budget hostels to hotels, making it a relatively affordable option compared to purpose-built ski resorts. The Ski + City pass provides access to 12 ski areas and cultural attractions, offering good value for those wanting variety.
familyDescription: The Nordkette has a dedicated children's area at the Seegrube with a magic carpet, snow hill, and childcare facilities. Free fun sports equipment hire is available. However, the ski area is small and primarily suited to intermediate and expert skiers, so families with beginners may find the limited gentle terrain less suitable than larger family resorts. The proximity to Innsbruck means families can easily combine skiing with city activities.
apresSkiDescription: On-mountain apres-ski is centered on the Cloud 9 Igloo Bar at the Seegrube, known for its parties and igloo setting. The Seegrube restaurant offers dining with panoramic views over the Inn Valley. For a wider range of nightlife, skiers can return to Innsbruck city center, which has numerous bars, restaurants, and cultural venues.
resortCharacterDescription: Nordkette is not a standalone resort village but a mountain ski area directly above Innsbruck. The base station is located on the edge of the old town, and the cable car provides a 20-minute ascent to the high-alpine terrain. The setting is dramatic, with views over the city and the Inn Valley. The infrastructure includes modern cable cars and a few mountain restaurants, but the character is that of a natural mountain environment rather than a purpose-built resort.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of a three-stage cable car from Innsbruck city center to the Hafelekar summit, comprising a funicular (Hungerburg) and two cable car sections (Seegrube and Hafelekar). Additionally, there are two double chairlifts, a T-bar, and a magic carpet for beginners. The journey from the city to the slopes takes about 20 minutes. The lifts are modern and efficient, with the cable cars offering panoramic views.
linkedResortsDescription: Nordkette is one of 12 ski areas covered by the Innsbruck Ski + City pass, which also includes larger resorts such as Stubai Glacier, Kühtai, and Axamer Lizum. While not physically connected by lifts, these areas are accessible by public bus from Innsbruck, allowing skiers to explore different terrain on different days. The pass also provides access to over 20 cultural attractions in Innsbruck.

terrainDescription: The terrain includes two black pistes 'Agonistica alta' and 'Variante turistica', and the 'piste del Sole' accessed by the Sole-Sponda Vaga chairlift. A snowpark is available for freestyle skiers and snowboarders. Cross-country loops of 3.5 km and 7 km are present. A baby park with inflatable games and a conveyor belt serves beginners. Off-piste itineraries lead to Monte Sansna, Pizzo dei Tre Confini, and Pizzo di Petto.
offPisteDescription: Off-piste itineraries are available towards Monte Sansna, Pizzo dei Tre Confini, and Pizzo di Petto. These routes are used by freeride enthusiasts, but no further details on snow quality or difficulty are provided in the sources.
familyDescription: The resort features a baby park with inflatable games and a conveyor belt for young children. Cross-country skiing loops of 3.5 and 7 km are available. The ski school is noted for teaching beginners effectively, as per a long-time visitor review. The area has a high number of second homes (12,000 within 15 minutes), indicating a family-oriented visitor base.
resortCharacterDescription: Nuova Lizzola is a small, family-oriented ski station in the Alta Val Seriana, set in a natural alpine environment between Monte Vago and Val Sedornia. The resort has been operating for 66 years and is characterized by its traditional mountain setting, though recent years have seen a lack of major investment, leading to a risk of closure. A planned redevelopment aims to connect it with Colere via a tunnel.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of 5 lifts: one triple chairlift, three double chairlifts, and one surface lift (tapis roulant for beginners). The lifts are named Campo scuola, Cavandola, Due Baite, Rambasì, and Sponda Vaga. While described as modern, the resort has suffered from a lack of significant investment in recent years, with plans to replace some chairlifts with a cable car as part of a redevelopment project.
linkedResortsDescription: Nuova Lizzola is currently a standalone resort, but a major project is underway to connect it with the nearby Colere ski area via a 500-meter tunnel under Pizzo di Petto. The combined ski area would offer 50 km of pistes, creating one of the largest ski domains in Lombardy. The project, with a budget of 70 million euros, aims for completion by December 2026. Nearby resorts include Colere, Spiazzi di Gromo, Foppolo, and Carona, but they are not currently lift-linked.

terrainDescription: The ski area is dominated by wide, open, above-the-treeline cruising runs, with over half the pistes classified as easy blue runs. Most red runs are only slightly steeper, making the terrain ideal for intermediates and confident beginners. The longest descent, from Wurmkogl to Obergurgl/Pill, measures approximately 8 km with 1,260 m of vertical. The Hohe Mut blue run is a long, scenic, gentle run suitable for progressing families. The Festkogl summit offers brilliant intermediate descents, and the Hochgurgl side has a higher proportion of blue runs, while Obergurgl has more red runs. The terrain is mostly above the treeline, with a few tree-lined pistes on runs 22 and 33 in Hochgurgl and 12, 3, 4, and 5 in Obergurgl.
offPisteDescription: Off-piste potential is good after snowfall, particularly on the Hohe Mut side, though not as extensive as in Sölden. Marked freeride routes lead through high alpine terrain, including the Hohe Mut to Rotmoostal route and Festkogl to Ferwalltal powder runs. There is easy access off-piste just off the edges of runs, and tree skiing below the Steinmannbahn. Stronger skiers often hire a guide for ski touring, as the official slopes offer limited challenge. The Wurmkogel area provides steep descents with 1,200 m of vertical.
valueDescription: Lift passes are relatively expensive, with a one-day adult ticket around €72 and a six-day pass about €340 in high season. However, children under 8 ski for €2 per day, and booking online up to five days in advance offers discounted rates. A three-day pass or more automatically upgrades to the Ötztal Superskipass covering 365 km including Sölden, which adds significant value. Pre-season (20 Nov – 5 Dec) and group discounts (20+ people up to 12% off) further reduce costs. Accommodation is primarily hotel-based with limited self-catering, so half-board packages are common.
familyDescription: Obergurgl is a strong choice for families due to its compact, walkable village where ski school, hire shops, restaurants, and accommodation are all within a few minutes' walk. The beginner area at the top of the Festkogl gondola is wide, sunny, and sheltered from wind. The Gaisberg nursery area is also wind-sheltered. The Hohe Mut blue run is recommended as one of the best family runs in Austria. Children are cared for in ski schools and the BOBO's Kids Club, with dedicated Kinderclubs for young children. Under 8s ski for €2 per day. The car-free village and free ski bus make logistics easy.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski is fun yet civilised, centred on the legendary Nederhütte at 2,104 m, which features live music from the Nederlumpen band since 1988, with table-dancing and steins. On-mountain options include the Top Mountain Star at 3,080 m for cocktails with panoramic views, and the Zirben Alm for cosy hut evenings. In the village, umbrella bars like Schirmbar Edelweiss and hotel bars such as Fassl Bar and Krumpn's Stadl offer a sociable atmosphere. The only nightclub is Josl-Keller. For a rowdier scene, Sölden is 20 minutes away by bus.
resortCharacterDescription: Obergurgl retains the feel of a real Tyrolean village with wooden chalets, an onion-domed church, and warm hospitality, while Hochgurgl is a sleek, high-altitude resort with ultra-modern hotels perched above the valley. Obergurgl is one of the most attractive high-altitude ski resorts in the Alps, and the highest church parish in Austria. The resort is at the end of the Ötztal Valley with no through traffic, creating a calm, upmarket atmosphere. Hochgurgl is quieter and more exclusive, with premium hotels and sweeping glacier views.
liftSystemDescription: The lift network consists of 25 modern lifts, including eleven gondola lifts, seven chairlifts, and seven drag lifts. High-speed chairs and gondolas dominate, with heated seats on key lifts for comfort. The Top Express gondola connects Obergurgl and Hochgurgl in under 10 minutes over a 3.6 km span. Grooming is consistent even late season, and lift queues are rare due to high capacity. The system is efficient, with short walks to lifts and excellent ski-in/ski-out access from most hotels.
linkedResortsDescription: Obergurgl and Hochgurgl are two linked villages connected by the Top Express gondola at mid-mountain, forming the Gurgl ski area. With a three-day or longer lift pass, skiers automatically receive the Ötztal Superskipass, which covers all Ötztal resorts including Sölden (145 km of pistes), giving access to a combined 365 km of terrain across 90 lifts and two glaciers. A free ski bus runs between Obergurgl, Hochgurgl, and Sölden, taking about 25 minutes to Sölden.

terrainDescription: The terrain at Obersaxen-Mundaun-Val Lumnezia is a ski circuit around the mountains Piz Sezner, Stein, and Mundaun, with 120 km of marked pistes. The piste breakdown is 50 km blue (easy), 50 km red (intermediate), and 20 km black (advanced). The longest run is a 13 km red/blue descent from Piz Sezner to Vella. The ski area is primarily north-facing, which helps preserve snow quality. The terrain is suitable for intermediate skiers, with large expanses of off-piste skiing available. The area spans about 5.5 miles across, comparable to the combined size of The Canyons and Park City.
offPisteDescription: Off-piste skiing at Obersaxen-Mundaun is extensive, with large expanses of ungroomed terrain accessible from the lifts. The north-facing slopes hold soft snow well after a storm, as reported in a 2020 trip report describing bootcuff-deep powder. However, visitor reviews rate off-piste quality at 2.7 out of 5, suggesting variable conditions. The area includes 30 km of cross-country trails but no dedicated off-piste zones marked. The terrain park and halfpipe are present for freestyle.
valueDescription: Obersaxen offers good value compared to larger Swiss resorts. A day ski pass for an adult costs 67 CHF, and a 6-day pass is 331 CHF (as of 2026). Children's day passes are 30 CHF, 6-day 166 CHF. The resort is less busy than nearby Flims-Laax, with lower lift ticket prices and free parking at all cable car car parks. Accommodation in the surrounding villages like Brigels is reasonably priced. The resort is described as a 'locals’ mountain' with a pleasant, unpretentious vibe.
familyDescription: Obersaxen is family-friendly with dedicated children's facilities. There are two child ski lifts (Kinderlift Kartitscha and a children's area) and a family-friendly run at Chummenbühl. The ski area has a relaxed, locals' atmosphere that suits families. The village of Obersaxen is compact, and the ski school offers lessons. However, the resort is not as purpose-built for families as some larger resorts, and the beginner terrain is limited compared to the intermediate and advanced offerings.
apresSkiDescription: Apres-ski at Obersaxen is low-key, with 10 restaurants and bars on the mountain and in the village. Visitor reviews rate apres-ski at 2.8 out of 5. The mountain has a panoramic umbrella bar at the top of Piz Sezner and several mountain lodges like Berghaus Triel offering food and drinks. The scene is more about relaxed mountain dining than nightlife.
resortCharacterDescription: Obersaxen is a traditional Swiss village resort in the Surselva region of Graubünden. It is not a purpose-built resort but a collection of villages (Obersaxen, Mundaun, Lumnezia) with a locals' mountain feel. The ski area has an old-school logo and a mix of modern and older lifts. The scenery is spectacular, with views of the Alps and numerous churches. The resort is less crowded and more authentic than larger, flashier destinations.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system at Obersaxen-Mundaun-Val Lumnezia consists of 18 lifts: 9 chairlifts (including 4-seater and 6-seater chairs), 4 surface lifts, and 2 child ski lifts. The lifts are a mix of modern high-speed quads and six-passenger chairs, as well as older T-bars. The system connects the three areas of Obersaxen, Mundaun, and Lumnezia, allowing a full circuit. Parking at all lift bases is free. The season runs from mid-December to early April.
linkedResortsDescription: Obersaxen is part of the linked ski area Obersaxen-Mundaun-Val Lumnezia, which connects the three sectors via lifts and pistes. The area includes the villages of Obersaxen, Mundaun, and Lumnezia (Vella). The longest run connects Piz Sezner (2,310 m) down to Vella in the Lumnezia valley, a 13 km descent. The ski area is also part of the larger Surselva region, with nearby resorts like Brigels, Laax, and Savognin accessible by car or bus. The lift system allows a full circuit around the mountains.

terrainDescription: The ski area is arranged in a circular bowl around the village, with slopes facing all directions. The Tauernrunde circuit allows skiers to complete a full loop clockwise or anticlockwise without repeating a run. The majority of pistes are on the eastern side above the treeline, with wide, well-groomed blue and red runs dominating. The Gamsleiten 2 is a 45-degree black piste, one of the steepest in Europe. The resort has 11 entry points, so skiers are dispersed and morning bottlenecks are avoided. Ski bridges cross the main road at four points, linking the two sides of the valley.
offPisteDescription: Open freeride zones exist between pistes, with powder snow accumulating in the natural bowl. From chairlifts, lines can be seen dropping into bowls and open faces. Riders duck ropes and hike short sections to access challenging terrain. The resort markets itself as Austria's snowiest, with an average maximum snow depth of 2.6 metres, which supports off-piste skiing. The Super 7 Summit Tour takes skiers to the seven highest mountain stations, providing access to varied off-piste lines. The Gamsleiten 2 area, when open, offers steep mogul fields and avalanche-protected faces.
valueDescription: A one-day adult lift pass costs approximately €69.50 in Season B (off-peak) and €76 in Season A (peak). A six-day pass ranges from €320 to €350. Children under six ski free with a parent. The LUNGO pass covers four resorts on one card, and a 1.5-day or longer pass is also valid at the nearby Grosseck-Speiereck ski area. Point cards offer flexible pay-per-use options. Early booking and off-season travel (November, early January, late April) provide significant savings. Family bonuses include the third child and subsequent children skiing free on winter cards.
familyDescription: The BiboBär Family Ski Paradise is a dedicated children's area with magic carpets, conveyor belts, and themed features. The Fairy Tale Park and Geisterbahn (ghost train) add entertainment for younger skiers. Under-sixes ski free with a parent, and the family bonus means the third child and any additional children ski free on winter cards. Ski schools offer English-speaking instruction and free under-six passes during lessons. Digital safety wristbands are available for children. The resort's ski-in/ski-out layout means families can step onto the slopes directly from most hotels, avoiding long walks or bus rides.
apresSkiDescription: The Après-Ski Mile features over 25 ski lodges and bars along the slopes and through the village. Lürzer Alm is a legendary spot that transitions from après to late-night dancing. Edelweissalm at 2,070m has DJs from 3pm and a classic Obertauern party vibe. Gruber Stadl offers a sunny terrace by day and full party energy by night. Hochalm at around 2,000m provides a relaxed mountain après with panoramic views. In the village, Römer Bar has live music, Hotel Edelweiss hosts live bands, and Latsch’n Stubn offers tea dancing. The Beatles Bar at Das Seekarhaus is run by the family of George Harrison's stunt double. Turmbar at the Gamsleiten 1 valley station is a popular tower bar.
resortCharacterDescription: Obertauern is a purpose-built resort village dedicated to ski tourism, consisting almost entirely of hotels, apartments, ski rental shops, restaurants, and bars. It lacks the historic alpine charm of traditional Austrian villages like Schladming or Kitzbühel. The village sits on the Radstädter Tauern Pass and is arranged along a main road, with accommodation on both sides offering direct slope access. The architecture is functional rather than picturesque, with concrete and modern buildings. The resort's layout is designed for ski-in/ski-out convenience, with no need for a car once arrived.
liftSystemDescription: The lift network comprises 26 modern lifts, mostly detachable chairlifts with weather-protection domes and heated seats. The Plattenkarbahn is a Telemix system combining Diamond 10-passenger cabins with Premium 8-seater chairs upholstered in loden fabric, with a capacity of 3,600 people per hour. The Grünwaldkopf and Plattenkar lifts are combination gondola/chairlifts. One area is served by a fixed-grip quad chairlift. The resort uses 25 snowcats to groom the pistes daily. Lift operation runs from approximately 8:30/9:00 AM to 4:00 PM, with early bird openings on selected days and night skiing on Mondays and Thursdays.
linkedResortsDescription: A lift pass valid for more than 1.5 days is also valid at the Grosseck-Speiereck ski area in Mauterndorf/St. Michael, approximately 15 km away, with a free hourly ski bus connection. The LUNGO pass covers four resorts on one card, including Obertauern and three neighbouring areas. The Tauern Ski Pass network provides access to Obertauern, Grosseck-Speiereck, Fanningberg, Katschberg, and other regions, allowing unlimited skiing across multiple ski areas without additional charges.

terrainDescription: The ski area consists of two main slopes: a 2.3 km north run (intermediate) and a 1.9 km south run (easy), both among the longest in northern Bavaria. The terrain is predominantly easy (62% of runs) with some intermediate (8%) and advanced (31%) sections. The slopes are partially covered by snowmaking (70% of terrain) and four runs are floodlit for night skiing until 10 pm. A snowboard park at Geiersberg and Hempelsberg in Oberwarmensteinach offers freestyle features. The vertical drop is 379 m from summit (1,024 m) to base (645 m).
valueDescription: Lift pass prices are low compared to Alpine resorts: a one-day adult pass costs €34, youth €27, and child €17. Accommodation in the surrounding villages averages around £113 per night. The resort is marketed as an accessible and budget-friendly option in northern Bavaria, with several ski rental stations and two ski schools offering lessons at competitive rates.
familyDescription: Two ski schools serve the area: Schneesportschule Nordbayern at the north base and Hottenroth Ski School at Mehlmeisel. The Nordbayern school runs a Nubi-Club for young children, using a dedicated practice area with themed features and short skis (65 cm). Children's downhill runs with separate lifts are available on both the north and south sides. The villages of Bischofsgrün, Fichtelberg, Mehlmeisel, and Warmensteinach offer family-friendly accommodation and flat nursery slopes near the valley stations.
apresSkiDescription: The Bleaml-Alm restaurant in Fichtelberg is a popular après-ski meeting place, located directly next to a ski lift. On the south side of the mountain, the party tent Hirscheck provides another venue for evening socialising. The cosy inn at the Bleaml-Alm winter sports centre is also noted as a gathering spot after skiing.
resortCharacterDescription: The resort is not a single purpose-built village but a collection of four traditional Franconian towns—Bischofsgrün, Fichtelberg, Mehlmeisel, and Warmensteinach—that nestle around the mountain. Bischofsgrün is a climatic health resort with a real, friendly town centre, historic value, and local shops. The area has a relaxed, authentic Bavarian character rather than a modern ski-station feel.
liftSystemDescription: Two detachable double chairlifts with weather shields serve the summit: the north chairlift from Bischofsgrün (built 1991) and the south chairlift from Warmensteinach (built 1997). Both take nine minutes to reach the top. An additional ten drag lifts serve lower slopes, including some with floodlighting for night skiing. Total lift capacity is 2,925 persons per hour. Snowmaking covers 70% of the slopes with 34 machines.
linkedResortsDescription: The Ochsenkopf ski area is part of the 'Ochsenkopf plus' tariff association, which includes additional slopes and lifts in Mehlmeisel, Fichtelberg-Neubau, Warmensteinach/Fleckl, Selb, and Immenreuth. Nearby, the Oberwarmensteinach area (Hempelsberg and Geiersberg) offers a snowboard park and additional runs. These linked areas are accessible with a common lift pass, expanding the total skiable terrain beyond the main Ochsenkopf slopes.

terrainDescription: The terrain at Ofterschwang-Gunzesried features a mix of gentle family runs and steep World Cup slopes. The blue run from Ofterschwanger Horn to the middle station of the Ossi-Reichert gondola is ideal for beginners. The red Märchenwiese run descends from Ofterschwanger Horn to Gunzesried valley. The black Weltcup slope is the most challenging, with two routes down to Ofterschwang. A terrain park under the summit offers freestyle elements.
valueDescription: A one-day adult lift pass costs €54.50, with youth passes at €41.50 and child passes at €24. The resort is part of the Hörnerdörfer ski pass network, which includes five villages and offers a combined pass for access to all lifts in the area, potentially providing better value for those wanting to explore multiple ski areas.
familyDescription: The resort has two dedicated beginner areas: a practice area at the valley station in Ofterschwang with a beginner lift, and a children's park at the mountain station of the Weltcup-Express, which offers panoramic views. The Ossi-Reichert gondola has slow boarding, making it easy for families. The nursery slopes are separate from main traffic. The resort is ranked as one of the top family-friendly ski areas in Germany.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski is low-key, with two bars and pubs in the resort. The Leitenbach Hütte near the base station becomes a meeting point for après-ski in the evenings. Several mountain huts offer local Allgäu specialties and sun terraces. The resort is not known for a lively nightlife scene.
resortCharacterDescription: Ofterschwang is a traditional Bavarian village in the Oberallgäu region, known for hosting World Cup alpine events. The resort is centered around the Ofterschwanger Horn mountain, with modern lift infrastructure. The village has a rural, alpine character with local restaurants and guesthouses. It is not a large purpose-built resort but a small municipality with a ski area.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of 7 lifts: two gondolas (including the 8-person Ossi-Reichert gondola), a high-speed 6-person chairlift (Gipfel 6er, built 2013), a high-speed 4-person chairlift (Weltcup-Express) with bubble covers, and three surface lifts. The Weltcup-Express takes 6 minutes to the summit, and the Ossi-Reichert gondola takes 8 minutes. All chairlifts have detachable technology for easy boarding. The lifts are modern and well-maintained.
linkedResortsDescription: Ofterschwang is part of the Hörnerdörfer ski pass network, which includes the villages of Balderschwang, Bolsterlang, Fischen, Obermaiselstein, and Ofterschwang. A combined pass gives access to all lifts in the area, totaling 35 km of pistes and 13 lifts. A free shuttle bus connects the villages.

terrainDescription: The ski area at Orcières Merlette 1850 offers 52 marked runs (10 green, 17 blue, 18 red, 7 black) spread across 100 km of pistes, with a vertical drop of 875 m from the summit at 2,725 m to the base at 1,850 m. The terrain is dominated by wide, sun-exposed slopes that benefit from a mix of Alpine and Mediterranean climates, providing good visibility and often blue skies. Long panoramic descents such as the blue Bouquetins and Freissinières runs start from the two highest points (Drouvet at 2,655 m and Freissinières at 2,725 m) and run all the way down to the resort. The area includes four dedicated beginner learning zones, two boardercross courses, and a 16,000 m² Fun Zone with a cable car and ski lift. 70% of the pistes are equipped with snowmaking, and the FIS-approved Camile Ricou downhill slope (created in 2014) allows for high-level training and competitions.
offPisteDescription: Off-piste skiing at Orcières Merlette is mostly on south-facing slopes, which can soften quickly after snowfall, but the high altitude (up to 2,725 m) and north-facing aspects around the Col de Freissinières can hold cold powder for days after a storm. The Le Gnourou chairlift at the top, often closed in high winds, opens up a good area of untracked sidecountry directly under the chair. A short 20-minute skin from the top lifts gives access to slack-country terrain with untracked lines. The resort sits on the edge of the Écrins National Park, offering ski touring potential via the Col de Freissinières, with a line down to the village of Freissinières. The sidecountry and touring potential are extensive, though the predominantly south-facing exposure means conditions are best shortly after a storm.
valueDescription: Lift pass prices for the 2025/26 season are €46 for an adult day pass and €255 for a six-day adult pass, with child passes at €38.50 per day and €216.50 for six days. Family passes range from €207.50 to €244.00. In spring, the ski pass is free for all children under 12. The Mon Pass Vallée offers access to five Champsaur resorts (Ancelle, Chaillol, Laye, St Léger, and Orcières Merlette 1850) with discounts of 20–30% on standard passes. Self-catering apartments and accommodation are located close to the slopes, keeping costs down. Parking in the resort now costs €8 per day, which has drawn criticism in reviews. The resort also offers multi-activity passes combining skiing with ice rink and swimming pool access.
familyDescription: Orcières Merlette is marketed as a family resort with 52 runs suitable for all levels, including 10 green and 17 blue runs. There are four dedicated beginner learning zones spread across the resort, providing safe, gentle areas for children to learn. The 16,000 m² Fun Zone includes a boardercross and a cable car, catering to all ages. The resort offers a swimming pool, ice rink, cinema, and a weekly market on Thursdays, giving families plenty of non-skiing activities. Ski school programmes are available, and the resort is compact enough that most services are within walking distance. Reviews highlight the friendly staff and the resort's suitability for families with young children, though some note that the village lacks a strong Christmas atmosphere.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski at Orcières Merlette is low-key and family-oriented, with no high-energy bar scene. The resort has a swimming pool, ice rink, cinema, and several bars and restaurants. The Palais des Sports swimming pool is a key après-ski facility. Reviews describe the atmosphere as quiet, with some noting a lack of après-ski buzz and loud music during the day. There is a market on Thursdays, and the resort offers activities like sledging and snowshoe walks. For those seeking a livelier scene, the resort is not known for nightlife; the focus is on relaxed evenings and family-friendly dining.
resortCharacterDescription: Orcières Merlette is a purpose-built ski resort constructed in the 1960s and opened in 1962, owned by Labellemontagne since the early 2000s. It is located on the edge of the Écrins National Park, 5 km from the village of Orcières. The architecture is typical of 1960s French resorts, with apartment blocks and modern amenities. The resort has a swimming pool, ice rink, cinema, and a large ski area. Reviews describe it as an 'old-fashioned resort' with some empty pizzerias and a lack of Christmas decorations, but also note the friendly shopkeepers and the beautiful mountain scenery. Parking charges were introduced in recent years, causing some discontent. The resort is car-friendly but offers free shuttles within the area.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system at Orcières Merlette consists of 26 lifts, including 3 gondolas, 2 high-speed six-person chairlifts, 2 quad chairs, 1 double chair, and 18 surface lifts. The lifts are modern and efficient, with a cable car serving the Fun Zone. 70% of the pistes are covered by snowmaking, ensuring reliable conditions. The lifts provide access to a vertical drop of 875 m, with the highest point at Col de Freissinières (2,727 m). The system is well-suited to the resort's layout, with beginner zones served by gentle lifts and the upper mountain accessed by chairlifts and gondolas. Queues are generally not a problem except during peak weeks.
linkedResortsDescription: Orcières Merlette is a standalone ski area not physically linked by lifts to other resorts. However, the Mon Pass Vallée provides access to four other Champsaur resorts: Ancelle, Chaillol, Laye, and St Léger les Mélèzes, with discounts of 20–30% on standard lift passes. These resorts are within a short drive (11–12 km) and offer additional terrain, but there is no direct ski connection. The wider Champsaur area thus functions as a multi-resort pass option rather than an interconnected ski domain.

terrainDescription: The Orelle-Val Thorens ski area sits almost entirely above 2,000m, with wide, open slopes above the treeline. The Orelle sector itself has 9 marked runs, including the 4km Lory track which is free of lift crossings. The terrain is predominantly blue and red runs, with a few black runs from the summit of Bouchet. Slopes face south-east, so conditions can become icy in the morning and slushy in the afternoon.
offPisteDescription: Off-piste terrain is abundant around the Cime Caron and Bouchet chair areas, with steep north-facing couloirs and wide open powder fields. The Combe de Caron and the Pierre Lory itinerary are popular off-piste routes. The high altitude ensures good snow quality, and the area is known for holding powder days after storms.
valueDescription: Orelle offers some of the most affordable accommodation in the 3 Vallées, with self-catering apartments from around €550 for a family of four for a week. The new Hob Orelle hostel provides half-board family rooms from €230 per night in peak season. Lift passes are cheaper than in the main 3 Vallées resorts, with a local Val Thorens-Orelle pass available. Parking at the gondola is free, and the village has free activities.
familyDescription: The nursery slope at Plan Bouchet has a magic carpet and is served by a dedicated beginner lift. The ski school offers lessons for children, and the Hob Orelle hostel is family-oriented with family bedrooms. The village is quiet and safe, with a free shuttle between hamlets and the gondola. Children under 5 ski free, and junior passes cover up to age 17.
apresSkiDescription: Apres-ski is very limited, with only a small bar near the gondola base and a crepe/pizza van. The Hob Orelle has a bar and restaurant, but there are no nightclubs or late-night venues. Most visitors return to their accommodation after skiing. The nearest nightlife is in Val Thorens, a 15-minute gondola ride away.
resortCharacterDescription: Orelle is a collection of 10 traditional Savoyard hamlets scattered between 880m and 1200m, with narrow cobbled streets and slate-roofed houses. The village has a quiet, authentic character, far from the purpose-built resorts of the 3 Vallées. The main hub is Francoz, where the gondola base, ticket office, and a few shops are located.
liftSystemDescription: The resort is served by a modern two-stage gondola from Francoz to Plan Bouchet (13 minutes) and then to Cime Caron (20 minutes total). The Orelle sector has three chairlifts: Rosael, Bouchet, and Peyron. Lifts are generally uncrowded, but the entire area can close in high winds due to the exposed high-altitude terrain.
linkedResortsDescription: Orelle is the 'fourth valley' of Les 3 Vallées, the world's largest lift-linked ski area. From the Orelle gondola, skiers can access Val Thorens directly, and from there the entire 600km 3 Vallées network including Les Menuires, St Martin de Belleville, Méribel, and Courchevel. The link is via the Cime Caron and Col de Rosael.

terrainDescription: The Ovindoli-Monte Magnola ski area comprises three main sectors: Le Fosse, Monte Freddo, and Magnola, all accessible directly from the resort. The 21 runs (32% green, 41% blue, 23% red, 4% black) total approximately 30 km of marked pistes. The resort is north-facing, with slopes between 1,450 m and 2,220 m. Night skiing is available on two runs: Dolce Vita and Topolino. A snowpark is split into two areas: Magnola Snow-Park at 1,980 m and the Lower-park at 1,650 m.
offPisteDescription: Off-piste skiing is available in the Sirente Velino Natural Park, which offers steep chutes and snowy bowls. The most popular off-piste area is Vallone delle Rose, providing a 700 m vertical descent. A local guide is recommended for safety when venturing off-piste.
valueDescription: A daily adult lift ticket in high season costs around 44–46 euros, dropping to about 33 euros at the end of the season. Full-day equipment rental is approximately 40 euros. The resort offers various ski pass options including daily, multi-day, monthly, and seasonal passes, as well as the 'Skipass dei Parchi' (covering Ovindoli, Campo Felice, and Gran Sasso d'Italia) and the 'Skipass Nevi Gemelle' (covering Ovindoli and Gran Sasso d'Italia). Accommodation and dining are generally more affordable than in major Alpine resorts.
familyDescription: Ovindoli is family-friendly with a pedestrianised centre, child-friendly restaurants, a cinema, and a sledging area. Family lift pass deals and discounts for children under 16 are available. The resort has a peaceful atmosphere and a wide array of family-friendly accommodations. In summer, an adventure park with seven routes for children and teenagers is open.
apresSkiDescription: The après-ski scene includes several bars and restaurants in the village centre, such as Bar del Corso, Bar Centrale (often hosting live music), and the rustic Rifugio La Capanna with mountain views. For nightlife, Discoteca Magnola and Disco Bar La Storia offer a lively atmosphere. Dining options include Ristorante Pizzeria La Capannina, Ristorante L’Angolo, and Ristorante La Stozza on the slopes.
resortCharacterDescription: Ovindoli is a historic Apennine village at 1,375 m, with a medieval core featuring the Porta Mutiati gate and the 15th–16th century Church of San Sebastiano. The village is not purpose-built; it retains traditional stone architecture and a compact layout. The resort area (Monte Magnola) is about 2 km from the historic centre. The overall atmosphere is relaxed and less crowded than larger Alpine resorts.
liftSystemDescription: The resort has 11 lifts: 1 gondola, 2 high-speed six-packs, 3 triple chairs, and 5 surface lifts. The lift system is described as state-of-the-art and modern, with a total uplift capacity of 12,000 skiers per hour. Snowmaking covers 13 miles of runs with 51 cannons. Night skiing is available on two runs.
linkedResortsDescription: Ovindoli is part of the 'Tre Nevi' ski area, linked via the 'Skipass dei Parchi' to Campo Felice and Gran Sasso d'Italia (Campo Imperatore). The 'Skipass Nevi Gemelle' also links Ovindoli with Gran Sasso d'Italia. Additionally, the resort is connected to the neighbouring resort of Rocca di Mezzo, allowing access to more extensive terrain.

terrainDescription: The ski area extends from 1,350 m to 2,500 m on a south-facing terrace above the Rhône valley. Lower slopes near the village are wide and gentle, suitable for beginners and families, while the upper mountain around Jorasse and Bougnonne offers more sustained, technical pitches with panoramic views of the Valais Alps and the Mont Blanc massif. The terrain is predominantly intermediate cruising runs, with a few steeper advanced sections.
offPisteDescription: User ratings describe a wide range of off-piste routes that can stay untracked for several days after a storm. The terrain is playful and includes tree runs and open slopes, though the overall off-piste area is relatively limited compared to larger resorts. Conditions depend heavily on fresh snowfall, and the resort occasionally enjoys powder days.
valueDescription: Lift passes are among the most affordable in the Valais canton: a one-day adult pass costs CHF 61, a six-day pass CHF 226, and a child day pass CHF 37. Budget accommodation is available, with several hostels and guesthouses, and a variety of lodging options to suit all budgets. The resort offers good value for a Swiss ski holiday.
familyDescription: Ovronnaz is ranked among the top 10 most family-friendly ski resorts in Valais. The lower slopes are wide and gentle, with a ski school known for quality teaching. Child-care facilities are excellent, including at least one reasonably priced nursery. Ski-in/ski-out accommodation is available, and the compact village is easy to navigate on foot.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski is low-key and centred on the thermal baths, which feature three pools (two outdoor) fed by natural springs at over 32°C, offering relaxation with mountain views. The resort has 2 bars and pubs, 1 club, and 11 restaurants ranging from casual to upscale. Some mountain restaurants operate on the slopes.
resortCharacterDescription: Ovronnaz is an authentic traditional alpine village with wooden chalets, family hotels, and restaurants with sunny terraces. It sits on a south-facing terrace above the Rhône valley, providing spectacular views and abundant sunshine. The atmosphere is calm and peaceful, unlike larger purpose-built resorts, and the village retains its Swiss character.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system comprises 8 lifts: 4 quad chairlifts and 4 surface lifts. Lower-mountain lifts are gentle and suitable for beginners, while chairlifts serve the upper slopes. Queues are rare outside peak weekends and school holidays, according to user ratings, and the lifts operate from 09:00 to 16:30.

terrainDescription: The Paganella ski area offers 50 km of pistes spread across two faces (Andalo and Fai), with 11 blue runs for beginners, 19 red intermediate runs, and 1 black expert run. The slopes are wide and long, with many gradient changes. The longest run is 5 km from summit to valley. A notable run is the Olimpionica 2, a black run with 1,000 m vertical drop used by the Norwegian Alpine Ski Team for training. Night skiing is available on the Cacciatori 1 piste twice a week. The terrain is set amidst thick fir forests with 14 mountain huts along the slopes.
valueDescription: Lift pass prices are generally lower than in neighbouring South Tyrol resorts. The Paganella ski pass is part of the Skirama Dolomiti network, allowing access to multiple resorts with a Combi or Superskirama pass. Online purchases receive a 5% discount. Accommodation ranges from 3-star to 4-star superior hotels, with many offering wellness centres. Self-catering apartments are also available. Group discounts are offered for parties of 20 or more paying persons.
familyDescription: The resort is well-suited for families, with four dedicated beginner areas (Rindole, Gaggia Fields, Meritz, and the ski school area) served by moving carpets and easy chairlifts.
