The terrain is characterized by wide, gentle slopes suitable for beginners and intermediates, with the summit at 2,260 m providing panoramic views of the Dolomites and Ortler Alps.
valueDescription: A day lift pass for the 2024/25 season costs €42 for adults, €29.50 for children, and children under 8 ski free. The resort is part of the Ortler Skiarena, which offers a larger area pass covering 15 resorts.
familyDescription: The resort offers a 3.7 km sled run starting from the mid-station, 3.5 km of cross-country skiing trails, and 30 km of winter hiking trails. A ski school is available. The slopes are predominantly easy (61% beginner), making it suitable for families with children. The gondola and surface lifts provide easy access.
apresSkiDescription: Apres-ski options include the Stube at the mountain hut and the Pizzeria Rittner Horn, offering a friendly atmosphere. Traditional mountain huts serve meals. The resort also hosts cultural events and Christmas markets during Advent.
resortCharacterDescription: The resort is located on the Ritten plateau, a mountain ridge above Bolzano with traditional villages such as Klobenstein and Oberbozen. The area is known for its earth pyramids and offers panoramic views of the Dolomites. The ski area is accessed via a gondola from the plateau, with the base at 1,530 m and summit at 2,260 m. The character is that of a local, family-oriented ski hill rather than a large purpose-built resort.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of three lifts: an 8-person gondola from 1,530 m to 2,070 m (2,230 m length), and two surface ski lifts (Rittner Horn from 2,022 m to 2,270 m, and Pennleger from 1,840 m to 2,010 m). The total uplift capacity is 5,600 skiers per hour. The gondola mid-station operates only in winter.
linkedResortsDescription: Rittner Horn is part of the Ortler Skiarena, which includes 15 ski resorts with a total of 123 marked pistes and 79 ski lifts. The linked resorts include Haideralm, Latsch Tarscheralm, Maseben, Mendel Ruffrè, Meran 2000, Pfelders, Reinswald, Schnalstal, Schöneben, Schwemmalm, Sulden am Ortler, Trafoi am Ortler, Vigiljoch, and Watles.

terrainDescription: The terrain is divided into three main sectors: Aremogna, Pizzalto, and Monte Pratello. Aremogna offers wide, sun-lit blue runs through pine forests, ideal for beginners and intermediates. Pizzalto provides a mix of red and black runs with steeper pitches, while Monte Pratello is the highest sector with steep black runs and off-piste opportunities through beech forests. The slopes are wide, sunny, and well-groomed, with a higher treeline than the Alps allowing tree skiing in poor weather. The longest run is 5 km, and the total skiable terrain is 81 km (OnTheSnow) or 110 km (Alto Sangro area).
offPisteDescription: Off-piste skiing is available around Monte Pratello and Pizzalto, with beech forest glades and steep terrain. The higher treeline allows skiing in trees during poor weather. The Aremogna area also has a dedicated snowpark for freestyle. Off-piste opportunities are not extensive but provide good variety for advanced skiers.
valueDescription: Lift passes are notably cheaper than Alpine resorts, with a four-day high-season pass from €202. Accommodation and dining in the traditional village are reasonably priced, and children under 16 get discounts on ski passes. The resort is less expensive than comparable French or Swiss resorts, making it a budget-friendly option for families and groups.
familyDescription: Roccaraso is well-suited for families with dedicated beginner zones at Aremogna and Pizzalto, gentle blue slopes, and ski schools offering English-speaking instructors. The pedestrianised village centre is safe for children. Off-slope activities include sledding, ice skating at the Palaghiaccio Bolino (Olympic-standard rink), and an adventure park. Children under 16 receive discounted lift passes, and the resort has kid-friendly restaurants.
apresSkiDescription: The apres-ski scene is lively but relaxed, with bars like Campitello Matese offering live music and Bar del Sole for drinks. Evening dining includes rustic trattorie serving local dishes such as arrosticini (grilled lamb skewers) and maccheroni alla chitarra. The town also hosts small concerts and festivals, and there are modern wine bars for a more refined evening.
resortCharacterDescription: Roccaraso is a traditional Italian mountain town with 19th-century character, rebuilt after WWII damage. The village features alpine chalets, cobbled streets, and a pedestrianised centre. It is not purpose-built but retains authentic charm, with a mix of family-run hotels, B&Bs, and self-catering apartments. The nearby villages of Rivisondoli and Pescocostanzo offer additional historic character.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system has been modernised recently, with new gondolas and chairlifts installed since 2017. The area has 24–28 lifts including a cable car, chairlifts, and surface lifts. The snowmaking system is one of the largest in Italy, covering over 80% of terrain, ensuring reliable conditions. Queues are rare outside Italian holiday periods.
linkedResortsDescription: Roccaraso is part of the Alto Sangro ski area, which includes five resorts: Barrea, Pescasseroli, Pescocostanzo, Rivisondoli, and Roccaraso. The area offers over 110 km of groomed pistes. The Roccaraso and Rivisondoli sectors are directly connected by lifts, with ski-bus links to the other resorts. The entire area is covered by a single skipass.

terrainDescription: The Roc d'Enfer ski area offers 32 pistes (2 black, 12 red, 11 blue, 7 green) totaling 51 km, served by 16 lifts. A signature 20 km circular circuit, the Tour du Roc d'Enfer, winds through snow-covered forests and open valleys, providing views of the Graydon valley, the Dents du Midi, and Lake Geneva. Two dedicated beginner areas exist: one at 1,500 m at the top of the Grande Terche gondola and another at La Chèvrerie in Bellevaux. Slopes range from 1,000 m to 1,800 m in altitude.
valueDescription: Lift passes are priced modestly: an adult day pass costs €40 at the ticket desk (€38 online), and a 6-day adult pass is €205 (€194.50 online). Parking at the base station is free. The ski area is included in the Portes du Soleil lift pass, so visitors with that pass can ski here at no extra cost. Restaurants such as L'Escale Blanche on the Saint-Jean-d'Aulps side and Café de la Chèvrerie in Bellevaux are noted for good value. Free shuttle buses operate from Morzine and Prodains during February and March school holidays.
familyDescription: The resort has two dedicated beginner areas (one at 1,500 m at the top of the gondola, one at La Chèvrerie) with gentle slopes. Low skier density makes the slopes feel safe for families. Free parking at the base and free shuttle buses from Morzine during school holidays add convenience. Off-slope activities include an outdoor ice rink. The ESF (French Ski School) offers ski and snowboard lessons in Saint-Jean-d'Aulps.
resortCharacterDescription: The Roc d'Enfer ski area is a small, family-oriented resort set in a wild and preserved environment between 1,000 m and 1,800 m. It is not a purpose-built high-rise village; instead, it consists of two access points: Saint-Jean-d'Aulps (a traditional Savoyard village) and Bellevaux-La Chèvrerie. The atmosphere is notably quiet and uncrowded, often described as a 'secret domain' with a serene, confidential feel. Parking is free at the base station.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system comprises 16 lifts: 1 gondola (Grande Terche), 4 chairlifts, and 11 drag lifts. Queues are rare, allowing more time on the slopes. The lifts serve 32 pistes over 51 km.
linkedResortsDescription: Roc d'Enfer is part of the Portes du Soleil ski area, which includes 12 interconnected Franco-Swiss resorts (Abondance, Avoriaz 1800, Champéry, La Chapelle d'Abondance, Châtel, Les Gets, Montriond, Morgins, Morzine, Saint-Jean-d'Aulps, Torgon, Val-d'Illiez – Les Crosets – Champoussin) offering 650 km of slopes. However, the Roc d'Enfer ski area itself is not physically connected by lifts to other resorts; a shuttle service (€1.60 per trip) links Saint-Jean-d'Aulps to the wider Portes du Soleil network.

terrainDescription: The Roc d'Enfer ski area has 32 slopes (8 green, 9 blue, 13 red, 2 black) covering 51 km, with a 20 km circuit called the Tour du Roc d'Enfer that winds through forest. The beginner area at 1500 m, reached by the Grande Terche gondola, has two button lifts on gentle green slopes. The Les Tetes chairlift serves an easy blue run and a more challenging red. The Col de Graydon offers a long blue run. The only black run is a short un-pisted forest section on the circuit. Slopes are tree-lined and often deserted, with views of the Dents du Midi and Lake Geneva.
offPisteDescription: The off-piste terrain around Roc d'Enfer is steep and avalanche-prone, with forest glades and powder stashes accessible with a local guide. The area is not heavily skied, so fresh tracks can be found after snowfall, but caution is essential. Hiring a local guide is strongly recommended; exploring alone is not advised.
valueDescription: Lift passes are affordable: a Roc d'Enfer-only adult day pass costs €40 (€38 online) and a 6-day pass costs €205 (€194.50 online). Parking at the base station is free. During February school holidays, a free shuttle runs from Prodains and Morzine to the resort, and in March a free minibus can be pre-booked. Restaurants like L'Escale Blanche on the Saint-Jean-d'Aulps side and Cafe de la Chèvrerie in Bellevaux offer good value meals.
familyDescription: The resort has dedicated beginner areas at the top of the gondola (1500 m) with gentle green slopes served by button lifts, and a separate beginner zone at Bellevaux-La Chèvrerie with green and blue runs. The slopes are uncrowded, making it safe for families. Off-slope activities include an outdoor ice rink, mountain kart descents (minimum age 14), and a free shuttle service during school holidays. The ESF ski school offers group and private lessons.
resortCharacterDescription: The Roc d'Enfer ski area is accessed from the traditional Savoyard village of Saint-Jean-d'Aulps, with a gondola lift rising from the village to the slopes. The resort is uncrowded and peaceful, with free parking at the base station. The area is known for its forested runs and views of the Dents du Midi and Lake Geneva. It is a family-friendly, relaxed alternative to the busier Portes du Soleil resorts.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of 1 gondola (Grande Terche telecabine), 4 chairlifts, and 11 drag lifts, totaling 16 lifts. The gondola takes skiers from the village to the beginner plateau at 1500 m. The Tour du Roc d'Enfer circuit uses mostly chairlifts and two button lifts, including the long TK Des Follys double button lift. Queues are rare.
linkedResortsDescription: The Roc d'Enfer is part of the Portes du Soleil ski area (650 km of slopes across 12 Franco-Swiss resorts) but is not directly lift-connected to other resorts. A single Portes du Soleil lift pass grants access to all resorts, and a shuttle bus (€1.60 per trip) connects Roc d'Enfer to the wider Portes du Soleil network. The area also includes the Bellevaux-La Chèvrerie sector.

terrainDescription: The Rofan ski area is a small, sunny high-mountain bowl above Maurach, reached by a five-minute cable car. The terrain is dominated by intermediate red runs (8 km out of 11 km), with a black piste (Rofanabfahrt) for advanced skiers and a long valley run (Skiweg/Talabfahrt) that descends 860 vertical metres. The easiest run is the Heimgrubenabfahrt. The slopes face the sunny side of the massif, with views over Lake Achensee. There is a practice area at Wasserboden near the top station.
valueDescription: A one-day adult lift pass costs €44.50, with a child pass at €26.50. The resort offers half-day tickets and beginner-area tickets, making it affordable for short visits. Accommodation is available in Maurach, a village on Lake Achensee, with self-catering apartments and hotels. Regional passes like Snow Card Tirol provide access to multiple areas.
familyDescription: The Rofan ski area is designed for families, with a dedicated children's area at the top station featuring Bobo's children's club, magic carpets, and a practice slope. In Maurach, about one kilometre from the bottom station, the local ski school runs a large children's ski area with conveyor belts. Additional activities include tobogganing, the Airrofan Skyglider (a flying machine reaching 80 km/h over 200 metres), snowtubing, and winter hiking. The ski school offers courses for all ages, and lunch supervision is available.
resortCharacterDescription: The Rofan ski area is a small, family-friendly resort perched above the traditional village of Maurach on the shores of Lake Achensee. The cable car takes five minutes from the village centre to the mountain station at 1,840m. The area is known for its sunny slopes and stunning views over the lake and the Karwendel and Rofan mountain ranges. It is not a purpose-built resort but a natural mountain setting with a few mountain huts and a relaxed, uncrowded atmosphere.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of one cable car (Rofan cable car) that takes five minutes from Maurach to the top station, two modern double chairlifts (Rofan and Mauritz), and two conveyor belts for beginners. The lifts are modern and efficient for the small area, with a capacity of 3,092 persons per hour. Queues are rare due to the low visitor numbers.
linkedResortsDescription: Rofan is a standalone ski area, but it is part of the Achensee region which includes three other small ski areas: Christlum in Achenkirch, Karwendelbahn in Pertisau, and lifts in Steinberg and Wiesing. These are not linked by lift, but a regional Snow Card Tirol pass provides access to multiple areas across Tyrol.

terrainDescription: The terrain at Rogla is set in the pine forests of the Pohorje Hills, with a total of 13.5 km of groomed slopes. The longest run is Jurgovo I at 1.32 km. The slopes are divided into 5.7 km of easy runs, 5.3 km of intermediate runs, and 2.5 km of advanced runs. The Jurgovo area features steep black runs, while the lower slopes offer wide, gentle terrain for beginners. There are also 24 km of cross-country ski trails.
offPisteDescription: Off-piste skiing opportunities exist in the forest around Jurgovo and Mašinžaga, particularly on powder days. The tree runs are not extreme but require caution, as the forest can be dense. These areas are best suited for experienced skiers and snowboarders looking for untracked snow.
valueDescription: Lift pass prices are moderate, with a one-day adult ticket at €47, a youth ticket at €43, and a child ticket at €30.50. Accommodation ranges from four-star hotels like Planja and Natura to three-star hotels, bungalows, and a youth hostel, offering options for different budgets. Dining in the resort is reasonably priced, with traditional Slovenian dishes available at mountain huts and restaurants.
familyDescription: Rogla is ranked as the number one family-friendly resort in Slovenia. The resort is car-free with a safe pedestrianised centre, and two nursery slopes are located in the village. There is a snow park, a sledding area, and a range of family activities including dog sledding, an alpine coaster, and a forest canopy trail. Ski-in/ski-out accommodation and child-friendly restaurants make it convenient for families.
apresSkiDescription: The après-ski scene in Rogla is relaxed and low-key, with a handful of bars and restaurants near the slopes. The Alpine Pearl bar at the bottom of the Planja slope is a popular spot for a drink. The resort does not have a party atmosphere, but there are enough options for a quiet evening meal or a drink after skiing.
resortCharacterDescription: Rogla is a purpose-built resort that opened in 1975, but it has a charming character with wooden chalet-style accommodation. The resort is car-free, with snowmobiles and snowcats as the only vehicles. It is set in the pine forests of the Pohorje Hills, offering a peaceful and relaxed atmosphere. The architecture blends with the forest environment.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system at Rogla comprises 13 lifts, including two quad chairlifts and a six-seater chairlift with heated seats and protective cabins. The remaining lifts are surface lifts (T-bars and button lifts). The total lift capacity is 13,500 skiers per hour, and queues are rare. The Mašinžaga six-seater chairlift is the most modern, providing comfortable access to the main slopes.

terrainDescription: The ski area at Rosskopf/Monte Cavallo features predominantly advanced (red) terrain, with 68% of runs classified as red, 27% as intermediate (blue), and 5% as expert (black). There are 10 named runs including the 5 km descent to Sterzing, the 3 km Telfer run, the 2.7 km Favorit Variante, and the 1 km Training slope. A separate practice area with a magic carpet is available for beginners at the top station. The slopes mostly run below the treeline and are described as wide and well-prepared.
valueDescription: A one-day adult lift pass costs €61, with youth at €55 and children at €43. The Rosskopf ski area is part of the Wipptaler Skiverbund, which with a single pass gives access to 50 km of slopes across Rosskopf, Ladurns, Ratschings-Jaufen, and Kalcher Alm. It is also part of the Ortler Skiarena, a 15-area association with over 300 km of slopes in South Tyrol.
familyDescription: The resort is well-suited for families, with a dedicated Kinderland area for children and beginners, a children's ski school that holds a GOLD quality seal, and a covered beginner lift. The 10 km illuminated toboggan run, the longest in Italy, is a major family attraction, with night sledding on Fridays until midnight. The slopes are described as wide and uncrowded, and the valley station is a short walk from the town centre.
apresSkiDescription: Apres-ski in Sterzing is centred on the historic pedestrian zone with traditional inns and restaurants. The mountain huts like Rossstodl and Enzianhütte offer South Tyrolean specialties. For fine dining, the restaurant Kleine Flamme in Sterzing holds a Michelin star and two Gault Millau toques. The Biwak pizzeria-pub at the valley station provides a casual option. Night sledding on the illuminated toboggan run runs until midnight on Fridays.
resortCharacterDescription: Rosskopf is the local mountain of Sterzing (Vipiteno), a historic town in South Tyrol with a medieval pedestrian zone. The ski area is accessed from a valley station just a 5-minute walk from the town centre. The town offers traditional architecture, boutique shops, and a range of accommodation in the historic centre.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of a 6-person gondola (Rosskopf Cable Car) that rises 910m over 2.8 km, a Panorama Telemix 6/8 chairlift, and the Stock chairlift. There are also two magic carpets for beginners. The gondola station is a 5-minute walk from Sterzing town centre and operates from 08:30 to 17:00.
linkedResortsDescription: Rosskopf is part of the Wipptaler Skiverbund, which links it with Ladurns, Ratschings-Jaufen, and Kalcher Alm, offering a combined 50-66 km of slopes on a single pass. It is also part of the larger Ortler Skiarena, a 15-area association with over 300 km of slopes across South Tyrol.

terrainDescription: The ski area has 25 km of pistes with blue, red, and black runs. The Stafellift takes skiers to 2,600 m where a wide black piste descends. A 10 km run through the trees leads to the valley. The slopes are well-groomed. The terrain is limited but offers a couple of easy black runs and lovely blue runs.
valueDescription: Lift passes are affordable: a day pass for adults costs 41 CHF, for children 24 CHF. A six-day pass is 212 CHF for adults. Accommodation includes affordable chalets and holiday flats. The resort is marketed as a good-value skiing area.
familyDescription: The resort is family-oriented with a Kidspark featuring a magic carpet at the mountain station. The Swiss Ski and Snowboard School offers group and private lessons for children. The car-free village and ski-in/ski-out accommodation make it convenient for families. The lifts are particularly suitable for families with small children.
resortCharacterDescription: Rosswald is a car-free village on a sunny terrace above the Rhone valley. The village has a quaint atmosphere with holiday flats, a mountain hotel, and restaurants with sunny terraces. It is not a purpose-built resort but a traditional alpine settlement.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of a 6-seater gondola from Ried-Brig to the village (7 minutes), plus four T-bar lifts serving the slopes. The Stafellift reaches 2,600 m. The lifts are T-bars, which may be less comfortable for snowboarders. The gondola operates year-round.

terrainDescription: The ski area covers 30 km of slopes with 20 runs: 2 green, 5 blue, 12 red, and 2 black. The longest run is 3 km. The terrain is fairly quiet, with slopes that are not crowded. From the top, there are views of the Mediterranean Sea on clear days. The resort's base is at 1,410 m and summit at 1,920 m, giving a vertical drop of 520 m.
valueDescription: Roubion-Les-Buisses was ranked the cheapest ski resort in Europe by Holidu, with a daily lift pass costing €20 and average accommodation per person per night at €20, totalling €40 per person per day. Ski lessons start from €27 per hour. The Bus 100% Neige ski bus from Nice costs €14 return. Self-catered gîte accommodation starts from €450 per week for four people. The family-run Hotel Restaurant Le Chalet des Buisses offers doubles from €60 per night.
familyDescription: The resort is family-friendly with a dedicated luge area and a tobogganing area ideal for families. New for 2025-2026 are Mountain Cart on snow, virtual paragliding, and dog-sledding. Ski lessons start from €27 per hour and can be booked at the tourist office. The slopes are quiet and suitable for beginners, with green runs spreading out from the base. The resort also offers yoga sessions and knitting workshops during school holidays.
apresSkiDescription: There is no lively apres-ski scene; the resort is quiet and peaceful. A few restaurants serve Savoyard specialities such as fondue and raclette. The atmosphere is relaxed, suitable for families and those seeking a calm evening.
resortCharacterDescription: The village of Roubion is a traditional perched village built on a rockface, with cobbled streets, traditional stone houses, and a 12th-century church. The ski resort is located at the bottom of the village, with a scattering of pretty, pitched-roof chalets. The village has a Mediterranean feel and is car-free in the centre. Accommodation includes family-run hotels and self-catered gîtes.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of 6 lifts: one quad chairlift and five surface lifts. The lifts are modern and efficient, providing quick access to the slopes. The resort has no gondolas or cable cars.

terrainDescription: The ski area at Saas-Almagell (Furggstalden/Heidbodme) consists of 14–15 km of pistes, predominantly red (intermediate) graded runs. The main attraction is a top-to-bottom red run descending over 700 vertical metres from the summit at 2,400 m to the village at 1,672 m. The red runs under the Furggstalden chairlift are in shade and can become icy in the afternoon. A long winding blue run also descends to the base but is less enjoyable. The summit area at Heidbodme offers limited but scenic terrain with a glass lodge and panoramic views. The beginner area at mid-mountain (Furggstalden) features a gentle slope serviced by a button lift and an Adventureland with an igloo, tubing area, and mini skidoos.
offPisteDescription: Off-piste terrain at Saas-Almagell is limited and not recommended for freeriding. The area outside the marked runs is wild, rocky, and cliffy, with no designated freeride zones. The summit area offers some steep faces but they are not easily accessible and are more suited to expert mountaineers. Most skiers stick to the groomed pistes.
valueDescription: Saas-Almagell is one of the more affordable ski destinations in the Swiss Alps. A full-day adult lift pass costs CHF 55 (2024/25 prices), significantly cheaper than nearby Zermatt or Saas-Fee. The resort is included on the Swiss Magic Pass (399 CHF for access to 40 resorts), offering excellent value for multi-resort skiers. Accommodation in the village is generally less expensive than in Saas-Fee or Zermatt, with self-catering apartments and family-run hotels available. The free bus service between Saas-Almagell and the other Saas Valley resorts adds further value by eliminating transport costs.
familyDescription: Saas-Almagell is particularly well-suited for families with young children. The mid-mountain area at Furggstalden features Kians Adventure Land, a dedicated children's zone with a carousel for practicing pull lifts, a snow tube run, themed obstacle courses (e.g., Paw Patrol), a slalom track, and mini skidoos for an extra charge. An igloo with couches and a TV provides a cozy break spot. The beginner slope is gentle and serviced by a button lift, with friendly staff assisting young children onto the chairlift. The village is quiet and car-accessible, with a free bus connecting to Saas-Fee and Saas-Grund for additional family activities. The Saas-Almagell ski pass offers discounted rates for children (6–15 years: CHF 33 full day) and free skiing for under-6s.
apresSkiDescription: Apres-ski in Saas-Almagell is low-key and relaxed. The village has a few bars, including the Groovy Bar in the centre, and restaurants such as Pizzeria da Rocco. For a livelier scene, visitors take the free bus to Saas-Fee, which offers bars like the Fee-Iglu-Bar and Black Bull Bar with music and a terrace. Night skiing is available in nearby Saas-Grund on a floodlit blue run (Ziebel T-bar) between December and February, by prior booking. The village also has a natural ice rink for skating and Bavarian curling, and a 3-km floodlit toboggan run from Furggstalden.
resortCharacterDescription: Saas-Almagell is a traditional Swiss mountain village at the southern end of the Saas Valley, with a population of around 370. It retains an authentic, unpretentious character, with a small town square containing a grocery store, bank, and souvenir shops. The village is car-accessible (unlike car-free Saas-Fee) and consists of a mix of traditional chalets and modern apartment blocks. The surrounding landscape is dominated by 4,000-metre peaks including the Allalinhorn and Weissmies. The village has a youth hostel and sports centre. It is less picturesque than Saas-Fee but offers a quieter, more local atmosphere.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system at Saas-Almagell consists of 6 lifts, including two slow double chairlifts and several surface lifts. The main chair from the village to mid-mountain is an old-school two-person chair that takes about 9.5 minutes. The summit chair (Heidbodme) also runs at modest speed. The Furggstalden button lift serves the beginner area. The lifts are sufficient for the terrain but are not modern or high-speed. Queues are rare due to low visitor numbers. The resort is not connected by lift to Saas-Fee; a free bus is required to access the larger Saas Valley ski area.
linkedResortsDescription: Saas-Almagell is part of the Saas Valley (Saastal) ski region, which includes Saas-Fee, Saas-Grund, and Saas-Balen. The resorts are not connected by ski lifts; instead, a free public bus service runs between them, taking about 15 minutes to Saas-Fee. A combined lift pass (Destinationspass or Magic Pass) grants access to all 150 km of pistes in the valley, including the glacier terrain at Saas-Fee (up to 3,600 m) and the Kreuzboden area in Saas-Grund. The Saas-Fee glacier offers year-round skiing and the world's highest revolving restaurant.

terrainDescription: The terrain is dominated by wide, gentle blue and red runs above the treeline, with a 1,750m vertical drop from the glacier summit to the village. The ski area is compact at 100 km of pistes, with roughly 20 km easy, 60 km intermediate, and 20 km difficult terrain. Upper slopes offer long, flowing reds and blues with glacier and rock wall views, while lower slopes include tree-lined runs. The Allalin glacier sector provides high-altitude cruising, and the Plattjen area has a few short, ungroomed ski routes. The village beginner zones are on the valley floor, and progression to higher blue runs is straightforward.
offPisteDescription: Off-piste terrain is limited close to the lifts because the ski area is hemmed in by glaciers. There are about five short, avalanche-secured ski routes, mostly in the Plattjen sector, which require good snow cover. The steep natural terrain above the glacier is glaciated and crevassed, so off-piste skiing typically requires a mountain guide. Ski touring possibilities are extensive, with eighteen 4,000-metre peaks surrounding the valley offering a vast Alpine wilderness for guided tours and variants of the Haute Route.
valueDescription: Saas-Fee is an upmarket resort in Switzerland, an expensive country. It is not an exclusive luxury resort like St Moritz or Gstaad, but those on a tight budget will struggle to keep within it and should look elsewhere. Accommodation and dining are priced accordingly, and the car-free village adds to the premium feel. There are no specific budget-friendly packages mentioned in the source text.
familyDescription: The car-free village is compact and walkable, making it safe and easy for families. The Stafelwald beginner area is gentle and well maintained, with an inexpensive beginners' pass. Ski schools (Swiss Ski School Saas-Fee and Eskimos Ski School) have high standards and English-speaking instructors. The village has a leisure centre with a pool, a marmot zoo, a toboggan run, and an ice rink. The resort is family-friendly rather than chaotic, with a quiet, relaxed atmosphere.
apresSkiDescription: Saas-Fee has a lively but not rowdy apres-ski scene. There are lots of bars, some with live bands, and a few proper nightclubs. High-quality gourmet restaurants are available. Nighttime tobogganing on the Hannig mountain and the ice rink are popular evening activities. The overall vibe is good bars and food with a buzz, then people drift into cosy evenings rather than all-night chaos.
resortCharacterDescription: Saas-Fee is a traditional, car-free Swiss village surrounded by thirteen 4,000-metre peaks and glaciers. It has narrow twisting streets, wooden chalets, and a compact layout that is walkable in minutes. The village is not purpose-built; it has a genuine Alpine character with a quiet, intimate atmosphere. Some buildings are less attractive, but overall it is one of the most charming ski resorts in Europe.
liftSystemDescription: The lift network is modern and efficient, with 23 lifts including gondolas, cable cars, chairlifts, and surface lifts. The Metro Alpin funicular takes skiers directly into the glacier at 3,500m. The Alpin Express lift near the village centre improves access. The highest lift reaches 3,573m. Lifts are practical for high terrain and glaciated zones, with quick access to upper sectors. Queues are minimal outside peak weeks.
linkedResortsDescription: Saas-Fee is part of the Saas Valley (Saastal) ski area, which includes Saas-Grund (Hohsaas) and Saas-Almagell, linked by a free bus service with the SaastalCard. The combined area offers about 150 km of pistes. Saas-Grund has 40 km of pistes with gentle blues to steeper reds and blacks, and Saas-Almagell has 30 km of wide, gentle slopes. The Saastal ski pass covers all three areas, and there are separate passes for individual resorts. Zermatt is about 1 hour 15 minutes away by car and train, but is not lift-linked.

terrainDescription: The Hohsaas ski area above Saas Grund offers wide, gentle slopes ideal for intermediates, with two main pistes from the summit at 3,200 m. The terrain is carved into a rocky, glacial landscape; off-piste areas between prepared runs are wild and inhospitable, consisting of harsh cliffs and debris. Mid-mountain at Kreuzboden (2,400 m) features a T-bar and poma serving a fun park, skier cross course, and a family learning area. The lower mountain has a single long run (piste 7) that may not be skiable to the base in low snow years, requiring a gondola download. The resort has 23 km of marked pistes (24% beginner, 64% intermediate, 11% advanced).
offPisteDescription: Off-piste skiing is limited to a single marked yellow route from the summit and some unmarked terrain between pistes. The terrain is rocky and glacial, making it challenging and requiring caution. According to visitor ratings, off-piste remains fresh for one to two days after snowfall, with some routes staying untracked longer. A guide is recommended for serious off-piste.
valueDescription: Saas Grund offers more affordable accommodation than nearby Saas-Fee and Zermatt. Budget options include hostels and pensions, with a variety of self-catering apartments. The Montela Hotel & Resort provides modern condo-style apartments at reasonable rates. Lift pass prices are lower than in larger resorts; a day pass is around CHF 62 (as per skiweather.eu). The village is small, so dining and shopping costs are moderate.
familyDescription: Saas Grund is a quiet, family-friendly resort with a dedicated family learning area at Kreuzboden, including a fun park and skier cross. The ski school offers English-speaking instructors and is praised for its patience with children. Childcare facilities are available, with at least one reasonably priced nursery. The village is small and walkable, with ski-in/ski-out accommodation options. The nearby Saas-Fee is car-free, adding safety for families.
apresSkiDescription: Apres-ski in Saas Grund is low-key, centered on a few bars and restaurants such as the Saas Grund Bar and Restaurant Pizzeria Alpin. The atmosphere is relaxed, with visitors typically enjoying a glass of wine by the fire rather than dancing. For livelier nightlife, Saas-Fee is a short bus ride away and offers more bars and clubs.
resortCharacterDescription: Saas Grund is a traditional Swiss village with narrow streets and old chalets, though it is less picturesque than its neighbour Saas-Fee. The village is not car-free, but it is compact and walkable. The architecture is a mix of traditional wooden chalets and modern apartment buildings. The setting is dramatic, surrounded by 4,000 m peaks and glaciers.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of a two-section gondola from the village to Kreuzboden (2,400 m) and then to Hohsaas (3,200 m). There are also a T-bar and poma at mid-mountain. Total of 6 lifts with an hourly capacity of 11,000 skiers. The lifts are modern and queues are rare outside peak periods. A free ski bus connects Saas Grund to Saas-Fee and Saas-Almagell.
linkedResortsDescription: Saas Grund is part of the Saastal ski region, which includes Saas-Fee and Saas-Almagell. The three resorts are connected by a free ski bus rather than lifts, and a combined Saastal ski pass gives access to over 150 km of pistes across all three areas. Saas Grund's own ski area (Hohsaas) is independent but linked by bus.

terrainDescription: The St-Luc Chandolin ski area spans from 1,680 m to 3,000 m, offering about 60–65 km of groomed pistes. The terrain is dominated by wide, well-groomed blue and red runs above the treeline, with a south-west aspect that provides abundant sunshine but can soften snow later in the season. The longest run is a 6 km red from the Bella Tola summit down to Le Prilet. Black runs account for roughly 17% of the marked slopes and are mostly short, with steeper pitches found on the Illhorn and Bella Tola pomas. The ski area is split between two villages—St-Luc and Chandolin—connected by the Le Roste chairlift, allowing skiers to explore multiple mountain faces.
offPisteDescription: Off-piste skiing is a major draw, with a vast expanse of north-facing terrain that holds powder well after storms. The Illhorn and Pas de Boeuf sectors offer steep couloirs and wide open faces, accessible from the top lifts with short hikes. Guided descents are recommended for the more technical lines, and the area between pistes provides plenty of untracked snow for experienced skiers. The high altitude (up to 3,000 m) and north-facing aspects mean good snow quality can persist for days after a snowfall.
familyDescription: St-Luc Chandolin is well suited to families, with a pedestrian-friendly village, ski-in/ski-out accommodation, and a dedicated beginner area at Tignousa with gentle nursery slopes. The ski school offers English-speaking group lessons for children from age four, and the 'Swiss snow kids village' provides a secure learning zone. A kindergarten, Les P'tits Filous, accepts children from 18 months to 6 years. Off-slope activities include a natural ice rink at Prilett, a 7 km sledge run from the top of the funicular, snowshoe trails, and the François-Xavier Bagnoud Observatory for stargazing.
apresSkiDescription: The après-ski scene is quiet and relaxed, centred on a handful of cosy bars and cafes in St-Luc and Chandolin. The Tignousa Planetarium offers a unique evening activity with stargazing and hot drinks. Most restaurants serve traditional Valais cuisine such as raclette and fondue, and the atmosphere is family-friendly rather than rowdy. Nightlife is limited compared to larger resorts, with no nightclubs; the focus is on winding down after a day on the slopes.
resortCharacterDescription: St-Luc and Chandolin are traditional Swiss mountain villages with narrow winding streets, historic wooden chalets, and a preserved alpine character. St-Luc sits at 1,650 m on a sunny balcony of the Val d'Anniviers, while Chandolin is higher at 1,936 m and even more rustic. The architecture is authentic, with many buildings dating from the 19th century, and the villages have avoided large-scale purpose-built development. The setting offers panoramic views of the Imperial Crown peaks including the Matterhorn, Weisshorn, and Dent Blanche.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system comprises 15 lifts, including a funicular (St-Luc–Tignousa, built 1994) that carries skiers from the village to 2,180 m in a few minutes. The rest of the network is a mix of modern high-speed chairlifts (e.g., Le Roste quad, Foret chairlift) and older T-bars and platter lifts, some of which are notably long and steep (e.g., Bella Tola poma, Illhorn poma with a 60% gradient). The funicular operates over 300 days a year, and the system generally runs efficiently, though queues can form during peak weeks. The link between St-Luc and Chandolin is seamless via the Le Roste chairlift.
linkedResortsDescription: St-Luc and Chandolin are connected to form the Saint-Luc–Chandolin ski domain. The same Val d'Anniviers lift pass also grants access to the neighbouring ski areas of Grimentz-Zinal and Vercorin, which lie on the opposite side of the valley. Together, the three areas offer a total of 210–220 km of groomed pistes. A free hourly bus service links the resorts, with a journey time of about 30 minutes by car or one hour by bus between St-Luc and Grimentz-Zinal.

terrainDescription: The ski area is compact with 7 km of pistes, predominantly gentle green and blue runs suitable for beginners and families. A gondola from the village centre provides access to the slopes, and a chairlift reaches the summit at 1743 m, where a few red runs offer progression for intermediates. The terrain is set in forested surroundings within the Chartreuse Regional Nature Park.
valueDescription: Lift passes are very affordable, with a 6-day adult pass costing around €113.50 in high season and children's passes at €79.50. The resort is known for its budget-friendly prices compared to larger Alpine resorts. Accommodation and dining are also reasonably priced, and the resort's proximity to Grenoble allows for day trips without expensive lodging.
familyDescription: The resort is particularly well-suited for families with young children, offering a snow nursery and ski lessons from age 4. The slopes are predominantly gentle greens and blues, with a gondola from the village providing easy access. The compact layout and peaceful atmosphere make it stress-free for parents. Off-slope activities include sledging and snowshoeing.
resortCharacterDescription: Saint-Pierre-de-Chartreuse is a historic alpine village that has developed into a small ski resort. The village centre retains traditional stone and wood buildings, with a baroque church and local shops. The ski lifts, including a gondola, depart from the heart of the village, giving it a convenient village-resort character. It is not purpose-built but rather a genuine mountain community.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of 10 lifts, including a gondola from the village centre, three chairlifts, and several drag lifts. The lifts are simple and functional, providing direct access to the slopes. The resort is small, so queues are rare outside peak periods.
linkedResortsDescription: The resort is part of the Coeur de Chartreuse ski area, which also includes the Le Planolet sector. The combined area offers additional slopes, though the Saint-Pierre sector itself has 7 km of pistes. The wider Chartreuse massif contains several small ski areas, but the main linked domain is Coeur de Chartreuse.

terrainDescription: The ski area of Sainte-Anne la Condamine is located on the Massif du Parpaillon, with slopes between 1830m and 2400m. The terrain is characterized by gentle, wide slopes, with 13 marked runs: 6 green, 4 blue, 2 red, and 1 black. The longest run is 3 km. The majority of the terrain is suitable for beginners and intermediates, with 20% beginner, 46% intermediate, 23% advanced, and 11% expert according to OnTheSnow. The slopes offer panoramic views of the surrounding mountains and are mostly south-facing, benefiting from good sunshine.
offPisteDescription: Sainte-Anne la Condamine is known as a freerider's paradise, with off-piste terrain that includes powder snow between fir trees and open slopes above the treeline. The resort offers long off-piste runs that can be linked together for a full day of skiing. The gentle gradient of the mountain makes the off-piste accessible for intermediate skiers looking to venture off the groomed runs. Tree skiing is a highlight, with gladed areas providing protection from wind.
valueDescription: Sainte-Anne la Condamine offers some of the most affordable skiing in the French Alps. A day lift pass costs from €21, and weekly accommodation starts at €410 for a studio apartment. The resort is described as having an unbeatable price-quality ratio, with low-cost lift passes and reasonably priced restaurants. Parking is free at the base of the slopes, and the resort's small size means no queues for lifts, saving time and money.
familyDescription: The resort is well-suited for families, with a dedicated snow garden (jardin des neiges) for young children and a family toboggan area. The ski school offers lessons for all ages, and the gentle green and blue runs provide safe learning terrain. The village is compact and traffic-free, making it easy to walk to the slopes. Family activities include sledging, snowshoeing, and cross-country skiing. The resort's family-friendly atmosphere is frequently praised in reviews.
apresSkiDescription: Apres-ski at Sainte-Anne la Condamine is low-key and focused on dining. There are several restaurants in the village offering local cuisine, such as oven-baked ravioles and fumeton (dried lamb). Bars are quiet, with no loud nightlife. The resort's small size means that après-ski activities are limited to a few cosy venues. Night skiing is occasionally offered as a special event.
resortCharacterDescription: Sainte-Anne la Condamine is a traditional mountain village resort, retaining its pastoral roots and authentic alpine character. The village features traditional architecture with wooden chalets and stone buildings. It has been awarded the 'Station Village des Alpes du Sud' label, recognizing its commitment to preserving local heritage and a family-friendly atmosphere. The resort is not purpose-built; it has grown organically from a farming community.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of 7 lifts: 1 quad chairlift, 1 double chairlift, and 5 surface lifts (téléskis). There is also a magic carpet for beginners. The chairlifts are not high-speed; the quad chair is noted as being slow and exposed to wind, making it cold on windy days. The surface lifts are the primary means of accessing the upper slopes. The lift network is adequate for the size of the ski area, with no queues even on weekends.
linkedResortsDescription: Sainte-Anne la Condamine is directly connected to Val d'Oronaye, which serves as a gateway to Italy. The resort is also in close proximity to other Ubaye Valley resorts such as Pra-Loup, Sauze, and Barcelonnette, though these are not linked by lifts. The wider area offers access to the Espace San Bernardo via Val d'Oronaye, but the connection may require a short transfer.

terrainDescription: The Sainte-Foy-Tarentaise ski area has 26 marked runs covering 2 green, 7 blue, 12 red, and 4 black pistes, with a vertical drop of 1,100m from 1,550m to 2,620m. The terrain is north-west facing, with the upper half of the mountain forming a treeless powder bowl and the lower trails winding through larch forests. Runs like Grand Solliet and L'Aiguille offer long red descents, while the Morion and Crystal Dark blacks provide steep challenges. Three marked but ungroomed Natur' runs (Natur'Crystal Dark, Natur'Morion, Natur'Shaper Paradise) allow controlled freeride practice. A boardercross and a freestyle area with multiple difficulty levels are also present, along with a playful sliding area called Nuts & Foxy on the La Chapelle slope.
offPisteDescription: Sainte Foy is renowned for off-piste skiing, with the Fogliettaz descent offering a sustained 1,500m vertical drop from the north face. The upper slopes are north-facing with a steep, even pitch, holding powder days after a storm. Tree skiing is a highlight on storm days, and the backcountry extends into the Italian valleys of Valgrisenche and Val d'Aosta via the summit ridge. Heli-skiing is permitted, with drops from around 3,400m on the Ruitor glacier. Three marked but ungroomed Natur' runs provide a safe environment to practice off-piste skills. Ski touring is popular, with achievable summits between 3,000m and 3,800m. A qualified guide is recommended for the best lines, including the legendary Fogliettaz and routes like Le Monal.
valueDescription: Sainte Foy offers affordable lift passes compared to neighbouring resorts: a six-day adult pass costs around €240 with a seventh day free, and children under eight ski for free. Dynamic pricing can reduce a day pass to as low as €28 on quiet days. Skiing is free for all beginners on the village slopes using the two magic carpets. Accommodation and dining are well-priced, with self-catered apartments and chalets available. The resort is noticeably cheaper than Val d'Isère or Tignes for lift passes, food, and accommodation.
familyDescription: Sainte Foy is family-oriented with two free magic carpets on the village nursery slopes, allowing beginners to try skiing without a lift pass. The Plan Bois area provides gentle progression terrain. The resort has a compact layout with ski school meeting points, hire shops, and restaurants within walking distance. Children's ski schools include ESF with Club Piou Piou for ages 3-5, and Les P’tits Trappeurs runs activities for children 3-11. Chairlifts are equipped with stopper bars for safety. The Nuts & Foxy sliding area on the La Chapelle slope features playful modules. Children under seven ski for free, and under-fives get free lift passes.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski in Sainte Foy is low-key and intimate. Yeti Boots Bar is the main gathering spot, often buzzing from late afternoon with live music. L’Après offers terrace beers. Dining options include Black Diamond for modern French dishes and La Maison à Colonnes for traditional Savoyard cuisine. Le Monal is a long-established restaurant serving Savoyard classics. There is no late-night party scene; for livelier nightlife, Val d'Isère is a short taxi ride away.
resortCharacterDescription: Sainte Foy is a purpose-built resort from the 1990s, but it was designed to blend with traditional Savoyard architecture using wood-and-stone chalets with slate roofs. The village is compact and walkable, with most accommodation offering ski-in/ski-out access. It is calm, family-oriented, and uncommercialised, with a classified hamlet featuring historical houses with sculpted pillars and chapels. The resort preserves a traditional alpine character, in contrast to the larger purpose-built stations in the Tarentaise.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of 6 lifts: 4 chairlifts and 2 magic carpets. The main chairlifts include the L'Aiguille chairlift serving the upper mountain. Two of the chairlifts are older and slower, but the system is efficient for the terrain. The La Marquise lift provides access to additional blue runs. Lifts operate from 9am to 5pm. The resort has snowmaking on 100% of slopes below 2,100m, ensuring coverage on lower runs.
linkedResortsDescription: Sainte Foy is within 30 minutes' drive of Val d'Isère, Tignes, Les Arcs, and the cross-border French-Italian domain of La Rosière/La Thuile. Shuttle services run to these resorts during the season. Holders of a six-day or longer Sainte Foy lift pass receive discounts on day passes in Paradiski, La Rosière, Val d'Isère, and Tignes. The backcountry also links to the Italian valleys of Valgrisenche and Val d'Aosta, with heli-skiing available on the Ruitor glacier.

terrainDescription: The ski area is set in a natural cirque, making navigation easy. Slopes are south-facing, receiving generous sunshine. Terrain is balanced: 27% beginner, 37% intermediate, 29% advanced, 7% expert. Runs include wide blue cruisers, green scenic trails, and black runs in the Lauzière sector. The area has 70 km of pistes locally, with 30 km covered by snowmaking.
valueDescription: Lift passes are affordable compared to larger resorts. A 6-day adult pass for the Grand Domaine costs around €235, or €209 for the local area only. Accommodation is reasonably priced, with many self-catering apartments and budget-friendly options. The resort offers family rates and early-booking discounts. Dining and ski rental are also competitively priced.
familyDescription: Saint François Longchamp holds the Famille Plus certification. It features dedicated children's areas: Opoualand with themed trails, La Tanière de Boris fun slope, and La Comète toboggan run. Childcare is available from 36 months, with a nursery and club for children. The ESF ski school has 40 instructors, and there are multilingual options. The resort has a mascot, Goupy the fox, who participates in activities. Ski-in/ski-out accommodation is available, and the compact layout makes it easy for families.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski is low-key. There are 4 bars and cafés, 1 disco, and a cinema. The resort is quiet in the evenings, appealing to families and those seeking relaxation. Dining options include 21 restaurants, with local Savoyard cuisine. The scene is more about a relaxed drink or meal than partying.
resortCharacterDescription: The resort consists of several small hamlets, giving it a traditional, uncommercialised character. It is not a purpose-built station but grew organically from a mountain parish. The architecture is Savoyard, with wooden chalets and stone buildings. The resort has a balneotherapy centre and modern amenities, but retains an authentic alpine feel. It is south-facing and sunny.
liftSystemDescription: The local lift system has 15 lifts: 3 high-speed six-packs, 4 quad chairs, and 8 surface lifts. Some lifts are older and can be slow or prone to breakdowns, especially during peak periods. The Grand Domaine connection adds more lifts, but locally the network is modest. Queues are rare outside school holidays.
linkedResortsDescription: Saint François Longchamp is part of the Grand Domaine ski area, which connects it to the resort of Valmorel. The combined area offers 165 km of slopes with 48 lifts. The link allows skiers to explore both valleys, with a variety of terrain. Additionally, the area is sometimes referred to as linking Doucy and Celliers, but the primary connection is with Valmorel.

terrainDescription: The ski area includes a black run as the most demanding, along with red and blue runs. The longest possible descent is 2 km. A baby park with a moving walkway is available for beginners. The resort has 6 runs in total.
familyDescription: The resort has a baby park equipped with a moving walkway to help children familiarise with snow and skis. A skating rink is also available on site. The resort is recommended for beginners and families.
apresSkiDescription: There are two dining options on site. After skiing, visitors can go to Bar des Neiges or Le Chalet Savoyard for food and drink, and Club de la Montagne for nightlife.
resortCharacterDescription: A small, intimate resort established in the 1970s, located on the extreme southern edge of the western Alps. It hosted the Italian Alpine Ski Championships in 1981. The village is described as picturesque and cosy.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of 4 lifts: one double chairlift and three surface lifts. The total uphill capacity is 5,300 skiers per hour.
linkedResortsDescription: The resort is not part of a larger linked ski area. Nearby resorts include Sangiacomo di Roburent and Garéssio 2000, but they are not connected by lifts.

terrainDescription: The ski area at Sampeyre comprises 11 named runs totaling 14 km of skiable terrain, with a longest run of 2 km. The runs are predominantly red (intermediate) and black (advanced), including Chamartin (1,931 m red), SUS (1,338 m red), S. Anna (1,174 m red), Agonistica (405 m black), and Cros (698 m black). There is also a short baby run (152 m) classified as red. The slopes wind through forests of larch and stone pines, with a vertical drop of approximately 860 m from the summit at 1,851 m to the base at 947 m. According to OnTheSnow, the terrain breakdown is 17% beginner, 17% intermediate, 49% advanced, and 17% expert, though the piste list shows no dedicated beginner runs.
familyDescription: Sampeyre is family-friendly, with a baby run (152 m) and dedicated sledding and bobsleigh areas. Ski lessons with qualified instructors are available. Off-slope activities include an ice skating rink, snowshoeing trails, and bobsleigh tracks. The village is described as suitable for families and outdoor lovers.
resortCharacterDescription: Sampeyre is a traditional alpine village located in the Varaita Valley at an elevation of 1,000 m. It offers a relaxed, family-friendly atmosphere with local charm. The ski station is a short distance from the village, and the area is known for its natural environment and local food, including dairy products and traditional Ravioles.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of 4 lifts: 2 double chairlifts and 2 surface lifts (ski lifts or carpets). No gondolas or high-speed lifts are present. The lifts serve a vertical drop of about 860 m, connecting the base at 947 m to the summit at 1,851 m. The lifts are described as efficient but are not modern high-speed models.

terrainDescription: The terrain at Sangiacomo Cardini is concentrated on the Monte Alpet side, accessed by a double chairlift to 1,611m. Beginner slopes are located at the summit, served by a magic carpet and the Cronista skilift. Intermediate and advanced skiers use the chairlift and the Pianea and Alpet skilifts, which serve medium-difficulty runs and three black runs named Mozzi, Gialletto, and Durelli. The slopes offer views from the Alps to the Gulf of Genoa.
valueDescription: Lift passes are notably affordable: a daily adult high-season ticket costs €24, and children under 6 ski for free. The resort is marketed as particularly suitable for families and young people due to these low prices. Accommodation is available in the immediate vicinity, and the resort offers a ski school, equipment rental, and two mountain huts.
familyDescription: The resort is specifically recommended for families and young people. Children under 6 ski for free, and there is a dedicated beginner area at the summit of Monte Alpet served by a magic carpet and a skilift. A ski school and equipment rental are available, and the season includes a calendar of musical and children's events. The village of San Giacomo di Roburent is nearby with accommodation.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system on the Cardini-Monte Alpet side consists of one double chairlift, three skilifts (Cronista, Pianea, Alpet), and one magic carpet. The double chairlift also serves pedestrians and operates in summer for the bike park. The Colmé side, which had a separate chairlift and two skilifts, is closed due to fire damage. The total lift count is 5 on the Cardini side.

terrainDescription: The ski area is split into three zones: the main Tognola area (peak 2,385 m at Cima Tognola) with a mix of blue and red runs, Passo Rolle with groomed runs served by five lifts, and the Col Verde gondola which serves a single slope also used for night skiing (4 km of night skiing available). The Fantasia 2000 run is a 5 km red slope from 2,345 m down to 1,411 m, combining sections of Cima Tognola, Cigolera, and Tognola-Tre. Other named runs include the red Rekord (1,350 m, 366 m vertical), Colbricon (1,460 m, 405 m vertical), Cristiania (1,980 m, 464 m vertical), and Cigolera (778 m, 189 m vertical), plus black runs Tognola 1 (1,040 m, 315 m vertical) and Paradiso I (1,030 m, 320 m vertical). Slope orientation is 34% east-facing, 26% south, 25% north, and 15% west. The terrain is predominantly intermediate (46% of marked runs) with 48% beginner and 7% advanced. Snowmaking covers 58 km of the 60 km of slopes.
valueDescription: A six-day adult lift pass costs approximately €263, with a single-day adult pass at €70 and a child day pass at €23. Visitor reviews consistently note that prices for accommodation, dining, and lift passes are roughly half those of nearby resorts such as Cortina d'Ampezzo and Selva Val Gardena. The resort is described as a bargain, with affordable hotels and guesthouses. Self-catering apartments and family-run hotels are common, keeping overall trip costs low compared to the major French and Swiss resorts.
familyDescription: A dedicated beginner area sits on the edge of town, separate from the main slopes, providing a safe learning zone. The ski school requires advance booking as it often reaches capacity. The village is compact with shops, bars, and restaurants, but the main lift bases are a short bus ride away (a free skibus service operates). The resort is rated as family-friendly, with gentle slopes and a snowpark for adventurous children. Cross-country ski trails (30 km) are also available.
apresSkiDescription: The apres-ski scene is quiet, with most restaurants and bars closing by 6:00 PM. There are no real restaurants on the slopes, though mountain huts serve local specialties during the day. The village has a few bars and cafes, but nightlife is subdued. Visitor ratings give apres-ski a score of 3.2 out of 5.
resortCharacterDescription: The resort is a traditional mountain village set within the UNESCO-listed Dolomites, surrounded by the serrated limestone spires of the Pale di San Martino. The village center has shops, bars, and restaurants, but some parts of the resort feature large, modern buildings that detract from the alpine charm. The scenery is the main draw, with dramatic views from every slope. The village is compact and walkable, with a relaxed atmosphere.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system comprises 22 lifts: 3 gondolas, 14 chairlifts (including a recent high-speed six-seater Cigolera chairlift that takes 3.5 minutes from bottom to top), and 5 surface lifts. Total uphill capacity is 26,800 persons per hour. The lifts are modern and efficient, with queues rarely forming outside peak weeks. The main drawback is that all lift bases are a short bus ride from the town center, though the free skibus service is frequent and reliable.
linkedResortsDescription: The San Martino di Castrozza ski area is directly linked to Passo Rolle, forming a combined 60 km ski area with 22 lifts. It is part of the Dolomiti Superski network, which with a single pass provides access to 12 ski areas totaling 1,220 km of slopes, including Cortina d'Ampezzo, Kronplatz, Alta Badia, Val Gardena, and the Sellaronda circuit. However, these other areas are not physically connected by lifts from San Martino and require road travel (typically 30–60 minutes by car).

terrainDescription: The ski area is split into three sectors: the main zone at Cima Tognola (2,385 m), the Ces area, and Passo Rolle. The main zone offers a selection of blue and red runs, with the longest run being Fantasia 2000, a 5 km red run descending from 2,345 m to 1,411 m. Passo Rolle features a collection of groomed runs linked by five lifts, and the Col Verde gondola serves a single slope that is also open for night skiing. The terrain is predominantly intermediate (53% red runs), with 27% easy and 20% difficult runs. The slopes are within the Paneveggio-Pale di San Martino Nature Park and face south-east, receiving long hours of sunshine. Specific black runs include Tognola 1 (1,040 m, 315 m vertical) and Paradiso I (1,030 m, 320 m vertical). The area is best suited to beginner and intermediate skiers, with limited expert terrain.
offPisteDescription: The resort has a growing reputation as an under-the-radar freeride destination. Local mountain guides offer guided off-piste tours, and the Ski Club of Great Britain's Freshtracks program visits the resort to explore the freeride terrain. Visitor ratings on snow-forecast.com give off-piste a score of 4.3 out of 5. The terrain around the Pale di San Martino provides opportunities for off-piste skiing, though no specific marked off-piste routes are detailed in the source text.
valueDescription: Lift pass prices are relatively affordable compared to other Dolomites resorts. A one-day adult ski pass costs €67 (peak season online price), with a six-day pass at €348. Visitor reviews note that the resort costs about half of Cortina or Selva. Accommodation is described as very good value, with most hotels and guesthouses being affordable. The resort is considered a bargain for the quality of scenery and skiing.
familyDescription: The resort is well-suited for families, with two dedicated children's zones featuring conveyor belts and practice lifts for ski lessons. The Rododendro Minipark offers a snow- and funpark tailored to children and beginners, with jumps, tunnels, and colourful installations. Ski school is available but requires advance booking due to popularity. The resort is described as ideal for families, with a free skibus connecting the village to the lift bases. The nursery slopes are separate from faster traffic, providing a safe learning environment.
apresSkiDescription: The après-ski scene is relatively quiet. Several après-ski spots near the lifts offer music and drinks, but many restaurants close by 6:00 PM, and there are no restaurants on the slopes. The town has a selection of bars and restaurants, but the overall atmosphere is sleepy rather than lively. Visitor ratings for après-ski average 3.2 out of 5 on snow-forecast.com.
resortCharacterDescription: San Martino di Castrozza is a historic mountain resort with origins dating back to a 19th-century hospice. The first alpine hotel was built in 1873, and by the 1900s it was a destination for the Austro-Hungarian elite. The village is situated at 1,487 m in a valley of green meadows within the Paneveggio-Pale di San Martino Natural Park, surrounded by the Dolomite peaks of the Pale di San Martino. The resort was rebuilt after World War I and features a mix of historic buildings and some modern structures. The church of San Martino with its Romanesque bell-tower remains from the original hospice. The scenery is dominated by the serrated limestone spires of the Pale.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of 22 lifts: 3 gondolas, 14 chairlifts, and 5 surface lifts. A new six-seater chairlift, Cigolera, has been added on Alpe Tognola, reducing travel time to three and a half minutes. The lifts are modern and efficient, with a total capacity of 30,650 persons per hour. However, the lift bases are not directly in the village; a free skibus service connects the town to the three sectors (Tognola, Ces, and Passo Rolle). The Col Verde gondola serves a single slope that is also illuminated for night skiing.
linkedResortsDescription: The San Martino di Castrozza – Passo Rolle ski area is part of the Dolomiti Superski network, which includes 12 ski areas with 1,220 km of slopes and 450 lifts. The area is directly connected to Passo Rolle, a small ski area with five lifts and groomed runs. The wider Dolomiti Superski pass grants access to other renowned resorts such as Cortina d'Ampezzo, Kronplatz, and the Sellaronda circuit, though these are not physically interconnected and require road travel.

terrainDescription: Sansicario sits on sunny, south-facing slopes with wide, open pistes that are well-groomed. The local terrain is predominantly intermediate, with smooth, flowing runs ideal for cruising, while beginners have gentle learning areas near the village centre. Advanced skiers can tackle runs like the Fraiteve and Moncrons, and the resort also features the former Olympic women's downhill course. The signature piste is a long, tree-lined run popular with intermediates. The Cesana-Sansicario sector offers long descents, and the slopes are sheltered and rarely crowded.
offPisteDescription: Off-piste skiing is accessible on the Fraiteve massif, which offers wide, easy-access terrain suitable for budding powderhounds. The area is known for its open slopes that hold snow well after storms, though the off-piste is not as extensive or challenging as in some neighbouring resorts. Most off-piste opportunities are found above the treeline, with gentle gradients that make it approachable for intermediates looking to venture off the groomed runs.
valueDescription: Sansicario is part of the Via Lattea, often described as one of the most affordable mega ski areas in the Alps. A six-day ski pass costs around €256 (as of 2026) and includes one day across the border in Montgenèvre, France. Food and drinks on the slopes are notably reasonable compared to French resorts, with hearty Italian meals at rifugios. Accommodation is mostly self-catering apartments, which keeps costs down, and the resort is popular with budget-conscious families and school groups.
familyDescription: Sansicario is well-suited to families, with dedicated beginner areas and gentle nursery slopes right by the village centre. The resort has a Baby Fun Park with inflatable structures to introduce young children to skiing. Ski schools and rental shops are conveniently located, and the calm, uncrowded environment makes it easy for parents to supervise. Ski-in/ski-out accommodation is common, reducing the need to carry equipment. The resort comes alive on weekends with local families, but midweek it remains quiet and relaxed.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski in Sansicario is limited midweek, with a handful of bars and restaurants such as Bar Restaurant des Alpes and Ristorante La Baita offering a relaxed atmosphere. The resort livens up on weekends when local families visit, but it is not a destination for those seeking vibrant nightlife. For more energetic options, nearby Sestriere has a lively scene with clubs like Tabata Disco. Most evenings in Sansicario are quiet, suited to skiers who prefer an early night.
resortCharacterDescription: Sansicario is a purpose-built, modern resort consisting mainly of apartment blocks grouped around a small shopping precinct. It is self-contained and offers ski-in/ski-out convenience, but lacks traditional alpine charm, being described as rather soulless. The village is a quieter alternative to nearby Sestriere, with a calm atmosphere and stunning views of the surrounding mountains. The original hamlet of San Sicario lies lower down and has some traditional stone buildings, but the main resort is entirely modern.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system in Sansicario is modern and efficient, with the Sansicario Express gondola providing quick access from the village to the upper slopes. The resort's lifts are part of the wider Via Lattea network, which totals 70 lifts with a capacity of over 75,000 people per hour. Snowmaking covers 40 km of runs with 950 cannons, ensuring reliable conditions. The local lifts connect seamlessly to neighbouring resorts such as Sestriere and Sauze d'Oulx, allowing skiers to explore the entire Milky Way area without taking off their skis.
linkedResortsDescription: Sansicario is part of the Via Lattea (Milky Way) ski area, one of Europe's largest interconnected ski regions, linking seven resorts across Italy and France. The linked resorts include Sestriere, Sauze d'Oulx, Cesana, Claviere, Pragelato, and Montgenèvre in France. All are connected ski-on-ski-off by a network of lifts, giving access to over 400 km of pistes. A single Via Lattea ski pass covers the entire area, allowing skiers to cross the border into France and explore diverse terrain from high-altitude cruising to long valley runs.

terrainDescription: The ski area is on the slopes of Monte Sobretta (3296m), mostly above the tree-line. The Seggovia chairlift in sunny Valle del l'Alpe extends the terrain to 3000m. Most pistes are accessed via a two-stage, 8-person tele-cabin from the village at 1738m. The resort has 16 runs: 2 blue (2 km), 12 red (27 km), and 2 black (6 km). Intermediates drop over the ridge for long reds back to the village; advanced skiers take black runs alongside the gondola dropping over 1000m. Cross-country trails run through the forested lower slopes.
offPisteDescription: Off-piste skiing is accessed via a chairlift near the top of the gondola, providing access to couloirs. The area becomes expansive after fresh snowfall, but requires local knowledge and avalanche checks. The resort is within Stelvio National Park, offering unspoiled terrain. Reviews note that fresh tracks are still possible days after a dump, and the off-piste is underutilised.
valueDescription: A 6-day adult lift pass costs approximately €226, with a day pass at €56. Mountain restaurants are noted for reasonable prices compared to French resorts. Accommodation ranges from budget hostels to luxury hotels, with a variety of options suitable for all budgets. The resort offers ski-in/ski-out lodging and a free ski bus to Bormio, expanding the value of the lift pass.
familyDescription: The resort has a dedicated children's area with treadmills and ski school instructors. However, there is no dedicated beginner's area; gentle slopes are accessed from the top of the gondola or chairlift. The village is quiet and family-friendly, with a focus on safety. Child-care facilities are available, and the resort is described as a great quiet family resort by visitors.
apresSkiDescription: The resort has 4 restaurants and 5 bars. The après-ski scene is cozy, focusing on local Valtellina cuisine and wine. The atmosphere is relaxed rather than rowdy, with a focus on mountain dining and local food. Visitors mention amazing local food and wine in a nature reserve setting.
resortCharacterDescription: Santa Caterina is a traditional Italian village in the Valfurva Valley, within Stelvio National Park. It is described as pretty and user-friendly, with a focus on unspoiled nature. The village has a few hotels and restaurants, and is less crowded than nearby Bormio. The resort is a typical alpine village, not purpose-built, with a traffic-free centre and ski-in/ski-out accommodation.
liftSystemDescription: The main access is via a two-stage 8-person gondola from the village. The Seggovia chairlift extends to 3000m. The lift system includes gondolas, chairlifts, and surface lifts. Ski-in/ski-out accommodation is available. The lifts are well-maintained and the resort has a high rating for ski-in/ski-out convenience.
linkedResortsDescription: Santa Caterina is linked to Bormio, Cima Piazzi–San Colombano, and Livigno via a free bus service. A multi-day skipass gives access to all three (or four) areas, covering 110 km of slopes and 36 lifts. The Bormio Skipass includes Santa Caterina, Bormio, and Cima Piazzi; the Alta Valtellina pass adds Livigno for over 200 km of slopes.

terrainDescription: San Vigilio's local slopes are limited to a few short runs, but the resort provides direct gondola access to the expansive Kronplatz ski area. Kronplatz features wide, well-groomed pistes across a high plateau at 2,275 m, with long red runs such as the 7 km Ried descending to Perca. There are also challenging black runs including Piculin (max gradient 71%) and Erta, which hosts World Cup giant slalom events. The terrain is predominantly intermediate-friendly, with runs facing multiple directions and extensive snowmaking ensuring reliable conditions.
familyDescription: San Vigilio offers dedicated nursery slopes and gentle runs suitable for beginners, with a gondola from the village providing easy access to the wider Kronplatz area. The resort is known for its quiet, relaxed atmosphere, making it suitable for families with children. The village has a few shops and excellent restaurants, and the slopes are directly accessible from the village.
apresSkiDescription: The après-ski in San Vigilio is relaxed and informal, with many good bars and fabulous restaurants serving South Tyrolean cuisine. There are no nightclubs, so the scene is quiet and family-oriented. Visitors can enjoy cozy bars and mountain huts on the slopes, but the village does not have a rowdy nightlife.
resortCharacterDescription: San Vigilio is a traditional South Tyrolean village with mountain charm, featuring a quiet and relaxed atmosphere. The village has a few shops and excellent restaurants, and the slopes are directly accessible from the village via gondola. It is a family-friendly resort with a focus on skiing and outdoor activities rather than bustling nightlife.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system at San Vigilio and Kronplatz is modern and efficient, with 32 lifts including high-speed gondolas and heated chairlifts. The Miara gondola from San Vigilio village provides direct access to the plateau. Lifts are generally queue-free, and the system can move over 70,000 people per hour. The resort also features the 10-passenger Helmjet Sexten gondola, one of the most modern lifts in the Alps.
linkedResortsDescription: San Vigilio is directly connected to the Kronplatz ski area via gondola. A ski bus service runs every 20 minutes from the Piculin base to Alta Badia, linking to the Sella Ronda circuit and the wider Dolomiti Superski network of 1,200 km of pistes. The resort is also part of the Dolomiti Superski pass, which provides access to 12 ski regions including Cortina, Val Gardena, and Arabba.

terrainDescription: The San Vito di Cadore ski area offers nine named slopes: Donarié (easy), I Prati (easy), Caprioli (moderate), Ciastel (difficult), Brosolas (easy), Tambres (easy), Bianca (moderate), Antelao – Cino Menegus (moderate), and Serpentina (moderate). The terrain is predominantly easy to moderate, with one black run. The area sits at the foot of Monte Antelao in a sunny, south-facing position. The NeveSole Park provides a dedicated family area with treadmills, toboggan runs, and inflatable play structures. Slopes are groomed daily and benefit from snowmaking coverage.
valueDescription: A day lift pass for the San Vito di Cadore area costs €41 for adults and €21 for children (as of 2023). Multi-day passes are not available for the local area; only day, morning, and afternoon passes are sold at the ticket offices. A valley skipass covering San Vito, Cortina d'Ampezzo, and Auronzo-Misurina (120 km of slopes) is available, as is the Dolomiti Superski pass (1,200 km). A free on-call skibus operates within the municipality, reducing transport costs. Accommodation options include traditional hotels and apartments, with prices generally lower than in nearby Cortina.
familyDescription: The resort is marketed as a family destination, with the NeveSole Park offering a dedicated snow play area with two treadmills, two toboggan runs, bobsleighs, rubber dinghies, a school field with learning routes, and an inflatable play area. The ski school (Tambres Ski School) provides lessons and equipment rental on the slopes. The free skibus service connects the village center and parking areas to the lifts. The slopes are mostly easy to moderate, suitable for beginners and intermediates. The village itself is quiet and walkable, with traffic-free areas.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski in San Vito di Cadore is low-key. Local bars include Bar Miro, and restaurants such as Ristorante Pizzeria La Pieve serve hearty meals. The atmosphere is relaxed rather than lively, with no large nightclubs. Visitors seeking more vibrant nightlife can drive 9 km to Cortina d'Ampezzo.
resortCharacterDescription: San Vito di Cadore is a traditional alpine village in the Dolomites, with historic stone and wood buildings, a church dating to the 15th century (Church of the Blessed Virgin of Defense), and quiet streets. It is a year-round tourist destination, located 9 km from Cortina d'Ampezzo on the SS51 Alemagna road. The village sits at the foot of Monte Antelao (3,264 m). It retains an authentic, non-purpose-built character, with a small town center and local amenities.
liftSystemDescription: The ski area is served by a chairlift (Tambres), a surface lift (Donarié), and the NeveSole Park lifts. The total uphill capacity is approximately 3,520 persons per hour. Lifts are mostly surface lifts, with one chairlift. The area is small and suitable for families and beginners. A free on-call skibus connects the village to the lifts. The lifts are part of the Dolomiti Superski network, but the local area is limited.
linkedResortsDescription: San Vito di Cadore is part of Area No. 1 of the Dolomiti Superski network, together with Cortina d'Ampezzo and Auronzo-Misurina. A valley skipass provides access to 120 km of slopes across these three resorts. The wider Dolomiti Superski pass covers 1,200 km of slopes in 12 valleys, including famous areas like Val Gardena, Alta Badia, and Kronplatz. The nearest linked resort is Cortina d'Ampezzo, 9 km away by road.

terrainDescription: The ski area has 15 km of pistes, with 30% beginner, 53% intermediate, and 17% advanced runs. The slopes are mostly blue and red, well-maintained, and not too crowded. School zones are located in the centre of the village. Two chairlifts, Miravalle and Hochbolt, lead to higher altitude red and black slopes, while the Telecabina Sappada 2000 gondola provides access to a sunny area with views.
valueDescription: A one-day adult lift pass costs €44, with youth passes at €10 and children free, making Sappada one of the three cheapest ski resorts in Veneto. The resort offers good value for families and beginners, with affordable lift tickets and a range of accommodation options.
