liftSystemDescription: The lift system includes 7 lifts: 1 gondola, 1 high-speed six-seater chairlift, 1 quad chairlift, and 4 surface lifts. In 2024, a new 10-seater panoramic gondola (Loser Panorama Gondola) replaced the Loser-Jet 6-seater chairlift and the Loser 2 double chairlift, connecting the base station, mid-station at Augstalm, and summit station at Loser Alm. Lifts are equipped with hoods and heated seats for comfort. Wait times are short due to modern infrastructure.
linkedResortsDescription: Loser-Altaussee is part of the Schneebärenland tariff association, which includes Tauplitz, Planneralm, and Riesneralm. A single ski pass grants access to these resorts. The wider Ausseerland/Salzkammergut region also offers over 200 km of cross-country trails and other winter activities. Nearby ski areas include Dachstein Krippenstein and Tauplitzalm.

terrainDescription: The ski area offers a variety of runs including the Lella downhill run from the Mönichkirchner Schwaig, a black FIS downhill run of 1,450 m that leads into the 1,380 m Marienseer run, and a 2.2 km easy valley run. The Hallerhaus run and the Schwaiglift area include a fun park with jumps, rails, and a carving slope. The slopes are wide, particularly the family slope bypass, and there are gentle beginner slopes and a dedicated children's area. The terrain is suitable for all levels, with a mix of easy, intermediate, and advanced runs.
valueDescription: The resort uses a dynamic pricing system where online purchases are cheaper than on-site. Early bird bonuses and reduced weekday prices are available. Season passes for the 2024/25 season cost €380 for adults and €180 for children during the early bird period (September–October), rising to €475 and €236 respectively. Family discounts apply when purchasing two adult passes. Day tickets are available in 2-hour and 4-hour options. The resort offers reasonable prices for day and half-day excursions.
familyDescription: The resort has two dedicated children's areas: the Kinderland on the Mönichkirchner Schwaig with a platter lift and fun figures, and the valley area (Seiser's schräge Wiese) with two conveyor belts, a ski carousel, and a toboggan slope. Chairlifts are equipped with child safety locks. The Lella Tunnel, a giant milk can, is a highlight on the valley run. Ski schools are available directly on the mountain, and special prices are offered for school ski courses. The slopes are wide and clearly laid out, making navigation easy for families.
resortCharacterDescription: Mönichkirchen is a small market town in Lower Austria with a population of around 600, situated on the east side of the Wechsel mountain with 68% forested area. The ski area originated in 1949 with the first chairlift and expanded through a fusion of Mönichkirchen and Mariensee in 2000. The resort has a traditional alpine character with rustic huts and a family-friendly atmosphere, not a purpose-built resort.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of three modern 4-seater chairlifts (Sonnenbahn, Schwaigbahn, and Panoramabahn), a platter lift built in 2018, and a rope tow for children. The chairlifts are equipped with child safety features. Free Wi-Fi is available at all lift stations. A new service and rental station was built at the Mönichkirchen valley station in 2021. The resort also offers a ski movie track and photopoint.
linkedResortsDescription: The ski area is a connection between the villages of Mönichkirchen and Mariensee, known as the Schischaukel Mönichkirchen-Mariensee. The two areas were merged in 2000 and are linked by several lifts, allowing skiers to move between them. The combined area offers a variety of slopes and lifts.

terrainDescription: The Nebelhorn ski area offers 13 km of marked slopes, with a mix of blue (4.8 km), red (5.1 km), and black (4 km) runs. The upper bowl near the summit has four groomed slopes, while the main intermediate terrain is at Seealpe and Koblat. The valley run from the summit to Oberstdorf is 7.5 km long, the longest in Germany, and is rated red-black, requiring advanced skills on its lower section through forested trails. Beginner areas are located at Speicherteich and Koblat with gentle blue runs, and at Seealpe there is a dedicated learning area with a trainer tow-line and a short T-bar. The slopes face predominantly east (41%) and north (19%), with 13% being steep and 10% very steep. Snowmaking covers 65% of the area.
offPisteDescription: Off-piste skiing is available in the upper bowl and around the summit, but conditions are highly dependent on recent snowfall. During hard-packed periods, off-piste is not enjoyable, but after a fresh snowfall the terrain is described as 'absolutely insane' and provides hours of fun. The Seealpe valley also offers small off-piste areas in soft snow when conditions allow. The resort has a freeride rating of 4.0 out of 10 from user reviews, indicating moderate off-piste potential. No designated freeride zones or marked off-piste routes are mentioned.
valueDescription: A six-day adult ski pass in the Oberstdorf Kleinwalsertal region costs €342.80 in low season and €360.80 in high season, with a day pass for adults at €68–€69. Children's day passes are €29, and children under 5 ski free. Parking at the Nebelhorn valley station costs €6 per day. The resort is considered relatively expensive for its size by some visitors, but the 2-country ski pass provides access to 130–150 km of slopes across seven mountains. Budget accommodation is available in Oberstdorf, though finding lodging in winter can be challenging due to popularity.
familyDescription: The Nebelhorn is family-friendly with dedicated beginner slopes at Speicherteich and Koblat, and a learning area at Seealpe with a trainer tow-line and short T-bar. The NTC Winter Adventure Park at Seealpe offers snow tubing, snow bikes, air boards, and other fun activities. A natural toboggan run of 2.5 km starts at Seealpe. Families benefit from a special offer: only the first child pays for the lift ticket; additional children ride free. The Berggasthof Seealpe restaurant has a see-through bar allowing parents to watch children playing on the nearby playground. Ski schools are available, including NTC Oberstdorf with locations at multiple ski areas.
apresSkiDescription: Apres-ski in Oberstdorf centers on bars such as Schantossibar and Traube Winterstadel, which offer live music and a lively atmosphere. The annual winter festival at Kurpark features apres-ski music. The village also has several restaurants, including the Michelin-starred Maximilians and the Italian restaurant Ai Quattro Canti known for its limoncello. The scene is described as relaxed rather than rowdy, with a focus on good food and drink after skiing.
resortCharacterDescription: Oberstdorf is a traditional Bavarian alpine village, the highest town in Germany, known for its historic center and the Heini-Klopfer-Skiflugschanze ski flying hill. The Nebelhorn cable car departs from the edge of town, with parking and easy access. The village has a lively, friendly atmosphere with many hotels, restaurants, and shops. It is not a purpose-built resort but a genuine mountain town with alpine charm.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of a modern 10-person gondola (Adler7) installed in 2021, which takes skiers from the valley (828 m) to the Höfatsblick station (1,932 m) without needing to change at the intermediate Seealpe station. From Höfatsblick, a large-capacity cable car (Gipfelbahn) continues to the summit at 2,224 m. The original Nebelhorn cable car, built in 1930 and rebuilt in 1977 and 1991, operates in three sections. In total, there are 7 lifts (1 gondola, 1 cable car, 2 chairlifts, 1 T-bar, 1 drag lift, and 1 surface lift) according to snow-online, though other sources count 9 lifts. The lifts operate from 8:30 to 16:30 daily during the season.
linkedResortsDescription: The Nebelhorn is part of the 2-country ski region Oberstdorf Kleinwalsertal, which includes seven ski mountains: Nebelhorn, Fellhorn-Kanzelwand, Söllereck, Ifen, Walmendinger Horn, and Heuberg Arena. The entire area offers 48 lifts and 130–150 km of groomed slopes, connected by free ski buses. A single 2-country ski pass grants access to all mountains, allowing skiing across the German-Austrian border. The linked resorts provide a wide variety of terrain from gentle family runs to challenging high-alpine pistes.

terrainDescription: The terrain on the Niedere is predominantly gentle and wide, making it well-suited for beginners and families. There is one steep black run for more advanced skiers. The area also features 3 km of ski routes for off-piste exploration. The slopes are well-groomed and the layout is compact, with most runs accessible from the top of the gondola.
offPisteDescription: The Niedere is known for its deep snow slopes (Tiefschneehänge) off the groomed pistes, offering good freeriding opportunities. When snow conditions allow, attractive firn runs on the Bezau side are popular. The area is also a starting point for ski tours in the Niedere-Winterstaude region. The Baumgarten mountain station is a common access point for off-piste and ski touring.
valueDescription: Lift pass prices are low compared to larger Austrian resorts. A day ticket for adults costs €38 (main season), with youth at €34.50 and children at €21.50. The resort is part of the 3TälerPass (3Valley ski pass), which provides access to 32 ski areas in the region for a single pass, offering good value for those wanting to explore multiple areas.
familyDescription: The Niedere ski area is explicitly family-friendly, with a dedicated children's area at the valley station in Andelsbuch featuring a practice lift and a magic carpet (Zauberteppich) on a gentle ski meadow. There is also a ski kindergarten and a children's restaurant. The slopes are mostly easy and the area is compact, making it easy for parents to keep an eye on children. The gondola from Bezau provides quick access to the mountain.
resortCharacterDescription: The ski area is located on the Niedere Höhe mountain in the Bregenzerwald, a traditional Austrian region. The base village Bezau is a small Tyrolean town with a cable car station. The area offers scenic views across the Bregenzerwald and as far as Lake Constance on clear days. The mountain station has a panorama restaurant. The resort is not purpose-built; it retains a natural alpine character with traditional architecture in the villages.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of a 60-person gondola from Bezau to the Baumgarten mountain station, covering 930 vertical meters in a few minutes. On the mountain, there are two T-bar lifts (drag lifts) serving the slopes. The gondola operates on a 30-minute interval schedule. The lifts are modern and well-maintained, but the area is small with limited capacity.
linkedResortsDescription: The Niedere ski area is part of the 3TälerPass (3Valley ski pass) network, which grants access to 32 ski areas in the Bregenzerwald, Großes Walsertal, Lechtal, and Balderschwang (Allgäu). The pass also covers ski bus transport between areas. Additionally, the Niedere area is connected to the Winterstaude area for ski tours, and the Baumgarten mountain station serves as a starting point for tours into the wider region.

terrainDescription: The ski area is centered on the Fichtelberg mountain (1,215 m) and the small Fichtelberg (1,206 m). Most beginner trails are located on the small Fichtelberg in the valley, served by t-bars. Blue forest runs (No. 9 and 10) at the top of Fichtelberg provide easy descents to the Himmelsleiterlift. The black race slope at Schönjungferngrund runs 1.2 km alongside the chairlift and suspension railway with a 300 m vertical drop. Night skiing is available on the main slope until 9 pm on Fridays and Saturdays. A terrain park near the suspension railway includes a kicker line, wood line, and barrel rail combo. The resort is linked to the Klinovec ski area in the Czech Republic via the Interskiregion Fichtelberg-Klinovec, adding more terrain.
valueDescription: Lift pass prices are moderate: a 1-day adult pass costs €41, a 2-day pass €79, and a 3-day pass €115. Children's passes are €31, €58, and €86 respectively. A 4-hour pass is €35 for adults and €25 for children. Ski rental is also affordable: children's equipment (skis, boots, poles) €13 per day, top ski set €19 per day, premium set €23 per day, helmets €6 per day. Ski school for children full-day starts at €58 for one day and decreases per day for longer bookings. The Interskiregion pass covering both Fichtelberg and Klinovec is slightly more expensive but still reasonable.
familyDescription: The resort is rated among the 10 most family-friendly ski resorts in Germany. The children's ski school (Vereinigte Skischule Oberwiesenthal) offers half-day (10-12 or 14-16) and full-day courses with optional lunch supervision. The nursery area features a magic carpet and ski carousel. Most beginner trails are on the small Fichtelberg in the valley, easily accessible. The compact village layout means ski school, rental shops, and accommodation are within walking distance. The Summit of Saxony apartments are directly adjacent to the children's ski school and slope, allowing parents to watch from the accommodation.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski venues include Sabines Schirmbar, a popular meeting spot. Prijut 12 at the valley station of the suspension railway and chairlift offers live music. Kiwis provides a lounge atmosphere with a chimney and cocktails. The wine cellar at Rotgierhaus allows guests to tap their own beverages from wine casks. The Berggasthof Fichtelberg and Skihaus also offer cozy atmospheres.
resortCharacterDescription: Oberwiesenthal is a traditional town in the Ore Mountains, at 914 m the highest town in Germany. It has a historic center with a market square, town hall, and St Martin Luther's Church. The town's history dates back to a mining settlement founded in 1527. The resort character is that of a classic German winter sports destination, with a compact village layout and a mix of traditional and modern accommodation.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system includes Germany's oldest suspension railway (Fichtelberg Schwebebahn) which takes skiers from Oberwiesenthal to the top of Fichtelberg. There is also a 4-person chairlift and several surface lifts including the Kurvenlift, Nachtskilauflift, Himmelsleiter, and Höhenlift. A magic carpet serves the beginner area. The lifts are modern and well-maintained, with a total capacity of 7,712 persons per hour according to one source. Night skiing is possible on a 0.8 km lit run.
linkedResortsDescription: The resort is linked to the Klinovec ski area in the Czech Republic, forming the Interskiregion Fichtelberg-Klinovec. With a combined pass (1215pass), skiers can access both areas. A free ski bus connects the resorts. Klinovec adds approximately 12 km of additional pistes, bringing the total to about 27.5 km of classified runs. The link allows cross-border skiing and access to different terrain.

terrainDescription: The terrain at Petzen consists of 11 km of groomed pistes, with 40% beginner, 53% intermediate, and 7% advanced runs. The longest run is a 7.5 km valley descent that is easy enough for beginners. A Snowpark on the red run 14 offers rails, boxes, and kickers for freestylers. The vertical drop is 1,270 m from summit to base.
offPisteDescription: Petzen offers a 2 km freeride route and is a popular area for ski touring, with designated parking for ski tourers. The Snowpark on run 14 provides freestyle features. No specific off-piste routes are detailed beyond the freeride route.
valueDescription: Lift pass prices are €45–€47.50 for a day adult pass, €23–€24 for a child day pass, and €165 for a 6-day adult pass. The resort is small and offers affordable skiing compared to larger Austrian resorts.
familyDescription: The resort features 'Petzi's Kinderland' children's ski school with a magic carpet for beginners. The nursery area is at the base, and the easy valley descent is suitable for families. Ski school offers courses for all ages and skill levels.
apresSkiDescription: Apres-ski is limited to one bar, the Petzen Nest at the car park, which serves Slovenian specialities. The resort has a quiet atmosphere with no significant nightlife.
resortCharacterDescription: Petzen is a small, traditional ski resort on the Austrian-Slovenian border, with a valley station, gondola, and several mountain huts. Accommodation is limited to a few holiday apartments in huts. The resort is not a purpose-built village but a natural mountain area.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system comprises 5 lifts: 1 gondola, 1 chairlift, and 3 surface lifts, plus a magic carpet for beginners. Snowmaking covers 90% of the slopes with 26 snow machines, ensuring reliable conditions on lower runs.

terrainDescription: The ski area spans 20 km of marked pistes between 1,100 m and 1,608 m, with a mix of easy (24%), intermediate (55%), and advanced (21%) runs. The terrain includes wide, gently sloping family runs, a dedicated beginner area called Purzelwiese with a magic carpet, and a Funslope featuring bumps, small jumps, a giant snow snail with a tunnel, and a High Five element with sound effects. The Schlumperwald adventure run offers an audio-guided experience through quirky obstacles. For advanced skiers, the Polsterrinne is the longest developed steep trough in the Eastern Alps, providing a challenging freeride descent. Carving slopes and the 'Die Wilde' fun park add variety.
offPisteDescription: Off-piste skiing centers on the Polsterrinne, a steep trough that holds powder snow and is popular with freeride enthusiasts. The 'The Wild One' area is designated for slope fun off the groomed trails, allowing skiers to leave tracks in deep snow. Ski touring routes are available in the surrounding Eisenerz Alps, with guided options from the Alpfox ski school. The resort's location in a lesser-known area means untracked snow can persist for days after a storm.
valueDescription: Day lift passes are priced at €48.50 for adults, €36.50 for youths, and €24.50 for children (2024/25 season), making it one of the more affordable ski areas in Styria. Accommodation ranges from pensions and holiday apartments to hotels, with many offering package deals including lift passes. The small scale of the resort means no long lift queues and lower overall costs compared to larger Alpine destinations. Self-catering options and proximity to the A9 motorway reduce travel expenses.
familyDescription: The resort is explicitly designed for families, with a dedicated children's area (Purzelwiese) featuring a magic carpet and gentle slopes. The Alpfox mountain and ski school offers day courses, group lessons, and private instruction for skiing, snowboarding, and freeriding. The Schlumperwald adventure run and Funslope provide entertainment for children and parents alike. Tobogganing, winter hiking trails, and horse-drawn sleigh rides are available off the slopes. The compact layout means ski school, hire shops, and accommodation are within easy walking distance.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski is relaxed and manageable, centred on cosy huts and small pubs at the valley station and in the nearby village of Eisenerz. The Panorama Café and Pizzaria offer warm atmospheres with food and drinks. Venues serve regional delicacies, Styrian wine, and mulled wine. Regular events include live music, themed evenings, and joint tobogganing trips. The scene is rustic and low-key, avoiding large party crowds.
resortCharacterDescription: Präbichl is a traditional, unspoiled ski area in the Hochsteiermark region of Styria, set in the Eisenerz Alps. The resort is not purpose-built; it consists of a small base area with a few buildings and is closely tied to the historic mining town of Vordernberg and the village of Eisenerz. The atmosphere is authentic and quiet, surrounded by dramatic peaks and snowy forests, with a focus on family and nature rather than commercial development.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system comprises 8 lifts (per known facts), including two four-seat chairlifts, two T-bar lifts, one one-seat chairlift, one platter lift, and a magic carpet for beginners. The chairlifts are modern and provide efficient access to the upper slopes. Surface lifts serve the lower and intermediate terrain. The magic carpet is located in the Purzelwiese beginner area. Lift capacity is approximately 11,000 skiers per hour, and queues are rare outside peak periods.
linkedResortsDescription: The ski area is linked with Vordernberg, forming the combined Präbichl–Vordernberg ski region. The two areas share a lift pass and offer a total of 20 km of interconnected pistes. Vordernberg provides additional accommodation and access to the same slope network. The resort is also close to Eisenerz, which offers further winter activities and services.

terrainDescription: The ski area is located on the east-facing slopes of Hochalm, with tree-free upper sections offering an open feel and plenty of sunshine. The terrain consists of 32.5 km of pistes, predominantly blue and red runs suitable for beginner and intermediate skiers, with a few black runs including a challenging descent from the main gondola to the valley. The longest run is the 10 km Gipfelbahn-Rauris descent with a vertical drop of 1,235 m. The slopes are well-groomed and the upper area is largely treeless, providing panoramic views of the Hohe Tauern National Park. The area also includes a terrain park (Nugget Park) with rails and obstacles.
valueDescription: A day lift pass costs approximately €60-65 for adults, with a 3-day pass from £166. Children's tickets are around €30 per day, and youth tickets €47.50. From 28 March to 6 April 2026, children up to age 15 ski for free when accompanied by an adult holding a pass for at least three days. The resort offers family discounts and is described as having fair prices. Accommodation includes self-catering apartments and hotels, with 3,200 guest beds in the valley. The ski area is one of Austria's first climate-neutral resorts, which may reduce environmental costs.
familyDescription: The resort is specifically designed for families, with a dedicated family slope and a practice lift that is free for children. Two ski schools (Adventure Rauris and Skischule Maier) offer lessons for all ages. There is a family fun slope, a small family park (Nugget Park), and a 2.5 km floodlit toboggan run that is also open for night tobogganing twice a week. The valley station has a children's play area and two huts serving affordable food. The village is walkable and traffic-free, and free ski buses connect the accommodation to the lifts. The resort also offers guided snowshoe tours and winter hiking trails.
apresSkiDescription: Apres-ski is quiet and family-oriented, with 2 bars and pubs and 17 restaurants in the village. The scene is relaxed rather than rowdy, with traditional Austrian hospitality. Visitors recommend the resort for those who want to improve skiing rather than party. There are some nice restaurants and bars, but the nightlife is subdued.
resortCharacterDescription: Rauris is a traditional Austrian village located in a dead-end valley within the Hohe Tauern National Park. It has 3,100 inhabitants and 3,200 guest beds. The village is charming and authentic, with supermarkets, hotels, rental shops, and a few restaurants and bars. The ski area is modern with gondola lifts, but the village retains a quiet, traditional character away from mass tourism. The resort is one of Austria's first climate-neutral ski resorts, with a hydroelectric power plant and sustainable practices.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of 10 lifts, including 3 gondolas (Gipfelbahn, Waldalmbahn, Kreuzbodenbahn), 1 chairlift, and 6 surface lifts. The gondolas provide fast access to the upper slopes at 2,175 m. The lifts are modern and efficient, with chip card technology for ski passes. Queues are rare due to the resort's small size and low crowds. A free ski bus operates within the valley. The system is well-suited for a compact area, with the main gondola serving most of the terrain.

terrainDescription: The resort offers 27 km of pistes with a vertical drop of 853 m (base 966 m, summit 1819 m). Beginner terrain dominates (61%), including Austria's first dedicated children's ski area with 5 hectares of blue runs, the Krispini Express conveyor belt, magic carpets, and a kids' ski circuit. Intermediate and advanced runs include a mogul slope, a 500 m Cross-Ride-Track with steep turns and jumps, and a FIS race slope at the Alpin-Base training center. The longest run is the 7 km Panorama-Abfahrt. All trails are covered by snowmaking.
valueDescription: Lift pass prices are among the more affordable in Styria: a one-day adult pass costs €43, a six-day pass €227, and a child day pass €22 (2025/26 season). The resort is part of the Schneebärenland ski pass, covering five mountains (Tauplitz, Kaiserau, Planneralm, Loser-Altaussee, Riesneralm) for one price, offering good value for exploring multiple areas. Free Wi-Fi is available throughout the resort.
familyDescription: The resort is particularly family-friendly, featuring Austria's first separate children's ski area completely isolated from main pistes. The kids' zone covers 5 hectares with four blue runs, served by the Krispini Express (Austria's longest conveyor belt), magic carpets, and children's lifts. A ski school with English-speaking instructors operates on-site, and the area includes a playground house, wave tracks, and an adventure course. Night skiing tours are held every Friday. The free ski bus connects the village to the slopes, and family-oriented accommodation like JUFA Hotel is steps from the children's area.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski is low-key and rustic, centered on a handful of bars and mountain huts. The Roßstallbar is a popular starting point, while O-Bar and Riesnertreff at the valley station host events. There are 18 bars and pubs in the area, but the scene is more about relaxed evenings with local food and drink than high-energy nightlife. Friday night ski tours and torchlight walks offer alternative evening activities, and the resort also has a bowling alley at Stegerhof.
resortCharacterDescription: Donnersbachwald is a small, traditional village in the Wölzer Tauern (Niedere Tauern) of Styria, offering a quiet and authentic alpine atmosphere. The resort is purpose-built around the Riesneralm ski area but retains a village character with local architecture. It is known for being uncrowded and family-oriented, with modern amenities like free Wi-Fi and a sustainable energy approach using local hydropower and green electricity. The village has a natural ice rink and historic toboggan rides.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of 7 lifts, including a modern 6-seater covered chairlift (Panorama lift) and a 4-seater chairlift, along with several surface lifts. The children's area features the Krispini Express, Austria's longest conveyor belt. Lifts operate from 09:00 to 16:00, with a total capacity of 4,500 persons per hour. Queues are rare due to the resort's small size. The resort also has 139 snowmaking machines covering 95% of trails.
linkedResortsDescription: Riesneralm is part of the Schneebärenland ski pass, which includes five ski areas: Tauplitz, Kaiserau, Planneralm, Loser-Altaussee, and Riesneralm itself. The nearby Planneralm (10.6 km away) offers an additional 13 km of pistes, and together the two resorts provide around 45 km of slopes. The wider Donnersbacher Tauern region also includes other resorts like Galsterberg and Stoderzinken.

terrainDescription: The ski area features gentle, rolling hills with predominantly easy and intermediate runs. The slopes are wide and well-groomed, with a dedicated children's area called 'Schetti's Kids' Paradise' that includes a ski carousel, magic carpet, igloo, climbing hill, and tubing course. Runs are named after animals with mascots indicating required skill levels, from snowplough turns on the hedgehog circuit to dynamic carving on the lynx piste. The terrain is sheltered and ideal for beginners and families.
valueDescription: Lift passes are affordable, with a day pass for adults at €36 and children at €20. Family discounts of up to 20% are available. The resort is known for its good value compared to larger Austrian resorts, with budget-friendly accommodation and dining options in the village of Egg.
familyDescription: The resort is specifically designed for families, with a dedicated children's area called 'Schetti's Kids' Paradise' featuring a ski carousel, magic carpet, igloo, climbing hill, and tubing course. The ski school offers lessons for children, and there is free child-minding during the Austrian semester break in February. All slopes have mascots that describe the required skill level, making it easy for parents to choose appropriate runs. The village of Egg has a childcare center and family-friendly amenities.
apresSkiDescription: Apres-ski is low-key, with a few restaurants and bars in the village of Egg. On the mountain, the Lifthus self-service restaurant at the valley station, the Gasthaus s'Amagmach, and the Teehütte hut provide dining options. The resort has 13 restaurants and 1 bar/café in the area, offering a relaxed atmosphere rather than a party scene.
resortCharacterDescription: Schetteregg is a small, tranquil ski area located in a side valley of the Bregenzerwald. The village of Egg has traditional Austrian charm with cottages and a relaxed atmosphere. The ski area is not a large purpose-built resort but rather a local, family-friendly destination with a focus on gentle skiing and winter activities such as winter hiking, cross-country skiing, and tobogganing.
liftSystemDescription: The resort has 6 lifts, including a chairlift and surface lifts, plus a children's carousel and magic carpets in the dedicated kids' area. The lifts are efficient for the size of the area, with minimal queues. The lift system is well-maintained and suitable for beginners and intermediates.
linkedResortsDescription: Schetteregg is part of the 3Valley ski pass, which connects 38 ski areas in the Bregenzerwald, Großes Walsertal, Lechtal, and Allgäu regions. The pass includes free bus travel between resorts, allowing access to a much larger ski area.

terrainDescription: The terrain at Söllereck is dominated by wide, gentle blue runs, with over half the slopes classified as easy. Intermediate red slopes include the Höllwies piste, which was once a FIS race course and offers more challenging gradients. The slopes are set on a partly wooded, grassy mountain, with the intermediate runs towards Ziegelbachhütte providing views of Oberstdorf. A dedicated practice area near the mid-station features magic carpets and a children's play area.
valueDescription: A day lift pass in the main season costs €56.90 for adults, €48 for youth, and €29 for children. Parking at the valley station is free, with around 575 spaces available. The resort is part of the Oberstdorf Kleinwalsertal ski region, where a 6-day pass for the wider area is €342.80 for adults in low season. Self-catering accommodation in nearby Oberstdorf offers budget-friendly options, and the resort's focus on beginners means cheaper beginner-only passes are available.
familyDescription: Söllereck is marketed as a family-friendly ski area and is the home mountain of the Oberstdorf ski schools. Children have a dedicated area called 'Söllis Winterwelt' near the mid-station, with magic carpets, a practice area, and a giant wooden piste basher for climbing and playing. The Allgäu Coaster, a year-round toboggan run, provides additional family fun. Wide blue slopes and a balanced ratio of blue and red runs make progression easy for beginners. A cheaper beginner ski pass is available for those sticking to the learning area.
resortCharacterDescription: The ski area is located on the slopes of the Söllereck mountain, accessible via a modern 10-person gondola from a large parking lot. There is no resort village at the base; the nearest town is Oberstdorf, 4 km away. The area features a mix of wooded and open slopes, with a family-friendly atmosphere and a focus on beginner and intermediate skiing. The mountain itself is partly wooded and grassy, popular for hiking in summer.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system comprises 8 lifts: a 10-person gondola (Söllereckbahn) from the valley to the top station, one chairlift, three drag lifts, and three magic carpets. A new 6-person high-speed detachable chairlift (Wannenbahn) is planned for the 2026/27 season. The traditional Höllwies drag lift was modernized and reopened for the 2025/26 season with a new energy-saving drive. The gondola ride takes less than 8 minutes, and lifts operate from 8:30 to 16:00.
linkedResortsDescription: Söllereck is part of the two-country ski region Oberstdorf Kleinwalsertal, which includes ski areas in Germany and Austria. It is primarily responsible for easy slopes within this region. From the Söllereck valley station, other ski areas such as Fellhorn-Kanzelwand are accessible by bus or car. The wider region offers over 200 km of slopes and is linked by a common ski pass.

terrainDescription: The ski area centres on the Stümpfling and Roßkopf mountains. The Stümpfling side offers wide, groomed slopes suitable for beginners and intermediates, with gentle gradients on the Osthang and Nordhang lifts. The Roßkopf, accessed by a double chairlift, provides a short challenging run and the long black Grünseeabfahrt. A funslope with wave track, kickers, and boxes is available on the east-facing lift depending on snow conditions. The slopes are tree-lined, offering shelter in poor visibility. The area has 16 km of pistes with a mix of easy and intermediate terrain, and 2.5 km of night skiing on the Stümpfling slope. Snowmaking covers 10 km of pistes.
offPisteDescription: Off-piste skiing is concentrated on the Taubenstein, which is outside the groomed ski area and accessible only by ski touring. The terrain is challenging, with no groomed runs, and is recommended for experienced skiers comfortable in deep snow. The east side of the lake also offers ski touring routes. Avalanche conditions should be checked via the Bavarian avalanche warning service before heading out.
valueDescription: The Spitzingsee-Tegernsee ski area is noted as the best value for money in Bavaria. A day ski pass costs €51 for adults and €24 for children, with a six-day pass at €297 and €140 respectively. The Alpen Plus lift pass provides access to 79 km of pistes across four ski areas (Brauneck-Wegscheid, Wallberg, Spitzingsee-Tegernsee, and Sudelfeld) for a single ticket. Free shuttle buses operate from the Enterrottach toll station to the Moni-Alm during peak periods, reducing transport costs. The resort is popular with daytrippers from Munich, indicating good value for a day out.
familyDescription: The Stümpfling area is specifically designed for beginners and families, with gentle, wide slopes and professional DSLV-certified ski schools offering courses from toddlers to seniors. The Kirchberg lift in Kreuth provides easy terrain for families. A funslope with wave track is suitable for all ages. Night skiing on Thursdays and Fridays from 6:30 to 9:30 pm on the floodlit Stümpfling slope is a family-friendly activity. Additional winter activities include toboggan runs (including a 6.5 km run on the Wallberg), curling, carriage rides, and ice skating. The area has rustic huts and mountain inns with regional cuisine.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski is low-key, centred on mountain huts and a few bars. The Basislager umbrella bar at the Stümpflingbahn valley station and 's Hosendial' at the Suttenbahn offer mulled wine, Jagertee, and snacks. The Tegernsee brewery serves original Tegernsee beer. For relaxation, there are wellness facilities in accommodation and the Bad Wiessee iodine-sulphur bath. The resort is primarily a day-trip destination, so nightlife is limited, but there are occasional events and the Olaf Gulbransson Museum and Tegernsee Valley Museum for cultural evenings.
resortCharacterDescription: The resort is centred on the natural Spitzingsee lake, with the ski lifts starting from its western side. The village of Spitzingsee is small and picturesque, located directly on the lake shore. The nearby town of Tegernsee is a traditional spa town with a historic Benedictine monastery (now Schloss Tegernsee) and a brewery. The area is set in the Bavarian Alps, with alpine scenery and a relaxed, traditional atmosphere. Accommodation ranges from hotels to self-catering apartments, with ski-in/ski-out options available.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system comprises 11 lifts: two high-speed quad chairlifts (Stümpflingbahn and Suttenbahn), one double chairlift to the Roßkopf, and eight surface lifts (T-bar and platter). The lifts are modern and efficient, with the quad chairlifts providing quick access to the main areas. Night skiing operates on the Stümpfling slope using the 4-seater chairlift. The lifts are generally reliable, though the area is low altitude and can be affected by weather.
linkedResortsDescription: The Spitzingsee-Tegernsee ski area is part of the Alpen Plus network, which includes four Bavarian winter sport regions: Brauneck-Wegscheid, Wallberg, Spitzingsee-Tegernsee, and Sudelfeld, offering a combined 79 km of pistes and 34 lifts. With the Alpen Plus Holiday Card upgrade, access is extended to the Wendelstein and Oberaudorf-Hocheck ski areas, adding another 19 km of pistes. The area itself connects the Spitzingsee and Rottach-Egern valleys via the Stümpfling and Sutten chairlifts.

terrainDescription: The terrain is dominated by wide, gentle cruising runs, with a vertical drop of nearly 1,000 meters allowing for continuous descents of up to 5 km. Most runs are suitable for beginners and intermediates, with a few steeper sections for advanced skiers. The resort features two snow parks with kickers, rails, and boxes, as well as a children's adventure course. Night skiing is available on a floodlit slope until 8:30 pm.
offPisteDescription: Off-piste skiing is limited; the resort is primarily known for its groomed runs. There are a few light off-piste sections accessible from the lifts, but the terrain is not extensive for off-piste enthusiasts. The area is best suited for those who prefer prepared slopes.
valueDescription: A day lift pass costs €64.50 for adults, with season passes available at €635 and 6-day passes at €233. The resort's proximity to Vienna (about 1 hour drive) makes it a cost-effective option for day trips, avoiding accommodation costs. Self-catering apartments and hotels in Spital am Semmering offer budget-friendly options, and the resort has 11 restaurants and cafés with reasonable prices.
familyDescription: The resort is highly family-friendly, with a dedicated children's area called Semmi's Kinderland featuring magic carpets and gentle slopes. Ski schools offer lessons for children, and there is a 5 km natural toboggan run and snow tubing. The slopes are mostly wide and easy, suitable for beginners. The resort gets busy on weekends but is quiet during weekdays, making it ideal for short family trips.
apresSkiDescription: The après-ski scene is low-key, centered around mountain huts rather than nightclubs. There are 11 restaurants and cafés serving traditional Austrian cuisine. Night skiing is available on a floodlit slope until 8:30 pm, and occasional torchlight descents and music events are held. The atmosphere is relaxed and family-oriented.
resortCharacterDescription: The resort is based in the village of Spital am Semmering, a traditional Austrian alpine village with charming architecture. The slopes are accessed from the village, and the lift base area has modern facilities. The surrounding Semmering region is known for its scenic beauty and historic Semmering Railway, a UNESCO World Heritage site. The resort is purpose-built but blends into the alpine setting.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of 12 lifts, including 2 high-speed six-seater chairlifts, 2 quad chairlifts, and 8 surface lifts. The chairlifts are modern and comfortable, with protective hoods on some. The lifts provide quick access to the slopes, minimizing waiting times. The resort also has efficient snowmaking covering 26 km of slopes, ensuring good coverage throughout the season.

terrainDescription: The ski area offers 16 prepared pistes covering 12 km, with a mix of blue, red, and black runs including a FIS downhill course. The slopes are on the sunny side of the Mürztal valley, featuring wide-open pistes and forest trails. The upper area provides steeper terrain, while the lower slopes are gentler. The vertical drop is 386 m from 1,050 m to 1,450 m.
valueDescription: Lift passes are affordable, with a day pass for adults at €36 and a six-day pass at €155. Children's day passes are €19. The resort offers a free magic carpet for beginners. Mountain restaurants serve Austrian specialties at fair prices, and multi-day ski tickets are available at a discount.
familyDescription: The resort is marketed as family-friendly, with a dedicated children's area featuring a free magic carpet at Gamsmugl. The slopes are uncrowded, with gentle learning slopes and professional ski instruction. Forest trails and routes provide a safe environment for children. The resort has a relaxed atmosphere and is listed among the top family ski areas in Styria. Accommodation includes the JUFA Hotel with a swimming pool and slide.
resortCharacterDescription: The resort is set in a natural alpine landscape on the sunny side of the Mürztal valley, with stunning views of the Hohe Veitsch massif. It is a small, family-oriented ski area with a relaxed, authentic Austrian atmosphere, not a large purpose-built resort. The village of Veitsch offers basic amenities and accommodation.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of one quad chairlift, two T-bar lifts, one platter lift, and a free magic carpet for beginners. The lifts are modern and well-maintained, with minimal waiting times even during peak periods. Snowmaking covers the entire ski area, ensuring reliable conditions.

terrainDescription: The Ski Juwel area comprises three main sectors across two valleys. On the Alpbach side, the Wiedersbergerhorn (2,128m) offers 55km of pistes, mostly north- and east-facing, with a large open area of easy red and blue runs between 1,900m and 1,700m above the treeline, and tree-lined descents from 1,850m down to 1,350m. Two top-to-bottom runs drop nearly 1,000m to Inneralpbach and the main lift base. The Auffach sector on the Schatzberg (1,903m) features unusually wide, confidence-boosting pistes above the treeline, mostly north-facing, with one red run (13) dropping over the south side. The Niederau sector is separate, with a steep former FIS race course from the Lanerkopf (1,621m) and long, flat nursery slopes in the meadows. The longest run in the area is 7km from summit to base. Snowmaking covers over 85% of pistes.
offPisteDescription: Off-piste skiing is available but not the main draw. Marked ski routes on the piste map offer an introduction to unpisted terrain. After fresh snow, the north-facing slopes above Inneralpbach hold powder longest, and tree skiing through the forests below the main lifts can be excellent. Local guides recommend the Greitalm for tree runs and the Kohlgrube for speed runs, accessed from the Hornbahn 2000. A ski touring route (route 66) from Inneralpbach to the summit of the Wiedersbergerhorn is suitable for first-time tourers, with a 2-3 hour climb and a descent on groomed pistes or powder. The area does not have a freeride reputation like Fieberbrunn, but offers good powder days with a guide.
valueDescription: Lift passes are among the more affordable in Austria. A six-day adult pass costs €288 in low season and €319.50 in high season (2023/24 prices). Children aged 6-15 get a 50% reduction, and under-6s ski free. A one-day pass is €56.50 low season, €62.50 high season. Accommodation is also good value: a junior suite at the four-star superior Boglerhof in Alpbach costs less than a standard room in a three-star hotel in Méribel. The Alpbachtal Seenland Card and Wildschönau Card provide free guided snowshoe walks and free ski bus. Many package deals are available, and the ski school offers competitive rates.
familyDescription: Ski Juwel has won multiple awards as a top family resort, including Skiresort.info Test Winner 2025 for Families and Children. There are two dedicated children's areas: the Juppi-Do Kinderland at the Reitherkogel gondola base in Reith, with obstacle courses, ski trails, and magic carpets; and the Schatzberg Zwergenland at the Schatzbergbahn mid-station, featuring a snowball playground, igloo, ski carousel, three magic carpets, a T-bar lift, and a mini fun park. Ten ski schools operate in the area, with English-speaking instructors. The nursery slopes at the base of the Wiedersbergerhorn gondola are gentle, wide, and separated from faster traffic. The compact village of Alpbach is easy to navigate on foot, and the relaxed pace suits families. Off-slope activities include toboggan runs, ice skating, swimming pools, and the Alpine Coaster.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski is low-key and friendly. In Niederau, the Hexenhäusl bar in the village centre is popular with locals and visitors. In Alpbach, a typical après-ski tour starts at Joe's Salettl in Inneralpbach, then moves to the Postalm balcony for Aperol, and finishes at the Jakober. Auffach and Niederau have sociable bars, while Alpbach and Reith are quieter, with cosy hotel lounges. Night skiing is available on the Reither Kogel on Thursdays and Fridays, with 6km of floodlit slopes. The area is not a party destination; those seeking big nightlife should go to Mayrhofen or Ischgl.
resortCharacterDescription: Alpbach has been voted Austria's most beautiful village and has a strict building code since 1953 limiting buildings to three storeys with wooden upper floors, preserving its traditional character of wooden chalets and flower-filled balconies. The village is compact and walkable, with a calm, unhurried atmosphere. The Wildschönau villages (Niederau, Auffach, Oberau) are also traditional, with wooden and stone chalets giving an authentic Tyrolean feel. The area is not purpose-built; it consists of working farming communities. The resort is car-free in the village centre, and the pace is relaxed, focusing on skiing rather than partying.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system comprises 45 lifts, including 9 gondolas, 3 high-speed six-seater chairlifts, 4 quad chairs, and 29 surface lifts. The key connection is the Verbindungsbahn gondola, installed in 2012, linking Inneralpbach to the Schatzberg above Auffach, creating a fully integrated ski area. The Wiedersbergerhornbahn and Pöglbahn gondolas serve the Alpbach side, with a new high-speed six-seater chairlift (Hornlift 2000) linking the mid-station at 1,638m to the top at 2,030m. The Schatzbergbahn is a high-speed gondola from Auffach. The Reither Kogel has a two-stage gondola and is open for night skiing. Free ski buses connect the villages to the lifts.
linkedResortsDescription: The Ski Juwel Alpbachtal Wildschönau links two valleys – Alpbachtal and Wildschönau – via a gondola built in 2012 from Inneralpbach to the Schatzberg. The combined area includes four main villages: Alpbach and Reith im Alpbachtal in the Alpbachtal, and Auffach and Niederau in the Wildschönau. The total ski area is 113-145km of pistes (depending on measurement), making it one of the top 10 largest ski areas in Tyrol. The connection allows skiers to explore both sides in a single day, with free ski buses linking the villages.

terrainDescription: The Ibergeregg ski area consists of two T-bar lifts: the 400-metre-long Alpstubli and the 800-metre-long Kulm, serving five pistes classified as easy to medium difficulty. The Alpstubli lift provides flat to gently sloping terrain ideal for beginners, with a mix of flat sections and slightly steeper pitches. The Kulm lift offers easy to medium-difficulty descents. The vertical drop from summit to base is 250 metres. From the top station of the Kulm lift, there is a panoramic view extending from Pilatus to Säntis. The terrain is open and well-suited to beginners and intermediates, with 61% of runs rated beginner, 32% intermediate, and 6% advanced.
valueDescription: The day pass at Ibergeregg costs CHF29, making it one of the five cheapest ski resorts in the Lucerne – Lake Lucerne Region. Combined ski passes are available with the neighbouring areas Hoch-Ybrig and Mythenregion, offering access to a wider range of slopes. Self-catering accommodation and the small scale of the resort help keep costs down for budget-conscious visitors.
familyDescription: Ibergeregg is marketed as a snow-sure family destination. The Alpstubli lift serves flat and open terrain ideal for beginners and children, with a toboggan run starting next to the lift. The Swiss Ski School Ibergeregg offers group lessons for children and private lessons for adults. There are approximately 18 kilometres of winter hiking trails in the area, and the pass-top location provides easy access to the slopes from the adjacent hotel and restaurant.
resortCharacterDescription: Ibergeregg is a small ski station located at the summit of Ibergeregg Pass at an elevation of 1,406 metres. The pass has an inn, a chapel, and a ski station, with a paved road crossing since 1873. The ski area consists of two T-bar lifts and a handful of pistes, giving it a low-key, traditional alpine character rather than a large purpose-built resort. The area is also a summer hiking destination.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system comprises two surface T-bar lifts: the 400-metre Alpstubli and the 800-metre Kulm. Total lift capacity is 2,150 persons per hour. The Alpstubli lift serves as a connection from Hoch-Ybrig into the Mythenregion, while the Kulm lift provides access to both the Mythenregion and Hoch-Ybrig. Lifts operate from 08:45 to 16:30 during the season.
linkedResortsDescription: Ibergeregg is part of the Mythenregion and directly connected to the Hoch-Ybrig ski area. The Alpstubli lift serves as a connection from Hoch-Ybrig into the Mythenregion, and the Kulm lift provides access to both areas. Combined ski passes allow skiers to explore the wider Mythenregion, which offers 50 kilometres of pistes, and the Hoch-Ybrig area with its snow park and varied slopes. A ski safari route links Ibergeregg to Hoch-Ybrig.

terrainDescription: The ski area lies on the western slope of the Kahler Asten (842 m), allowing skiers to start the day with a downhill run from the village. The terrain includes a mix of blue, red, and black runs, with two black runs including the only FIS-certified race piste in the Sauerland (Black Run 3, maximum gradient 40%). The Kapellenabfahrt is a gentle, family-friendly descent served by a new four-seater chairlift. Runs are wide with panoramic views, and the area is predominantly snow-covered by snowmaking.
valueDescription: Day lift passes cost €42 for adults and €27 for children (main season). The ski area is part of the Skiwelt Winterberg ticket network, which allows access to five ski areas with a single ticket. The area is known for being quieter and more affordable than the neighbouring Winterberg resort, making it popular with day-trippers and families.
familyDescription: The ski school meeting point and children's area are located next to the main car park in the village. The children's area includes a magic carpet, child lift, children's carousel, adventure park, and playground. Childcare is available from 36 months. The ski school has 15 instructors and offers lessons in Dutch. The Kapellenabfahrt is a gentle run served by a chairlift, suitable for families. A mascot named Pingu entertains children.
apresSkiDescription: Altastenberg has five restaurants and two bars or cafés. The Schwedenhütte is a popular après-ski spot. There is one cinema. The village also offers a public sauna, fitness centre, and wellness facilities. Nightlife is limited, with no discos.
resortCharacterDescription: Altastenberg is a traditional mountain village, the highest in the Sauerland at just under 800 m. It is a state-recognised spa (health resort) due to its therapeutic climate. The village has a compact layout with hotels, guesthouses, and a tourist information office. It is not purpose-built; it has a historic core dating back to the 16th century.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of 7 lifts: one four-seater chairlift (Kapellenbahn, installed in 2023/24) and six surface lifts (T-bars and a magic carpet). The chairlift replaced two old tow lifts and extended the Kapellenhang run by 700 m. The area has 30 snow guns, with 70% snowmaking coverage. GPS-supported piste preparation is used. Lift capacity is 5,649 persons per hour. Night skiing is available on 1.5 km of slopes.
linkedResortsDescription: Skikarussell Altastenberg is part of the Skiwelt Winterberg, a network of five ski areas: Skiliftkarussell Winterberg, Skidorf Neuastenberg, Skikarussell Altastenberg, Familienskigebiet Sahnehang, and Skigebiet Ruhrquelle. A shuttle bus (orange line) runs every 15 minutes between the areas, free with a Skiwelt Winterberg ticket. The combined area offers 45 km of slopes and 46 lifts. Additionally, the Wintersport-Arena CARD provides access to about twenty other ski areas in the Sauerland.

terrainDescription: The Skiliftkarussell Winterberg spreads across seven hills (Bremberg, Kappe, Poppenberg, Herrloh, Sürenberg, Kahler Asten, and others) with a total of 27.5 km of marked runs. 14 km are classified as easy (blue), 12 km as intermediate (red), and 1.5 km as difficult (black). The longest run is the 1.6 km connection from Bremberg to Büre. The most challenging slope is the slalom slope at Kappe, with a gradient of up to 50%, and the St. Georg-Schanze downhill run (0.2 km, 72 m vertical drop). Poppenberg offers slopes ideal for fast carving turns, while Herrloh is dominated by very gentle gradients. The Kappe hill provides the greatest vertical drop in a single run at 190 m. Most runs are short and under tree line, with forest runs such as the Waldschneise at Kappe (1.2 km). 80% of slopes have artificial snow-making, and 26 km of runs can be machine-groomed.
valueDescription: A six-day adult lift pass for the Skiwelt Winterberg (five linked areas) costs €214, with a children's pass at €126. A single-day adult ticket is €52, and a morning ticket (valid until 12:30) is €30. The resort is part of the Wintersport-Arena Sauerland, and the Skiwelt Winterberg ticket grants access to 45 km of pistes across five areas. Parking is free at most lots, though some charge €4 per day. Compared to Alpine resorts, daily costs for accommodation, food, and lift passes are noticeably lower, making it a budget-friendly option for day trips and short stays.
familyDescription: The resort has eight dedicated children's areas, including the gentle slopes at Herrloh and the beginner zones at Bremberg and Kappe. The lift system is designed like a carousel, with short, fast chairlifts connecting hills, which children enjoy riding repeatedly. Many runs are short and not steep, ideal for beginners and families. The Skiwelt Winterberg ticket includes a free ski bus connecting the five areas, making it easy to move between family-friendly zones. The town of Winterberg is compact, with ski school meeting points, hire shops, and restaurants within walking distance of most accommodation.
apresSkiDescription: Night skiing is available on 6 km of illuminated runs, with several runs lit until late evening. Après-ski bars include Der Brabander, known for its lively atmosphere, and authentic ski huts serving food and drinks. The town of Winterberg offers a range of restaurants, pubs, and a spa. The après-ski scene is active but not as intense as in major Alpine resorts, with a focus on relaxed evening activities.
resortCharacterDescription: Winterberg is a traditional German town with a history dating back to the 13th century, not a purpose-built ski station. It has a small-town feel with shops, hotels, restaurants, and spas clustered around a historic centre. The ski area is located about 1 km from the town centre, accessible by road or ski bus. The town is car-free in parts and has a pedestrianised zone. The surrounding Sauerland region is characterised by rolling hills, forests, and meadows, giving the resort a rural, alpine-adjacent character rather than a high-mountain feel.
liftSystemDescription: The Skiliftkarussell has 26 lifts (as of 2019), including 2 eight-person chairlifts, 6 six-person chairlifts, 4 four-person chairlifts, 1 two-person chairlift, 8 surface lifts, and 4 conveyor belts. It has the highest number of chairlifts of any ski resort north of the Alps, and the most modern lift fleet in Germany. The first eight-person chairlift in Germany was installed here in 2012. Total lift capacity is about 37,000 persons per hour. Lifts are fast and short, connecting the seven hills efficiently. The Skiwelt Winterberg combined area has 46 lifts across five linked zones.
linkedResortsDescription: The Skiliftkarussell Winterberg is part of the 'Skiwelt Winterberg', a group of five nearby ski areas: Skiliftkarussell Winterberg, Skidorf Neuastenberg, Skikarussell Altastenberg, Familienskigebiet Sahnehang, and Skigebiet Ruhrquelle. A free ski bus runs every 15 minutes between Altastenberg, Nordhang, and Neuastenberg. The combined area offers 45 km of pistes, 46 lifts, 21 km of floodlit runs, eight children's areas, a fun park, and FIS-approved slopes. A single ticket grants access to all five areas. The Skiliftkarussell is also part of the larger Wintersport-Arena Sauerland ticket alliance, which includes other resorts like Willingen.

terrainDescription: The Titlis ski area offers slopes of all levels of difficulty, with a majority of intermediate runs. At the top of Titlis, wide glacier slopes allow carving above the treeline. The descent to Engelberg passes through forests and alpine meadows, covering 12 km and 2,000 m vertical drop. At Stand and Jochpass, skiing is in high alpine terrain above the treeline. Gentle and easy slopes are located on the Gerschnialp. The Trübsee-Hopper four-seater chairlift connects slopes over the Trübsee lake. The Jochpass sector offers a network of blues and reds connecting to Trübsee. The lower section of the blue home run through trees provides shelter in bad weather.
offPisteDescription: Engelberg is a freeride mecca with off-piste classics including the Laub, which offers 1,000 m of vertical on a large powder face, Galtiberg, and glacier routes from Titlis. The Laub couloirs have gradient sections requiring real technical ability. The Galtiberg bowl provides extensive off-piste terrain. A guide is strongly recommended for off-piste skiing. The resort also hosts the DareToRace course, a marked race-training piste for recreational skiers.
valueDescription: Lift pass prices for adults are approximately CHF 72–82 for a one-day pass and CHF 340–370 for a six-day pass. Children aged 6–15 receive a 50% reduction, and children under 9 ski free when accompanied by a paying adult. Ski or snowboard rental packages start around CHF 40–55 per day from village shops, with online booking discounts of 10–15%. Group ski lessons cost approximately CHF 59 for a half-day, private lessons CHF 90–120 per hour. Booking lift passes online through titlis.ch saves 5–10% compared to the ticket office. Rail passes such as the Swiss Travel Pass and Swiss Half Fare Card offer discounts on the cable car to Titlis.
familyDescription: The Brunni area on the eastern edge of Engelberg is family-friendly, with the Globis Winterland beginners' zone featuring a Poma lift, magic carpet, and ski schools. The Family Restaurant Ox overlooks the beginners' area. Gentle slopes at Klostermatte and Gerschnialp provide safe learning areas with dedicated children's zones. The Swiss Ski School Engelberg runs group lessons from the Brunni base, with a Snowli Kids Club for ages four and up offering full-day packages including instruction, supervision, and lunch for around CHF 130–160 per day. Children under nine ski free with a paying adult. The village is compact and walkable, with ski school meeting points within easy reach of accommodation.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski in Engelberg is centred on the Yucatan Bar at the Titlis base station, known for cold beers, loud music, and a lively atmosphere. The Ski Lodge Bar and Spindle bar in the village offer later evening entertainment with live music several nights a week during peak season. The atmosphere is international and sociable, with a strong Scandinavian influence. Mountain dining highlights include Trübsee Alpine Lodge and Panorama Restaurant Titlis, offering views across the Alps.
resortCharacterDescription: Engelberg is a traditional Swiss monastery village transformed into a ski hub. Its compact centre features historic buildings, cosy bars, and modern hotels, all surrounded by dramatic cliffs and peaks. The village sits in a natural bowl in the canton of Obwalden, with the Benedictine monastery as a historic landmark. The village is car-free in parts and walkable, with the train station a short walk from the main gondola base.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system includes 25 lifts, with the Titlis Xpress gondola connecting Engelberg to Trübsee and Stand, and the Rotair rotating cable car (the world's first) providing the final uplift to Klein Titlis at 3,020 m. The Trübsee-Hopper four-seater chairlift traverses the Trübsee lake. The Brunni area has a cable car from Klostermatte to Ristis, a three-person chairlift, and a T-bar. Snowmaking covers 40% of slopes with 164 snow cannons, ensuring lower slopes remain skiable. The system is efficient and reliable, with fast access to the glacier.

terrainDescription: The terrain in Dachstein West is dominated by intermediate runs, with 65% of the 51 km of marked trails classified as intermediate (red). The resort has 29% beginner and 6% advanced terrain, with no expert runs. The slopes are spread across three access points—Gosau, Russbach, and Annaberg—connected by a 44 km ski circuit called the Dachstein Tour. The vertical drop is 872 metres from a summit of 1,617 m to a base of 745 m. The terrain is generally gentle to moderate, with the advanced runs concentrated on a few steeper pitches such as the Törleck. The resort sits in the Salzkammergut region where the northern Alps transition into gentler hills, and the average run length is just over 1 km.
offPisteDescription: Off-piste skiing in Dachstein West is limited. The resort has no marked off-piste routes within its core area, and the moderate vertical and exposure restrict natural options. There is one ungroomed slope, the Törleck, which is classified as advanced and can be skied off-piste when conditions allow. A signposted skitouring route exists in Russbach, and the nearby Dachstein Krippenstein area (accessible with a multi-day pass) offers 30 km of off-piste variants with over 1,500 m of vertical descent, but that is separate from Dachstein West itself. For freeriders, the resort allows freeride skiing but does not have designated freeride zones.
valueDescription: Adult day lift tickets at Dachstein West cost €65 (2025/26 season), with junior passes at €47, children at €34, and seniors at €61.70. These prices are in the mid-range for Austrian resorts, roughly 20–30% below major destinations like the Arlberg. Multi-day passes from 1.5 days grant access to 70 lifts across 7 regions, including Dachstein Krippenstein and others, offering good value for those who want variety. Accommodation in the villages of Gosau, Russbach, and Annaberg ranges from guesthouses to family hotels, with self-catering apartments available. The resort is considered good value for families due to the extensive beginner and intermediate terrain and the free children's areas.
familyDescription: Dachstein West is specifically designed for families, with four dedicated children's areas: Fredy-Land at the Hornspitz valley station in Gosau, Zwisi-Land near the Panorama Jet Zwieselalm, Bärencamp in Russbach with mascot Bruno, and Fuxi's Kinderland in Annaberg. These areas feature magic carpets, practice lifts, and gentle slopes, and are free for children up to six years old when accompanied by a parent. The ski school offers English-speaking instructors. The villages are compact and walkable, with ski-in/ski-out accommodation available. A free ski bus connects the three villages and surrounding towns. The resort also has a natural toboggan run in Gosau's Hintertal valley, with free sled rental.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski in Dachstein West is relaxed and low-key. The resort has several traditional mountain huts and piste restaurants serving Austrian cuisine, such as the Ausblick and Sonnenalm huts in Gosau and d'Speis Food Truck at the Hornbahn valley station in Russbach. There is no exuberant nightlife; the atmosphere is more suited to families and those seeking a quiet evening. Some bars and restaurants offer drinks after skiing, but the scene is modest. The resort does not have nightclubs or large party venues.
resortCharacterDescription: Dachstein West is not a purpose-built resort but a collection of traditional Austrian villages—Gosau, Russbach, and Annaberg—in the Salzkammergut region, a UNESCO World Heritage area. Gosau is a ribbon village stretching 14 km along the valley, while Russbach and Annaberg are smaller mountain villages. The architecture is traditional, with wooden chalets and guesthouses. The area is known for its Dachstein panorama and is less commercialised than larger Austrian resorts. The resort has a calm, family-oriented atmosphere, with a focus on outdoor activities rather than après-ski.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system in Dachstein West consists of 24 lifts (as of 2025/26), including three modern gondolas: Hornspitz-Express, Panorama Jet Zwieselalm, and Donnerkogelbahn. Many chairlifts are equipped with weather protection hoods and heated seats. Older T-bar lifts serve as practice and connecting lifts. Two new 6-seater chairlifts (Angeralm and Aussichtsberg) with hoods, heated seats, and automatic locking are being built for the 2025/26 season to improve connections between Gosau and Annaberg. The uphill capacity is 27,600 persons per hour. Lift queues are generally short, even during Austrian school holidays. The resort also offers floodlit skiing every Friday evening on the Hornspitz slope from 18:30 to 20:30.
linkedResortsDescription: With a multi-day ski pass of 1.5 days or more, Dachstein West is linked to seven other ski regions, providing access to over 70 lifts and 160 km of slopes. The linked resorts include Dachstein Krippenstein Obertraun, Feuerkogel Ebensee, Karkogel & Sonnleitenlifte Abtenau, Skilift Lammertal Lungötz, St. Martin am Tennengebirge, Zinkenlifte Bad Dürrnberg, and Werfenweng (with a season pass). The Dachstein Tour, a 44 km ski circuit, connects the three base villages of Gosau, Russbach, and Annaberg within the core area. The nearby Dachstein Krippenstein is particularly noted for its freeride terrain and a long 11 km downhill run to Obertraun.

terrainDescription: The SkiWelt Wilder Kaiser-Brixental ski area is one of the largest interconnected ski areas in Austria, spanning nine villages: Söll, Scheffau, Ellmau, Going, Brixen im Thale, Westendorf, Hopfgarten, Itter, and Kelchsau. The slope network is well-balanced, with wide family runs, challenging red descents, and demanding black sections, plus 27 km of marked ski routes for experienced skiers. The most prominent peak, Hohe Salve (1,829 m), offers challenging on- and off-piste runs. Long on-piste descents exceed 1,200 m vertical from Hohe Salve into Hopfgarten (620 m) and over 1,000 m from Choralpe (1,820 m) into Westendorf (802 m). The black run from Choralpe has a maximum gradient of 80%. The area also includes three freestyle fun parks, race courses, and night skiing.
offPisteDescription: Off-piste terrain is limited compared to the groomed runs, but the Westendorf side offers good tree runs on powder days. The area includes 27 km of marked ski routes that provide off-piste-like experiences within the ski area. The Hohe Salve peak attracts advanced skiers with challenging off-piste lines. Overall, off-piste quality is not the resort's strongest feature, with limited advanced off-piste terrain available.
valueDescription: A day lift pass in main season costs €76 for adults, with youth and children tickets at €57 and €38 respectively. The SkiWelt is considered good value compared to larger Austrian resorts, with a value-for-money rating of 4.59/5 from user reviews. The Söll village in particular is noted for strong value relative to its access to the vast SkiWelt area. Multi-day passes and family packages offer further savings.
familyDescription: The SkiWelt has 22 ski schools with dedicated children's areas, offering courses from age two and childcare from five months (or from one year according to some sources). Free practice lifts are available in the valley for beginners. Special children's zones include the Igloo Village and natural ice sun lounge at the top of the Hochbrixen cable car. The area scores 4.89/5 for family friendliness in user reviews, with wide, gentle blue runs and reliable lift systems.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski is lively but not overwhelming, with a rating of 4.27/5. Notable venues include the Moonlightbar in Söll, a popular spot with DJ sets and a fun atmosphere, and the Brixner Stadl in Brixen im Thale, known for its lively scene and drinks. Over 80 mountain huts and inns across the villages provide a range of options from quiet to energetic. The resort also has Austria's largest night skiing area in Söll with 10 km of illuminated slopes.
resortCharacterDescription: The SkiWelt consists of nine authentic Tyrolean villages—Brixen im Thale, Ellmau, Going, Hopfgarten, Itter, Kelchsau, Scheffau, Söll, and Westendorf—each with distinct charm and family-run accommodations. The villages are not purpose-built but traditional, with the highest density of mountain huts and inns in Austria (over 80). The area offers ski-in/ski-out access and a car-free experience in many parts, with free shuttle buses between villages.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system comprises 81 lifts: 16 gondolas, 1 funicular, 32 chairlifts, and various surface lifts. The network is modern and efficient, with high transport capacity and short waiting times. The SkiWelt features the world's first 8-seater chairlift with an integrated photovoltaic system, and many lifts run on green electricity from local hydropower. Lift efficiency is rated 4.57/5 by users.
linkedResortsDescription: The SkiWelt is connected to the neighboring KitzSki ski region around Kitzbühel via the KitzSkiWelt Tour, which is the world's longest ski circuit, encompassing approximately 88 km of descents and 17,000 m of elevation gain. This connection gives access to a total of 284 miles (457 km) of slopes. The SkiWelt villages are all interconnected by lifts and pistes, allowing skiers to traverse the entire area without repeating runs. A marked SkiWelt Tour circuit covers 69 km clockwise or 84 km counter-clockwise through all villages.

terrainDescription: The Sölden ski area comprises two glacier sectors (Rettenbach and Tiefenbach) and two non-glacier sectors (Gaislachkogl and Giggijoch), connected by the Gletscherexpress two-way gondola. The terrain is dominated by wide, gentle cruising runs above the treeline, with most pistes facing south-east. The piste network includes 69.8 km of blue runs, 44.8 km of red runs, and 27.4 km of black runs. The longest groomed slope in Austria descends 15 km from the Schwarze Schneide viewing platform at 3,340 m to the valley, with a vertical drop of 2,000 m. The BIG3 Rally is a 50 km ski circuit linking the three peaks over 3,000 m: Gaislachkogl (3,058 m), Tiefenbachkogel (3,250 m), and Schwarze Schneid (3,340 m). The Gaislachkogl sector offers tree skiing on its lower slopes below 2,174 m, while the Giggijoch sector contains the snowpark and funslopes. The glacier sector has about 35 km of slopes, mostly blue, with one red and two black runs including the FIS World Cup course (piste 31). The home runs to the valley can become slushy in the afternoon, and there are bottlenecks at the glacier connection lifts (D51 and D53) during peak times.
offPisteDescription: Off-piste terrain in Sölden is accessible from several areas but requires a mountain guide. The Wasserkar area near Gaislachkogl offers lift-accessible off-piste, and there are routes next to the FIS World Cup slope on the Rettenbach glacier. Steep and dangerous lines descend from the ridge behind Gaislachkogl. The ski area also has extensive ski touring opportunities. The off-piste is described as varied and can stay fresh for a day or two after a snowfall. There are five ungroomed ski routes within the piste network, and an avalanche-secured ungroomed route on the glacier.
valueDescription: Sölden is considered an expensive resort, partly due to its proximity to Innsbruck. A 6-day adult lift pass costs €407.50 in low season and €438.00 in high season (2021 prices). Children
resortCharacterDescription: "Sölden is a modern

terrainDescription: The resort's 42 km of north-facing pistes run through dense Pohorje forest, with tree-lined slopes providing natural shelter and visual markers. The terrain is split into three sections: Snow Stadium (325–1,050 m), Bolfenk (937–1,150 m), and Areh (927–1,327 m). There are 13 blue runs (23.5 km), 9 red runs (13 km), and 3 black runs (5 km). The Turistična proga is a 4,595 m blue run from the gondola top station winding gently through forest. The Pisker piste (1,870 m, red) is wide and well-groomed for carving. The full top-to-bottom descent from Areh summit (1,327 m) to the Snow Stadium (325 m) drops 1,002 m through forest, ending at the city edge. Ten kilometres of illuminated slopes allow night skiing from Bellevue down to the valley.
offPisteDescription: Off-piste terrain is limited but includes the Jonatan route, a 1,535 m unprepared black descent beneath the gondola line. After fresh snowfall, the dense Pohorje forest between marked runs holds snow well and offers enjoyable tree skiing. The north-facing aspect helps preserve conditions. For more extensive off-piste, the Kanin resort near Bovec (about three hours' drive) provides genuine alpine off-piste terrain at 2,293 m.
valueDescription: Slovenia keeps costs well below western Alpine averages. A day lift pass costs €46 (2025/26 season), with discounts for children (€19) and students. Accommodation is inexpensive: a four-star hotel in Maribor city centre can be booked for around €50 per person per night. Self-catering apartments near the slopes are also affordable. Dining in Maribor's restaurants is significantly cheaper than in French or Swiss resorts, with traditional Slovenian meals at moderate prices. The resort's location just 6 km from Maribor means visitors can avoid expensive on-mountain accommodation.
familyDescription: The nursery area at the Snow Stadium base has gentle slopes, magic carpet lifts, and a children's play area with a timed race track. The Alpine School Pohorje offers preschool courses, family packages, and individual lessons. Tree-lined runs feel safe and enclosed, and the Turistična proga is a scenic blue run popular with families. The city of Maribor provides additional entertainment: bowling alleys, pool bars, escape rooms, a puppet theatre, and a Saturday market. Night skiing on 10 km of lit slopes is a family highlight. Mountain huts serve hearty, child-friendly food, and the city has many pizza and pasta restaurants.
apresSkiDescription: Maribor is a university city of 100,000 people, offering a lively nightlife scene with dance venues, bars, and clubs. The resort itself has a vibrant après-ski atmosphere, with mountain huts and bars near the slopes. An ice rink in the city centre features disco lights and music, open in three-hour slots. The Snow Stadium area has several bars and restaurants. Evening options include bowling, pool bars, and escape rooms. The city's old town has cosy bars and restaurants along the Drava river.
resortCharacterDescription: Maribor Pohorje is a city ski resort, not a traditional alpine village. The slopes descend from the forested Pohorje ridge directly into the city of Maribor, with the Snow Stadium at the base at 325 m elevation. The resort consists of three sections: the lower Snow Stadium, the middle Bolfenk area, and the upper Areh section. The mountain is heavily forested, and runs offer views of Maribor's rooftops and the Drava valley. The resort is purpose-built with modern lift infrastructure but retains a natural forest character. It is the largest ski resort in Slovenia.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system comprises 22 lifts: 1 gondola (Pohorska vzpenjača, 2,505 m, 6.75 min), 3 double chairlifts, 2 four-chairlifts, 1 six-chairlift, and 15 surface lifts, plus 3 magic carpets. Total lift capacity is 22,300 skiers per hour. The gondola connects the city directly to the slopes. Queues are rare outside peak periods. The lifts are modern and well-maintained, with recent investments in snowmaking infrastructure. Night skiing uses illuminated slopes served by the gondola and chairlifts.
linkedResortsDescription: Maribor Pohorje is the largest resort in the Pohorje mountain range and is directly connected to the Areh area, which is considered part of the same resort. The nearby Rogla ski resort (about 40 km away) is accessible as a day trip and offers additional terrain, including a snowpark with kickers, rails, and a halfpipe. Combined lift pass options are available. Other Pohorje resorts include Kope–Ribnica Pohorje and Trije Kralji, but they are not directly linked by lifts. A ski bus connects the Bolfenk and Areh sections of Maribor Pohorje.

terrainDescription: The terrain across Flachau, Wagrain, and St. Johann-Alpendorf ranges from gentle beginner slopes to challenging black runs. Flachau is known for its wide, sun-drenched pistes and hosts the Hermann Maier World Cup course. Wagrain features a FunSlope with over 30 elements and the SOKO Funslope. Alpendorf offers the Trio Infernale – three steep black runs – and a 1 km snow park with kickers and rails. The 12 Peaks Tour connects 12 peaks and 5 valleys for advanced skiers. Children have dedicated areas like the Devil's Route adventure descent and Wagraini's Winter World. The area also includes snow parks, race tracks, and SkiMovie tracks for timed runs.
valueDescription: Ski pass holders can travel for free on all public transport in the Salzburg region on the day of arrival and departure, including trains and buses. Free ski buses connect the villages of Flachau, Wagrain, and St. Johann. Accommodation guests receive a Mobility Card for free public transport throughout Salzburg. E-charging stations are available at valley stations for electric cars.
familyDescription: Wagraini's Winter World on Grafenberg is a children's paradise with practice slopes, a tubing track, tire carousel, and a heated dwarf house. The Fichtelland adventure zone offers playful trails and magic carpets. The Devil's Route is an adventure descent with characters like Krampus, witches, and ghosts.
