linkedResortsDescription: Tarvisio is part of the Friuli Venezia Giulia Ski Area 3 Confini, which includes the nearby Sella Nevea area. Sella Nevea offers 15 km of cross-border slopes (10.5 km in Italy, 4.5 km in Slovenia) and is a freeride paradise. Additionally, the Austrian resort of Nassfeld is just over the border and can be combined with Tarvisio for a larger ski area. The Dreiländereck area is also nearby.

terrainDescription: The ski area spans three mountains: Mitterstein, Lawinenstein, and Schneiderkogel, with naturally undulating slopes between 820 m and 1,965 m. Of the 43 km of pistes, 6 km are black runs including the Schneiderkogel, Osthand-Krallersee, and Mitterstein slopes. Two marked ski circuits (Panoramarunde and Große Almrunde) take 2 to 3.5 hours each. The Tauplitzalm plateau sits at 1,650 m, is car-free, and offers sunny, above-treeline skiing with views of the Dachstein and Totes Gebirge. The terrain mix includes wide blue runs, red intermediate slopes, and challenging black descents.
offPisteDescription: Tauplitz is a well-known natural freeride area with deep-snow descents around Schneiderkogel, Lawinenstein, and the Tauplitzalm. Specific lines include the Kraller east slope, Kleine Süd, and ski routes into the Totes Gebirge. Freeride camps are held by Die Bergstation. The area receives substantial snowfall (records of up to 5 m depth), and the terrain offers steep drops and cornices in the high alpine zone. A Freeride & Alpin Center provides tips and guiding.
valueDescription: A six-day adult Schneebären skipass costs €317, with children up to 6 years free when accompanied by parents. Day tickets are €59 for adults, €50 for youth, and €30 for children. The free ski bus between Bad Mitterndorf and Tauplitz reduces transport costs. Accommodation on the car-free Tauplitzalm includes rustic huts and hotels, and family ski packages are available. The resort is part of the Super Ski Card and Steiermark Joker card associations, offering multi-resort value.
familyDescription: The Kinderland children's area in the Hollhausmulde on the Tauplitzalm features an 80 m long magic carpet and fun attractions. Nursery slopes are separate from faster traffic, and the car-free Tauplitzalm village provides a safe environment. Children up to 6 years ski free with parents. The ski school offers English-speaking instructors, and there are childcare options. The free ski bus and family-friendly accommodation (e.g., Aldiana Club Salzkammergut) add convenience.
apresSkiDescription: The resort has 3 bars/pubs and 2 clubs, with a relaxed rather than rowdy scene. Mountain huts serve traditional Styrian dishes like goulash and Kaiserschmarrn. Night activities include an illuminated 600 m toboggan run with rental toboggans, and the GrimmingTherme thermal spa in Bad Mitterndorf offers pools, saunas, and wellness treatments for post-ski relaxation.
resortCharacterDescription: Tauplitz is an authentic, traditional ski area with a car-free alpine plateau village at 1,650 m, consisting of rustic huts and hotels with around 1,000 guest beds. The base villages of Tauplitz and Bad Mitterndorf have a traditional Austrian character with wooden chalets, thermal spas, and a relaxed atmosphere. The resort is not purpose-built; it has a ski history dating back to 1905 and retains a genuine alpine feel.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system comprises 17 lifts: 1 gondola, 4 chairlifts (including a six-pack and a quad), 10 T-bars, and 2 magic carpets, with a total capacity of 18,643 persons per hour. The first drag lift in Austria was built here in 1935. Recent investments include a new ski valley station completed in winter 2019/20, a new 1 km piste connection, and expanded snowmaking covering 99% of slopes with 150 machines. Lifts operate from 8:30/9:00 to 16:00.
linkedResortsDescription: Tauplitz – Bad Mitterndorf is part of the Schneebären ski association, which includes four other nearby ski centres under one skipass: Riesneralm (30 km of slopes), Planneralm (16 km), Loser (33 km), and Kaiserau (3 km). The combined area offers over 120 km of slopes. The Schneebären Card also provides access to these resorts, expanding the terrain options within a short drive.

terrainDescription: The ski area at Tegelberg offers 4 runs totaling approximately 7 km, with 2 km classified as blue (easy) and 6 km as red (intermediate). The longest run is a 4.2 km valley descent from the summit at 1,720 m down to the base at 830 m. The terrain is predominantly intermediate (42% of runs) and advanced (58%), with no expert runs. Slopes are mostly north- and west-facing (42% north, 51% west), and 1.9 km of runs are illuminated for night skiing. A dedicated children's area features a Sunkid magic carpet and gentle surface lifts (Gams and Falken), while the Adler and Reith lifts serve steeper terrain. The main ski route runs through alpine terrain and is also open for ski touring once a week until late night.
valueDescription: Lift pass prices are among the most affordable in Germany: a one-day adult ticket costs €32–€34, a youth ticket €31–€33, and a child ticket €16–€17.50. Accommodation in Schwangau ranges from luxury hotels to budget pensions and holiday apartments, with many options within walking distance of the cable car. Free parking is available at the valley station. The resort's compact size and low lift prices make it a budget-friendly choice for families and day-trippers.
familyDescription: The resort is well suited for families, with a dedicated children's learning area equipped with a Sunkid magic carpet and gentle surface lifts (Gams and Falken). The Schwangau snow sports school offers lessons for children of all ages. A wide family run (Familienabfahrt) provides a gentle descent for progressing skiers. Night skiing on illuminated runs (1.9 km) adds an after-dark activity. The nearby Buchenberg area offers tobogganing and a double chairlift. Non-skiing attractions include the Neuschwanstein and Hohenschwangau castles, which are within 2 km and provide cultural outings for rest days.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski is low-key, with two bars and pubs in the village of Schwangau. The mountain has two restaurants: the panorama restaurant at the top station and the rustic Tegelberghaus (a former hunting lodge of King Ludwig II), both serving Bavarian specialties such as white sausages, pretzels, and Germknödel. The scene is quiet and family-oriented, with no large nightclubs or late-night venues.
resortCharacterDescription: The resort is based in the Bavarian village of Schwangau, a traditional alpine settlement with a picturesque setting at the foot of the Ammergau Alps. The area is dominated by the famous Neuschwanstein and Hohenschwangau castles, which are visible from the slopes. The village has a mix of historic buildings and modern tourist infrastructure, with a relaxed, family-friendly atmosphere. The Tegelberg cable car provides access to the mountain, and the village is car-free in parts, with most amenities within walking distance.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of one modern gondola (Tegelbergbahn) and four surface lifts (drag lifts). The gondola is 2,146 m long, climbs 892.5 m, and has two cabins each carrying 44 passengers, driven by a 2,540 kW engine. It was built by Hölzl Seilbahnbau and has a 38 m high reinforced concrete support pillar. The surface lifts include the Adler, Falken, Reith, and Gams lifts, serving different parts of the mountain. Total uphill capacity is 4,180 persons per hour. The lifts operate from 09:00 to 16:30, with night skiing on illuminated runs until late evening.

terrainDescription: The Cedars sits in a single high-alpine bowl with nine groomed runs, five of which are rated for advanced skiers. The terrain is set among ancient cedar forests, with wide, gentle slopes at the top that are ideal for beginners and families. The bowl faces north, helping preserve snow quality, and the compact layout means all runs are within easy reach of the base lifts. The resort's higher elevation (base around 2,100 m, summit up to 2,850 m) provides reliable snow cover through the season.
offPisteDescription: The Cedars offers accessible off-piste terrain in the high-alpine bowl and adjacent slopes, with fresh lines that remain untracked due to low crowd density compared to Mzaar. The higher elevation and north-facing aspects preserve snow quality, making it a popular choice for advanced skiers seeking powder. Off-piste enthusiasts can explore the bowl's edges and the open faces below the summit, though no marked off-piste routes exist and caution is advised due to variable snow conditions.
valueDescription: Lift passes at The Cedars are among the most affordable in Lebanon, with adult day passes around $30 on weekdays and $40 on weekends. Accommodation in nearby Bsharri village offers budget-friendly guesthouses and traditional lodges, often cheaper than staying on the mountain. Equipment rental is available at competitive rates (around $15-25 per day). The resort's smaller size and lower infrastructure costs contribute to its value, though amenities are more basic than at larger resorts like Mzaar.
familyDescription: The Cedars is well-suited for families, with gentle, wide slopes at the top that are ideal for beginners and children. The resort's serene atmosphere and traditional lodges provide a relaxed environment. Ski schools and equipment rental are available, and the nearby Bsharri village offers family-friendly accommodation and cultural attractions such as the Gibran Museum and the Qadisha Valley monasteries. The compact layout means parents can easily supervise children on the slopes.
apresSkiDescription: The Cedars has a minimal après-ski scene, with a few cozy chalets and restaurants in the base area serving Lebanese cuisine and traditional music. The real nightlife is in Beirut, a two-hour drive away. Most visitors enjoy a quiet evening in Bsharri village, where local eateries offer hearty mountain dishes. The resort's atmosphere is serene and family-oriented rather than party-focused.
resortCharacterDescription: The Cedars is a traditional, rustic ski resort set in a high-alpine bowl adjacent to the UNESCO-protected Cedars of God forest. The base area features historic lodges and a few modern facilities, but the overall character is natural and unpolished, with a focus on the surrounding cedar groves and mountain scenery rather than purpose-built infrastructure. The resort's first ski lift was installed in 1953, making it Lebanon's oldest ski area, and it retains a time-capsule charm.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system at The Cedars consists of six lifts, including chairlifts and surface lifts, serving a compact ski area of about 11 km of marked piste. The lifts are older and less modern than those at Mzaar, but they are sufficient for the resort's size. Queues are generally short except on peak weekends. The resort's first ski lift was installed in 1953, making it Lebanon's oldest ski area, and the current infrastructure has seen limited upgrades since.

terrainDescription: The terrain at Thollon-les-Mémises consists of 16 pistes spread across 50 km of skiable area, rising from 1,400 m at the village to 1,974 m at the summit. The slopes are heavily weighted toward beginner and intermediate runs, with wide, tree-lined pistes that suit children learning to ski and improvers building confidence. Advanced skiers find challenge on steeper tree-lined runs descending toward Lake Geneva. The slopes are genuine mountain terrain adapted for skiing rather than purpose-built manicured runs, offering a more varied riding experience. The lake-view aspect is distinctive: from the top of the Mémises cable car and several pistes, the view sweeps across the resort and onto the full expanse of Lake Geneva with the Swiss shore and Jura mountains beyond.
valueDescription: Thollon-les-Mémises offers some of the most affordable lift passes in the French Alps. The 2025-26 adult day pass is €30, with a 6-day pass at approximately €170 and a full 7-day week at €170.50. This represents savings of 50-60% compared to major resorts like Val Thorens or Val d'Isère, where 6-day passes cost €395-450. For a family of four, the weekly lift-pass bill is around €680, compared to €1,600-2,000 in premium resorts. Accommodation prices are also significantly lower: typical resale chalet pricing is €3,000-4,500 per m², 60-80% below Portes du Soleil resorts. A 180 m² family chalet with lake views can trade at €540,000-650,000. Dining costs are also lower, with dinner for four at a village restaurant typically €80-120.
familyDescription: Thollon-les-Mémises is a family-friendly resort with dedicated facilities for children. The ski school offers teaching from age 4, with multilingual instruction and dedicated learner zones. Childcare is available from 6 months to 4 years old. The nursery slopes are located on the sunny village face, and the resort has a sledging run, play area, and a magic carpet for beginners. The village is compact and walkable, with ski school meeting points close to accommodation. The terrain is heavily weighted toward beginner and intermediate runs, with wide, tree-lined slopes that are sheltered and not overcrowded. The resort also has a snowpark and half-pipe for older children. Off-slope activities include ice skating, dog sledding, and the Aquacime leisure centre.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski at Thollon-les-Mémises is low-key and family-oriented. The village has a couple of bar-restaurants with outdoor fire pits, and the evening pace rewards early nights and early starts. There is a cinema and a book shop. The mountain-top restaurant Les Balcons du Lac, opened in 2020, offers Lake Geneva views and Savoyard cuisine. Dining options include casual Savoyard fare such as fondue, raclette, and tartiflette at local restaurants. For more lively nightlife, the nearby town of Évian (13 km away) offers more options. The resort does not have a rowdy après-ski scene.
resortCharacterDescription: Thollon-les-Mémises is an authentic Savoyard village perched at 1,100 m above sea level, overlooking Lake Geneva. The architecture reflects genuine Savoyard tradition: shuttered stone buildings, pitched roofs designed for snow load, and stone chimneys. The village has not been sacrificed to tourism; it has a whitewashed church, local shops including a Casino supermarket, and a handful of restaurants. There is no purpose-built resort centre or sprawl of concrete blocks. The village is compact and walkable, with the Mémises cable car base station at its heart. The resort is nicknamed the 'Balcony on Lake Geneva' due to its panoramic views of the lake and Jura mountains.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system at Thollon-les-Mémises consists of 14-18 lifts depending on seasonal operation, including a modern gondola as the primary uplift from the village base, plus chairlifts and drag lifts distributed across the domain. The lift network has a capacity of approximately 12,500 skiers per hour. On busy weekends, queues can form but are not as severe as at larger resorts. The lifts serve 16 pistes across 50 km of terrain. The village lift system allows ski-in/ski-out access from many accommodations. The lifts are generally well-maintained, though some reviews note that lifts are sometimes closed when there is snow in April or November.
linkedResortsDescription: Thollon-les-Mémises is part of the 'Les Montagnes d'Évian' linked ski area, which also includes the Abondance ski area. The Evian Mountain Year pass provides winter access to both Thollon and Abondance, as well as summer access to Thollon. Additionally, the Magic Pass network, active since the 2025/26 season, links Thollon to over 99 resorts across Switzerland and France, allowing pass-holders to ski at multiple resorts. A regular shuttle bus connects Thollon to Évian, and from there to other resorts. The nearby resorts of Bernex and La Chapelle-d'Abondance are also within easy reach.

terrainDescription: The Espace Killy ski area spans 300 km of marked pistes, with 22 green, 61 blue, 46 red, and 25 black runs. Val d'Isère's terrain is divided into three main sectors: Solaise offers long cruising blues, Bellevarde features steep blacks including the famous Face de Bellevarde, and Le Fornet provides access to off-piste terrain. Tignes contributes the Grande Motte glacier at 3,456 m with wide, gentle slopes at the top, and wooded runs around Les Brévières. Tignes also has long blue descents and expert routes like the black Sache run. The linked area includes two terrain parks and 44 km of cross-country skiing.
offPisteDescription: The Aiguille Percée sector above Tignes Le Lac features north-facing slopes that hold powder for days after a snowfall, with dramatic terrain and the famous rock arch. Val d'Isère's off-piste is particularly impressive around Le Fornet and La Daille, where couloirs and open bowls challenge experienced riders. The Col Pers and Pisaillas glacier areas offer serious backcountry for guided groups. The Chardonnet valley below Tignes also provides outstanding off-piste terrain.
valueDescription: Tignes offers better value than Val d'Isère, with a wider choice of mid-range apartments and more affordable restaurants. Accommodation costs are typically 20-30% lower in Tignes. Self-catering apartments dominate, keeping costs down. Lift passes are the same across both resorts, giving access to the full 300 km of pistes. For budget-conscious travellers or larger groups, Tignes provides significantly better value without compromising skiing quality.
familyDescription: Tignes has four dedicated beginner zones spread across Val Claret, Le Lac, Le Lavachet, and Les Brévières, offering gentle slopes and ski schools that cater to children and beginners in multiple languages. The resort's ski-in/ski-out setup means families spend less time walking and more time skiing. Off the slopes, the Lagon Centre features pools and slides. Val d'Isère also has nursery slopes in the village centre and snow gardens, and holds the Famille Plus label, but Tignes' layout is more convenient for families with young children.
apresSkiDescription: Val d'Isère offers one of the most iconic après-ski scenes in the Alps, with La Folie Douce on the Bellevarde slopes featuring live music and open-air shows, and village venues like Cocorico and Doudoune Club keeping the buzz going late. Tignes delivers a more laid-back but still vibrant offering, with popular venues including Loop Bar with its sunny terrace and live music, as well as DropZone and Le Moose for relaxed drinks and dancing. Dining in Tignes is diverse and generally more budget-friendly, with highlights including the panoramic Le Panoramic on Grande Motte and the Michelin-starred Ursus in Val Claret.
resortCharacterDescription: Val d'Isère is a historic Savoyard village with traditional stone-and-wood architecture, a baroque church, and narrow streets that predate ski tourism. It has a pedestrian-friendly centre with boutique shopping and fine dining. Tignes, by contrast, was rebuilt after the original village was submerged under the Lac du Chevril reservoir in the 1950s. The architecture is functional concrete from the 1960s and 70s, though recent renovations have improved comfort. The resort is split into several villages including Val Claret, Le Lac, and the more peaceful Les Brévières, which retains some old Savoyard character.
liftSystemDescription: The lift network comprises 90 lifts including 2 funiculars, 4 cable cars, 4 gondolas, 44 chairlifts, and 36 surface lifts. A new high-speed six-seater chairlift serving the Aiguille Percée sector was installed for winter 2025-26, travelling at six metres per second and carrying up to 2,800 skiers per hour, replacing an older quad. The system is modern and efficient, with heated chairlifts and high-speed gondolas. The Val d'Isère side has 71 lifts including 37 chairlifts, 6 gondolas, and 3 cable cars.
linkedResortsDescription: Tignes and Val d'Isère are linked to form the Espace Killy ski area, with over 300 km of marked pistes and 90 ski lifts. The two resorts are connected by multiple lift and piste links, allowing skiers to move freely between them on a single lift pass. The area also includes the Grande Motte glacier, which extends the season and provides reliable high-altitude skiing.

terrainDescription: The ski area at Torgnon covers 13 km of pistes, with 19% beginner, 60% intermediate, and 21% advanced runs. The terrain is predominantly intermediate, with wide red runs accessed from the Col Fenêtre and Collet chairlifts. A single blue run from Chantorné provides easy cruising, while two black runs offer more challenging descents. The slopes are situated on a sunny, south-facing plateau with views of the Matterhorn. A snow park and a snow playground are available for freestyle and family fun.
valueDescription: A daily lift pass costs €39 (as of 2025), which is lower than many larger Aosta Valley resorts. The resort is also included in the Cervinia multi-day ski pass, offering additional value for those staying in Cervinia. Accommodation and dining in the traditional village are reasonably priced compared to purpose-built resorts.
familyDescription: Torgnon is well-suited for families, with a dedicated snow playground and a ski school offering patient instruction. The gentle blue run from Chantorné and the wide red runs provide safe progression for beginners. The slopes are calm and rarely crowded, and the compact village is easy to navigate with children.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski in Torgnon is low-key, with a few mountain huts and village bars. Bar Ristorante La Vetta and Cafè La Vigne are popular spots for drinks and live music. The focus is on relaxed dining and enjoying the mountain atmosphere rather than a party scene.
resortCharacterDescription: Torgnon is a traditional Alpine village composed of 22 hamlets spread across a sunny moraine terrace. The architecture is typical of the Aosta Valley, with stone and wood buildings. The village is not purpose-built; it has a historic character with a 16th-century house and a parish museum. The setting offers panoramic views of the Matterhorn.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of 7 lifts, including an 8-seater cable car from Mongnod to Chantorné, a four-seater chairlift at Chantorné, a double chairlift at Col Fenêtre, and a four-seater chairlift at Collet. The main cable car has an external ski rack. The lifts are a mix of modern and older installations, with a total uphill capacity of 5,500 skiers per hour. Snowmaking covers 10 km of runs.
linkedResortsDescription: Torgnon is not directly lift-linked to other resorts, but it is included in the Cervinia multi-day ski pass, allowing skiers staying in Cervinia to access Torgnon's slopes without an additional ticket. The resort is a 30-minute drive from Cervinia. Similarly, the nearby car-free village of Chamois is also covered by the same pass.

terrainDescription: The local Torgon ski area offers 23 km of pistes, with 71% beginner and 29% intermediate runs. The slopes are wide and natural, with a vertical drop of 644 m from 1,335 m to 1,979 m. From the top of Plan de Croix, there are views of Lake Geneva, the Dents du Midi, and Mont Blanc. The terrain is generally mellow, with some steeper sections in the Torgon sector as noted by the Espace Liberté description.
offPisteDescription: Off-piste in Torgon is low-key but rewarding. The gentle, open terrain above the treeline holds powder longer than busier parts of the Portes du Soleil due to low skier numbers. There are tree runs in the lower sections, and the area is lift-accessible. The freeride is not technical but offers fun, low-risk lines after fresh snowfall.
valueDescription: Lift pass prices are lower than in larger Portes du Soleil resorts. A day pass costs 51 CHF for adults, and a six-day pass costs 243 CHF. Accommodation in Torgon is generally affordable, with self-catering apartments and small guesthouses. The resort is less developed, keeping costs down.
familyDescription: Torgon is a small family-oriented resort with a dedicated beginner area at Jorette, featuring magic carpets and gentle slopes. The Swiss Ski School offers lessons for all ages. The village is quiet and safe for children, with a relaxed atmosphere. The resort is part of the Portes du Soleil, giving families access to a wide range of slopes.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski in Torgon is very quiet. There are a few local restaurants and bars, such as Le Perroquet, and a nightclub, but the scene is low-key. Most visitors head to larger resorts like Châtel or Morzine for more lively options.
resortCharacterDescription: Torgon is a traditional Swiss mountain village with wooden chalets and a peaceful atmosphere. It sits on a sunny terrace above the Rhone valley, with views of Lake Geneva and the Alps. The resort has retained its authentic character with moderate development, unlike larger purpose-built stations.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system in Torgon has been reduced: the two main lifts from the village to the slopes have been permanently closed since 2016/17. Skiing is now accessed via the Plan-du-Croix sector, which has a quad chair and several surface lifts. The resort has 10 lifts in total, but access from the village requires a short drive or bus to the base area.
linkedResortsDescription: Torgon is part of the Portes du Soleil ski area, which links 12 resorts across France and Switzerland with 600 km of pistes and 202 lifts. It is also part of the smaller Espace Liberté area, which includes Châtel, La Chapelle d'Abondance, and Morgins, offering 120 km of slopes with 56 lifts. Direct connections from Torgon include the Tour de Don lift to Châtel and links to La Chapelle d'Abondance.

terrainDescription: The ski area consists of approximately 10–11 km of marked runs, with a longest descent of 4 km. The most difficult run is the black 'Scholmental' (2 km). A beginner slope called the 'Church Run' is located near the village and is served by a conveyor lift. Slopes end directly in front of accommodation, and the area is uncrowded with a raw, remote feel.
valueDescription: A day lift pass costs €46–€47.50, making it one of the cheapest ski resorts in South Tyrol. Child passes are €21.50–€22.50, and children under 6 ski for free. Accommodation ranges from budget to luxury, though budget options are limited (rating 2.0 for cheap rooms).
familyDescription: Childcare is available for children from 48 months, with 5 hours of care per day at €44 without lunch. A magic carpet and a dedicated child lift serve the beginner 'Church Run' slope. The ski school has 5 instructors, and a free ski bus connects the village. Lift queues are rare, and the resort is described as especially family-friendly.
apresSkiDescription: Apres-ski is very limited: there is 1 bar and 1 club. The Berggasthof Trafoi and Lounge 2 are the main venues, offering a friendly but quiet atmosphere. The overall apres-ski rating is 2.0, indicating few options.
resortCharacterDescription: Trafoi is a quiet, idyllic hamlet nestled in the Stelvio National Park, surrounded by glaciers and the Ortler peak. It features traditional alpine architecture and offers a remote, scenic setting far from the crowds of larger resorts.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of 4 lifts: one main chairlift (over 40 years old and slow), two drag lifts, and one conveyor. Total uphill capacity is 2,500–2,834 persons per hour. The resort is uncrowded, so lift queues are rare, and staff are rated as friendly (5.0).
linkedResortsDescription: Trafoi is part of the Ortler Skiarena, which includes 123 marked pistes served by 79 lifts. A single lift pass grants access to the other resorts in the arena: Haideralm, Latsch Tarscheralm, Maseben, Mendel Ruffrè, Meran 2000, Pfelders, Reinswald, Rittner Horn, Schnalstal, Schöneben, Schwemmalm, Sulden am Ortler, Vigiljoch, and Watles.

terrainDescription: The terrain consists of four named slopes on the north face of Mount Olympus: the Zeus slope (380 m, advanced, FIS homologated), Hera (350 m, beginner), Aphrodite (150 m, beginner), and Hermes (150 m, intermediate). The total groomed piste is approximately 3 km, with a vertical drop of about 80 m from 1,930 m to 1,850 m. The slopes are short and compact, suitable for a day trip, and the resort is the southernmost ski destination in Europe.
valueDescription: Cyprus is one of the cheapest skiing destinations in Europe. A family of four can ski for a week in late February for a total of £474, including return flights, car hire, and second-hand equipment. Lift passes are inexpensive and can be purchased online or at the resort. Accommodation in nearby villages like Prodromos or Platres is affordable, and the resort itself offers equipment hire and day passes.
familyDescription: The resort has a dedicated baby lift on the Sun Valley slopes, serving the 150 m Aphrodite and Hermes runs, which are suitable for beginners and children. Ski lessons can be arranged on the day at the ski school huts near the hire building. The compact layout and short slopes make it easy for families to stay together, and the resort is less crowded than major Alpine destinations. The Sun Valley Baby Lift is specifically noted for children.
resortCharacterDescription: The ski resort is located on the north face of Mount Olympus, the highest peak in Cyprus, at an elevation of around 1,850–1,930 m. The base area consists of the Cyprus Ski Club building with a shop, hire facilities, and refreshment areas. The nearest villages are Troodos (3 km) and Prodromos (5 km), which offer accommodation and traditional Cypriot tavernas. The surrounding Troodos Mountains are known for Byzantine churches and forested slopes, giving the area a natural, non-commercial character. The resort is not a purpose-built ski village but a mountain-top facility with a laid-back atmosphere.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of four lifts: one chairlift on the Zeus slope (380 m) and three T-bar surface lifts (Hera 350 m, Aphrodite 150 m, Hermes 150 m). All lifts are equipped with smart gates using chip-and-pin passes, which can be purchased at cash desks at Sun Valley and North Face locations. The lifts operate daily from 10:00 am to 5:00 pm during the season.

terrainDescription: The terrain at Tschiertschen consists of varied slopes covering forest and high alpine terrain, with wide natural pistes and tree-lined runs. The north-facing aspect helps preserve snow quality throughout the season. Most runs are suitable for beginner and intermediate skiers, with long red slopes ideal for cruising. The slopes are uncrowded, providing a relaxed skiing experience.
offPisteDescription: Off-piste skiing is a highlight at Tschiertschen, with a wide range of routes that can stay untracked for several days. The ski safari to Arosa Lenzerheide offers deep snow slopes and unspoilt descents with spectacular views. Local guides are available to ensure safety. The north-facing slopes and low visitor numbers contribute to good snow preservation in off-piste areas.
valueDescription: Tschiertschen offers good value compared to nearby mega-resorts, with lower lift prices. A midweek 'Pistenknüller' deal provides an all-day lift ticket plus lunch at any of the five on-mountain restaurants for around CHF 29. Children under 10 ski free. Accommodation options include budget rooms, though availability is limited. The calm, uncrowded slopes add to the overall value.
familyDescription: Tschiertschen is very family-friendly with uncrowded, safe slopes that are less intimidating for children. The resort offers excellent childcare facilities, including at least one reasonably priced nursery. Children under 10 ski free. There is a long toboggan run and idyllic winter hiking trails. Free parking and easy access from Chur (about 15 minutes by car) add convenience.
resortCharacterDescription: Tschiertschen is a traditional mountain village with charming old-Switzerland character, featuring wooden houses and a peaceful atmosphere. It is considered one of the most beautiful mountain villages in Graubünden. The resort is not purpose-built; it retains authentic alpine charm with no massive lift systems or busy après-ski scenes.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system is small and old-school, consisting of 5 lifts (2 chairlifts and 3 surface lifts). It is not modern or high-speed, but queues are rare due to low crowds. The lifts provide access to the entire ski area, including sectors like Waldstafel, Hüenerchöpf, Jochalp, and Gürgaletsch.
linkedResortsDescription: Tschiertschen is linked to the Arosa Lenzerheide ski area via a ski safari, allowing access to deep snow slopes and unspoilt descents. A special Skisafari ticket is available for those wanting to explore both areas, but the standard Tschiertschen pass only covers the local lifts. Local guides can accompany freeriders on the safari.

terrainDescription: The ski area offers 43 km of pistes between 1,400 m and 2,205 m, with 14.5 km of blue runs, 25 km of red runs, and 3.2 km of black runs. The terrain is dominated by wide, gentle slopes around Lake Turrach, suitable for beginners, and sporty red runs on the Kornock, Wildkopf, and Panorama lift sectors. The only black run is the 0.6 km Direttissima (piste 35). A 12 km circuit around the lake allows skiers to cross between Carinthia and Styria multiple times. The slopes are predominantly north-facing, with 44% north, 34% east, 6% south, and 17% west orientation. The highest point is at the Kornockbahn top station at 2,205 m, offering wide cruising runs.
offPisteDescription: Off-piste skiing is available from the top of the Kornockbahn at 2,205 m, with open terrain on the soft, rounded Nockberge mountains. The backcountry offers opportunities for powder skiing, though no specific routes or couloirs are described in the sources. The area is within the Nockberge Biosphere Reserve, with natural snow conditions.
valueDescription: Lift pass prices for the 2025/2026 season: adult day pass €68, 6-day pass €363; child day pass €35, 6-day pass €182. Children under 6 ski for free. Multi-day tickets are valid throughout Carinthia and East Tyrol. Parking is free at the valley stations. Accommodation ranges from luxury hotels (Hotel Hochschober from €370/night) to mid-range (€150-200/night) and chalets (€200-400/night). The resort is small and uncrowded, with minimal lift queues, offering good value for a snow-sure Austrian resort.
familyDescription: The resort is family-friendly with dedicated nursery slopes by Lake Turrach, served by the Übungswiesenlift, Ottifantenlift, and Mängländerlift. Children under 6 ski for free. Night skiing is available on Wednesdays from 19:30 to 21:00 on the Übungswiesenlift. The Nocky Flitzer alpine coaster (1.6 km) operates year-round. Ice skating is possible on the frozen Turracher See in winter. The Pistenbutler service provides small surprises and assistance on the slopes. Ski schools offer courses for children and adults, and there is a Kids' Slope and a Fun Mountain with a snow park.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski options include the K-Alm and Sonnalm bars, and the Nockalm pavilion serving beer on tap. There are 5 bars and pubs and 2 clubs in the resort. The atmosphere is relaxed rather than rowdy, with a focus on family-friendly activities. Night skiing and night sledding (Tuesdays and Thursdays) provide evening entertainment.
resortCharacterDescription: Turracher Höhe is a traditional mountain village on a pass summit at 1,795 m, straddling the border between Carinthia and Styria. The village is centered around Lake Turrach (1,763 m) and consists of two luxury hotels, over 30 small and medium-sized hotels and guest houses, and many vacation houses hidden in stone pine forests. There is no grocery store or gas station. An ecumenical church was built in 1985. The resort is located within the UNESCO Nockberge Biosphere Reserve, with soft, rounded mountain silhouettes. The atmosphere is calm and intimate, with a focus on nature.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system comprises 16 lifts: 6 six-seater chairlifts (Kornockbahn, Schafalmbahn, Zirbenwaldbahn, Panoramabahn, Turrachbahn, Wildkopfbahn), one combined chairlift and gondola (Panoramabahn), 8 drag lifts, and various training lifts. The hourly transport capacity exceeds 19,400 people, which is high for the size of the ski area, resulting in minimal queues. The lifts are modern, with the Wildkopf chairlift being a recent addition. The Kornockbahn is the main artery to the summit at 2,205 m. The Panoramabahn is accessible for guests with disabilities and families with strollers.

terrainDescription: The resort is divided into two distinct zones. The 1st Zone (original hub) has older, more established runs suitable for beginners and day-trippers, with a lively atmosphere and music from trackside cafes. The 2nd Zone features modern hotels, wider groomed slopes, and a calmer environment preferred by advanced skiers and families. The slopes are lightly forested and mostly intermediate in character. There is one terrain park and 20 km of cross-country trails. The longest downhill run is approximately 3.5 km. The resort has 26 trails in total, with 45% beginner, 35% intermediate, and 20% advanced.
offPisteDescription: Heli-skiing is available, and there are vast off-piste bowls to explore, though this activity is noted as a threat to conservation within the national park. Skilled skiers can navigate between the two districts off-piste. The off-piste terrain includes lightly forested areas and bowls. Visitor reviews rate off-piste quality at 3.4 out of 5.
valueDescription: Lift passes cost 1700 TL per day when purchased online, with on-site prices about 15% higher. Equipment rental (skis, boots, poles) ranges from 400 to 700 TL per day. Hotel accommodation is considered pricey, especially in the 1st Zone where older hotels still charge high rates; 2nd Zone luxury hotels are even more expensive. A visitor review describes prices as 'exorbitant' (fahiş fiyatlar). Overall, Uludağ is one of the more expensive ski resorts in Turkey.
familyDescription: The 1st Zone is lively and party-oriented, with music and crowds, which may not suit all families. The 2nd Zone offers a quieter environment with wider slopes and modern hotels, making it more suitable for families seeking calm evenings. Beginner-friendly slopes are available in the 1st Zone, and ski schools offer lessons. However, the resort is best known for its party atmosphere rather than dedicated family facilities.
apresSkiDescription: The après-ski scene is lively and central to the resort's popularity. The 1st Zone features music blaring from trackside cafes and a highly energetic atmosphere. An annual Winter Wonderland Festival in February draws young crowds with concerts and parties. There are 14 restaurants and bars in the resort. Visitor reviews rate après-ski at 4.1 out of 5. The 2nd Zone is quieter, with more relaxed evening options.
resortCharacterDescription: Uludağ is a large, purpose-built ski resort within Uludağ National Park, located on the slopes of the legendary Mount Olympus of Mysia (Kara Tepe, 2543 m). The resort is divided into two development zones: the 1st Zone (original hub with older hotels) and the 2nd Zone (modern luxury hotels). There are 27 hotels providing over 3,000 beds, with further development underway. The setting features pine forests and views of the Marmara region. Access is via a sealed road or a cable-car/bus combination from Bursa.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system comprises 22 lifts: 1 gondola, 5 chairlifts, and 16 surface lifts (including teleskis/platter lifts). Lifts connect the two districts. Visitor reviews note that lifts are often poorly maintained and queues can be long, especially on weekends. The cable car from Bursa (Teleferik) provides an alternative access route but does not allow ski equipment inside cabins. Online skipass purchase is recommended to avoid queues.

terrainDescription: The ski area Ginals-Unterbäch offers 20 km of marked pistes, predominantly blue and red runs. 63% of pistes are classified as beginner, 21% intermediate, and 16% advanced. The slopes are wide and uncrowded, with a vertical drop of 1,322 m from base at 1,228 m to summit at 2,550 m. The terrain includes a Swiss Snow Kids Village with a magic carpet, ski carousel, seesaws, jumps, and rails. The highest lift reaches 2,550 m, providing views of the Bietschhorn.
valueDescription: Lift passes are among the cheapest in Valais at CHF 49 per day for adults and CHF 33 for children. Accommodation options range from budget hostels and guesthouses to upscale hotels, with a wide variety available. The resort offers ski-in/ski-out chalets and apartments. A free ski bus connects Unterbäch to Eischoll and Bürchen for pass holders.
familyDescription: The resort is specifically designed for families, with a Swiss Snow Kids Village offering a magic carpet, ski carousel, and small terrain features. The ski school provides lessons for children and adults. The slopes are mostly gentle (63% beginner) and uncrowded. A free ski bus connects Unterbäch to Eischoll and Bürchen. Night skiing is available on Tuesdays during the main season from 4:30 to 6:30 p.m. for CHF 5.00.
apresSkiDescription: The village has two bars that often feature live music. There are five restaurants serving local specialties such as raclette, rösti, and Valais wine. The après-ski scene is relaxed rather than lively, with a focus on dining and drinks.
resortCharacterDescription: Unterbäch is a traditional Valais mountain village situated on a sunny high plateau at 1,200 m. It retains its alpine character with historic buildings, a church, and a municipal library. The village is not purpose-built; it has a genuine Swiss village atmosphere with local shops and restaurants. The entire Turtig/Wandfluh region is designated as part of the Inventory of Swiss Heritage Sites.
liftSystemDescription: The combined Unterbäch-Eischoll area has 11 lifts, including chairlifts, gondolas, and surface lifts. The main chairlift to Brandalp operates daily from 9:00 to 16:30. The aerial cableway from Raron to Unterbäch provides access from the valley, with cabins running every 30 minutes. Lifts are modern and queues are rare. Snowmaking covers 50% of the slopes with 60 machines.
linkedResortsDescription: Unterbäch is linked to the neighboring resort of Eischoll via the Strygge-Erez ski lift and connecting pistes, forming a combined ski area of 35 km of pistes and 7 lifts. The resort is also part of the Magic Pass, which provides access to 69 winter destinations in the region, including areas in Upper Valais, Disentis-Sedrun, Andermatt, and the Bernese Oberland.

terrainDescription: The Val Cenis ski area spans 125km of pistes across three linked villages: Lanslevillard, Lanslebourg, and Termignon. The terrain is predominantly south-facing, with wide, well-groomed cruising runs for intermediates, dedicated beginner areas at village level, and steeper black runs from the 2,800m summit. The lower half of the mountain is characterized by tree-lined runs through pine and larch forest below 2,100m, while the upper mountain opens into rolling wide-angle slopes and open bowls. The 10km L'Escargot green run is the longest in Europe, winding from the summit down to Lanslebourg. Red runs dominate the mid-mountain, providing progression terrain. The ski area is divided by the Combe de Clery, with the Lanslevillard side offering more open runs and the Lanslebourg side more varied gradients.
offPisteDescription: Off-piste skiing in Val Cenis is concentrated in the bowls below the Col de la Met and towards the Italian border, accessible via the Plan Cardinal chairlift. The treeline terrain offers glade-style skiing through powder pockets that remain untracked longer than in busier resorts. Above the lifts, the Mont-Cenis plateau provides easy-access powder fields and mellow backcountry routes suitable for ski touring. North-facing aspects hold snow well after snowfall. Local guides can open steeper couloirs and hidden descents, including routes towards Italy when conditions permit. The marked itinerary routes on the piste map offer controlled introduction to unpisted terrain. A guide is recommended for backcountry exploration.
valueDescription: Val Cenis is known for being one of the more affordable ski resorts in the French Alps. Lift passes, accommodation, and meals cost less than in larger resorts such as Val Thorens or Les Arcs. The resort offers a range of accommodation from self-catering apartments to traditional chalets at competitive prices. Mountain restaurants serve hearty Savoyard cuisine at reasonable rates. The Espace Haute Maurienne Vanoise pass provides access to five additional resorts at no extra cost for 6+ day passes, increasing value for longer stays.
familyDescription: Val Cenis is well-suited for families with dedicated beginner areas in each village, set away from faster traffic. The ESF runs children's programmes with snow gardens for young children, and there are two Mickey Mouse fun runs for children aged six to ten. The 10km L'Escargot green run is ideal for progressing beginners. Off the slopes, families can use ice skating rinks, sledging areas, and winter walking paths. The compact village layout means accommodation, ski school, and slopes are within walking distance. The resort also offers a terrain park and snowtubing run.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski in Val Cenis is relaxed and low-key. Bars such as Le Chardon Bleu and Le C2 offer informal settings for drinks. Dining options include Chalet La Ranôva for hearty local cuisine and The Artemisia for more refined dishes. The resort has one nightclub. The atmosphere is more about socializing over génépi than partying. Mountain restaurants like Le Vieux Moulin provide evening dining with traditional Savoyard dishes.
resortCharacterDescription: Val Cenis is a traditional Savoyard resort formed by the merger of five historic villages: Termignon, Bramans, Lanslebourg-Mont-Cenis, Lanslevillard, and Sollières-Sardières. The villages feature stone houses, narrow lanes, and a lived-in feel, with year-round communities rather than purpose-built tourist infrastructure. The resort sits in the Haute Maurienne valley near the Italian border, with views of the Vanoise National Park. It has a relaxed, authentic atmosphere without neon-lit megabars or large commercial developments.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system at Val Cenis includes 39 lifts comprising two gondolas, high-speed six-seater chairlifts, and multiple chairlifts and drag lifts. The system links the three base villages efficiently, with multiple access points that reduce queuing. Recent investments include new lifts in the Termignon sector. The lifts are modern and well-maintained, with snowmaking covering up to 2,400m. The Val Cenis Le Haut gondola and Colomba chairlift in Lanslevillard are key for beginners.
linkedResortsDescription: Val Cenis is part of the Espace Haute Maurienne Vanoise, a linked ski area covering 350km of downhill pistes across six resorts: Valfréjus, La Norma, Aussois, Val Cenis, Bessans, and Bonneval sur Arc. A 6+ day ski pass includes one day of skiing in each of the other resorts and unlimited access to the Nordic ski areas. The annual pass also includes days at Bardonecchia and Via Lattea in Italy. A shuttle bus connects the resorts. Additionally, the Eski-Mo pass covers other Maurienne Valley resorts including Bonneval-sur-Arc, Aussois, Valfréjus, and La Norma.

terrainDescription: The ski area at Padola Val Comelico consists of 12 km of slopes running through spruce forests on the northeast face of Col d'la Tenda, which catches morning sun. The runs are of varying difficulty: the Campo run is 4.5 km long and classified as easy, Tre Picchi is intermediate, and Tabiadel and Pra di Guardiani are difficult. All slopes are equipped with snow-making facilities. The base is at 1,211 m and the summit at 1,943 m, with a vertical drop of 732 m.
familyDescription: Padola is described as a perfect destination for families and beginners, with nearly empty slopes and the Vare Belle school camp. A sunny ski school area is located in the valley, and a Baby Parking Service is available on request. The resort offers a gentle learning environment away from crowds.
resortCharacterDescription: Padola is a hamlet within the scattered municipality of Comelico Superiore in the Veneto region, set in the Dolomites UNESCO World Heritage Site. The ski area is small and uncrowded, with slopes running through spruce forests and views of peaks such as Croda da Campo, Cima Bagni, and the Tre Cime di Lavaredo. The village retains a traditional alpine character with local Ladin culture.
liftSystemDescription: The main lift is a modern automatic coupled quad chairlift from Padola to Col d'la Tenda, reaching 2,000 m in just over 7 minutes at a speed of 5 m/s. It is the first chairlift in Italy with intermediate boarding stations, allowing skiers to board at 1,500 m or 2,000 m. The chairlift has a backup generator for operation during power outages. A Salto ski-bus service connects Padola to Sesto via the Plöcken Pass, linking to the larger 3 Zinnen ski area.
linkedResortsDescription: Padola Val Comelico is part of the 3 Zinnen Dolomiten ski network (Tre Cime di Lavaredo). A free Salto ski-bus runs from Padola to Sesto via the Plöcken Pass, connecting to the Sesto Val Pusteria ski area. With a single Dolomiti Superski pass, skiers can access 115 km of slopes and 31 lifts across the combined area. The 'Giro delle Cime' summit tour links Padola to Kleimbach and back, requiring intermediate ability and a guide from the ski school.

terrainDescription: The resort has 41 marked runs: 3 green, 20 blue, 14 red, and 4 black, with a vertical drop of 800 m. The local valley offers mostly gentle blue runs, while the connection to Pra Loup introduces steeper red and black slopes. A dedicated beginner area with magic carpets and a small chairlift provides safe learning space. The upper slopes are above 2000 m, offering open terrain. A terrain park is available for snowboarders.
valueDescription: Lift passes for the Espace Lumière area cost €45 per day for adults and €38 for children (5–17) for the 2023–2024 season. The resort offers free shuttles between villages and slopes. Accommodation is available close to the pistes, with ski-in/ski-out options. Self-catering apartments and residences are common, keeping costs moderate compared to larger purpose-built resorts.
familyDescription: Val d'Allos La Foux holds the Famille Plus Montagne label, with a snow garden, toboggan runs, a children's village, and a play library in the resort centre. There is a 600 m² ice rink, a cinema, and the Verdon Express rail toboggan run. Ski school offers lessons for all ages, and the resort has a daycare for children from 1 to 5 years old. The traffic-free village centre allows families to walk everywhere in boots.
apresSkiDescription: The resort has 15 restaurants and 6 bars. The après-ski scene is quiet, with a few bars and restaurants in the village centre. A cinema, ice rink, and toboggan run provide evening entertainment. High-altitude terraces offer dining with views. A nightclub is also available. The atmosphere is relaxed rather than rowdy.
resortCharacterDescription: La Foux d'Allos is a purpose-built resort with mostly wooden buildings, avoiding large concrete complexes. Accommodation is ski-in/ski-out or a short walk from the slopes. The village is small and convenient, with all amenities within walking distance. It blends authenticity and modernity, set in the Mercantour National Park.
liftSystemDescription: Val d'Allos La Foux has 17 lifts: 7 drag lifts, 8 chair lifts, and 2 magic carpets. The lift system is a mix of older and newer installations, with some slow chairs and drags, but recent upgrades have improved efficiency. Queues are rare outside peak weeks. The link to Pra Loup can close in high winds.
linkedResortsDescription: Val d'Allos La Foux is part of the Espace Lumière ski area, which links it to Pra Loup and Val d'Allos Le Seignus, offering a combined 180 km of slopes with 120 lifts. The connection to Pra Loup is via the Agneliers valley, subject to snow conditions. The linked area includes 136 marked runs and serves a total of 33 lifts across the whole Val d'Allos resort.

terrainDescription: The Le Seignus ski area offers 23 runs (1 green, 9 blue, 8 red, 5 black) winding through larch forests, with a vertical drop of 900 metres. The terrain is described as fairly challenging and technical, with a mix of easy beginner slopes and steeper red and black runs. The area is more intimate and family-oriented compared to the larger La Foux sector. The slopes face south-east, benefiting from afternoon sunshine.
valueDescription: Lift passes at Le Seignus are priced at €32.10 for a one-day adult pass and €139.70 for five consecutive days (2023-2024 season). The resort is known for being affordable compared to larger Alpine resorts, with many self-catering accommodation options available. The Famille Plus label indicates good value for families.
familyDescription: Le Seignus holds the Famille Plus Montagne label, indicating family-friendly facilities. It offers a beginner area with magic carpets, kindergartens, and toboggan runs. Free shuttles connect the village of Allos to the slopes. The resort is compact and intimate, making it easy for families to navigate. There are also play trails and a cinema in the nearby village.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski at Le Seignus is low-key, centered around a bar and restaurant at the foot of the slopes that is often busy during holidays. The terrace offers mulled wine and hot chocolate. The nearby village of Allos has additional restaurants, a cinema, and an ice skating rink.
resortCharacterDescription: Le Seignus is a purpose-built ski station with small wooden buildings that retain alpine charm, avoiding large concrete complexes. It is linked by cable car to the traditional mountain village of Allos, which offers a picturesque setting with narrow lanes and stone houses. The resort is described as authentic and modern.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system at Le Seignus includes a cable car from the village, chairlifts, and ski tows. Recent fast lifts have been installed. The lifts are modern and efficient, with minimal queues outside peak weeks.
linkedResortsDescription: Le Seignus is part of the Espace Lumière ski area, which also includes Val d'Allos La Foux and Pra Loup, offering a combined 230 km of slopes. However, the two Val d'Allos sectors (Le Seignus and La Foux) are not physically connected by ski; a free shuttle bus connects them. The Espace Lumière pass grants access to all three areas.

terrainDescription: The ski area Monte Bue – Santo Stefano d'Aveto spans altitudes from 1,264 m to 1,774 m, with 4.6 km of slopes. The terrain consists of blue and red runs, with a chairlift from Rocca d'Aveto to Prato della Cipolla and another to Monte Bue. Descents pass through wooded slopes of the Aveto Valley, offering views of the Ligurian Sea. The slopes are primarily suited to beginners and intermediates, with 70% beginner, 20% intermediate, and 10% advanced terrain.
resortCharacterDescription: Santo Stefano d'Aveto is a traditional mountain village with pitched roofs, timber balconies, and a medieval Castello Malaspina-Fieschi-Doria at its centre. The ski area is accessed from a parking area at Rio Freddo, with lifts rising from the valley. The resort is not purpose-built but a historic village that offers skiing in winter, with a compact layout and a relaxed, authentic atmosphere.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of 5 lifts, including chairlifts from Rocca d'Aveto to Prato della Cipolla and to Monte Bue. No further details on lift age or efficiency are available.

terrainDescription: The Val di Fassa ski area is spread across several distinct sectors above seven valley villages. Belvedere above Canazei offers 22 km of mainly intermediate runs with views of the Marmolada glacier. Col Rodella near Campitello provides 17 km of intermediate skiing under the Sassolungo peak. Buffaure-Ciampac, accessed from Pozza and Alba, has 32 km of mostly red runs and a black descent to Alba. Ciampedie near Vigo is largely beginner terrain with 16 km of blue and red runs beneath the Rosengarten/Catinaccio peaks. Carezza, a 30-minute bus ride from Vigo, offers 35 km of gentle blue and red runs. The areas are partly connected by lifts and ski buses, and the Sellaronda circuit passes through Canazei and Campitello, adding congestion on those slopes.
offPisteDescription: Off-piste opportunities include the Val Mezdi route from Sass Pordoi (2950 m) down to Passo Pordoi (2239 m), accessible from the top cable car. Northwest-facing slopes beneath Sella Brunech (2428 m) in the Buffaure-Ciampac area offer off-piste skiing. Carezza has a designated freeride zone to the left of the Monte Coronelle chairlift. The steep Aloch run is illuminated for night skiing but is a groomed piste.
familyDescription: Families are well catered for with dedicated beginner areas at Ciampedie (Vigo di Fassa) and Carezza, which together provide most of the valley's blue runs. Ski schools operate in each village, with quieter learning environments in Vigo and Pozza. The Aloch run offers night skiing under floodlights, and the QC Terme Dolomiti thermal spa in Pozza provides off-slope relaxation. The Sellaronda circuit is not suitable for beginners due to mandatory red runs.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski options include Après Ski Paradìs in Canazei, a small venue with DJ sets and dancing in the afternoon. Rosengarten Après Ski offers a selection of national wines and craft beers. Dolomites Geyser, at the foot of the Vulcano slope, has a large terrace with panoramic views. Restaurants such as El Zedron serve traditional Italian cuisine with terrace views, while La Stua de Jan specialises in grilled meats and Ladin cuisine.
resortCharacterDescription: Val di Fassa is not a single resort but a valley of seven traditional Ladin villages (Canazei, Campitello, Pozza, Vigo, Moena, etc.) spread over 20 km, each with its own character. The architecture is alpine and historic, with no purpose-built high-rise developments. The valley is set within the UNESCO Dolomites, with dramatic limestone peaks surrounding the villages.
liftSystemDescription: The lift network includes several cable cars and gondolas: the Pecol gondola and Col dei Rossi cable car at Canazei, the Campitello-Col Rodella cable car, the Pozza-Buffaure gondola, and the Vigo-Catinaccio cable car. Chairlifts and surface lifts supplement these. Some areas are not lift-linked, requiring ski bus connections (e.g., between Vigo and Carezza). The highest lift is the Sass Pordoi cable car at 2950 m. The Sellaronda circuit uses many of these lifts, which can lead to queues during peak weeks.
linkedResortsDescription: Val di Fassa is part of the Dolomiti Superski network, with direct lift connections to the Sellaronda circuit, linking to Arabba-Marmolada, Val Gardena, and Alta Badia (over 500 km of slopes). The nearby Tre Valli area (Alpe Lusia and Passo San Pellegrino) is accessible via ski bus or short drive. Carezza ski area is a separate sector within the same valley but requires a bus from Vigo di Fassa.

terrainDescription: The ski area spans 70 km of groomed trails between 1,550 m and 2,737 m, with north-facing slopes predominantly above treeline. The Arrondaz gondola lifts skiers to a high plateau with wide green and blue runs, while the upper mountain via chairlift offers access to Punta Bagna's couloirs and powder fields. The longest run, 'Le Jeu', descends over 10 km from summit to village. On-piste advanced runs are limited; the black run 'Souches' into the village is the most challenging. Several blue runs are deceptively steep and could be rated red. Flat traverses exist, particularly at the start of Jeu and around Plateau d'Arrondaz, which can be challenging for snowboarders.
offPisteDescription: Off-piste terrain is abundant and high-quality, centered on Punta Bagna (2,737 m) with north-facing couloirs and bowls on both sides. Short hikes from the summit open additional backcountry playgrounds. The north aspect preserves snow quality, and the 'retours d'est' weather pattern brings dry, deep snow from Italy. Access to the best freeride lines requires local knowledge and challenging exits. The Refuge du Thabor area is a notable off-piste route. Speed riding, a hybrid of paragliding and skiing, originated here and remains popular.
valueDescription: Lift passes are affordable: a day ticket for Valfréjus and La Norma costs €42.50 for adults (2025/26), with a 5% discount for online purchase. A six-day adult pass is around €225. Accommodation is reasonably priced, with many self-catering apartments and a hostel (BodyGo Hostel) near the Charmasson chairlift. The combined pass with La Norma (135 km) adds value. However, children aged 12 and over are charged adult rates. Dining and groceries are moderately priced, though some reviews note high supermarket costs in the village.
familyDescription: The resort is family-oriented with two dedicated beginner areas: the sheltered Chamasson chair near the village and the Plateau d'Arrondaz mid-mountain. A Kid Park on the Arrondaz plateau features children's games, a merry-go-round, and a snow castle. A 2 km Bob Park toboggan run provides off-slope fun. Ski schools (ESF, ESI) operate at the foot of the slopes, and the compact pedestrian village makes logistics easy for families. Free magic carpets are available at the beginner areas.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski is low-key and centered on village bars. The Morricone and Rock n' Beer, both near the Charmasson chairlift base, offer extensive beer selections. On-mountain dining is limited to two restaurants, including La Bergerie. Village restaurants serve Savoyard cuisine. The scene is relaxed rather than rowdy, with no large nightclubs. Live music is occasional.
resortCharacterDescription: Valfréjus is a purpose-built resort founded in 1983, designed to blend into the forest with wooden and stone chalets. The village is compact and pedestrianized, with most accommodation ski-in/ski-out. It has a modern lift system and a family-friendly atmosphere, lacking the historic alpine charm of older villages but offering convenience and integration with nature. The village centre is traffic-free, with shops, restaurants, and services within walking distance.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system is modern, with most major lifts replaced between 2009 and 2013. It comprises 1 gondola (Arrondaz) and 4 chairlifts, with a total capacity of 17,480 people per hour. The lifts are efficient and comfortable, with minimal queues outside peak weeks. However, some surface lifts remain older. The resort has 9 lifts in total (1 gondola, 4 chairs, 4 surface lifts).
linkedResortsDescription: Valfréjus is linked to La Norma via a common ski pass and free shuttle every 45 minutes, giving access to 135 km of pistes. It is also part of the Espace Haute Maurienne Vanoise, allowing access to Aussois, Val Cenis, Bessans, and Bonneval-sur-Arc with a 6-day pass (1 free day each). The combined area offers over 550 km of slopes across the region.

terrainDescription: The ski resort has 8.3 km of slopes split across easy (46%), intermediate (29%), and difficult (25%) runs, situated between 1,460 m and 1,729 m elevation with a vertical drop of 269 m. The ski area offers views of the plateau and peaks of the Majella National Park, and also features a cross-country ski trail. Pescocostanzo village is noted as one of the most beautiful villages in Italy with a beautiful old town and ancient craft tradition.
familyDescription: The ski area includes a rope tow/baby lift called Lupetto with a length of 109 m and carrying capacity of 400 passengers per hour, suitable for beginners and children. The resort has ski-in/ski-out accommodation available.
resortCharacterDescription: Pescocostanzo is described as one of the most beautiful villages in Italy, featuring a beautiful old town and ancient craft tradition. The village offers various winter activities beyond skiing, including cross-country skiing on a trail that is one of the most popular in the region.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of 4 lifts with a total capacity of 4,217 passengers per hour and total lift length of 1.7 km. The main chairlift Vallefura was installed in 2009 by LEITNER, measuring 929 m with a carrying capacity of 2,010 passengers per hour. Additional lifts include the 2006 Valle Gelata 2-person chairlift (358 m), the 1977 Monte Calvario J-bar (341 m), and the Lupetto rope tow/baby lift (109 m).

terrainDescription: The ski area is centered on the broad plateau of Alpe Palù, reached by the Snow Eagle cable car. The terrain features wide bowls that spill into forested corridors, with long cruising blues such as Motta–Dosso dei Vetti, technical reds like Bocchel del Torno, and steep blacks including the Thoeni run. The Barchi sector offers playful terrain and training slopes. The geometry of the mountain provides constant shifts of perspective, from open alpine space to quieter stretches of larch and spruce.
offPisteDescription: Off-piste skiing is available near Pizzo Bernina, with deep snow areas accessible for experienced skiers. The resort also offers ski touring routes with dramatic views of the Bernina glaciers. The terrain includes powder zones that are popular with locals when conditions allow.
valueDescription: A six-day adult lift pass costs approximately €300, and ski rental for six days starts from about €100. The resort is known for good value compared to larger Italian resorts, with affordable dining and accommodation options. The Valmalenco Card allows online booking of ski passes for a small fee, though the first card must be issued in person.
familyDescription: The resort is highly family-friendly, with wide, gentle slopes and dedicated learning areas. Ski schools offer lessons for all ages, and off-slope activities include snowshoeing, ice skating, and bobsleigh rides. The Snow Eagle cable car provides easy access to the main plateau, and the village of Chiesa is pedestrian-friendly.
apresSkiDescription: The après-ski scene is relaxed and low-key, with a few bars and restaurants in Chiesa. Mountain huts like Rifugio Motta serve local specialties such as pizzoccheri and polenta. The atmosphere is more about enjoying a quiet drink and local cuisine than lively nightlife.
resortCharacterDescription: Chiesa in Valmalenco is a traditional alpine village with charming stone buildings and a relaxed, authentic atmosphere. The resort is not purpose-built; it retains an Italian character with family-run hotels and local eateries. The surrounding landscape is dominated by the Bernina Range and Pizzo Scalino.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system is centered on the Snow Eagle cable car, one of the largest in Europe, which carries 160 passengers from the valley floor at 985m to Alpe Palù at 2,050m in four minutes. From there, chairlifts including Alpe Palù–Cima Motta and Scerscen–Cima Motta access the upper slopes. The system includes 10 lifts in total: one gondola, six high-speed quads, one double chair, and two surface lifts. Queues can form at the cable car during peak periods.

terrainDescription: The Grand Domaine ski area offers 165 km of pistes linking Valmorel with Saint-François-Longchamp, Doucy, and Celliers. Valmorel's own 95 km of runs are spread across wide, sunny bowls above the treeline and tree-lined slopes. The terrain is divided into sectors: the Pierrafort and Mottet sectors above the village offer the broadest terrain, while the link to Saint-François-Longchamp adds variety. Beginners start on gentle zones at Rocher and Bois de la Croix, then progress via the Côte Soleil and Pierrafort lifts. Intermediates can ski long blues and reds, including day trips over the Col de la Madeleine. Advanced skiers have black runs around Le Mottet and the Combe de Riondet snowcross, plus four secured freeride zones (Le Gollet, Le Mottet, Le Riondet, La Procureuse). The resort also has a snowpark and boardercross.
offPisteDescription: Valmorel has four secured freeride zones: Le Gollet, Le Mottet, Le Riondet, and La Procureuse, which are marked on the piste map but left ungroomed. Off-piste terrain also exists on the Massif de la Lauzière, accessible with a guide. The upper bowls and slopes below the ridge hold powder stashes after fresh snowfall. For serious off-piste, the resort's location provides day-trip access to La Plagne, Les Arcs, and the Three Valleys within an hour's drive. Ski touring is popular in the area, with routes towards the Col de la Madeleine.
valueDescription: Lift pass prices for the 2024-2025 season were 54€ for an adult day pass and 44.50€ for a child day pass (ages 5-12). The resort is often cheaper than the neighbouring Three Valleys area. Accommodation is mostly ski-in/ski-out chalets and apartments, with many self-catering options that keep costs down. The compact, pedestrianised village means no need for car hire or shuttle buses within the resort.
familyDescription: Valmorel was designed as a pedestrianised, car-free village, allowing children to move safely. The Piou Piou kids' club caters to children from 18 months to six years old, with childcare and ski lessons. There are four dedicated beginner zones across the ski area: Rocher-Blanchot, Bois de la Croix, Malatrai-Roches Blanches, and Echappeaux-Charmettes, each with free lifts or magic carpets. The Plan de l'Eau learning area sits at the village edge with gentle slopes. Family fun areas include the Pirates' Village and a toboggan run at Doucy. The resort also has an ice rink, swimming pool, and snowshoeing trails.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski in Valmorel is low-key and relaxed. Mountain restaurants include L'Altipiano at the top of the Pierrafort gondola, serving traditional Savoyard food on a sunny terrace, and Les Voiles du Nant at the Altispace chairlift. In the village, restaurants include La Marmite, Le Jimbo Lolo (Tex-Mex), and La Table du Berger. Bars are cosy rather than rowdy; Hotel du Bourg has a fireplace and whiskey selection, and SkiRoc offers French and European dishes. The resort has 14 restaurants and 8 bars in total.
resortCharacterDescription: Valmorel is a purpose-built resort constructed in the 1970s, but it deliberately avoided concrete tower blocks in favour of traditional Savoyard architecture: wooden chalets, stone facades, and slate roofs. The village centre is entirely pedestrianised, with cobbled streets and fairy lights. Most accommodation is ski-in/ski-out. The resort is compact, with shops, restaurants, and lift bases within walking distance. The architecture blends with the natural landscape, giving it a village feel rather than a stark resort.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system includes 36 lifts: 8 chairlifts, 26 surface lifts, and 2 gondolas (according to snow-forecast). The system was upgraded in 2011 to improve access to the Grand Domaine, and recent years have seen the addition of high-speed six-seater chairlifts and a new gondola. Detachable lifts minimise queue times, though surface lifts (drag lifts) are still common in some sectors. The Télébourg gondola and Pierrafort gondola provide quick access from the village to the upper mountain.
linkedResortsDescription: Valmorel is part of the Grand Domaine ski area, which links the resorts of Valmorel, Saint-François-Longchamp, Doucy, and Celliers via the Col de la Madeleine. The connected area offers 165 km of pistes. Skiers can cross the Col de la Madeleine on a blue run. The link to Saint-François-Longchamp opens up additional terrain on the other side of the mountain.

terrainDescription: The ski area on the Dachberg is accessed by an 8-seater gondola from the village to Gadastatt at 1,817m, where a practice area (Kleinvalser Arena) with a conveyor belt and platter lift serves beginners. Three T-bar lifts ascend from Gadastatt to nearly 3,000m, offering wide intermediate cruising runs and a blue slope from the Faltschona drag lift. The valley run is a 2.5km advanced descent; most skiers take the gondola back down. The terrain includes high alpine runs with views from the Bernina massif to the Valais 4,000m peaks, and the longest run is 10km over a vertical drop of 1,630m.
offPisteDescription: Freeriders can access powder snow slopes away from the pistes, with the high summit altitude (nearly 3,000m) and reliable snowfall providing good conditions. The area is described as having abundant freeride terrain, with untouched powder off-piste that can remain fresh for days after a storm. The off-piste includes high alpine slopes around the Dachberg and is a key draw for the resort, which markets itself as a freeride destination.
valueDescription: Vals is considered the best value ski resort in Switzerland, with an adult day lift pass costing CHF39 for the 2025/26 season and children under 16 skiing for free. The gondola from the village to the mountain is free of charge. A season ticket costs CHF399. These prices are among the most affordable in the Alps, and the resort offers fair pricing for lift passes and accommodation.
familyDescription: The Kleinvalser Arena at Gadastatt (1,817m) features a conveyor belt and platter lift for beginners, plus a pony lift for children. The Swiss Snowsports School Vals offers children's ski school, private and group lessons. The gondola is free for all, making access easy for families. The valley run is difficult, so families can take the gondola down. Ski rental is available at the valley station and in the village.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski is low-key, with a few cozy bars and mountain restaurants such as Bergrestaurant Gadastatt, Dachberghütte, and scHIRMBAR. The main evening attraction is the world-famous Therme Vals thermal spa, a listed architectural masterpiece of stone and light, open until late. Nightlife beyond dinner and drinks is limited, with the village offering a relaxed, tranquil atmosphere.
resortCharacterDescription: Vals is a traditional, picturesque Swiss village with a population of 946, featuring Walser architecture with timber-rich dwellings and sharply pitched roofs. The village centre, Vals Platz, lies secluded behind two gorges, accessed via a single valley corridor. It is not purpose-built; the historic character is complemented by the modern Therme Vals spa (built 1996 by Peter Zumthor) and the surrounding alpine landscape.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of one modern 8-seater gondola from the village to Gadastatt (500m vertical), three older-style T-bar lifts from Gadastatt to the Dachberg summit (remaining 1,130m vertical), and two conveyor belts/platter lifts for beginners at Gadastatt. The gondola is free. Operating hours are 9:00–16:30 for the gondola and 9:15–15:45 for the upper lifts. The system is small but efficient for the terrain.

terrainDescription: The ski area is centered on the Hochjochferner Glacier, with slopes from 3,212m down to 2,011m. The terrain is dominated by wide, smooth glacier cruising runs above the treeline, suitable for intermediates. The standout feature is the 8 km long Smuggler Run (Schmuggler Abfahrt) valley descent, dropping 1,200m vertical. Lower mountain runs like Teufelsegg and the black-rated Leo Gurschler slalom training piste offer more challenging terrain. The piste distribution is approximately 35% easy (blue), 45% intermediate (red), and 20% difficult (black), with a total of 27–42 km of marked runs depending on the source. Most runs are above 2,000m, with few below the treeline.
valueDescription: Lift pass pricing varies by season: early glacier season (Sep–Nov) adult daily €68, mid-season (Nov–Dec) €49, low season (Jan–May except holidays) €56, and peak holiday periods €62. A 6-day adult pass ranges from €282 to €313. Children (2–14) pay €32–40 daily, and the Snowkids ticket allows children born 2016 or later to ski for €5 per day when accompanied by a parent with a full-price pass. A points-based Schnupperkarte is available for beginners: 20 points for €36 (10 rides on beginner lifts). Half-day passes are also offered from 12:30.
familyDescription: The resort has dedicated beginner areas at Lazaun and Glockenlift, with two magic carpets, a children's carousel, and an Indian tipi-themed children's course. Ski school offers courses for children and adults. The Snowkids ticket makes family skiing affordable: children born 2016+ pay only €5/day with a parent holding a full-price pass. The practice slopes are separate from main runs, providing a safe learning environment. The village of Kurzras is compact and car-free, with ski-in/ski-out access from many accommodations.
resortCharacterDescription: The resort is centered on the small village of Kurzras (Maso Corto) at 2,011m, at the end of the Val Senales valley. It is a quiet, authentic alpine settlement rather than a purpose-built resort, with just a few hotels and Europe's highest hotel, the Glacier Hotel Grawand at 3,212m. The atmosphere is sporty and minimalist, popular with professional ski teams for training. The valley has a primitive, timeless beauty, far from the crowds of larger resorts. Accommodation options are limited but unique, including a caravan park at altitude.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system comprises 11 lifts: one gondola (the Schnalstal Glacier Cable Car, inaugurated in 2024 with 80-passenger cabins and cabrio modality, ascending from Kurzras to 3,212m in 6 minutes), two quad chairlifts, one triple chairlift, three double chairlifts, and four surface lifts. The cable car is modern and efficient, while the chairlifts serve the glacier and lower mountain. Lift queues are minimal due to the resort's small size and capacity of 15,500 persons per hour. The system is well-maintained and suitable for the compact ski area.

terrainDescription: Val Thorens sits at 2,300m in a vast natural cirque dominated by six glaciers. The terrain is mostly above the treeline, with long sweeping runs that suit intermediates particularly well. Nursery slopes are located right in the village for first turns, while long cruising reds link down to Les Menuires. Most slopes face north, protecting them from spring sunshine, and many border glaciers with year-round snow. The Val Thorens-Orelle sector offers about 150 km of pistes with 60 runs, including wide glacier runs and steep faces like Combe de Caron.
offPisteDescription: Off-piste terrain includes glacier couloirs off Pointe de Thorens, steep chutes on Combe de Caron and Cascades, and a vast freeride domain around the Glacier de Thorens and the Orelle Valley. Freeriders get more frequent mid-winter powder top-ups than most other resorts. The area has a world-class snowpark spanning 90,000 m² with jumps, rails, and airbags.
valueDescription: Val Thorens carries a premium for its altitude and snow reliability, with the 3 Vallées lift pass being expensive (€409 for a 6-day adult pass in 2025-2026). However, a cheaper local Val Thorens-Orelle pass is available, covering about 150 km of slopes and costing only about 10-25% less than the full area pass. Accommodation ranges from ski-in/ski-out apartments to luxury hotels, with dining options from affordable casual eateries to Michelin-starred restaurants. Self-catering apartments can help manage costs, but overall the resort is pricier than nearby Les Menuires.
