familyDescription: Val Thorens has four nursery slopes accessed via free magic carpet lifts near the resort, with a low-cost Mini ski pass for the extended beginner area. ESF-run snow gardens and private nanny services are available. The Lagon sports centre offers slides, trampolines, and a dedicated toddler fun park. The resort is pedestrianized and compact, making it easy to walk between accommodation and slopes. However, the high altitude can be cold and windy, and there are no tree-lined slopes for shelter on bad weather days.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski starts on-mountain at La Folie Douce, then continues in the village at clubs like Malaysia and Saloon Bar, known for high-energy parties that last late into the night. Bar 360 is another popular venue. Dining ranges from Michelin-starred restaurants such as L’Oxalys and Le Diamant Noir to casual Savoyard eateries. The scene is lively and vibrant, with a focus on partying rather than quiet relaxation.
resortCharacterDescription: Val Thorens is a purpose-built resort established in 1972, sitting at 2,300m above the treeline. The village is compact and car-free, with most lodgings clustered around the central Place Caron and opening directly onto the snow. Pedestrian zones and a free shuttle service make it easy to navigate. The architecture is modern concrete apartment blocks, lacking alpine charm but offering ski-in/ski-out convenience. The high exposed location can be bleak, windy, and very cold.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system in Val Thorens is modern and state-of-the-art, with fast, high-capacity lifts including the Cime Caron cable car reaching 3,200m and the Bouchet lift at 3,230m. The Val Thorens-Orelle sector has about 30 lifts. However, strong winds can force lift closures due to the exposed high-altitude location. The 3 Vallées area as a whole has 183 lifts with a capacity of 260,000 skiers per hour.
linkedResortsDescription: Val Thorens is part of the Les 3 Vallées ski area, the largest lift-linked ski area in the world with 600 km of pistes and 183 lifts. It connects to Les Menuires, Saint-Martin-de-Belleville, Méribel, Courchevel, and Brides-les-Bains via lifts. Additionally, the Orelle resort in the Maurienne valley is directly linked to Val Thorens via the 3 Vallées Express gondola (5,122m long), providing access from the other side of the mountain. The combined Val Thorens-Orelle sector offers 150 km of slopes.

terrainDescription: The Valtournenche ski area covers 54 km of pistes, with 17 km easy, 35 km medium, and 2 km difficult. The slopes above the Salette gondola terminal at 2,245m are mostly gentle blue runs, well above the treeline. Two chairlifts provide uplift to further straightforward blues, with one linking to the more challenging upper sector and the Cime Bianche col connection to Cervinia. The terrain is predominantly undemanding and intermediate-friendly, with wide, flowing runs ideal for relaxed cruising.
offPisteDescription: Off-piste in Valtournenche benefits from the high-altitude terrain and links to Cervinia and Zermatt. The Theodul Glacier offers some of the best snow in the region. A guide is essential due to the high mountain terrain. Heli-skiing is available. It is illegal to ski off-piste in Italy without full avalanche safety equipment.
valueDescription: Valtournenche is less expensive than neighbouring Cervinia, offering a more budget-friendly base for accessing the same ski area. Lift pass prices for the local Valtournenche area are €43 for one day, €121.50 for three days, and €227 for six days. The village has good-value family-run restaurants and a range of accommodation options. It is considered one of the best-value gateways into the Matterhorn ski region.
familyDescription: Valtournenche offers a dedicated children's area called the Wild West Zone, and an excellent beginners' zone near the Salette gondola. The village has a large leisure centre with a swimming pool, sauna, gym, climbing wall, indoor tennis courts, and a multi-sports hall. Other family activities include snowshoeing, sled dog rides, ice skating, and ice climbing. The relaxed, traditional village atmosphere is suitable for families seeking a quieter base.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski in Valtournenche is low-key, with a handful of local bars and hotel lounges such as Bar Maquignaz, Bar Pub 4810, and Bar La Copa Pan. The latter sometimes hosts live music. For a livelier scene, Cervinia is 9 km up the valley, but the bus service does not run late and taxis are expensive. Evening entertainment is relaxed rather than rowdy.
resortCharacterDescription: Valtournenche is a traditional Italian mountain village terraced into the west-facing flank of the Valtournenche valley. It has much more traditional charm than neighbouring Cervinia, with narrow streets, local shops, and a relaxed atmosphere. The village is surrounded by softer yet dramatic alpine scenery, and offers a picturesque, authentic base for exploring the wider ski area.
liftSystemDescription: The Valtournenche ski area is served by a 12-person gondola from the village to Salette at 2,245m, with a capacity of 2,400 persons per hour. Above Salette, two chairlifts provide uplift, and a new 6-seater Gran Sometta chairlift (installed for winter 2023/24) improves access to the Cervinia link. The lift system is modern and fast, with minimal queuing. A ski-bus connects the village to the gondola base, and many hotels offer their own shuttles.
linkedResortsDescription: Valtournenche's ski area is linked to Breuil-Cervinia and, via the Theodul Pass, to Zermatt in Switzerland, forming the Matterhorn Ski Paradise with over 360 km of pistes. The link allows skiers to cross the border and access Zermatt's more challenging terrain. A new 6-seater Gran Sometta chairlift (2023/24) improves the connection to Cervinia, and the Matterhorn Alpine Crossing (opened March 2024) allows year-round travel between Cervinia and Zermatt without skis.

terrainDescription: The Venet ski area sits on a north-facing slope between 972m and 2,212m, with most pistes graded red (intermediate). The longest run is 6 km. The terrain is manageable and beginner-friendly, with a dedicated children's area and childcare facilities. A Panorama Lifestyle Park offers an airbag, lines, and obstacles for freestylers.
offPisteDescription: Off-piste and ski touring routes are available with up to 1,200m of vertical descent. Fresh snowfall creates good conditions for freeriding, but the terrain requires appropriate experience and avalanche safety equipment. The Venet Gipfelhütte offers overnight stays for early morning first tracks before lifts open.
valueDescription: A 6-day adult lift pass for Venet costs €249 (2025/26 season), with children's passes at €148. The resort is part of the Ski6 pass, which covers 350 km of slopes across multiple Tyrolean resorts. Accommodation in the nearby towns of Zams and Landeck is generally affordable, and the resort is only 70 km from Innsbruck, keeping transfer costs low.
familyDescription: Venet is a quiet, beginner-friendly ski area with a dedicated children's zone and childcare facilities. The resort is accessible by public transport via the train station in Zams. Ski school and ski rental are available at the valley station. The manageable size and lack of crowds make it suitable for families with young children.
apresSkiDescription: The Venet Panorama Restaurant at the summit offers views of the Arlberg, Zugspitze, and Ortler group. The Zammer Alm and Gogles Alm provide traditional Austrian meals. In the evenings, the Venet Gipfelhütte offers stargazing sessions at the observatory on clear nights.
resortCharacterDescription: Venet is a small, traditional Tyrolean ski area serving the towns of Zams, Landeck, and Fliess. It is not a purpose-built resort but a local mountain with a relaxed, uncrowded atmosphere. The base area is in the town of Zams, accessible by an aerial tramway from the center. The surrounding villages offer typical Austrian character with hotels and restaurants.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system comprises 7 lifts: 1 gondola, 2 quad chairlifts, 1 double chairlift, and 3 surface lifts. The uphill capacity is 6,760 persons per hour. The gondola from Zams provides direct access to the mountain. Lifts are generally uncrowded even in high season.
linkedResortsDescription: Venet is part of the Ski6 ski pass, which covers 350 km of slopes across six Tyrolean resorts: Nauders, Serfaus-Fiss-Ladis, Kaunertal, Fendels, and Venet-Zams-Landeck. There is no direct lift connection between these resorts, but the pass allows access to all areas. The nearby towns of Zams, Landeck, and Fliess are served by the Venet ski area.

terrainDescription: The terrain is a high-alpine basin with easy and intermediate slopes, offering panoramic views of three-thousand-meter peaks and glaciers. The main run is a medium-difficulty downhill from the Wilder Mann mountain station, and Run #1 winds from mid-mountain back to the base village. The ski area has 15 km of groomed pistes, with no advanced runs, served by 4 lifts including a 6-seater chairlift (opened in 2018) and a double chairlift.
offPisteDescription: Vent is a popular base for ski touring, with classic routes to peaks like the Wildspitze (3,770 m) and Similaun (3,606 m). Off-piste terrain includes varied ungroomed slopes that can stay fresh for a day or two after snowfall. Hiring a mountain guide is recommended for exploring the extensive freeride and touring opportunities.
valueDescription: Lift passes are reasonably priced, with a day ticket for adults at €51 (main season), youth €37.50, and children €25. The village offers affordable accommodation with several hotels and chalets. The Ötztal Guest Card provides discounts on lifts, pools, and public transport, adding further value.
familyDescription: Vent is a family-friendly resort with a dedicated beginners' area near the village, served by two T-bar lifts. The ski school offers lessons for children and adults, and there is a children's carousel and magic carpet. The village is small and safe, with no through traffic, and families can walk everywhere. Childcare is available from 36 months for up to 6 hours a day.
apresSkiDescription: Apres-ski is low-key, with a few bars and restaurants in the village. The Stablein mountain hut occasionally hosts live music evenings, followed by a 5 km sledding run down to the base. For more lively nightlife, Sölden is a 20-minute drive away.
resortCharacterDescription: Vent is a traditional mountaineering village at 1,900 m, with a beautiful church as its centerpiece. It is small and cozy, with farmers walking cattle through town past the ski lift. The resort is purposefully small-scale, with no through traffic, surrounded by three-thousand-meter peaks and glaciers. Accommodation consists of hotels and chalets, and the atmosphere is authentic and relaxed.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of 4 lifts: a double chairlift from the village to mid-mountain, a high-speed 6-seater chairlift (opened in 2018) from mid-mountain to the upper slopes, and two T-bar lifts for the beginners' area. Queues are rare, and the lifts operate from 09:30 to 16:00.
linkedResortsDescription: Vent is part of the Ötztal Super Ski Pass, which gives access to 6 ski areas in the Ötztal valley, including Sölden, Obergurgl-Hochgurgl, and others. However, the ski areas are not physically connected by lifts; travel between them requires a bus or car (about 20 minutes to Sölden).

terrainDescription: The ski area is divided into two main sectors: the main slope from the top of the gondola down to the base, and a more advanced area under the Mont Major chairlift. The long 6 km Sigeroulaz run from Crêt du Midi to the base offers a mix of red and blue sections. The steep pitches of Tracui and Sigeroulaz provide challenge, and there are wide, open tree runs. A small beginner area with a covered magic carpet is at the top of the gondola, but it shares space with through traffic. The resort has 35 km of prepared slopes on Crêt du Midi.
offPisteDescription: Off-piste skiing is limited within the resort itself, but an itinerary run (Vallee Orzival) connects Grimentz to Vercorin, ending near the Tracui lift. The trees on the mountain are wide and open, offering some gladed skiing after fresh snowfall.
valueDescription: Lift passes are reasonably priced compared to larger Swiss resorts, with a 6-day adult pass costing CHF 295 in both low and high season. The Val d'Anniviers pass includes Vercorin, St-Luc-Chandolin, Grimentz, and Zinal. The Magic Pass offers access to 52 Swiss resorts for a single price, making Vercorin a good value base for exploring the region. A 1-day adult pass is CHF 61.
familyDescription: Vercorin is marketed as a family-friendly resort with a dedicated beginner area at the top of the gondola, including a covered magic carpet. However, the beginner slope shares space with through traffic, which may be challenging on busy days. The village is quiet and laidback, with cobbled streets suitable for evening strolls. One ski school is available, and the resort has a snowpark and a fun track for children.
apresSkiDescription: Apres-ski is low-key, with two bars and one club in the village. The resort is quiet after 10 pm, and the atmosphere is relaxed rather than lively. There are 8 restaurants in the village.
resortCharacterDescription: Vercorin is a traditional Swiss village with cobbled streets and historic wooden chalets, perched on a plateau overlooking the Rhone valley. The village has a quiet, authentic character, with the gondola station located at the edge of the village. The Swiss Peaks Resort building is a modern addition near the gondola.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of a two-stage gondola from the village to Crêt du Midi, a high-speed quad chairlift (Mont Major) that replaced two surface lifts, a covered magic carpet for beginners, and several surface lifts. The gondola runs every 15 minutes and connects Vercorin to Chalais. Total lift count is around 9–10 lifts.
linkedResortsDescription: Vercorin is part of the Val d'Anniviers ski area, which also includes Grimentz-Zinal and St-Luc-Chandolin, offering a combined 220 km of pistes and 44 lifts. A single Val d'Anniviers lift pass covers all four resorts, and the Magic Pass provides access to 52 Swiss resorts. An itinerary run connects Vercorin to Grimentz via the Vallee Orzival.

terrainDescription: The terrain at Verena 2000 is split roughly 39% beginner, 22% intermediate, and 39% advanced, with slopes ranging from a nursery area equipped with a magic carpet and baby ski lift to more challenging descents. The off-piste terrain is described as magnificent, with a series of off-piste routes available.
familyDescription: The resort is marketed as a safe, uncrowded environment for families with children. It features a dedicated nursery area with a magic carpet and a baby ski lift. The ski school has 36 instructors experienced in teaching children, and offers end-of-course races with medals for all participants. First-time snowboarders get a free lesson and ski pass on the first day.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of 3 chairlifts, a magic carpet, and a baby ski lift. The main lift is the Forte Verena 6-person detachable chairlift with a bubble, installed in 2020, which covers 1500m in under 5 minutes with an initial capacity of 1800 persons per hour. The lifts operate from 09:00 to 16:30.

terrainDescription: The ski area sits between the Mont-Blanc and Grand-Combin massifs, offering wide, groomed slopes that are predominantly east-facing (80% of terrain). The runs are set in unspoilt nature with panoramic views. A training stadium is available for ski clubs, and the slopes are groomed daily. A restaurant at the base and a sunny refreshment bar provide on-mountain dining.
valueDescription: A day lift pass costs CHF 46 for adults and CHF 28 for children (per skiweather.eu). Cheaper local passes are available for those staying only within Vichères, and upgrade options exist for the wider Saint-Bernard network. Self-catering accommodation is available in the nearby village of Liddes, keeping costs moderate compared to larger resorts.
familyDescription: The resort features a dedicated beginner area with a mini ski lift for children and a rope tow. The slopes are wide and uncrowded, providing a safe environment for learning. A restaurant at the base of the slopes offers family-friendly dining, and the area is known for its calm, relaxed atmosphere suitable for children and beginners.
resortCharacterDescription: Vichères-Liddes is a small, peaceful ski area above the village of Liddes in the Valais region. The base area consists of a car park, an old two-seater chairlift, and a restaurant. The focus is on simple, enjoyable skiing in a natural alpine setting, with wide mountain views and quiet slopes. It is not a purpose-built resort but a traditional Swiss ski area with minimal development.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system comprises one double chairlift and three surface lifts, including a mini ski lift for children. The chairlift is an older two-seater model. Lifts operate from 09:00 to 16:00, with varying hours depending on the period (weekends, holidays, and school breaks). The system is small and uncrowded, with no queues.
linkedResortsDescription: Vichères-Liddes is part of the Pays du Saint-Bernard network, which includes two other ski areas: Champex-Lac and La Fouly. A single regional ski pass (Saint-Bernard PASS) grants access to all three areas, with ski bus connections linking them. Champex-Lac offers 15 km of runs (70% blue) from 1,486 m to 2,200 m, while La Fouly provides 20 km of runs (30% blue, 20% red, 50% black) from 1,600 m to 2,200 m. The combined network totals 113 km of pistes across 17 lifts.

terrainDescription: The terrain is centered on the Ciampedie natural terrace at 2,000 m, with slopes facing the Catinaccio massif. Most runs are easy (69% beginner), with wide, gentle blue cruisers suitable for families and intermediates. The valley run to Vigo di Fassa is a long intermediate descent. More challenging options include the Thöni run, a long red with sweeping curves, and the short black Tomba run (almost 1 km) used by World Cup skier Alberto Tomba for training. The highest point, Pra Martin, offers an easy descent. The ski area has 13 km of pistes, with 6.4 km of easy slopes.
valueDescription: Lift pass prices are moderate: a one-day adult pass costs €76, youth €52, and child €38. Accommodation in the valley is noted for good value, with many affordable options. The FlySki Shuttle offers free transfers for children under 8 and a 50% discount for ages 8–12, reducing family travel costs. Buying a Val di Fassa/Carezza pass online saves 5%.
familyDescription: Ciampedie is especially suited for families, with a dedicated Kinderpark managed by the Vigo di Fassa Passo Costalunga Ski School, where children can ski and play. The beginner area includes a magic carpet, the Campo Scuola Ciampedie chairlift with a very easy slope, and a Baby Park for first-timers. Two ski schools operate on-site: Scuola italiana di Sci Vigo di Fassa and Scuola Sci Vajolet Pozza. Non-skiers can enjoy snow walks from Ciampedie to Pian Pecei and Gardeccia.
resortCharacterDescription: Vigo di Fassa is a traditional Ladin village in the Fassa Valley, with historic architecture and a compact center. It is not purpose-built; the resort retains alpine charm. The Ciampedie area is a natural terrace at 2,000 m with stunning panoramic views of the Dolomites, including the Catinaccio/Rosengarten group, a UNESCO World Heritage site. The village has a relaxed, authentic atmosphere.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of 6 lifts: a modern cable car from Vigo to Ciampedie (built 1985, rebuilt 2005 by Doppelmayr) and five chairlifts. Four are high-speed detachable chairlifts, most with bubbles: Vajolet 1 and 2 (installed 2017 by Leitner), Pian Pecei-Pra Martin (2003, Doppelmayr), and Pian Pecei-Ciampedie (2018). A fixed-grip chairlift (Campo Scuola Ciampedie, 1995) serves the beginner area. An escalator connects the village center to the cable car base station.
linkedResortsDescription: Vigo di Fassa is part of the Val di Fassa/Carezza ski area, which includes several sectors (Catinaccio, Buffaure, Ciampac, Belvedere, Col Rodella, Carezza) linked by ski bus. With a Dolomiti Superski pass, skiers can access the entire 1,200 km network, including the Sellaronda circuit that connects Val Gardena, Alta Badia, Arabba, and Val di Fassa. The Catinaccio area itself is not directly connected by piste to other sectors, but a ski bus runs from Pera to the Buffaure lift. The Panorama Ski Tour links the area to other valleys.

terrainDescription: The resort has 20 km of slopes consisting of 11 km easy/blue runs (55%) and 9 km intermediate/red runs (45%), with no difficult/black runs. The ski area is located above Lago Ampollino in the Sila National Park in Calabria, between elevations of 1,350 m and 1,650 m. A valley run is available, and artificial snow-making is operational. A snow park and night skiing at Palumbo Sila are also offered.
offPisteDescription: No dedicated off-piste terrain is described in the source material. The resort lacks advanced/difficult runs entirely, and the low-to-moderate altitude (1,350-1,650 m) in Calabria limits natural off-piste opportunities. Skiers seeking off-piste terrain would need to look to nearby larger ski areas.
valueDescription: The ski pass costs €35 per day according to visitor reviews, with complaints that the price increased while only 2 of approximately 20 km of slopes are consistently open during weekdays. Visitors note limited services during the week and describe the resort as small but sufficient for southern Italy.
familyDescription: The resort offers beginner-friendly terrain with easy runs making up 55% of the total slope network. A snow park is available for children and beginners. However, user reviews report that only the main chairlift operates during weekdays, with additional lifts opening on weekends when more visitors arrive, potentially limiting variety for families visiting mid-week.
apresSkiDescription: Visitor reviews describe very limited après-ski options, noting that after the slopes close, the only option is an ice rink (palaghiaccio), then nothing. The resort has Bar Ristorante Il Rifugio and Club Palumbo mentioned as dining and nightlife options, but one reviewer stated it is difficult to even buy groceries in the evening. Another noted that pisteurs use the "pista guido" for snowmobile excursions, creating conflicts with skiers.
resortCharacterDescription: Villaggio Palumbo is a small resort village located 0.1 km from the ski area in the Province of Crotone, Calabria. The area sits in the Sila National Park at an altitude of 1,338-1,651 m. Nearby villages include Caprara (1.3 km), Trepidò (5 km), and Cotronei (21 km). Accommodation options include rental houses, apartments, and a 4-star hotel directly on the slopes.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of 3 main lifts: Palumbo Sila I (3-person fixed-grip chairlift, 745 m, 1,500 persons/hour), Palumbo Sila II (2-person fixed-grip chairlift, 744 m, 1,200 persons/hour), and Palumbo Sila III (T-bar lift, 300 m, 1,200 persons/hour). A people mover is also present. Total capacity is 3,900 passengers/hour across 1.8 km of lift length. Fixed-grip lifts indicate an older infrastructure without high-speed capability.
linkedResortsDescription: Villaggio Palumbo is part of the Palumbosila ski area located above Lago Ampollino in the Sila National Park. Nearby ski areas include Lorica (16.9 km), Camigliatello Silano (24.9 km), and Fago del Soldato (27.5 km). The resort is one of 4 ski resorts in Calabria and one of 30 in Southern Italy.

terrainDescription: The Villard-Corrençon ski area offers 125 km of pistes across 36 runs: 12 green, 9 blue, 7 red, and 8 black. The terrain includes wide, gently sloping runs above the treeline, forest passages, and powder fields. The black run Carole Montillet is a long, steep descent from the top of the Villard sector. The Grande Moucherolle and Corrençon areas provide intermediate cruising runs with scenic variety. The Pre des Preys gondola serves gentle beginner slopes near a mountain restaurant with a sun terrace. The Rhodo moguls, Le Grand Couloir, and Chamois are among the most challenging black runs.
offPisteDescription: Off-piste opportunities exist around the Petit Moucherolle peak (2,150 m), with technical couloirs and lines off the summit. After fresh snowfall, inter-piste powder and tree runs in the lower sections provide good skiing. Some marked slopes are left ungroomed to allow mogul development. The local ESF offers freeride courses, and hiring a guide is recommended on powder days. The limestone geology limits hut-to-hut touring but allows day-touring on the plateau.
valueDescription: A six-day adult lift pass costs €242, with a day pass at €43 and a three-hour pass at €35. Child passes are €179 for six days, €32 for a day, and €27 for three hours. Accommodation is available at budget-friendly prices, with self-catering apartments and mid-range hotels. The resort is known for affordable dining and lift passes compared to larger Alpine resorts. The Hidden France article notes a one-day lift pass at £33 (approximately €38).
familyDescription: The resort holds the 'Famille plus' quality label. There are four dedicated beginner areas, including the Rambins children's hamlet in Corrençon. The sports centre in Villard de Lans contains a swimming pool with waterslides and a wave machine, an ice rink home to the local ice hockey team 'The Bears', a bowling alley, and a casino. Six toboggan runs (two green, three blue, two red) are available. The ESF offers group and individual lessons for children from age three. The village is compact with pedestrian streets, and the transfer from Grenoble Airport takes about 65 minutes.
apresSkiDescription: The après-ski scene is low-key and local. Bar du Sapin at the foot of the slopes offers a musical atmosphere. Le Country is a club option. The Hidden France article describes late-night bars populated more by locals than tourists. Restaurants include unpretentious options serving Savoyard cuisine. The resort does not have a Folie Douce or large party scene.
resortCharacterDescription: Villard de Lans is a traditional Savoyard village with cobbled streets, a pretty square, and independent shops selling local produce. The ski area is accessed from Le Balcon de Villard / Cote 2000, a modern lift base about 100 m uphill from the village, which has a purpose-built feel. Corrençon-en-Vercors, 5 km to the south, is a smaller, quieter village with an unhurried atmosphere. The resort is located within the Vercors Regional Natural Park, which imposes building restrictions, preserving the traditional character.
liftSystemDescription: The lift network consists of 25 lifts, including gondolas, high-speed quads, chairlifts, and surface lifts. The OnTheSnow data lists 2 gondolas, 2 high-speed quads, 4 quad chairs, 1 triple chair, 1 double chair, and 8 surface lifts. Queues are rare even on powder days, as noted in multiple reviews. The lifts provide access to the full 125 km of pistes connecting Villard and Corrençon.
linkedResortsDescription: The Villard-Corrençon ski area connects the villages of Villard de Lans and Corrençon-en-Vercors via lifts and pistes. The two entrance gates are at Balcon de Villard/Cote 2000 and Les Glovettes on the Villard side, and at Hameau des Rambins and Clos de la Balme on the Corrençon side. The linked area forms the largest alpine ski domain in the Vercors massif, with 125 km of pistes. There is no connection to other ski areas outside the Vercors.

terrainDescription: The Villars-Gryon-Les Diablerets ski area is a connected set of mountain neighbourhoods with wide, sunny slopes. The central hub is Bretaye (around 1,800m), accessible by cogwheel train and gondola. From there, lifts fan out to peaks like Grand Chamossaire (2,120m) and Chaux Ronde. The lower mountain can be marginal in lean snow years, and several lower surface lifts are defunct. Advanced terrain is limited to a few black runs such as the Combe d'Audon and the Willy Favre piste, which hosted the 2020 Youth Olympic Games.
offPisteDescription: Off-piste skiing is limited but possible. Between the prepared trails, gently sloped pastures offer easy access on powder days, and there are a few expert off-piste opportunities through the trees around Grand Chamossaire. However, the region is prone to avalanches, so a guide is recommended for any steep terrain. The lower mountain off-piste is often not skiable in lean snow years.
valueDescription: The resort is part of the Magic Pass, which covers 30 resorts in the region, offering good value for those who want to explore. Lift pass prices are moderate for Switzerland: a day pass for adults is around 84 CHF, and a 6-day pass around 349 CHF. Free practice areas with conveyor belts are available in Villars (Palace) and Gryon (Frience), allowing beginners to learn without buying a full pass. Free public transport within the ski area (Mobilis zones 154-156 and Les Diablerets skibus) is included with a lift ticket. Accommodation ranges from apartments to hotels, with early January and late March offering lower prices.
familyDescription: The resort is highly family-friendly with dedicated children's areas at all three villages. In Villars, the free Palace practice area has several conveyor belts, and the Bretaye station has a children's area with a conveyor belt. In Gryon, the Frience area has a covered conveyor belt and a toboggan run. In Les Diablerets, the valley station has a children's area with conveyor belt and rope lifts, and the Les Mazots station has another. Ski schools offer special courses for children. For sledding, Les Diablerets has a 7 km sled run, and Villars has a 1.6 km forest run at Col de Soud. The village centres are walkable and traffic-free in parts.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski in Villars is relaxed rather than raucous. The Roc Star bar at the Roc d'Orsay gondola station hosts après-ski evenings and concerts. In the village, there is a bowling alley and sports bar with pool tables and games. The scene is family-friendly, with most venues closing by early evening. Les Diablerets and Gryon have quieter options with a few local bars.
resortCharacterDescription: Villars is a traditional Swiss village with a walkable centre, upscale hotels, and chalet-style accommodation. It is not purpose-built but has grown organically around the railway. Les Diablerets is known for its traditional mountain architecture and small town centre. Gryon is a quieter, more local village. All three have a picturesque alpine character with views of Mont Blanc and the Rhône valley.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system combines modern and historic elements. The Bex-Villars-Bretaye cogwheel train provides a scenic route to the central hub at Bretaye. Gondolas from Villars (Roc d'Orsay), Gryon (Barboleuse-Les Chaux), and Les Diablerets (Diablerets Express) provide quick access to the slopes. A two-way quad chair connects Villars to Les Diablerets. The network includes high-speed six-packs and an eight-person chairlift. Some older surface lifts remain, and lower mountain lifts can be defunct in lean snow years. Queues are rare outside peak weeks.
linkedResortsDescription: The Villars-Gryon-Les Diablerets ski area is interconnected by lifts, forming a single domain of over 100 km of pistes. A two-way quad chair connects Villars to Les Diablerets. Additionally, Glacier 3000 is accessible via a free 10-minute bus from Les Diablerets, offering higher altitude skiing on a glacier. The Magic Pass also covers 30 resorts in the wider Alpes Vaudoises region, including Leysin and others.

terrainDescription: The ski area is compact and predominantly west-facing (73% of slopes), with 23% facing north. The terrain includes 8 km of novice runs, 8 km of intermediate runs, and 10 km of expert runs, offering a balanced mix for all ability levels. A 7 km toboggan run descends from Giw to the village, and 60 km of winter hiking and snowshoe trails traverse the surrounding landscape. The slopes are well-prepared and the resort's sunny, high-altitude position provides reliable snow conditions.
valueDescription: A day lift pass costs CHF53 for adults and CHF35 for children, with a six-day pass at CHF271 for adults and CHF181 for children. The resort is small and less expensive than major Swiss destinations, and self-catering accommodation is available. The compact layout means most amenities are within walking distance, reducing the need for additional transport costs.
familyDescription: The resort is child-friendly with a compact, convenient layout. A 7 km toboggan run, a natural ice rink (free of charge), and 60 km of hiking and snowshoe trails provide off-slope activities. The ski school has 10 instructors and offers lessons for all ages. The predominantly easy and intermediate terrain makes it suitable for families with beginners, and the free ski bus connects to neighbouring resorts for more variety.
apresSkiDescription: The après-ski scene is quiet, with 6 restaurants, 1 bar/café, and 1 disco. Nightlife is limited, focusing on relaxed dining and small-scale evening entertainment. The resort's small size means most venues are within walking distance of accommodation.
resortCharacterDescription: Visperterminen is a traditional alpine village with a relaxed, authentic atmosphere. It has 2 hotels and 69 tourist beds, along with a supermarket and a tourist information office. The village retains its original character, with a compact layout that makes it easy to walk between slopes, accommodation, and amenities. The resort is car-free in the centre, adding to its peaceful charm.
liftSystemDescription: The resort has 5 lifts: 2 chairlifts and 3 drag lifts, with a total capacity of 4,040 persons per hour. Lift lines are typically short due to the small size of the area. The lifts provide access to a vertical drop of 998 m, from the base at 1,340 m to the summit at 2,338 m.
linkedResortsDescription: Visperterminen is part of the Rund um Visp ski area, which includes several small resorts such as Bürchen, Unterbäch, Eischoll, and Gspon. These are connected by a free ski bus service, offering over 100 km of slopes in total. The area is also accessible from Visp, which serves as a base for 20 ski areas within an hour's travel, including the Aletsch Arena, Saas-Fee, and Zermatt.

terrainDescription: The Vogel ski area is accessed by a cable car from the valley floor at 569m to a plateau at 1535m. The resort has 22 km of groomed slopes, with 9.5 km of easy blue runs and 12.5 km of intermediate red runs. Most slopes are above the treeline, offering open terrain. The longest run is Žagarjev Graben, a 6.8 km red run descending 1,200m from the top of the Šija chairlift to the lower gondola station. The resort has a dedicated nursery area near the upper cable car station for beginners, and a snow park of approximately 2 hectares. No snowmaking is permitted as the resort lies within Triglav National Park, so all skiing is on natural snow.
offPisteDescription: In good snow conditions, Vogel offers lift-accessible off-piste terrain between the groomed runs, mostly above the treeline. The resort is considered Slovenia's most popular destination for ski touring and backcountry skiing, with peaks such as Suha and Rodica accessible from the Kratki plaz drag lift and the Šija single chairlift. The off-piste slopes are generally moderate in pitch, resulting in lower avalanche danger compared to other European freeride areas. A six-day hut-to-hut ski safari is also available.
valueDescription: Vogel is one of the cheapest ski resorts in Europe. A one-day adult lift pass costs approximately €38-€45, with multi-day passes offering slight discounts. Accommodation in nearby villages such as Ribčev Laz and Bohinjska Bistrica ranges from €25 to €50 per night for hostels and guesthouses. A free ski bus connects all local villages to the cable car base. Food costs are low: a budget lunch on the slopes costs €10-€15, and a dinner in a traditional gostilna costs €18-€30. Ski rental is around €25-€40 per day. The total cost for a four-day solo trip including accommodation, food, lift pass, and rental can be under €400.
familyDescription: Vogel is well suited for families and beginners. There is a dedicated children's park with a magic carpet lift near the upper cable car station, where complete beginners can learn. The nursery slopes are separate from the main ski traffic. The terrain is predominantly easy blue and intermediate red runs, with no advanced black runs. The ski school offers private coaching for all levels. The resort is uncrowded on weekdays, and the free ski bus makes it easy to access from family-friendly accommodation in the Bohinj valley. The atmosphere is quiet and relaxed, with low-key evenings.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski at Vogel is virtually non-existent. The resort itself has no village; the base station is at the valley floor with a few bars such as Macesen. The surrounding villages around Lake Bohinj have a handful of friendly bars, but there is no party scene. Evenings are low-key, with most dining options hotel-based. The Ski Hotel Vogel has a terrace bar with panoramic views, but it closes early. For those seeking nightlife, the nearest lively resort is Kranjska Gora, about 1.5 hours away.
resortCharacterDescription: Vogel is a scenic, high-altitude ski resort located within Triglav National Park above Lake Bohinj. The resort is accessed by a cable car from the valley; there is no village at the base. The setting offers extraordinary panoramas of the Julian Alps, Lake Bohinj, and the surrounding mountains. The resort was opened in 1964 and has a mix of older and newer lift infrastructure. The area is known for its natural beauty and is popular for day trips from Ljubljana and Bled. Accommodation is in the traditional villages around Lake Bohinj, which have a relaxed, rural character.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system at Vogel consists of 9 lifts: one cable car (a Leitner-Poma 80-person gondola built in 2001), one circular cabin cable car (Zadnji Vogel, new in 2023/24), two four-seater chairlifts (Brunarica and Orlove glave, Doppelmayr 2007), one single-seater chairlift (Šija, built in 1967), three T-bar lifts (Storeč, Konta, Kratki plaz), and one magic carpet. The cable car from the valley operates every 30 minutes with a capacity of 950 persons per hour, which can lead to queues on busy weekends and holidays. The upper mountain lifts have a combined capacity of 8,911 persons per hour. The Šija chairlift is a historic one-seater, while the newer Doppelmayr chairs are modern. The new circular cable car replaced an old two-seater and offers panoramic views.
linkedResortsDescription: Vogel is part of the Julian Alps Ski Pass, which also includes Kranjska Gora, Krvavec, Cerkno, and 3Laendereck (Austria). A combined ticket with Kranjska Gora is available. The All Slovenia Ski Pass covers multiple resorts across the country, allowing day trips to other areas such as Krvavec and Kanin-Bovec.

terrainDescription: The ski area consists of a single slope on the Sonnenhügel, with 5 km of pistes. The terrain is predominantly intermediate (83%) with 17% beginner runs and no advanced runs. The piste is often sunny, with flat and moderately steep sections, offering views of the Gail valley and the Gailtal Alps. The run faces south-east.
valueDescription: Lift tickets are affordable: a day ticket for adults costs €40, children €20, and a family ticket (Familienticket 1) is €51. A children's season pass is €205, and a Bambini season pass is €63. The resort is known for being budget-friendly for families, with low prices compared to larger Austrian resorts.
familyDescription: The resort is marketed as family-friendly, with a children's ski school, a Kinderland with a dedicated children's lift, and 37 km of cross-country trails, 18 km of winter hiking paths, and a natural toboggan run. The slopes are predominantly easy to intermediate, suitable for families. The village of Kötschach-Mauthen offers a range of accommodation and dining options.
resortCharacterDescription: The resort is a small, family-oriented ski area on the Sonnenhügel above the town of Kötschach-Mauthen in the Gailtal valley in Carinthia. The village has a traditional Austrian atmosphere with rustic inns serving regional cuisine such as Kärntner Kasnudeln and Gailtaler Speck. The area is known for its quiet, uncrowded slopes and natural landscape.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of three surface lifts (T-bars) and one double chairlift, according to recent sources. The lifts are low-speed and serve a single piste. There is a dedicated children's lift in the Kinderland. Queues are rare due to the small size of the resort.

terrainDescription: The terrain in Wagrain is predominantly tree-lined, with the vast majority of runs rated red (intermediate). The Grafenberg area offers excellent beginner terrain with blue runs to progress onto, and the ski area includes short, varied runs that cycle back to lifts quickly. The connection to Zauchensee provides a longer journey-style skiing experience, and the terrain is described as varied with a mix of easy family runs and challenging World Cup courses.
valueDescription: Lift passes are reasonably priced, with a one-day adult pass around €78.50. Free parking is available at all three valley stations (Grafenbergbahn, Flying Mozart, Rote 8er), and a free ski bus connects the villages. The Wasserwelt Amade swimming complex offers free entry in the evenings, adding value for families. Food and drink prices in town and on the mountain are considered very reasonable by visitors.
familyDescription: Wagrain is particularly family-friendly, with Wagraini's Winter World on Grafenberg offering a tubing track, practice slopes, tire carousel, and a heated dwarf house for creative play. Ski schools offer courses for children from age 4, and there are ski kindergartens for children from age 2. The Wasserwelt Amade swimming complex provides free evening entry, and the Grafenberg area is ideal for beginners with good blue runs to progress onto.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski options include the Kuhbergalm at the base of the Grafenberg lifts and the Kuhstall at the base of the Flying Mozart lift. The village itself is quiet in the evenings, focusing on relaxation rather than nightlife. The Wasserwelt Amade swimming complex offers free entry in the evenings, providing an alternative to bar-hopping.
resortCharacterDescription: Wagrain is a traditional Austrian market town with a local feel, spread along the valley. The village centre is about 500 metres from the main lift bases, with accommodation also located between the centre and the lifts. It retains a scenic, romantic character rather than being a purpose-built resort, and is described as having a traditional Alpine setting.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system is modern and efficient, with heated six- and eight-seater chairlifts as standard. The G-Link is one of the world's largest aerial tramways, connecting the Grießenkareck and Grafenberg mountains in six minutes. The Flying Mozart is a 10-seater gondola, and the Red 8-seater gondola provides direct access. The Panorama Link connects to the Zauchensee area. Queues are rare, even during peak periods, with most of the time skiers able to ski straight onto lifts.
linkedResortsDescription: Wagrain is centrally located within the Snow Space Salzburg ski area, directly connected to Flachau and St. Johann-Alpendorf via modern lifts. It is also part of the wider Ski Amadé network, which offers 760 km of slopes. The Panorama Link connects Wagrain's Grießenkareck to the Flachauwinkl-Kleinarl shuttle mountain, providing access to the Zauchensee-Flachauwinkl ski area. The 12 Peaks Tour allows skiers to traverse 12 peaks and 5 valleys in a single day.

terrainDescription: The Warth-Schröcken sector has 15 blue pistes, 10 red pistes, 10 black pistes, and 7 ungroomed ski routes. The steepest marked run is black piste 253 (Ochsakopf), which reaches a gradient of 70% in two places. The terrain includes north-facing slopes that hold snow well, and the local slopes are low-lying but benefit from heavy snowfall. The sector is at the northern edge of the Ski Arlberg area, with access to the wider Arlberg via the Auenfeldjet gondola.
offPisteDescription: Warth-Schröcken receives an average of 11 metres of natural snow per winter, making it one of the snowiest resorts in the Alps. The north-facing slopes and high snowfall create deep powder conditions. Ungroomed ski routes and areas under lifts and next to groomed runs offer off-piste opportunities. The Arlberg region is known for its legendary off-piste terrain, and Warth-Schröcken provides access to it via the linked ski area.
valueDescription: The Warth-Schröcken local lift pass is available for 1 to 2.5 days and is cheaper than the full Ski Arlberg pass. For beginners, there are comparatively low-cost day passes or per-ride tickets for the nursery lifts. The resort has a small supermarket and a handful of restaurants with reasonable prices. Accommodation options include self-catering apartments and hotels, with the village being less expensive than nearby Lech or St Anton.
familyDescription: Warth has child-friendly nursery slopes on the edge of the village, with practice areas at Steffisalp-Express (Pauli’s Children’s Land) and at Salober (Pauli's Ski Arena). Ski school standards are high, with English-speaking instructors available. There are toboggan runs, winter hiking trails, and cosy huts suitable for families. Schröcken’s nursery slopes are a bus ride away at the Saloberjet base station. The village is car-free in parts and has a quiet, safe atmosphere.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski in Warth-Schröcken is low-key, with a handful of local bars in the village. The resort is not known for lively nightlife; instead, it offers a quiet, relaxed atmosphere. There are a few restaurants serving traditional Austrian cuisine. For more vibrant après-ski, visitors can take the gondola to Lech or St Anton.
resortCharacterDescription: Warth and Schröcken are small, traditional Alpine villages with a combined population of around 183 in Warth. The architecture is typical Vorarlberg style, with wooden chalets and a church spire. The villages are not purpose-built; they have retained their original character. Warth is car-free in the centre, and the road between Warth and Lech is closed in winter, adding to the secluded feel.
liftSystemDescription: The Warth-Schröcken lift system includes modern high-speed six-seater chairlifts and gondolas. The Auenfeldjet gondola (built 2013/14) connects Warth-Schröcken to Lech-Zürs, and the Flexenbahn (2016/17) links to St Anton, making the entire Ski Arlberg area accessible. The lifts are efficient with minimal queues, and the piste map is dominated by fast 6-seaters and slick gondolas.
linkedResortsDescription: Warth-Schröcken is part of the Ski Arlberg, the largest connected ski area in Austria, with 300 km of groomed slopes and 200 km of deep snow runs. The Auenfeldjet gondola links directly to Lech and Zürs, and the Flexenbahn connects to St Anton, Stuben, and St Christoph. The Run of Fame is an 85 km ski circuit that goes from Warth to St Anton and back, covering the entire Arlberg. Skiers can also access the Arlberg's 85 lifts and 305 km of marked runs.

terrainDescription: The terrain consists of 13.5 km of easy and intermediate slopes, with a short beginner slope (Tschunggai) at the mountain station. A ski cross slope with jumps, waves, and curves, a snow park, and a Family Fun Line provide variety. The Sautal freeride descent runs through a larch forest. The run back to the base station passes through woods. Slopes are wide and groomed.
offPisteDescription: Off-piste skiing is available on the Sautal freeride descent, which runs through a larch forest. A freeride area is designated, but specific terrain details are limited.
valueDescription: A 6-day adult lift pass for Watles costs €211 in low season and €241.50 in high season. The cross-border Two Country Skiarena pass (covering three areas) costs €272.50 low season and €301 high season for adults. Children's passes are cheaper. Accommodation in the nearby village of Mals is described as inexpensive, with many family-run hotels and apartments.
familyDescription: The resort features a dedicated children's area (Bubo Kinderpark) with a magic carpet and child lift. The ski school holds a Gold certificate for children's instruction and offers group and private lessons. A 4 km natural toboggan run operates during the day and on certain evenings for night sledging. The Family Fun Line provides progression for beginners. Childcare is available for children from 36 months.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski in the nearby village of Mals is quiet, with 34 restaurants, 9 bars and cafés, and one disco. The atmosphere is more Tyrolean than Italian, with cozy bars and good restaurants. Nightlife is limited, suitable for families and those seeking a relaxed evening.
resortCharacterDescription: The resort is based around the small village of Mals (Malles Venosta), located 15 minutes by car from the slopes. The village has a traditional character with barns and hotels side by side. It is quiet and rural, with a Tyrolean rather than Italian feel. The ski area itself is on Mount Watles above the hamlet of Prämajur.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of 3 lifts: two chairlifts and one surface lift. The main chairlift is modern. Total lift capacity is 4,720 persons per hour. The valley station is at Prämajur, accessible by free ski bus from Mals.
linkedResortsDescription: Watles is part of the cross-border Two Country Skiarena, which includes the Belpiano/Schöneben-Malga S. Valentino/Haider Alm ski area and Nauders in Austria, totalling 159 km of pistes and 41 lifts. It is also part of the Südtirol Skiarena.

terrainDescription: The ski area sits between 1,480m and 2,600m in the Hohe Tauern National Park, with 23 km of groomed pistes. Of these, 6 km are blue (easy), 15 km red (intermediate), and 2 km black (advanced). The longest piste descends 8 km from Medelz to Enzingerboden with a vertical drop of 1,100m. The terrain is above the treeline, consisting of wide, open slopes in a glacial valley. The resort also has extensive deep snow slopes for off-piste skiing.
offPisteDescription: The resort offers extensive off-piste terrain in the Hohe Tauern National Park, with deep snow slopes away from the groomed pistes. Freeride opportunities are concentrated around the Rudolfshütte area, with guided freeride days available. The terrain includes untouched side valleys and routes suitable for ski touring. The area is known for safe yet thrilling off-piste skiing, with local guides recommended.
valueDescription: Lift passes are among the cheapest in Salzburg, with a day pass at €51. Children under 12 ski free when accompanied by their parents. Guests with the Uttendorf guest card receive a 20% discount on ski passes, and there is a one-off taster offer of a day pass plus a free day. The resort is known for affordable family packages.
familyDescription: The resort is marketed as a family ski area. There is a kids park with a magic carpet at the middle station, where children learn with lunchtime supervision. The ski school Lederer offers lessons for beginners and advanced. Children under 12 ski free with parents. The gentle learning areas and snow play zones are suitable for introducing kids to skiing.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski is tranquil, with two bars and pubs in the area. The Berghotel Rudolfshütte offers a sauna with glacier views and candlelit dinners. For those seeking nightlife, there is a disco in Uttendorf village. The overall atmosphere is quiet and relaxed.
resortCharacterDescription: The resort is set within the Hohe Tauern National Park, a protected area. The base station is at Enzingerboden, and the ski area is accessed by gondola, leading to a car-free high-alpine environment above the treeline. The Berghotel Rudolfshütte, located at the middle station, is the only on-mountain accommodation, offering ski-in/ski-out access. The setting is natural and remote, with no purpose-built village; the nearest town is Uttendorf in the valley.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of 7 lifts: 2 gondolas, 1 double chairlift, and 4 surface lifts. The capacity is 8,648 persons per hour. The lifts are older, with some user reports of closures during low season. A free ski bus runs from Uttendorf to the Enzingerboden base station.

terrainDescription: The ski area consists of six runs totalling 11 km, served by a rack railway, a cable car, and two drag lifts. The terrain is predominantly steep and rugged, with no beginner runs. According to the source, 63% of runs are intermediate and 37% advanced, though other sources indicate a higher proportion of advanced terrain. The north-facing aspect helps preserve snow quality. The longest run is 4 km. The runs are described as steep rugged slopes suitable only for experienced skiers.
offPisteDescription: The Wendelstein is promoted as a freeride area, with off-piste terrain accessible from the lifts. The Hotalehang run is a short black run that leads into steep terrain. The West run is a 5 km red run. However, the ski area is small and off-piste opportunities are limited; the source notes that it is not appropriate for those expecting extensive off-piste.
valueDescription: Adult day lift tickets are €47, with junior tickets at €27. The pricing is reasonable for German day areas, but the value is more about the mountain experience and historic railway access than skiing volume, as comparable Austrian areas offer more terrain for similar prices. The cogwheel railway and cable car are additional costs if not included in the ski pass. The ski area is small with only 11 km of runs, so the cost per km of piste is high.
familyDescription: The ski area has no beginner runs, so it is only suitable for families with children who are already confident on intermediate (blue/red) terrain. The cogwheel railway and cable car provide easy access to the summit, and there are some play elements for children near the restaurant area. However, the lack of nursery slopes and the steep nature of most runs make it a poor choice for families with beginners or very young children.
resortCharacterDescription: Wendelstein is a natural mountain peak with a summit complex that includes a church, observatory, weather station, restaurants, and lift terminals. The ski area is accessed via a historic cogwheel railway and a cable car from the valley villages of Brannenburg and Bayrischzell. The summit area is busy with sightseers and has a developed feel, but the mountain itself is a prominent peak in the Bavarian Pre-Alps. The resort character is that of a day-trip mountain rather than a traditional ski resort village.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of a historic cogwheel railway (built 1912, renovated in the 1990s with modern railcars reducing journey time to 30 minutes), a cable car (2,953 m long, 6.5 minutes, 50-person cabins, 450 people per hour capacity), and two drag lifts. The total lift count is 4. The railway and cable car provide access from two different valleys. The infrastructure is old but maintained, and the small scale means limited uphill capacity.
linkedResortsDescription: There are no linked resorts. Plans to connect to the Sudelfeld ski area were abandoned.

terrainDescription: The Wildhaus ski area offers 23 km of groomed pistes (13% easy, 74% intermediate, 13% advanced) plus 12 km of ungroomed ski routes. Beginners have gentle nursery zones and wide blue runs near Oberdorf (1230 m). Intermediates can ski long, flowing red pistes on the Gamsalp and Gamserrugg sectors. Advanced skiers can tackle the steep, narrow Warmtobel slope (1.7 km, 500 m vertical drop) and the marked but ungroomed Galfer ski route (9 km, 1600 m descent) from Gamserrugg to Grabs. The terrain includes forest runs, open alpine slopes, and a funslope with curves and tunnels. A snowpark (Snowland.ch) with about 20 obstacles is also available.
offPisteDescription: The main off-piste attraction is the Galfer ski route, a 9 km marked but ungroomed descent from Gamserrugg (2076 m) to Grabs in the St. Gallen Rhine valley, with a vertical drop of 1600 m. This route is suited to advanced skiers and offers deep snow freeriding. Additionally, the higher terrain around Gamserrugg provides freeride-style terrain and ungroomed areas. The Warmtobel slope, while groomed, is narrow and steep, and once a year it is transformed into Switzerland's largest natural terrain park with 30 obstacles. Freeriders can also explore ungroomed deep snow areas outside the marked pistes.
valueDescription: A one-day adult lift pass costs CHF 61 (as of 2025), with youth passes at CHF 52 and child passes at CHF 31. Half-day tickets and beginner-area-only passes are available at lower prices. The resort has 13 hotels with 788 beds, plus self-catering options like the Reka-Feriendorf. Two supermarkets are in the village. Accommodation ranges from budget guesthouses to mid-range hotels. The ski school offers group lessons (5 days, 4 hours) for CHF 285 for both skiing and snowboarding. Compared to larger Swiss resorts, Wildhaus is moderately priced.
familyDescription: Wildhaus is well-suited for families with children. The resort offers a dedicated children's area in the valley with three practice lifts, a children's carousel, a magic carpet, and a child lift. Childcare is available for children from 48 months, with 7 hours of care per day. The ski school has 100 instructors and offers English-speaking lessons. A mascot named Snowli entertains children. There is an adventure park, playground, and a skating rink. The village is traffic-free in the centre, and the slopes are uncrowded, allowing beginners to progress without pressure. The Toggenburg region promotes itself as a family-friendly holiday paradise.
apresSkiDescription: Apres-ski in Wildhaus is low-key. The Gräppelen-Bar is a popular spot, frequented by locals during the week and by the freestyler scene on weekends, with regular concerts and live DJs. The Funi Bar at Berggasthaus Oberdorf offers a relaxed atmosphere for après-ski drinks. The Snowland terrain park has a snow bar that hosts events until sundown. In total, there are 6 bars and pubs in the resort. The scene is described as having a few bars but nothing special, with a friendly atmosphere. There is no disco, cinema, or casino in the village.
resortCharacterDescription: Wildhaus is a traditional Swiss alpine village situated on the Wildhaus Pass at 1095 m, between the Churfirsten peaks and the Säntis massif. It is not a purpose-built resort but a historic settlement with a church, traditional hotels, and farmhouses. The village has a population of about 1,200 and retains a local, uncrowded character. The ski slopes are accessed from the edge of the village. The area is known for its scenic beauty, with views of the seven Churfirsten and the Alpstein massif. The birthplace of reformer Huldrych Zwingli is a heritage site.
liftSystemDescription: The Wildhaus lift system consists of 7 lifts: 2 chairlifts (including a quad chair to Oberdorf and a chair to Gamserrugg), 1 gondola lift to Gamplüt, and 4 drag lifts. The lifts have a total capacity of 7,804 persons per hour. Many of the lifts are older drag lifts, but they offer scenic views of the Säntis and Churfirsten. The gondola to Gamplüt provides access to the lower mountain. The lift system is not modern but functional, and queues are rare outside peak periods. The resort operates from 08:30 to 16:30 daily.
linkedResortsDescription: Wildhaus is part of the Toggenburg ski region, which also includes the neighbouring resorts of Unterwasser and Alt St. Johann. Together they form Eastern Switzerland's largest winter sports area, with a combined 60 km of pistes. The Chäserrugg ski area (Unterwasser/Alt St. Johann) is connected by a gondola and offers additional terrain up to 2,262 m. A single lift pass covers all three resorts, allowing skiers to explore the wider area. The Galfer ski route descends from Gamserrugg to Grabs, linking the ski area to the Rhine valley.

terrainDescription: The Wildkogel Arena features wide, open slopes set in a high basin above 2,100 metres, with views of the Hohe Tauern National Park. The terrain is predominantly intermediate, with a mix of blue and red runs. Two long valley runs descend to Neukirchen (5 km) and Bramberg (14 km), the latter also serving as the world's longest illuminated toboggan run. The area includes a family park with features like steep curves, snow waves, and a fun slope, as well as a speed check and ski movie course.
offPisteDescription: Off-piste skiing is available on open snow fields between the groomed trails, particularly around the Steinkogel, Gernkogel, and Laubkogel peaks in the Kitzbühel Alps. From March to May, ski touring to the peaks of the Hohe Tauern National Park, such as the Großvenediger (3,657 m), is popular. These areas require hiking or touring and are not lift-served.
valueDescription: A six-day adult lift pass costs €344.50, with a day ticket at €69.50. Children's day tickets are €34.50, and children born in 2020 or younger ski free. Combination tickets with the Zillertal Arena are available from two days. The villages of Neukirchen and Bramberg offer a range of affordable accommodation, and the resort is considered good value compared to larger Austrian ski areas.
familyDescription: The resort is purpose-built for families, with dedicated children's areas featuring mascots Kogel-Mogel and Kogel-Mia, ski carousels, magic carpets, and a family park with snow waves and steep curves. Ski schools have practice areas within the ski area, and nursery slopes are separate from faster traffic. The world's longest illuminated toboggan run (14 km) is a major family attraction. An electric shuttle bus connects the villages, and children born in 2020 or younger ski free. Austrian law requires children under 15 to wear helmets.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski is low-key and centred on the villages of Neukirchen and Bramberg, with several bars and restaurants. On the mountain, three mountain huts offer food and drinks on the descent. The atmosphere is family-oriented and relaxed, with no large-scale nightclubs.
resortCharacterDescription: The resort is based on the traditional Austrian villages of Neukirchen am Großvenediger and Bramberg am Wildkogel, both with small, charming centres featuring local shops, restaurants, and accommodation. The base areas are unassuming, with old gondola stations. The upper mountain is a wide, open basin with modern lift infrastructure. The setting is within the Hohe Tauern National Park, offering scenic views of 3,000-metre peaks.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system includes a 6-seater cable car with a stopover, an 8-person gondola (Smaragdbahn), two high-speed six-chairlifts, two high-speed quad chairlifts, a double chairlift, and several T-bars and surface lifts. The lifts are modern and efficient, with a total capacity of 27,000 persons per hour. The eLINER, a CO2-free mini train, connects the end of the valley run to the Wildkogelbahn valley station. Queues are rare outside peak periods.
linkedResortsDescription: The Wildkogel Arena is not directly linked by lifts to other resorts, but it is in close proximity to the Zillertal Arena and the Kitzbühel Alps, each offering around 300 km of slopes. Combination ski passes are available with the Zillertal Arena from two days, and the SuperSkiCard provides access to multiple ski areas in the Salzburg and Kitzbühel region. A free ski bus connects to the Zillertal Arena.

terrainDescription: The ski area is spread across altitudes from 560m to 840m, with a vertical drop of 260m. The longest run is 2 km, descending from the summit. The terrain is predominantly beginner and intermediate, with wide, gentle slopes that favour progression. Grooming standards are high, and snowmaking covers most of the terrain. Night skiing is available on 6 km of floodlit runs on Wednesdays and Fridays, extending ski time into the evening.
valueDescription: Adult day lift tickets cost €40, with children at €27. Children up to age five ski for free when accompanied by a paying adult. Parking is free with 1,700 spaces available at the lifts. The resort offers early bird tickets from 8:30 am and floodlit evening tickets. Multi-day passes and the Wintersport-Arena Card provide access to multiple Sauerland ski areas, adding value for longer stays.
familyDescription: Family-friendliness is a priority: children up to five years old ski for free with a paying adult. The beginner zones are gentle and separate from main traffic, with dedicated children's areas, magic carpets, and ski schools. The town base means amenities are within walking distance, and the lift infrastructure is modern and user-friendly. Ski school programmes cater to various ages, and the resort has a relaxed, family-oriented atmosphere.
apresSkiDescription: Willingen has a lively après-ski scene centred on mountain huts. The most famous is Siggi's Hütte (Ettelsberghütte) at the summit, known for its party atmosphere and pea soup. The SeilBar at the Ettelsberg gondola base station is a popular spot for end-of-day drinks. The Willinger Brauhaus hosts events and parties. Night skiing on floodlit runs is also available on Wednesdays and Fridays, adding an evening activity option.
resortCharacterDescription: Willingen is a purpose-built winter sports facility in the Sauerland low mountain range, designed to serve the densely populated Rhine-Ruhr region. The town surrounds the ski areas rather than sitting at their base, so most accommodation involves a short walk or shuttle to the lifts. The resort has a functional, no-frills character with modern infrastructure, free parking, and a focus on reliability and accessibility rather than alpine charm.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system includes 15 lifts, among them the K1 8-seater chairlift, noted as the most modern chairlift in Germany, with a capacity of 3,000 people per hour. The Ettelsberg cable car is an 8-seater gondola with a capacity of 2,800 people per hour. There are also 6 T-bar lifts and 6 conveyor belts. The system features contactless 'hands-free' access at all lifts, and online tickets are available. Night skiing uses 6 lifts on floodlit runs.
linkedResortsDescription: Willingen is part of the Wintersport-Arena, a network of Sauerland ski areas. The Wintersport-Arena Card provides access to over 70 lifts across Willingen, Winterberg, Neuastenberg, Altastenberg, Züschen, and Bödefeld, offering a combined 66 km of pistes. This allows skiers to explore multiple connected areas within the region.

terrainDescription: The ski area on the Wurmberg offers 12–13 km of groomed slopes, with 16 marked runs. The longest run is the 4.3 km Panoramaabfahrt (also called Schanzenhang), which descends 405 vertical metres to Braunlage and is the longest ski slope in Germany north of the Alps. The terrain is predominantly easy (blue), with three red runs (Nordhang, Sonnenhang, and the upper part of the Hexenritt) and one black run (Hexenrittpiste, 550 m long with a 65% gradient). Two ungroomed ski routes (Hexenritt and Schanzenauslauf) are reserved for experts. Forest runs provide easy descents into the valley. Snowmaking covers 50% of the slopes using over 100 snow lances, ensuring reliable conditions from December through March.
offPisteDescription: The ski area includes two ungroomed ski routes: the Hexenritt ski route and the Schanzenauslauf ski route, both unmarked and considered expert terrain. The Hexenritt route is a narrow, natural run that is the most difficult descent in the area. Off-piste skiing is possible in the surrounding forest, but there are no designated freeride zones. The resort's low elevation (summit 971 m) and reliance on natural snowfall mean that off-piste conditions are highly dependent on recent storms and can be variable.
valueDescription: Lift pass prices are relatively low compared to Alpine resorts: a one-day adult pass costs €45, a youth pass €39, and a child pass €28. Six-day passes are also available at reduced rates. The resort offers free parking at the base station and a free shuttle bus within Braunlage. Accommodation in Braunlage ranges from budget guesthouses to mid-range hotels, with self-catering apartments available. The overall cost of a ski holiday here is significantly lower than in the Alps, making it a budget-friendly option for families and beginners.
familyDescription: The resort is well-suited for families, with 71% of the slopes rated easy (blue). A magic carpet (Zauberteppich) provides a gentle learning area for beginners. The Ski School Oberharz, located at the Hexenritt base station, offers group and private lessons for children from age four, as well as adult classes. A 1,600 m floodlit toboggan run and a snowtubing area provide additional family activities. Night skiing is available on the 500 m Walpurgis slope every Wednesday, Friday, and Saturday from 18:00 to 21:00. The village of Braunlage is traffic-free in the centre and has a pedestrian-friendly layout.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski is centred on the umbrella bar at the Hexenritt base station, which hosts a daily party from late afternoon. The mountain restaurant Wurmberg Alm, at the summit, offers a cosy interior with a fireplace and a panoramic sun terrace. In Braunlage there are five bars and pubs, two nightclubs, and an ice disco (Eisdisco) held at the local ice rink. The atmosphere is relaxed and family-oriented rather than rowdy, with most venues closing by midnight.
resortCharacterDescription: The Wurmberg is the highest mountain in Lower Saxony (971 m) and the second highest in the Harz, offering panoramic views of the Brocken. The ski area is accessed by a modern gondola lift built in 1963, which is the longest in northern Germany (2.7 km). The historic Wurmberg Ski Jump, built in 1922, was dismantled in 2014 due to rust; an observation tower now stands on the site. The base village of Braunlage is a traditional Harz tourist resort with half-timbered buildings and a car-free centre. The overall character is that of a small, low-mountain ski area with a mix of natural terrain and modern infrastructure.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of five lifts: a monocable gondola (Wurmbergseilbahn, 2.7 km long, built 1963, with a middle station), a four-seat chairlift (Hexenexpress), two T-bar lifts (Hexenlift and Nordlifte), and a magic carpet. The gondola has a capacity of 1,200 persons per hour, and the total lift capacity is 6,534 persons per hour. The lifts are modern and well-maintained, with the gondola providing a 12-minute ride from valley to summit. Queues are rare except during peak holiday periods.

terrainDescription: The Wiriehorn ski area offers 11.5 km of pistes on the north slope of the mountain, with 3 km covered by snowmaking. The terrain is 30% blue (easy), 50% red (intermediate), and 20% black (advanced), including the challenging Hohmad descent. Runs are served by a six-person chairlift and surface lifts, and the compact layout makes navigation straightforward. The area is suitable for both groomed cruising and freeride pistes in steeper sections.
offPisteDescription: Freeride pistes are available in steep terrain, particularly on the north side of Wiriehorn. The resort advises that off-piste skiing is only for experienced riders who know the area, as avalanche conditions and weather must be respected. The Diemtigtal Nature Park emphasizes wildlife protection, and skiers are encouraged to follow designated routes and avoid wildlife resting areas.
valueDescription: Wiriehorn is considered a low-budget ski resort. A one-day adult lift pass costs CHF 55, and children's day tickets are priced at the child's age in Swiss francs (e.g., CHF 6 for a 6-year-old). The resort is part of the Magic Pass, a season pass that includes access to about 40 winter sports areas and 20 summer destinations, offering good value for frequent visitors. Self-catering apartments and group houses directly on the slopes keep accommodation costs down.
familyDescription: The resort is explicitly family-friendly, with a Swiss Snow Kids Village featuring a magic carpet, children's lifts, and a beginner area separate from main slopes. The Swiss Ski School Diemtigtal offers lessons for children and adults at the Wiriehorn location. Accommodation such as the Berghotel Wiriehorn and holiday apartments are ski-in/ski-out, making it easy for families. The compact, easy-to-navigate area means children are unlikely to get lost.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski is low-key, centered on the snow bar at the mountain station and the Berghotel Wiriehorn's sun terrace. Every Friday evening (weather permitting), night skiing and sledging are offered on the illuminated main slope, often combined with a fondue dinner at the mountain hotel. The scene is relaxed, with a focus on family-friendly activities rather than late-night partying.
resortCharacterDescription: Wiriehorn is a small, family-oriented ski area within the Diemtigtal Nature Park, offering an authentic, quiet winter experience close to the city of Bern. The resort consists of a mountain hotel, self-catering apartments, and group houses located directly on the piste, giving it a village-like feel rather than a purpose-built concrete station. The setting is natural and unspoiled, with easy access from the motorway.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system comprises 7 lifts: one six-person chairlift, four T-bars, and two children's lifts, plus a magic carpet in the beginner area. The chairlift is modern and efficient, and the overall network is easy to navigate. Queues are rare outside peak periods. The lifts operate from 9:00 to 16:30 on weekdays and 8:30 to 16:30 on weekends.
linkedResortsDescription: Wiriehorn is the largest of three small ski areas in the Diemtigtal valley, the others being Grimmialp and Springenboden. These areas are not connected by ski lifts, but all are included in the Magic Pass, which also provides access to higher-altitude resorts such as Saas-Fee and Glacier 3000. Within the Diemtigtal, each area operates independently with its own lifts and pistes.

terrainDescription: The terrain at Wolzenalp consists of rolling hills and forest-lined pistes with wide-open views toward the Alpstein mountains. There are gentle nursery slopes for beginners, long red runs that flow naturally through the landscape, and steeper black sections for more confident skiers. The resort relies primarily on natural snow rather than heavy grooming, giving the slopes a more natural feel.
offPisteDescription: Natural snow freeride areas are available when conditions allow, particularly after fresh snowfall. Snowboarders and off-piste skiers use the natural terrain for freeriding, especially in the upper sections where the snowpack is deeper. The resort does not have designated off-piste routes, but the open landscape offers possibilities for adventurous skiers.
valueDescription: A one-day adult lift pass costs CHF 38, and a child pass costs CHF 25. The resort is small and locally oriented, with no flashy infrastructure, keeping overall costs low. Accommodation and dining options in the nearby villages are reasonably priced compared to larger Swiss resorts.
familyDescription: Wolzenalp is considered a family-friendly secret tip. There are dedicated practice lifts and a children's area, with wide, gentle slopes around the Stalden lift where learners can progress safely. The lack of crowds is a major advantage for families, as there are no busy junctions or intimidating lift queues. The resort's simple layout and relaxed atmosphere make it suitable for children and beginners.
resortCharacterDescription: Wolzenalp is a small, natural ski area tucked away in the Toggenburg region of eastern Switzerland. It offers a peaceful, authentic Swiss mountain experience with rolling hills, forest-lined pistes, and wide-open views. The resort has a strong local feel and is not purpose-built, with no flashy infrastructure or high-speed lift networks. It is the kind of place where skiing is about enjoying the mountain rather than chasing vertical.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of 6 lifts: a chairlift from Rietbach (the main access point), a skilift on the upper mountain, a skilift at Stalden (beginner-friendly zone), and practice lifts including a Kinderland area. There are no high-speed gondolas or detachable chairlifts; the infrastructure is simple and low-key, reflecting the resort's natural character. Lifts are uncrowded and queues are rare.

terrainDescription: The ski area sits on a plateau at 1,400 m, accessed by funicular. Of the 22.5 km of pistes, 10.5 km are easy (blue), 10 km intermediate (red), and 2 km difficult (black). The two valley runs are the 4.5 km Gammeringabfahrt (795 m vertical) and the 1.5 km Panoramaabfahrt (500 m vertical). The highest point is Frauenkar at 1,870 m, which offers a black run and a race track. Adventure slopes near the Junior Jet drag lift include a wave run, snake run, speed run, and WISBI run. The terrain is predominantly gentle and well-groomed, with most runs facing north or east, holding snow well into the afternoon.
offPisteDescription: The Frauenkar area (1,870 m) provides the best off-piste access, with a two-seater lift dropping skiers into open slopes and a mix of larch and spruce forest. There are several remote, unprepared slopes and gullies that hold powder after a snowfall. A separate uphill track is marked for skinning, and many skiers use trail skins along the pistes. The terrain includes bump-and-jump forest skiing on the left side of the Frauenkar lift. A guide is recommended in uncertain snow conditions.
familyDescription: The Sunny Kids Park at the plateau includes a practice lift, conveyor belt, ski carousel, and wave runs. The Junior Jet drag lift area features adventure slopes with a wave run, snake run, speed run, and WISBI run. The nursery slopes are wide, gentle, and separated from faster traffic. The ski school offers English-speaking instructors. The resort is compact and walkable, with most accommodation in Spital am Pyhrn a short bus ride away. The free regional ski bus connects the village to the valley station.
apresSkiDescription: The apres-ski scene is low-key. There are 7 bars and pubs, with the Arena Treff near the valley station being the main après-ski spot. Mountain huts such as Linzerhaus and Zellerhütte serve traditional Austrian food and drinks with a mix of music. Occasional torchlight descents and piste-walker evenings are held. The village of Spital am Pyhrn has limited nightlife, with a few hotel bars and a restaurant by the church. Most evening activity centres on the mountain restaurants.
resortCharacterDescription: The resort is set on a natural alpine plateau at 1,400 m, surrounded by the peaks Stubwies, Rote Wand, and Warscheneck. The base village of Spital am Pyhrn is a traditional Austrian town with historic buildings, not a purpose-built resort. The ski area itself consists of rustic wooden huts and modern lift infrastructure. The atmosphere is quiet and authentic, with a focus on nature and uncrowded slopes.
